chapters.title: Episode 1900 with Scott Hanselman!
chapters.description: Experienced podcaster Scott Hanselman discusses career goals and podcast origins. Topics include career transitions, recognizing individual strengths in programming, tech industry mentorship, and humorous anecdotes from past episodes. Reflective atmosphere in hotel lobbies, impact of social media, and memorable moments with guests like Phil Hack and Rob Connery. Celebrating 1900 episodes with mutual appreciation and plans for future episodes.

verses.title: Fostering Success in Early-Career Developers
verses.text: The speaker's goal is to make kids and early-career individuals successful, regardless of age. They aim to identify and nurture the next generation of successful developers, be it young or in their 40s. The speaker praises the integrity of another person and their commitment to speaking the truth, even when unpopular. They also mention a diabetes video where the speaker showcases the reality of managing diabetes, highlighting that despite the pain, it's necessary and part of their routine.

verses.title: Give Feedback Constructively
verses.text: Feedback should be helpful and constructive rather than harsh or elitist. Valid feedback is valuable, but it must be communicated respectfully. Using harsh or mean language in feedback can overshadow the valid points being raised and deter the person receiving the feedback from considering the advice provided.

chapters.title: A Bit of Everything with Adam Grant
chapters.description: Adam Grant, an organizational psychologist and bestselling author, explores a range of intriguing concepts. He discusses the power of vulnerable expression and how sharing unrefined ideas can enhance public speaking. The conversation dives into navigating optimism versus defensive pessimism and the psychological nuances of emotional responses. Grant also highlights humble leadership, emphasizing collaboration over individualism, and the essence of creativity, balancing passion with curiosity. Lastly, he touches on the resurgence of altruism in leadership dynamics, advocating for the importance of collaboration.

verses.title: The Culture of Tearing Apart Ideas
verses.text:
1. Academic culture can prioritize tearing down others' work over building up ideas
2. Signaling intelligence by criticizing others' work may not always be productive
3. Building an idea can require greater intelligence than tearing it down

verses.title: Study shows recovery time for star investors who switch firms
verses.text:
1. Star security analysts on Wall Street leave for new firms thinking the grass is greener.
2. On average, it takes five years to recover your star status after leaving for a new firm.
3. Taking your team with you when you leave a firm has no impact on your performance.

verses.title: The Importance of Humility and Learning from Others
verses.text:
1. No single human being has ever achieved anything by themselves
2. Belief from others is necessary for success
3. Humility is being open to the ideas of others
4. Humility can be broken down into three buckets: learning from others, appreciating others' strengths, and recognizing your own fallibility

verses.title: Worthy Rivalry
verses.text:
1. In an infinite game, competitors are replaced by rivals.
2. Worthy rivals reveal our weaknesses and can cause insecurity or heightened competitiveness.
3. The human response to a worthy rival is often born out of insecurity.

verses.title: The Importance of Worthy Rivals in Personal Growth
verses.text:
1. Learning from people better than you is important
2. Respect those who do good work or something better than you

chapters.title: A Curious Mind with movie producer Brian Grazer
chapters.description: Brian Grazer, the acclaimed film and TV producer behind hits like "A Beautiful Mind,” discusses the rarity of authenticity in storytelling. He emphasizes the power of curiosity and asking questions to connect with others. Grazer shares his resilient journey in the film industry, overcoming challenges to achieve success. He explores the balance of power in creative sectors and reflects on his underdog mentality, viewing setbacks as opportunities for growth. Ultimately, he champions being true to oneself and the transformative influence of supportive relationships.

verses.title: Building Confidence and Defiance through Uncomfortable Experiences
verses.text: The key insight is that exposure to uncomfortable experiences, with the encouragement of someone supportive, can lead to building confidence and the ability to stand up to challenges and adversaries. Trying new things and overcoming humiliating experiences with determination can lead to newfound confidence and the ability to face and challenge the status quo. The message emphasizes the importance of constant exposure to discomfort, whether big or small, in order to build the habit of embracing the uncomfortable.

chapters.title: Ask Simon Anything: Part One
chapters.description: Listeners' burning questions lead to a deep dive into important topics. The conversation covers the necessity of reevaluating priorities post-lockdown and the concept of a 'circle of safety' at work for open expression. ADHD is reframed as a unique advantage in creativity, advocating for empathetic hiring practices. The podcast tackles imposter syndrome and effective leadership, sharing lessons from 'Unreasonable Hospitality.' Finally, it emphasizes adopting a growth mindset and contrasts military training with private sector approaches for a balanced ambition.

verses.title: Overcoming Imposter Syndrome
verses.text: "It's our belief that we're not deserving of being aware of where we are," she says. "I remind myself that I have something of value and I'm here to give it away" It's really about letting go of yourself and trusting in others, the author adds.

verses.title: College is halfway house to adulting
verses.text:
1. College provides an opportunity to learn important skills such as teamwork, creative thinking, pushback, forming an argument, and questioning authority.
2. College also teaches some degree of independence and dependence.
3. The biggest lesson from college is learning to take care of those around us and help others.
4. Despite the prevalence of self-help books, learning to help others is a crucial skill that can be learned in college.

chapters.title: Choose Your Seven Humans Wisely with author Fredrik Backman
chapters.description: Bestselling novelist Fredrik Backman, known for his heartfelt stories, shares insights about friendship and connection. He reveals that cultivating meaningful friendships takes effort, not luck. Fredrik discusses how true friends support and celebrate each other, acting as emotional editors. He emphasizes the importance of investing time in relationships and choosing friends who complement our flaws. With warmth and humor, he offers lessons on being a better friend, underscoring that depth trumps quantity in friendships.

verses.title: Panic-Fueled Viral Speech
verses.text:
- Fredrik Backman panicked giving a speech at Simon & Schuster's centennial after being invited unexpectedly.
- He held a paper as a life raft and delivered an honest, unpolished talk that resonated widely.

💬 Quote

It was pure panic, what you saw on the video.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman on his unexpected, nervous speech at Simon & Schuster's centennial.

verses.title: Bragging Circle Is Smaller Than Support Network
verses.text:
- The people you can brag to without jealousy are fewer than those you turn to in hard times.
- Genuine friends both celebrate your wins and help you edit your emotions before you react publicly.

💬 Quote

The number of people that we can brag to is smaller than the number of people we can go to in hard times.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek reflecting on types of close friendships and celebration.

verses.title: Brag-Worthy Friendships
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek points out that fewer people can be called to brag about successes than those who offer support in hard times.
- He says that the size of the former group speaks volumes about the quality of the friendship.

💬 Quote

It's a smaller group of friends that we can call to brag, that we can call to say, I did something great. Something great happened to me. My book hit the bestseller list. I'm so proud of myself. And there's no jealousy on the other side and there's no envy.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek discussing the different types of friendships.

verses.title: Friend Announces A Priority Shift
verses.text:
- Backman's best friend announced he'd stop Tuesday movie nights to invest time in his girlfriend and later his child.
- That honest conversation modeled mature prioritization and influenced the whole friend group.

💬 Quote

I think this is going to be the last time for a very long time that I watch a movie with you guys.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman recounting his friend's decision to prioritize his relationship.

verses.title: Be The Friend You Want To Get
verses.text:
- Choose to be the friend you want by showing up and offering tangible help like "I'm here, give me something to do." 
- Relationships reward visible effort and time investment more than luck.

💬 Quote

You choose to be that friend first. You choose to show up at someone's house and say, I'm here.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek urging listeners to actively become the friends they seek.

verses.title: Rules for Fighting
verses.text:
- Set up rules for fighting with your partner. 
- Never threaten to end the relationship during a fight, as it's an 'atomic bomb' that ends the discussion.

💬 Quote

We agreed that we would never in a fight no one could say no one could threaten with like maybe we shouldn't be together [...] like no one could do that because that's like an atomic bomb.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman on creating boundaries for fighting

verses.title: Invest In Yourself To Fix Relationships
verses.text:
- Work on yourself rather than the relationship; personal growth sustains relationships long-term.
- Communicate, practice emotions, and set boundaries so differences don't erode connection.

💬 Quote

The work isn't on the relationship... the work is on you.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman discussing how relationship work is personal growth.

verses.title: Fight Resolution
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek shares how he resolved a fight with his girlfriend by changing the rules.
- They focused on acknowledging each other's efforts and rights.

💬 Quote

I'm changing the rules and the rules are, I'm going to tell you what I did wrong. I'm going to tell you what you did right.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on resolving a fight with his girlfriend

verses.title: Show Effort, Don't Just Say It
verses.text:
- Let people see you're trying by taking visible, measurable actions instead of just promising change.
- Actions like showing up and offering help communicate respect and keep relationships alive.

💬 Quote

When you show up at someone's house and say, I'm here, give me something to do, that's the greatest indication of I'm trying.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek describing how visible effort signals commitment in relationships.

verses.title: Choose Your Seven Humans
verses.text:
- You likely only have about seven "humans" who shape your life deeply, separate from ordinary people.
- These chosen humans require ongoing effort and time to deserve and sustain.

💬 Quote

You have maybe seven humans that you chose.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman explaining his idea of a limited number of core people in your life.

verses.title: Few Friends, Deep Time Commitment
verses.text:
- Backman limits close friends because he needs sustained time for people to see his best side amid his extremes.
- He rejects having hundreds of friends to give each person the attention needed to matter.

💬 Quote

I can't have 300 friends because I'm never going to be able to give them the best of me.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman on why he keeps only a few close relationships.

verses.title: Fear Drives A Friend's Overwork
verses.text:
- Backman's friend Riyad overworks to prove he belongs whenever he gains new responsibility.
- That fear of not being enough shaped Riyad's drive and time-focused way of caring for others.

💬 Quote

He felt everyone knows more than I do... So he always felt that he had to outwork everybody.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman describing his friend's insecurity and work ethic.

verses.title: Fear of Disappointing People
verses.text:
- Fredrik Backman describes his fear of disappointing people, leading him to overcommit and react defensively to perceived disappointment. 
- He is actively working on managing this tendency, recognizing how it impacts his relationships, especially with his children.

💬 Quote

This is something that I learned later on in life that I'm dealing, trying to learn now, trying to deal with now. But I think I kind of, I developed this fear of disappointing people, which my wife has told me a lot.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman describing his fear of disappointing people.

verses.title: Expectations and Disappointment
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek says disappointment arises when reality doesn't match our expectations or fantasies. 
- Fredrik Backman adds that he's learned the gesture is the thing, not the reaction, but still struggles with this.

💬 Quote

What happens is we have these expectations we have these fantasies and then what happens is if if real life doesn't match that that's where the disappointment happens.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek discussing expectations.

verses.title: See Yourself In Those You Condemn
verses.text:
- Understanding bullies begins by recognizing shared traits rather than excusing behavior.
- Backman finds his worst fictional characters often mirror his own flaws, which fosters empathy.

💬 Quote

The worst people in my books... are very often from me.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman on writing flawed characters and relating to bullies.

verses.title: Fighting Narcissism
verses.text:
- Fredrik Backman fights his narcissism by surrounding himself with people who are better than him. He believes this helps him from becoming a jerk.

💬 Quote

I have to find people around me who are different from me and better from me so that they constantly remind me not to be a jerk.
— Fredrik Backman

Fredrik Backman on his survival mechanism

chapters.title: Courage with Guy Raz
chapters.description: In a thought-provoking discussion, popular podcaster Guy Raz shares insights from his diverse career as a war correspondent and storyteller. He explores the different dimensions of courage, from personal struggles to confronting uncomfortable societal truths. Raz emphasizes the importance of listening, understanding systemic issues, and engaging in difficult conversations. The conversation also tackles the intersection of American optimism with historical accountability while highlighting the significance of forgiveness and kindness in fostering deeper connections.

verses.title: Introversion and the coins
verses.text:
1. Introversion vs extroversion is about energy levels.
2. Introverts wake up with a limited amount of social energy that is drained with each interaction.
3. Extroverts gain energy from social interactions.

verses.title: The Fear of Judgment
verses.text:
1. The speaker discusses their lack of courage
2. Fear of judgment by outsiders is a vulnerability for the speaker
3. The speaker still desires positive judgment from others, even as a successful adult with a family
4. The idea of having courage to not care about outside opinions is a struggle for the speaker

verses.title: A Reminder Before Speaking: You're Here to Give
verses.text:
1. The speaker has a pre-talk ritual of reminding themselves that they're there to give a talk
2. The reminder helps the speaker stay focused on their agenda and vision
3. The reminder is useful even in unexpected speaking situations
4. The reminder encourages the speaker to take advantage of new opportunities in front of a new audience

verses.title: Courage Has to Have Pursuit
verses.text:
1. The importance of having a trusted friend to have judgment-free conversations with
2. The significance of history and shared background in deepening friendships
3. The idea that pursuing something diminishes the need for courage
4. A conversation about service and sacrifice with Maria Shriver

verses.title: Optimism and the tunnel
verses.text:
1. The book 'The Infinite Game' promotes the idea of playing the game infinitely and being optimistic about it.
2. The speaker, who hosts optimistic shows, sometimes struggles with deep pessimism.
3. The interviewee explains that his optimism is not a denial of reality, but rather a belief in a bright future.

verses.title: What's Your Legacy?
verses.text:
1. Having someone say "I got your back" can solve many problems
2. Courage comes from knowing others have gone before you
3. We are only a part of a journey and have to uphold the struggles of those who came before us

chapters.title: Designing the Future with Brian Collins
chapters.description: Brian Collins, a legendary designer renowned for his transformative work with brands like Spotify and Target, shares his unique perspective on creativity and the future. He discusses the balance between optimism and leadership, emphasizing resilience as a key driver for innovation. Collins reveals insights into navigating challenges like salary disputes with young designers, advocating for mentorship and supportive environments. He reflects on the power of proactive decision-making and the significance of personal growth through experiences of regret and forgiveness.

verses.title: The Power of Optimism and Hope
verses.text:
1. Optimism is a strategy for making a better future
2. Optimism is not blind positivity, but the undying belief that the future is bright
3. Design is hope made visible
4. Hope should push one to take action in emergency situations

verses.title: Instilling Confidence for Inevitable Success
verses.text:
1. Treating employees as if their success is inevitable, rather than just potential, can lead to greater success
2. Teaching young people to envision a bright future and instilling confidence in them can have a positive impact
3. The purpose of leadership is to create more leaders, not just followers
4. Creating a strong community can lead to long-term employee retention and even boomerang employees

verses.title: Culture Fit vs Cultural Contribution in the Workplace
verses.text:
1. The terminology of fitting someone into a culture can have a negative connotation.
2. Contribution is a growth mindset that emphasizes expanding and amplifying.
3. Thinking of someone as a cultural contributor is more positive than simply fitting them into a square hole.

chapters.title: Determination with Steven Pressfield
chapters.description: In this engaging conversation, Steven Pressfield, author of acclaimed works like The Legend of Bagger Vance and The War of Art, dives into the elusive nature of creative success. He introduces the idea of a muse as a crucial element alongside hard work, luck, and talent. Steven shares his winding path to authorship, emphasizing the balance between personal struggle and serendipity. He also explores rituals that invoke creativity and advocates for authenticity, illustrating how openness can create luck and unexpected opportunities.

verses.title: Working out for a hero's workout
verses.text: "Hero's workout" inspired by fallen airman brings new meaning to exercise and motivation. Participants were reminded they were not working out for themselves, but for the airman and pushed themselves to new levels of energy and discipline.

chapters.title: Down A Rabbit Hole with Seth Godin
chapters.description: Seth Godin, a prolific author and marketing whiz, shares his insights on transforming interactions from transactional to transformational through love. He discusses how great ideas emerge from dedication and mentorship, emphasizing authentic connections in a world driven by hustle. Godin also critiques advertising practices, highlighting the need for genuine engagement over interruptions. He delves into the evolving landscape of marketing, the essence of love in creativity and relationships, and the impact of emotional investments over financial ones.

verses.title: The Department of Human Resources Gives Away the Whole Game
verses.text:
1. The phrase 'Human Resources' gives away the idea that humans are treated as a resource rather than the point
2. Advertisers should treat people as people rather than as a bank

verses.title: This might not work
verses.text:
1. Saying no and failing are two of the speaker's best skills
2. Learning to fail was intentional for the speaker
3. Learning to embrace the expression 'This might not work' is important for the speaker
4. Adding 'This might not work' to generous acts brings joy to the speaker
5. The speaker finds setting expectations with 'This might not work' to be genius

chapters.title: Dr. Mark Hyman: To Live Longer You Need...
chapters.description: Dr. Mark Hyman, a leading figure in functional medicine and host of "The Doctor's Pharmacy" podcast, discusses the transformative power of friendship on health. He stresses that close social connections can boost well-being and combat loneliness, which can be as harmful as smoking. The conversation dives into how nutrition impacts relationships and critiques the supplement industry's lack of regulation. Ultimately, Hyman underscores that nurturing friendships and community ties are key to longevity and true happiness.

verses.title: Eating Well as an Act of Service
verses.text:
- We are bad at taking care of our health for ourselves, even if we know it's good for us.
- Consider healthy eating as an act of service to be a better friend, parent, or colleague.

💬 Quote

I choose to eat well so that I can be a better friend to you. I choose to eat well so that I can be a better parent to my kids. So I'm less grumpy and less agitated.
— Mark Hyman

Mark Hyman on eating well as an act of service.

verses.title: Social Networks' Influence
verses.text:
- Our social networks influence our health more than our genes or family history.
- We're more likely to be overweight if our friends are, highlighting the contagious nature of habits.

💬 Quote

Your social networks are driving your behavior for good or bad. [...] You're only as healthy as your five closest friends.
— Mark Hyman

Mark Hyman on the impact of social networks on health

verses.title: Community in Sardinia and Icaria
verses.text:
- In Sardinia and Icaria, strong community connections are evident, with people caring for each other and finding purpose in their communities.
- This demonstrates the importance of social connection for well-being, even in old age.

💬 Quote

Stunning to see the level of community and connection. [...] people just took care of each other and it was really remarkable.
— Mark Hyman

Mark Hyman on community in blue zones

verses.title: Loneliness: A Silent Killer
verses.text:
- Loneliness is a significant health risk, comparable to smoking two packs of cigarettes daily.
- Many men lack close friends, highlighting a need for fostering deeper connections.

💬 Quote

Loneliness is as big a killer as anything else. Some have said it's equivalent to smoking two packs of cigarettes a day.
— Mark Hyman

Mark Hyman on the dangers of loneliness

verses.title: Sociogenomics and Relationships
verses.text:
- Our social interactions directly impact our gene expression, influencing inflammation levels.
- Conflictual relationships turn on inflammatory genes, while loving relationships turn on anti-inflammatory genes.

💬 Quote

If you're in a conflictual relationship with someone, your inflammatory genes are turned on. [...] Conversely, if you have a connected, loving relationship with somebody, it turns on anti-inflammatory genes.
— Mark Hyman

Mark Hyman on sociogenomics

verses.title: True Friendship
verses.text:
- Close friends are there for you during struggles. 
- Even closer friends share your joy without jealousy when something amazing happens.

💬 Quote

But I think there's even a closer level of friendship, which is when you can call somebody when something amazing happened. [...] And there's no jealousy.
— Mark Hyman

Mark Hyman on levels of friendship.

verses.title: Finding Friends Through Hobbies
verses.text:
- Find friends by pursuing hobbies and joining groups with shared interests.
- Shared activities provide easy icebreakers and opportunities for connection.

💬 Quote

Starting with common interests [...] sign up for a ceramics class [...] or go with a friend, but talk to the person you're sitting next to.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek's advice on making friends

verses.title: The Value of Friendship
verses.text:
- Society often wrongly judges people for not having long-term romantic relationships.
- Strong friendships can provide deep fulfillment, even without a traditional partnership.

💬 Quote

I'm seen as unhealthy [...] having commitment issues. [...] I have a lot of really, really good friends. And I am fulfilled in almost every aspect of my life.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on societal judgment and the value of friendship

chapters.title: Exploration with Leland Melvin
chapters.description: Leland Melvin, a former astronaut, NFL player, and passionate educator, shares his journey of resilience and exploration. He discusses the transformative power of second chances and accountability in changing lives. Leland reflects on the unique optimism and camaraderie essential for astronauts during high-pressure situations. He also delves into the profound emotions experienced from the 'overview effect' of space travel, emphasizing Earth's interconnectedness and the need for sustainable practices. His insights celebrate curiosity and the value of mentorship in inspiring future explorers.

verses.title: Giving Second Chances
verses.text:
1. It's important to give people second chances, especially those who are disenfranchised and don't get a fair first shot
2. Giving someone a second chance can lead to them becoming your best employee
3. Accountability is key when giving second chances, as seen in the example of the professor making the speaker a research assistant

chapters.title: Failing Well with professor of leadership Dr. Amy Edmondson
chapters.description: Amy Edmondson, a Harvard Business School Professor of Leadership and Management, shares insights on transforming our relationship with failure. She discusses how failure can inspire innovation and growth, using examples from 3M’s culture of embracing mistakes. Edmondson highlights the importance of psychological safety in teams, where trust allows individuals to take risks without fear. She also addresses how effective leadership nurtures vulnerability and communication, paving the way for continuous improvement and creativity in organizations.

verses.title: The Semantic Difference Between Failure, Mistakes, and Discoveries
verses.text: The word 'failure' encompasses a broad range of situations, making it difficult to distinguish between different types of failures. To overcome this, we can use words like 'mistake' to describe a specific type of failure where we didn't do something the right way. On the other hand, 'discovery' is a word that represents a positive outcome, even though many discoveries are actually failures. It's important to recognize that failures can lead us closer to finding what works.

verses.title: The Importace of Psychological Safety in Building Trust
verses.text: Psychological trust in a group leads to greater trustworthiness among individuals. In a circle of safety, when there is a lack of psychological safety, people become less trustworthy and focus on themselves. This creates an uncomfortable and unhappy environment. It is more enjoyable to be part of a united 'we' working towards exciting goals. Leaders play a crucial role in offering team psychological safety, akin to a supportive parent. They focus on addressing problems collectively rather than blaming individuals.

verses.title: The Importance of Innovation and Efficiency in Toyota's Production System
verses.text: The Toyota production system emphasizes engineering learning into production, seeking ideas for improvement from the front line, and prioritizing constant improvement over merely enforcing efficiency. This approach involves encouraging employees to share concerns and improvement opportunities, and operationalizing a system for this purpose, rather than just focusing on efficiency. The system aims to promote a learning behavior and collaboration, overriding traditional human instincts and socialization habits.

verses.title: Overcoming Fear and Leading by Example
verses.text: Expressing fear as a leader can encourage team members to open up about their own fears, creating a supportive environment. Acknowledging and sharing mistakes as a leader makes it easier for others to do the same. Even successful performers like Lionel Richie have experienced crippling stage fright, but the key is to step forward despite the fear, distinguishing between a hero and a coward.

chapters.title: Finding your Tribe with Amy Cuddy
chapters.description: Amy Cuddy, a renowned social psychologist and Harvard professor, dives into the essence of belonging and community. She reflects on her love for the Grateful Dead and how their concerts foster unique social connections. The discussion covers authentic relationships in the digital age and the role of nonverbal cues in online interactions. Cuddy emphasizes the importance of kindness and shared values in creating a true sense of belonging, exploring how micro moments of love can significantly impact our lives.

verses.title: Culture is values plus behaviour
verses.text:
1. Nonverbal and vocal cues can provide context to communication, even when communicating online with just words.
2. Body language and other nonverbal cues can convey emotions and intentions to others.
3. Using signs or symbols can be a way to signal openness and kindness to others, even in online communication.
4. Signaling kindness and openness can potentially unlock similar feelings in others.

chapters.title: Guaranteed Joy with Richard Curtis
chapters.description: Richard Curtis, renowned writer and director behind iconic films like "Love Actually" and "Notting Hill," shares his insights on joy and love. He emphasizes that true happiness is found in ordinary moments, not just grand gestures. Curtis reflects on the beauty of creativity intertwined with philanthropy, highlighting the spirit of initiatives like Comic Relief. The conversation also navigates the complexities of love and relationships while showcasing how joy inspires generosity and meaningful connections.

verses.title: The Really Happy Moment
verses.text:
1. 95% of our happiest moments are nothing days and are easily forgotten
2. Winning awards or having parties are memorable, but the truly happy moments are in the everyday moments
3. It's important to treasure the small moments and appreciate them

chapters.title: Happier-ness with author Arthur Brooks
chapters.description: Arthur Brooks, an expert on happiness and co-author of "Build the Life You Want" with Oprah Winfrey, discusses the ongoing journey of achieving happiness, or 'happier-ness.' He emphasizes that true fulfillment isn't a destination but a practice rooted in relationships and service. Brooks reframes negative emotions as essential signals and promotes viewing mental health as a continuous journey. He highlights the importance of community and shared experiences in fostering genuine happiness, encouraging self-reflection and purpose through personal achievements.

verses.title: The Impact of Hierarchy on Business Priorities
verses.text: The hierarchy of business priorities often places growth and shareholder value above the importance of employees and social responsibility. This hierarchy, prevalent among CEOs and entrepreneurs, can hinder the emphasis on serving society and giving back. When the hierarchy is reversed to prioritize doing good and serving society over financial success, it is more conducive to driving innovation, inspiration, and ultimately fostering feelings of fulfillment, joy, and happiness.

verses.title: Belief in Something Bigger
verses.text: Belief in something bigger, whether it's a God, a higher power, or a cause, is essential. It's important to believe in something that is bigger than oneself and to choose to serve it.

verses.title: Ideas on Modulating the ADHD Dial
verses.text: Understanding the dial of adult ADHD can help therapeutically modulate it, rather than turning it off completely. The aim is not to switch off ADHD, but to learn techniques to mitigate the challenges and amplify the strengths associated with it.

verses.title: Understanding Autism Spectrum and Embracing Human Imperfections
verses.text: Autism is not a malady, but a set of conditions that become problematic when they interfere with daily life. Each person exhibits characteristics of various conditions, and being human means being imperfect, emotional, and irrational. Living the desired life involves understanding oneself, serving others, and accepting imperfections. Progress is about enhancing strengths, managing weaknesses, and mitigating distress, leading to constant progress and happiness.

verses.title: Understanding Happiness Through Science
verses.text: Happiness can be understood through science, and it involves three key components: enjoyment, satisfaction, and purpose. By focusing on elements such as faith, family, friendship, and service to others, it is possible to make emotional self-management a reality and build the life you desire.

verses.title: The Difference Between Pleasure and Enjoyment
verses.text: Pleasure is a limbic phenomenon involving the production of dopamine in the brain, while enjoyment involves the source of pleasure, people, and memory. The pursuit of pleasure never brings happiness, as it can lead to compulsive behavior and isolation, whereas enjoyment can be experienced in love and communion with others.

verses.title: The Problem with Social Media and Alone Pleasure
verses.text: The problem with social media is that it provides immense pleasure, but often in solitude. Engaging with people and creating memories together reduces the tendency for excessive solitary indulgence. This applies not only to social media, but also to other activities like alcohol, gambling, and sex. Therefore, the key lies in being with people and making memories, rather than seeking pleasure alone.

chapters.title: Happying with Derren Brown
chapters.description: Derren Brown, a renowned mentalist and illusionist, delves into the fascinating world of human behavior and happiness. He emphasizes that happiness is an evolving journey, shaped by our interactions and relationships. Derren discusses how defining actionable values can transform both corporate and personal connections. He shares insights on personal growth, ambitions, and the Stoic view of happiness, highlighting the significance of inner satisfaction over external validation. Ultimately, Derren advocates for a life focused on service and meaningful connections.

verses.title: How to Write Values as Verbs
verses.text:
1. Corporate values should be written as verbs instead of nouns to make them actionable
2. Verbs make values measurable and allow for accountability
3. Examples of value verbs include telling the truth, doing the right thing, and looking at problems from a different angle

verses.title: Was My Life Worth Living?
verses.text:
1. The first half of life is focused on ambition and staking a claim in the world, while the second half seems to be more about serving something bigger and finding a relationship between the ego and the self.
2. Once you've achieved personal goals, there is a need to shift focus towards involving oneself in something bigger.
3. Many people become aware of this shift in priorities after a certain point in their lives.
4. Digging into the relationship between the ego and the self can lead to a deeper understanding of one's purpose in life.

chapters.title: Having a Laugh with Michael McIntyre
chapters.description: In this lively discussion, Michael McIntyre, one of the world’s top comedians known for his infectious observational humor, shares hilarious anecdotes about performing during the pandemic. He reflects on the absurdity of virtual shows and the importance of laughter for connection. The duo exchanges playful stories about vaccine experiences and writing during lockdown, all while emphasizing the small joys that have emerged amidst challenges. Their comedic banter serves as a reminder of laughter's essential role in overcoming tough times.

verses.title: Laughter and ticking
verses.text:
1. Comedians play a vital role in society.
2. Laughter can relieve anger.
3. Tickling can stimulate the brain.

verses.title: Write rubbish
verses.text:
1. The speaker has produced a TV show that will be aired on MBC next year
2. The speaker keeps notes of funny things and performs them at gigs to build material
3. The speaker's home life is relaxed and not overly creative
4. The speaker finished writing a book and a TV show and now needs to keep busy

chapters.title: High Performance with Divesh Makan
chapters.description: Divesh Makan, founder of Iconiq Capital, expertly advises some of the wealthiest individuals globally. In this engaging conversation, he highlights the significance of seeking guidance from experienced mentors and using frameworks to boost productivity. Divesh shares insights on the challenges faced by high achievers, emphasizing the need for authentic dialogue to build trust. He discusses the intersection of technology and curiosity, stressing the importance of creativity through disconnection from digital distractions and nurturing personal relationships for professional success.

verses.title: Learning from the past to improve the future
verses.text:
1. Reflecting on the past can help identify reasons for not achieving goals.
2. Learning from past mistakes can improve future outcomes.
3. The Air Force uses evaluations to prevent making past mistakes and improve future missions.

verses.title: The Importance of Likeability, Trust, and Intelligence in the Workplace
verses.text:
1. People decide to work with you based on whether they like you, trust you, and think you're smart, in that order.
2. Proving how smart you are won't matter if people don't like or trust you.
3. Being yourself is important for building relationships in finance and in life.

chapters.title: How To Be A Friend with chef Christina Tosi
chapters.description: Christina Tosi, the innovative founder and CEO of Milk Bar, dives deep into the importance of friendship and vulnerability. She shares heartfelt stories about the comfort desserts can provide, especially during tough times. Tosi emphasizes that asking for help is a strength, not a weakness, and highlights the essence of nurturing relationships. The conversation also includes her unique virtual baking event, Bait Club, which celebrates community and connection. Plus, a delightful ice cream loaf recipe is revealed to sweeten your day!

verses.title: Empower Friends During Highs and Lows
verses.text: The insight here highlights the significance of being a supportive friend during both high and low moments, not just when someone is in need. By fostering open conversations and providing a safe space for vulnerability, friends can empower each other to navigate through difficult times with strength. This approach encourages individuals to embrace the fluctuations in emotions, acknowledging that highs and lows are a natural part of life. Rather than offering support only when someone is in pain, proactively building a foundation of trust and understanding enables friends to lean on each other during challenging moments, creating a deeper and more resilient friendship.

verses.title: Buying Insurance Before the Fire
verses.text: The insight emphasizes the importance of acknowledging and preparing for hard times during good times. It compares offering support to friends in times of need to buying insurance when the house is already on fire, highlighting the significance of proactively addressing challenges before they arise. By being prescient in high times, celebrating, and creating support systems in advance, individuals are better equipped to navigate difficulties together when they occur, thereby 'buying the insurance early.' This insight underscores the value of recognizing the impermanence of good times and fostering strong relationships to weather future storms.

verses.title: The Normalcy and Importance of Asking for Help
verses.text: Asking for help is normal and nice. It is essential to communicate clearly when in need, as sending casual texts may not convey the true emotional state or need for support. This story highlights the importance of being direct and reaching out for assistance when going through tough times.

verses.title: The Power of a Simple Text Message
verses.text: Sending a text message to a friend asking 'do you have eight minutes?' is a profound gesture of care and compassion. It signifies to the friend how much they are valued and how important their well-being is. The speaker believes that being the type of friend who sends such a message is the pinnacle of friendship. True friendship is shown through small but impactful actions, like a spontaneous call or a short walk, where meaningful connections are made. The duration of the interaction does not matter as much as the quality of the connection established during that time. It is emphasized that reaching out to friends during moments of darkness or need is not a burden, but a privilege. This kind of friendship is treasured over having a multitude of acquaintances, as it reflects a deep level of care and support.

verses.title: Sacrifice and Impact
verses.text: Sacrifice should feel worth it in terms of the impact it has. Working hard and sacrificing rest or personal time is justifiable when it brings joy and lasting memories to loved ones. Memories are precious and we mostly remember the things that matter the most, like moments of joy and shared experiences.

chapters.title: How To Be An Entrepreneur with Jeff Rosenthal
chapters.description: Jeff Rosenthal, co-founder of Summit Series and creator of Powder Mountain ski resort, dives into the power of partnerships in entrepreneurship. He argues that while solo ventures may be fast, true success comes from collaboration and community. Throughout the discussion, Rosenthal emphasizes the importance of generosity and ethical decision-making. He shares personal anecdotes that highlight the value of vulnerability, mentorship, and navigating one's entrepreneurial journey with enthusiasm and passion.

verses.title: Tell Me The Challenges
verses.text:
1. The speaker wishes they had harnessed the power of mentors earlier in their 20s
2. The speaker recognizes the presence of confirmation bias in their ideas
3. Mentorship can help people navigate paths that have already been walked by others
4. Being a good mentee can be learned through the guidance of experienced mentors
5. An effective mentor-mentee relationship involves openness about challenges and difficulties

verses.title: The Importance of Push Back
verses.text:
1. Partnerships are important in entrepreneurship
2. The original Star Wars episodes were made differently than the more modern ones
3. George Lucas had powerful special effects people following him around during the making of episode one, two, and three

verses.title: On Consistency and Growth Mindset
verses.text:
1. Being misunderstood is not necessarily a bad thing and can indicate growth
2. Having a growth mindset means being willing to be wrong and change
3. A corporate mentality seeks consistency while an entrepreneurial mentality embraces change and growth

chapters.title: Ideas That Stick with advertising legend Lee Clow
chapters.description: Lee Clow, a legendary advertising creative director known for his work with Apple and the Energizer Bunny, shares captivating insights from his illustrious career. He discusses the groundbreaking 1984 Apple commercial, emphasizing its storytelling impact and cultural relevance. Clow reflects on his collaboration with Steve Jobs and the importance of nurturing creativity in advertising. He also touches on the evolution of advertising and the power of mentorship in fostering talent. Expect anecdotes that reveal the boldness and innovation behind iconic campaigns.

verses.title: Challenging People to Exceed Their Limits
verses.text: A true genius, like Steve Jobs, has the ability to envision beyond the present, set high expectations for others, and push them to exceed their own limits. By challenging individuals to do more than they thought possible, he inspired them to achieve remarkable feats. For instance, Johnny Ives became a renowned product designer because Jobs pushed him to think innovatively. Jobs' fearless leadership style and trust in intuition set him apart from most CEOs, who typically shy away from creativity and intuition. Steve Jobs showed that by continually pushing people to be better than they believed they could be, extraordinary accomplishments can be achieved.

chapters.title: Insane Vacations with Mike Reiss
chapters.description: Mike Reiss, a legendary comedy writer famed for his work on The Simpsons, shares his wild adventures as a traveler. He regales listeners with thrilling tales of diving into the Titanic wreck in a homemade submarine, exploring the quirks of North Korea, and even humorously applying for a Mars mission. The conversation transitions into the creative process behind the beloved Minions from Despicable Me, underscoring the power of collaboration in animation. With each story, Mike's unique blend of humor and insight shines through, making for an unforgettable journey.

verses.title: Tips for a Successful Relationship and Embracing Adventure
verses.text: And I always say, the only two good things ever created by friction are fire and babies.

chapters.title: Life Worth Living with Miroslav Volf
chapters.description: Miroslav Volf, a Yale professor and theologian, dives deep into the essence of a fulfilling life. He critiques the conventional views of individualism, arguing for the vital role of community in our quest for self-actualization. Volf shares poignant stories, including a moving account of resilience amidst despair, illustrating how hope can thrive even in darkness. The conversation also highlights the transformative power of mutual support and the importance of cherishing the present in crafting a meaningful life.

verses.title: The Illusion of Success: Copying Habits and Studios
verses.text: We often seek to imitate successful people's habits in hopes of achieving the same level of success. But it's like thinking that building the same studio as Picasso will make us Picasso or worrying what others will think of our studio. It's important to focus on our own unique talents and creations.

verses.title: Stronger or broken
verses.text: A man's act of faith transforms his father, inspiring a realization of a more fundamental reality. Similarly, a Holocaust survivor's remarkable story of giving birth on a train to a concentration camp showcases the power of resilience.

chapters.title: Live Like You Have 2 Years Left with dancer and cancer survivor Angela Trimbur
chapters.description: Angela Trimbur, a dancer, actress, and cancer survivor, shares her transformative journey after a breast cancer diagnosis. She discusses how facing mortality pushed her to live intentionally, prioritizing joy and meaningful connections. Angela's irreverent teaching style brings dance to life, creating an inclusive space for self-expression. She emphasizes the liberating power of dance and its role in personal healing, urging us to embrace our time and foster community. Her infectious optimism inspires us to pursue fulfillment, no matter the circumstances.

verses.title: Viral Dance Video Origins
verses.text:
- Angela Trimbur's viral dance video "Dance Like Nobody's Watching" started spontaneously from a personal moment at a laundromat.  
- It unexpectedly launched her dance career and public recognition years before her cancer diagnosis.

💬 Quote

I just went and I put headphones in and I filmed myself dancing and nobody looked at me or anything. Nobody even acknowledged me. And it turned into like, okay, dance like nobody's watching because nobody was.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur on the origin of her viral dance video

verses.title: Slightly Guided Dance Parties
verses.text:
- Angela Trimbur started 'slightly guided dance parties' after noticing people at parties needed prompting to dance freely. 
- Her parties involved 60% guidance, and 40% free dance time, proving to be a fun and effective way to encourage participation.

💬 Quote

And everyone seemed relieved to know what they should be doing. And I was like, oh, that's what they just need to be slightly guided.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur talking about people needing guidance to dance.

verses.title: Choose Low-Stress Paths
verses.text:
- Choose any action that causes the least stress to preserve mental peace.  
- Let go of tense commitments and trust the process of life unfolding.

💬 Quote

Whatever makes you the least amount of stress, that's the decision that you make.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur on managing stress after cancer diagnosis

verses.title: The Two-Year Life Outlook
verses.text:
- Living as if you have two years left balances urgency and patience, inspiring meaningful life changes.  
- This mindset led Angela to sell everything and start fresh as a dance teacher in New York City.

💬 Quote

If I only had two years left... no, I'd want to let everything go that I own. I'd want to move to Manhattan and be a dance teacher.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur on adopting a two-year perspective after cancer diagnosis

verses.title: Say No Without Explanation
verses.text:
- Learn to say no without feeling the need to justify your decisions.  
- People generally accept your choices without demanding explanations, freeing you from unnecessary stress.

💬 Quote

The answer is I don't want to do it. And that's it. And people are fine with it. I found nobody... slams their hand on the table and says give me the reason.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on setting boundaries without justification

verses.title: Playful Beginner Ballet Class
verses.text:
- Angela created a beginner ballet class where participants playfully pretend to be professional ballerinas without pressure.  
- This approach addresses exclusion and ballet trauma by promoting fun over technique.

💬 Quote

I'm like, I want to do this... I don't care about technique I just want to wear the outfits and jump around and do arabesques...
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur on designing accessible ballet core classes

verses.title: Storytelling in Dance Healing
verses.text:
- Dance routines tell stories that allow emotional expression and personal healing.  
- The storytelling aspect helps participants feel seen and connected beyond just movement.

💬 Quote

That story is you. I mean, you did the thing not for the likes and not for the follows. You did the thing for yourself. You did the thing for the joy.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on meaning in Angela's dance stories

verses.title: Healing Through Dance Community
verses.text:
- Bring people together with shared goals to create healing communities.  
- Use intentional, guided activities to help participants open up and connect authentically.

💬 Quote

Even an introvert is there and maybe they're like I'm shy... but they are forced to do so when we're doing the awkward prom... to connect.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur on creating community in dance classes

verses.title: Childhood Homeschooled Isolation
verses.text:
- Angela was homeschooled as a child due to her mother's religious conversion, leading to a feeling of isolation.  
- She and her sister longed to be part of the outside world, vividly remembering watching kids at the bus stop.

💬 Quote

We wanted to go back to school so bad. So every day at 315, we'd watch the people get off the bus together... wishing we could be a part of it.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur recalling her homeschooling childhood experience

verses.title: Visible vs Invisible Healing
verses.text:
- Some healing is invisible, revealed only later when patterns and reactions improve.  
- Visible healing like therapy is obvious, but inner life lessons often go unnoticed outwardly.

💬 Quote

Healing we do that is invisible... you don't get to see like, oh, now I see why I made that mistake... but you'll see it within yourself next time.
— Angela Trimbur

Angela Trimbur on different types of healing

chapters.title: Mastery is an Infinite Game with performance psychologist Mike Gervais
chapters.description: In this engaging discussion, high-performance psychologist Mike Gervais shares his insights on achieving mastery without succumbing to burnout. He highlights how focusing on purpose over mere outcomes fosters sustainable excellence. Mike shares intriguing stories, including a samurai sword maker's journey, illustrating the pitfalls of conventional success metrics. He emphasizes the importance of developing a purpose-based identity and mental resilience, empowering individuals to navigate confidence, trauma, and the pressures of high performance.

verses.title: Confidence Comes From Self-Improvement
verses.text: Confidence stems from continuously working on mastering oneself and improving skills to meet the challenges ahead. It is a blend of lacking ego while possessing self-confidence to stick with the journey. True confidence only arises from a credible self-dialogue, where constant self-improvement is the key driver.

verses.title: Trauma of Layoffs and Loneliness in Younger Generation
verses.text: The younger generation is deeply impacted by the trauma of layoffs, as many have experienced family members or friends being laid off despite loyalty and hard work, leading to a fear of being re-traumatized. This has resulted in a generation feeling alone and under pressure, with loneliness now more prevalent among 14 to 21-year-olds than among the elderly. The younger generation resents demands for loyalty in work environments and is not simply seeking to work harder.

verses.title: Reframe Tense Moments as Exciting Opportunities
verses.text: Learning to reframe moments of self-doubt and fear as exciting opportunities can shift nerves into excitement. By reframing situations positively, one can let go of competitive pressure and focus on the privilege of sharing the stage with talented individuals, leading to a mindset shift from insecurity to enthusiasm.

chapters.title: Should You Work With Your Friends? with my friend Will Guidara
chapters.description: Will Guidara, renowned restaurateur and author of 'Unreasonable Hospitality,' explores the challenges of mixing friendship with business. He shares insights on balancing humility and confidence, emphasizing how support enhances personal success. The discussion dives into the emotional dynamics of professional partnerships, highlighting the importance of honesty and vulnerability. They also address the complexities of friendship breakups and the necessity of effective communication in maintaining both personal and professional relationships.

verses.title: Addressing Imbalance
verses.text:
- Guidara felt under-invested in a friendship, so he addressed the imbalance directly. 
- This honesty led to a stronger friendship, showing how addressing issues can be beneficial.

💬 Quote

I called him. I was like, hey, I'm investing too much into this friendship and not getting nearly enough back from you.
— Will Guidara

Will Guidara on a turning point in a friendship

verses.title: Deprioritizing Friends
verses.text:
- Don't cancel on friends for meetings, assuming they'll understand. 
- It's dismissive to assume friends are fine with being constantly deprioritized.

💬 Quote

Would you cancel on a meeting for a friend or would you cancel on a friend for a meeting?
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on how we treat friends

verses.title: Currencies of Friendship
verses.text:
- Different friendships have different currencies, like quality time versus frequent interaction. 
- Recognizing this helps understand varying needs and maintain healthy relationships.

💬 Quote

Every friendship has its own currency. Like you and I, when we are together, we are all in, whether it's for 20 minutes or an hour or a day.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the dynamics of friendships

verses.title: Business with Friends
verses.text:
- When considering business with friends, ensure similar motivations and clear expectations. 
- Discuss commitment levels openly to avoid assumptions and resentment.

💬 Quote

I think you can go into business with friends so long as everything's on the table. Don't leave anything up to assumption.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on going into business with friends

verses.title: Dual Relationships
verses.text:
- When going into business with friends, maintain both friend and colleague relationships. 
- This requires code-switching and compartmentalizing to respect each dynamic.

💬 Quote

- You don't replace the friendship relationship with a colleague relationship. You now have two relationships.
— Will Guidara

Will Guidara on managing multiple relationships

verses.title: Respect in Relationships
verses.text:
- Respect others for who they are and what they do, in addition to being their friend.
- Offer public support, fight in private, and once a decision is made, support it 100%.

💬 Quote

You have to respect the person for who they are, for what they are doing, what they're trying to do, in addition to being their friend.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on maintaining respect in friendships.

verses.title: Embracing Second Acts
verses.text:
- Guidara sees his career shift as a surprising but positive second act. 
- He emphasizes everyone's potential for new chapters, encouraging exploration and change.

💬 Quote

Everyone has another chapter in them [...] it's just a matter of flipping the page and seeing what's on the next one.
— Will Guidara

Will Guidara on life's different chapters

chapters.title: Supercommunicators with journalist Charles Duhigg
chapters.description: Charles Duhigg, a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist and author of Supercommunicators, shares insights on the art of communication. He reveals how great communicators are made through practice, not born with innate talent. The importance of storytelling in conveying complex ideas is emphasized, as well as the essential role of honesty in high-stakes situations, illustrated by a CIA officer's experience. Duhigg also discusses the power of vulnerability in fostering connections and how genuine dialogue can bridge differing viewpoints to create unity.

verses.title: The Importance of Bringing People on the Journey
verses.text: Bringing people on the journey is the crucial aspect of teaching ideas. Rather than being the expert, inviting others to join and experience the journey together creates joy and understanding. In storytelling, while the beginning and end get a lot of attention, the middle is where the significant events occur.

verses.title: The Power of Storytelling in Making Ideas Memorable
verses.text: Storytelling is powerful in making ideas memorable as it helps in embedding complex ideas or research findings in a narrative that people can easily carry with them. Unlike reading scientific studies that are quickly forgotten, stories stay in people's memories and are easier to recall. By incorporating ideas into a story, they become more relatable and portable, making them simpler for individuals to retain and share.

chapters.title: Teammateship with NBA champion Shane Battier
chapters.description: Shane Battier, a former NBA champion celebrated for his exceptional role as a teammate, shares insights on the importance of 'teammateship' in both sports and life. He discusses how true success comes from prioritizing team dynamics over personal accolades. Battier reflects on his journey, emphasizing resilience and trust to overcome challenges. He highlights the need for inclusivity and support within teams, advocating for a cultural shift towards collaboration. His anecdotes reveal that greatness is rooted in shared experiences and the strength of community.

verses.title: Team Mentality in Basketball
verses.text: The speaker highlights the importance of a team mentality in basketball, emphasizing the significance of helping teammates succeed for overall team success. The speaker reflects on their 30 years of experience at the highest level, realizing that by making friends look good and prioritizing team success over personal success, they were accepted, loved, and valued as a valuable team member. This mindset of contributing to the team's success and supporting teammates is key to achieving success in basketball.

verses.title: Building Trust and Consistency in Team Dynamics
verses.text: Building trust in a team is crucial to ensure consistent effort, especially during times of adversity and stress. This trust is developed through repetition and consistency, distinguishing between investing in underperforming teammates and gambling on inconsistent ones. By investing time and effort in coaching and supporting underperformers to improve gradually, teams can achieve consistent increases in performance and build a strong, reliable team dynamic.

verses.title: The Power of the Next Play in Basketball and in Life
verses.text: Consistency is developed through embracing the power of the next play, understanding that each basketball possession offers numerous ways to make an impact, be it positive or negative. The key is to focus on the next play rather than dwelling on past successes or failures. In both basketball and life, moving on to the next play with discipline, enthusiasm, and focus is crucial for success. Winners possess the mentality of next play speed, allowing them to outperform their opponents and achieve success.

verses.title: Introducing Chaos for Success
verses.text: To maintain success, regardless of skill level, introducing chaos into the system is crucial. Bringing in diversity and new experiences, like adding a different player to the team, forces individuals and the team as a whole to step out of their comfort zone, re-examine their methods, and stay alert. This controlled chaos is necessary to prevent complacency and continuously challenge oneself and the team. Without introducing chaos, the competitive edge is lost.

chapters.title: The Book of Charlie with David Von Drehle
chapters.description: David Von Drehle, an award-winning journalist and author of "The Book of Charlie," shares life lessons from his 109-year-old neighbor, Charlie. They discuss the secrets of longevity and happiness, emphasizing positivity, simplicity, and resilience in the face of life’s unpredictability. The conversation also touches on the intersection of personal struggles and leadership, as well as the importance of community and forgiveness in overcoming tragedies. With optimism, they explore how individual actions contribute to a better future.

verses.title: The Complexity and Simplicity of Life
verses.text: Meeting inspiring people is like looking into a mirror that reflects our own hopes and opportunities. Charlie challenged my world perspective by making me realize that as young people, we complicate things and see the complexity of the world. But as we grow older, we become simplifiers and understand that dealing with the complexity is actually simple.

verses.title: Life's Balancing Act
verses.text: Living in the present moment is essential, as depression dwells on the past and anxiety fears the future. Courageously sharing experiences of depression is important. Many successful individuals have faced depression, like Abraham Lincoln, who believed in moving forward. Life is a balance between challenges and opportunities.

chapters.title: The Brilliance of Boredom with creative polymath Elle Cordova
chapters.description: Elle Cordova, a creative polymath and singer-songwriter known for her nerdy comedy videos, discusses the surprising power of boredom to spark creativity. She shares her journey during the pandemic, discovering new artistic avenues while making viral sketches. The conversation touches on anxiety, writer's block, and finding what inspires you. Elle also dives into the fun debate of Star Wars vs. Star Trek and treats listeners to a live performance of her song "Roswell," blending humor and introspection effortlessly.

verses.title: Curiosity As a Driving Force
verses.text:
- Curiosity drives Elle Cordova's creative exploration across many subjects.
- She treats English and reading as gateways to broader knowledge and ideas.

💬 Quote

Curiosity is sort of my motivating force in life.
— Elle Cordova

Elle Cordova on why she explores many topics

verses.title: Blackjack Led To Bans
verses.text:
- Elle learned advantage play in blackjack to minimize losses and sit longer with her mom.
- That skill eventually got her backed off and banned from several Las Vegas casinos.

💬 Quote

So I've been backed off and banned from probably close to like six, seven casinos now in Vegas.
— Elle Cordova

Elle Cordova recounting being banned from casinos

verses.title: Lockdown Opened New Creative Doors
verses.text:
- When tours stopped in the pandemic, Elle Cordova suddenly had space to create again.
- She shifted from solely music to poems and comedic sketches that reached new audiences.

💬 Quote

When everything got canceled, it was such a blessing in disguise, because then I rediscovered my creativity.
— Elle Cordova

Elle Cordova on how the pandemic changed her creative path

verses.title: Boredom Sparks Creative Breakthroughs
verses.text:
- Let your mind idle; boredom gives the subconscious room to surface ideas.
- Simon Sinek says his best ideas arrive when he's not actively trying to think.

💬 Quote

Boredom is the ultimate space.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on boredom as creative space

verses.title: Capture Ideas Immediately
verses.text:
- Capture ideas the moment they arrive or you'll lose them to memory's limits.
- Simon Sinek literally uses sugar packets and binder clips to save sudden thoughts.

💬 Quote

I have binder clips of sugar packets with ideas on them.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on capturing sudden ideas

verses.title: Use Humor To Teach Curiosity
verses.text:
- Use humor as a lure: jokes motivate people to research unfamiliar topics to 'get the joke.'
- Elle crafts witty content that nudges viewers to learn a little more.

💬 Quote

And they do go look it up, not because they want to be smarter. It's because they want to get the joke.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on humor motivating learning

verses.title: Feed Your Brain Targeted Questions
verses.text:
- Ask specific questions and feed your brain diverse inputs before stepping away.
- Simon Sinek stresses questions plus reading plus space produce genuine creative breakthroughs.

💬 Quote

A question must be asked.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on priming the subconscious for ideas

verses.title: Human-Made Art Keeps Its Premium
verses.text:
- AI will speed solutions but won't erase the premium on human-made stories and mistakes.
- Simon Sinek argues audiences still pay extra for human authorship and narrative context.

💬 Quote

We're not buying the product, we're buying the story.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on why human-authored work retains value

verses.title: Schedule Time For 'Good Nothing'
verses.text:
- Do schedule blank blocks of time for 'good nothing' to force unstructured thought.
- Simon Sinek leaves Fridays unscheduled to allow creativity and rest.

💬 Quote

I actually, I won't schedule anything in my calendar for Fridays.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on scheduling free time

verses.title: Schedule 'Good Nothing'
verses.text:
- Schedule time for "good nothing" in your calendar to allow for creativity and problem-solving.
- Give yourself permission to be bored without pressure to be creative.

💬 Quote

I actually, I won't schedule anything in my calendar for Fridays. And I work on Fridays, but I will not pre-schedule anything on a Friday. It has to be blank.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on scheduling time for 'good nothing'.

verses.title: Living With Panic Disorder
verses.text:
- Elle Cordova reveals she has an intense panic disorder and once suffered severe agoraphobia.
- With treatment and routines she now manages attacks and largely lives a normal life.

💬 Quote

For me, it's anxiety. It's an intense panic disorder.
— Elle Cordova

Elle Cordova on her mental-health struggles

verses.title: Underrated, Effective, And Humble
verses.text:
- Elle identifies with R2-D2 as underestimated, prepared, and quietly essential.
- She values R2's capability, humility, and the way he always saves the day behind the scenes.

💬 Quote

R2-D2 is incredibly underestimated.
— Elle Cordova

Elle Cordova on Star Wars archetypes and R2-D2

verses.title: Stuck In Roswell Wrote A Hit
verses.text:
- A van breakdown left Elle stranded in Roswell for ten days and triggered songwriting time.
- She wrote and filmed "Roswell" there, turning enforced downtime into a creative win.

💬 Quote

We ended up being there for 10 full days while the van was being fixed.
— Elle Cordova

Elle Cordova on how the song 'Roswell' was written

chapters.title: The Extraordinary Magic of Ordinary People with author Brad Meltzer
chapters.description: In this lively conversation, bestselling author Brad Meltzer shares his remarkable journey from a debt-ridden writer to literary success, fueled by the kindness of strangers. He explores how ordinary people can inspire extraordinary change, delves into the humanity behind superhero myths, and reveals how negative experiences can ignite creativity. Meltzer emphasizes the power of mentorship, the importance of sharing struggles, and how true magic arises from uplifting others. His insights on creativity and connection are both profound and uplifting.

verses.title: Discovering His Why
verses.text:
- Brad Meltzer recounts how Simon Sinek helped him realize his 'why': his belief that ordinary people change the world. 
- Meltzer's mother affirmed this, saying it was obvious, but Sinek helped him articulate and embrace it.

💬 Quote

You look at me and you said, you believe ordinary people change the world. And I well up with tears. And I go, have you read any of my books? You said, no. I said, that's what every one of my books is about. And I didn't even know it until that moment.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer about Simon Sinek helping him discover his 'why'.

verses.title: Teacher's Life-Changing Words
verses.text:
- Brad Meltzer credits his ninth grade English teacher for changing his life by telling him, "you can write."  
- Her belief set him on the path to become a prolific writer despite his family's struggles.

💬 Quote

My ninth grade English teacher was this woman named Sheila Spicer... She said, you can write.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on his ninth grade English teacher

verses.title: Neighbor's Kindness Saved Family
verses.text:
- When Brad's family was at risk of eviction, neighbor Mircea offered her apartment to them.  
- This act of kindness gave them safety and space during a difficult time.

💬 Quote

This one neighbor comes to my grandmother and says, listen, I see what they're doing to your family. I'm going to move out of my apartment. You take my apartment for you.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on miraculous neighbor's help

verses.title: From Ice Cream to SeaWorld Trainer
verses.text:
- Nick Wicket, Brad's former coworker with humble beginnings, fulfilled his dream by training dolphins at SeaWorld.  
- Seeing his success inspired Brad and showed ordinary people can achieve extraordinary dreams.

💬 Quote

That kid who came from nothing... He's living his dream.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on old friend achieving dreams

verses.title: Nick Wicket's Dream
verses.text:
- Brad Meltzer recounts how his Haagen-Dazs colleague, Nick Wicket, overcame adversity to achieve his dream of training dolphins at SeaWorld. 
- Seeing Nick succeed moved Meltzer, reinforcing his belief that ordinary people can achieve extraordinary things.

💬 Quote

This kid who came from nothing, who that woman screamed at us and would say, we're never going to be anything. We're never going to have anything. He's living his dream.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on his Haagen-Dazs colleague, Nick Wicket, achieving his dream.

verses.title: True Heroes Are Flawed
verses.text:
- We wrongly idolize heroes as flawless, ignoring their struggles and failures.  
- Authentic stories include failure and humility, which show true heroism.

💬 Quote

We do a huge disservice to our heroes... We leave out the most vital part: the struggle and failure.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on authentic stories of heroes

verses.title: AI Lacks Emotional Depth
verses.text:
- AI can't replicate human emotion or soul, essential for authentic storytelling.  
- Real emotional connection comes from human experience, not algorithms.

💬 Quote

AI doesn't have taste and it has no soul... It could not get to that feeling because it never felt anyone do that.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on AI's limits in writing children's books

verses.title: Test Work with Honest Friends
verses.text:
- Give your work to five honest friends to get varied feedback.  
- Aim for surprise and excitement in their response; that's the true test of your work.

💬 Quote

Give it to five friends who really love you, who will be honest with you... They say, 'I didn't think it was going to be as good as it was.'
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on how to know if your book is good

verses.title: Use Yes or No as Fuel
verses.text:
- Use both positive belief and negative skepticism as fuel.  
- Whether someone says yes or no, that validation drives growth and motivation.

💬 Quote

You got to have either someone saying yes or someone saying no... The only way to move forward is through that.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on powerful motivation sources

verses.title: Clark Kent and Batman’s Power
verses.text:
- Superman's key strength is Clark Kent’s ordinariness, reflecting our desire to do good beyond ourselves.  
- Batman represents relentless persistence despite knowing he can't win, embodying human resilience.

💬 Quote

Clark Kent is the key ingredient... Batman knows he's going to fail, but he's going to try again tomorrow.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on Superman and Batman's symbolic meaning

verses.title: Love the Struggle
verses.text:
- Don't wait for perfection or the end result to find happiness.  
- Focus on loving the struggle and commitment to growth instead.

💬 Quote

The struggle matters... You like when it's really hard, when you have to really push through it, you need that struggle to go.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on why struggle matters most

verses.title: Failure Keeps Hunger Alive
verses.text:
- Brad replays his worst moment of failure daily as motivation: his agent saying, "sorry, kiddo."  
- This ritual keeps his hunger and gratitude alive for 27 years.

💬 Quote

Every day I sit down to write, sorry, kiddo, sorry, kiddo... I never want to be not thankful for this life.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on using failure as motivation

verses.title: Compete with Your Old Self
verses.text:
- Brad's best work came after reflecting on his old books and focusing on character depth.  
- His midlife crisis pushed him to compete with himself and improve continuously.

💬 Quote

I was like, don't be afraid to compete with your old self... Be honest again, show yourself again.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on improving as a writer over time

verses.title: Empathy is the Real Magic
verses.text:
- Brad's viral commencement speech emphasized kindness and empathy as true magic.  
- His authenticity and personal stories deeply moved 70,000 people and beyond.

💬 Quote

Cruelty and venom aren't signs of strength... What takes strength is switching places with someone else and showing kindness and empathy.
— Brad Meltzer

Brad Meltzer on his viral University of Michigan speech

chapters.title: The Future You Avoid Is Riskier Than the One You Face with LinkedIn co-founder Reid Hoffman
chapters.description: Reid Hoffman, cofounder of LinkedIn and venture capitalist, shares his insights on the power of storytelling in technology and society. He argues that optimistic futures must be imagined to be created. Hoffman discusses how AI changes our brains and the focus shifts from external to internal threats. He critiques short-term capitalism through the Kodak example, emphasizing a balance of selflessness and ambition. Ultimately, he advocates for a hopeful vision of technology while navigating the complexities of privacy and societal impact.

verses.title: First Paid Work From Obsession
verses.text:
- At 12 Reid walked into a game publisher's office and returned days later with a fully redlined scenario pack.
- The editor paid him because his obsessive revisions were genuinely useful.

💬 Quote

I came back monday afternoon with the entire thing like redlined ... and he started looking at it and went, I need to pay you for this because like, I want to use this.
— Reid Hoffman

Reid Hoffman recounting his first paid creative job at age 12

verses.title: Dystopia Limits Our Futures
verses.text:
- Science fiction today is overwhelmingly dystopian, which narrows our imagined possible futures.
- Reid Hoffman argues that avoiding bad futures won't create the futures we want; we must imagine positive destinations.

💬 Quote

You don't get a future that you want by avoiding the futures you don't want.
— Reid Hoffman

Reid Hoffman on why dystopian science fiction is insufficient

verses.title: Trust But Test AI Builders
verses.text:
- Trust most AI builders but remain vigilant about blind spots and incentives.
- Use coordinated safety measures like red teams while moving at the 'speed of traffic' in innovation.

💬 Quote

Call it 85% trust, 15% cynicism.
— Reid Hoffman

Reid Hoffman on trusting AI companies while accounting for blind spots

verses.title: Kodak's Short-Term Focus
verses.text:
- Kodak invented the digital camera in the 70s but suppressed the technology to protect existing sales. 
- The executives who made those decisions retired with bonuses even as the company went bankrupt due to their short-term focus.

💬 Quote

Kodak in the 1970s invented the digital camera, suppressed the technology because they didn't want to cannibalize film sales and paper sales and chemical sales.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the problem of short-termism in capitalism, using Kodak as an example

verses.title: Assessment Will Become Continuous
verses.text:
- AI will change educational assessment from one-time written exams to continuous, rigorous oral-style evaluation.
- Hoffman predicts lower exam cost and more frequent testing that better measures true mastery.

💬 Quote

What's going to happen is examination is all going to happen by AI.  And the examination will be like oral exams for PhDs.
— Reid Hoffman

Reid Hoffman on how AI will transform testing and education

verses.title: Jobs Shift Toward Orchestration
verses.text:
- Work skills will shift from doing raw tasks to orchestrating AI and judging outputs.
