chapters.description: Tina Seelig, an educator and bestselling author at Stanford University, shares insights on creating luck and leveraging opportunities. She introduces the sailboat metaphor, illustrating how to navigate life's winds of fortune. Seelig emphasizes the importance of knowing your core values and storytelling in forging your identity. Her practical advice includes embracing experimentation, learning from failures, and the value of mentorship. With engaging anecdotes, she encourages listeners to view luck as a long-term game, underscoring the power of compassion and support.

verses.title: Luck Versus Fortune
verses.text:
- Tina Seelig separates fortune (external circumstances) from luck (partially controllable outcomes). 
- You can create luck by taking actions that increase your exposure and readiness for opportunities.

💬 Quote

Luck is like the wind and you need a sail to catch it.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig defining luck vs. fortune

verses.title: Anchor Decisions With Core Values
verses.text:
- Know your core values so you can say yes to aligned opportunities and walk away from misaligned ones. 
- Use a personal board and imagine headlines to test whether opportunities fit your values.

💬 Quote

Core values are like your ballast at the bottom of the ship, holding you steady so you don't get knocked over.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig on knowing your core values

verses.title: Spy Accusation At A Conference
verses.text:
- Tina accepted a CEO's request to pose as a Stanford visitor and was accused of being a spy at a competitor's conference. 
- The episode taught her she had not defined values or boundaries before acting.

💬 Quote

Are you a spy?
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig recounting being cornered and accused after posing as a Stanford attendee

verses.title: Your Story Determines Scale
verses.text:
- The stories you tell about yourself shape the size of opportunities you pursue. 
- External messages and single affirmations can reframe your identity and open new paths.

💬 Quote

At that moment, I became a scientist.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig recounting how a professor's comment shaped her identity

verses.title: Know And Stretch Your Risk Profile
verses.text:
- Map your personal risk profile across social, emotional, intellectual, physical and financial domains. 
- Stretch deliberately outside your comfort zone while removing avoidable risk through preparation.

💬 Quote

There are so many different types of risk.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig explaining different kinds of risk and preparation

verses.title: Asking for Help
verses.text:
- Ask for help by making yourself easy to help with a simple, easy ask. Send a concise email: who you are, what you're working on, why you want to talk to them, and a tiny, specific ask.

💬 Quote

How do you make yourself easy to help? First of all, you have very simple easy asks.
— Heidi Roizen

Tina Seelig on how to ask for help

verses.title: Ask Clearly And Often
verses.text:
- Ask for help with short, 4‑sentence messages that state who you are, what you want, and a tiny ask. 
- Send many such asks because response rates are low early in a journey.

💬 Quote

The likelihood you get a response goes down with every sentence you add.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig advising on how to ask for help effectively

verses.title: Help And Appreciate Generously
verses.text:
- Help others with small, easy actions that are highly valuable to them. 
- Close the loop by showing appreciation to build long-term goodwill.

💬 Quote

If you ask someone for something and you do not say thank you and show appreciation, the likelihood that they do something for you in the future goes down precipitously.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig on helping others and expressing appreciation

verses.title: Experiment, Scale, And Record Failures
verses.text:
- Run many small experiments to test ideas and then build on initial footholds to scale success. 
- Keep a failure resume to codify lessons and reduce repeating mistakes.

💬 Quote

I keep a failure resume, very, very active failure resume.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig encouraging experimentation and documenting failures

verses.title: Pop‑Up Portraits Spark A Business
verses.text:
- Photographer Catherine Emery moved to rural Maine and ran low‑cost pop‑ups to build community and clients. 
- That small experiment led to referrals and a thriving local business.

💬 Quote

Three places said yes.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig describing Catherine Emery's experiments to start over in Maine

verses.title: Build on Success
verses.text:
- After getting your foot in the door with one success, keep pulling on those ropes.
- Leverage initial successes to create more opportunities for yourself.

💬 Quote

When you get your foot in the door and have one little success, you just keep pulling on those ropes.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig on how to build on initial success

verses.title: From Cold Email To Research Role
verses.text:
- Oliver reached out with a short email offering help, then sent a document listing value he could add after a call. 
- Tina hired him as a research assistant and his initiatives became part of her book.

💬 Quote

He sent me a document with all the ways he could help me with my book.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig recounting Oliver's initiative after reaching out about luck

verses.title: Oliver's Initiative
verses.text:
- Oliver offered to help Tina Seelig with her book, and impressed her with his initiative.
- He landed a research assistant role and became a key feature in one chapter.

💬 Quote

He sent me a document with all the ways he could help me with my book. Now, nothing that was on his list was exactly what I needed but I thought you know what I'm so intrigued I'm so impressed by his initiative that I hired him as a research assistant for my book.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig on hiring Oliver.

verses.title: Luck Is A Long Game
verses.text:
- Luck compounds over years; behaviors and choices reverberate long into the future. 
- Small acts of compassion today can unlock opportunities decades later.

💬 Quote

Luck is a long game.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig explaining how actions years earlier influenced later job decisions

verses.title: Luck is a Long Game
verses.text:
- Tina Seelig shares a story about a disastrous Stanford alumni trip to Costa Rica where she chose to be supportive of the trip leader.
- Fifteen years later, that trip leader was on the team evaluating her for her current job at Knight-Hennessey Scholars, and remembered Tina's compassion.

💬 Quote

It was an amazing thing because I had no idea. And I realized that if I had behaved badly on that trip 15 years earlier, do you think I would have gotten that job? No way.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig on how compassion led to a job opportunity 15 years later

verses.title: Choose How You Engage With Opportunity
verses.text:
- Choose daily how you engage with opportunities: ignore, observe, be carried, harness, or seek them out. 
- Aim to be a sailboat that actively seeks and captures winds of luck.

💬 Quote

Do you want to stay inside and sort of block the wind out and not pay attention?
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig prompting the audience to choose engagement modes with luck

verses.title: Learn from Everyone
verses.text:
- You can learn from everyone if you're paying attention.
- With the mindset to learn from everyone, you are more likely to learn something new every day.

💬 Quote

You can learn from the worst boss you ever had what not to do, right? You can learn from your spouse, you know, to have constructive conflict.
— Tina Seelig

Tina Seelig on mentors.

chapters.title: #408 How to Make a Few MORE Billion Dollars: Brad Jacobs
chapters.description: Brad Jacobs shares insights from his latest book, discussing his journey of building eight billion-dollar companies. He dives into effective fundraising strategies that helped him raise over $50 billion, emphasizing the strategic value of family offices. Jacobs also offers an overview of his integration playbook for successful mergers, highlighting organizational design and accountability. Additionally, he reveals techniques for maintaining mental clarity and resilience, including meditation and cognitive-behavioral strategies. A treasure trove for aspiring entrepreneurs!

verses.title: Company Building As Alchemy
verses.text:
- Creating shareholder value is more than money; it's bringing something extraordinary from nothing into existence. 
- Brad frames company building as the ultimate feat of business alchemy that starts with a single idea.

💬 Quote

Imagine this one moment, you've got an idea. And before you know it, you're looking at 150,000 employees, billions in profit, and a soaring stock price on the New York Stock Exchange.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on the magic of company building

verses.title: Shift To Generative Motivation
verses.text:
- Rearrange your inner monologue toward a positive, generative fuel source rather than negative validation-seeking. 
- Use the joy of creation itself to compound motivation and sustain long-term focus.

💬 Quote

A proper mindset is essential. The ability to rearrange your brain and create that mindset is entirely within your grasp.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on mindset and inner fuel

verses.title: Party Introduction That Proved A Point
verses.text:
- David Senra recounts introducing Apollo Ono to Brad Jacobs at a party to illustrate Brad's positive energy. 
- Apollo learned Brad started eight billion-dollar companies and was driven by positive fuel.

💬 Quote

That's Brad Jacobs. He started eight separate billion dollar companies. and he has some of the most positive, maybe the most positive infectious energy of any human I've ever been around.
— David Senra

David Senra recounting introducing Brad Jacobs to Apollo Ono

verses.title: Use Pressure As Fuel
verses.text:
- Be comfortable with some anxiety; pressure can be channeled into relentless drive. 
- View urgency and high stakes as healthy leadership traits that sharpen decision-making.

💬 Quote

I'm comfortable with a little bit of anxiety, even fear when facing a decision that has huge consequences, because I believe that's a healthy trait in a leader.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on handling stress and leadership temperament

verses.title: Go Public For Fuel And Discipline
verses.text:
- Treat going public as a strategic tool for fast, large-scale capital access and ongoing accountability. 
- Use quarterly reporting as a discipline that keeps management sharp and attracts deals.

💬 Quote

Being public makes it easier to build something big and lasting. It gives me deep access to capital markets on non-ownerous terms.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on advantages of running public companies

verses.title: Podcast Sparked Major Investments
verses.text:
- Brad credits a Founders podcast episode for bringing two new top-20 shareholders who invested $750M. 
- Media exposure materially increased investor awareness and capital inflow.

💬 Quote

Two of our current top 20 shareholders had never heard of me until I was profiled by David Senra on his founder's podcast...and have since invested $750 million into Brad's company.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs recounting investor discovery via podcast

verses.title: Make Dilution Intentional
verses.text:
- Be strategic about equity dilution and calculate how much ownership you're willing to trade for growth capital. 
- Avoid giving away too much early by modeling long-term value creation versus immediate needs.

💬 Quote

Your dilution should be strategic. Trading equity for capital sounds like pure upside until you realize you're selling off chunks of your company.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs advising on equity and dilution

verses.title: Tap Family Offices Early
verses.text:
- Prioritize family offices early: they move fast, act entrepreneurially, and offer strategic introductions. 
- Treat their network and operational experience as often more valuable than pure capital.

💬 Quote

Family offices are often the first outside capital source that I will approach. The good ones are thoughtful, patient, entrepreneurial.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on family offices as investors

verses.title: Private Equity Is A Mixed Bag
verses.text:
- Private equity can be opportunistic and self-interested, making them a poor fit unless necessary. 
- Brad avoids PE when possible but acknowledges exceptions where it worked well.

💬 Quote

I generally avoid taking money from them. However, I made four exceptions and all four have turned out very well.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on private equity tradeoffs

verses.title: Court Sovereign Wealth Slowly
verses.text:
- Cultivate sovereign wealth fund relationships years before you need capital because they perform exhaustive due diligence. 
- Their long-term perspective and networks unlock opportunities inaccessible otherwise.

💬 Quote

They're serious and professional investors, and they think in decades, not quarters. The downside is that they take a long time.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on sovereign wealth funds

verses.title: Use Pensions For Stable Capital
verses.text:
- Use pension plans for large, low-drama capital when your business delivers predictable long-term returns. 
- Expect methodical approvals and sticky capital that won't flee during short-term volatility.

💬 Quote

Pension plans are excellent sources of capital when you're looking for big checks and low drama. They're not after board seats or special terms.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on pension plans as investors

verses.title: Leverage Retail Investors' Voice
verses.text:
- Don't overlook retail investors as amplifiers who can generate organic marketing and community enthusiasm. 
- Their online advocacy can shape perceptions and attract customers and talent.

💬 Quote

Retail investors are often overlooked, but they can be a powerful part of your shareholder base. If they're excited about a stock, they'll become online crusaders for your company.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on retail investors' marketing value

verses.title: Use Debt To Enforce Discipline
verses.text:
- Use debt strategically to enforce financial discipline and target leverage of roughly 1x–3x EBITDA. 
- Debt aligns incentives because it must be repaid and keeps management focused on cash generation.

💬 Quote

Also, because debt must be repaid, it forces a company to be disciplined with its finances. I usually target a debt leverage ratio of about 1x to 3x EBITDA.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on the role of debt in capital structure

verses.title: Investors Should Add More Than Money
verses.text:
- The best investors add strategic value beyond capital by opening doors and improving strategy. 
- Your most consequential decision is who you choose to surround yourself with.

💬 Quote

The best investors don't just write checks. They open doors and sharpen your strategy.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on value of the right investors

verses.title: Integrate To Create Value
verses.text:
- Integration is where acquisitions create value; buy-and-hold without integration just moves capital. 
- Successful consolidators design integrations to transform separate entities into a cohesive profit machine.

💬 Quote

Most acquisitions do not create shareholder value. As an industry consolidator, I stake my reputation on my ability to transform separate entities into a cohesive profit machine through integration.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on mastering integration

verses.title: Access Teams Before Close
verses.text:
- Negotiate access at signing so you can start integration work before close. 
- Town halls, interviews, and frontline visits reveal what's working and what to fix faster.

💬 Quote

I negotiate explicitly for unrestricted access to the company from the moment we sign an agreement. Immediate access to employees is an essential condition for us.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on pre-close integration access

verses.title: Rip Off The Band‑Aid Quickly
verses.text:
- Take the 'Band-Aid' approach and integrate quickly because speed often outweighs short-term friction. 
- Fast migration to shared dashboards and systems aligns reporting and reduces interpretation gaps.

💬 Quote

I generally take the Band-Aid approach and rip it off because I found the pros vastly outweigh the cons. The sooner we get the operation running on our systems, the better.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on rapid integration timing

verses.title: Use Town Halls For Real Feedback
verses.text:
- Run candid town halls and ask frontline employees what to stop, start, or improve. 
- Turn answers into named workstreams with owners and deadlines to drive measurable change.

💬 Quote

Instead of lecturing about ourselves from on high, I gave a brief introduction and moved right into Q&A. It's essential to give new employees an open mic to share what's on their minds, and we respond just as openly.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs describing town halls after acquisitions

verses.title: Three‑Question Employee Survey
verses.text:
- Run simple employee surveys after acquisition with three questions: what's working, what needs fixing, and your best idea. 
- Use responses to prioritize initiatives and focus on high-impact work.

💬 Quote

Quarterly, all employee surveys are one of my absolute favorite tools for running a business. We also sent a version of this survey immediately after we buy a business.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on employee surveys post-acquisition

verses.title: Name A Single Owner For Every Task
verses.text:
- Assign a single owner ('throat to choke') to every integration line item with clear completion dates. 
- Individual accountability prevents tasks from slipping in complex integrations.

💬 Quote

A single integration can have thousands of specific line items. Each one is assigned a single throat to choke, an owner who is personally accountable for the outcome on a firm completion date.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on accountability in integrations

verses.title: Design Org Charts For Decisions
verses.text:
- Design org charts around where decision-making and P&L authority should sit, not people. 
- Keep charts simple, geometric, and one-page to avoid spaghetti structures that slow decisions.

💬 Quote

The first question to answer in organizational design is how is this business we're acquiring going to create value? I will start with the organizational chart, the blueprint for well-run companies.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on org chart design principles

verses.title: Prefer Empty Seats To Bad Fits
verses.text:
- Leave seats empty rather than hire poor fits and ruthlessly cut 'what the hell' roles after acquisition. 
- Prioritize must-haves, keep select nice-to-haves, and sweep away the rest to optimize headcount.

💬 Quote

An empty seat is less damaging than a poor fit. It takes commitment to let an org chart seat stay open until the ideal person is found.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on hiring and org discipline

verses.title: Bloat Lives In The Middle
verses.text:
- Bloat usually accumulates in mid-to-upper management, not frontline roles serving customers. 
- Replacing weak leaders matters because poor leadership cascades and rots organizational performance from the top.

💬 Quote

I've seen org charts where there are nine layers between the customer and the CEO. That's bureaucracy.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on layers and organizational rot

verses.title: Center Your Mind First
verses.text:
- Center your mind first; mental clarity is a competitive advantage for leadership. 
- Brad meditates twice daily (TM since 16) to improve decision-making, creativity, and emotional balance.

💬 Quote

Maintaining a clear, balanced mindset is essential to strong leadership... I first need to get myself centered. I've been doing [Transcendental Meditation] twice a day.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on meditation and mental clarity

verses.title: Use Perspective Rituals
verses.text:
- Use visualization practices that expand context, e.g., 'I am in the universe' then 'the universe is in me.' 
- These rituals induce perspective, reduce problem size, and stimulate creativity.

💬 Quote

I open my hands outward, close my eyes and think I am in the universe... Then I'll bring my arms inward and think, and the universe is in me. I will think, I love the universe and the universe loves me.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs describing a meditation technique

verses.title: Reframe 'Musts' Into Preferences
verses.text:
- Reframe irrational demands into flexible preferences using REBT; say preferences aloud to re-center faster. 
- Replace 'I must be perfect' with 'I prefer excellence but can learn from mistakes.'

💬 Quote

Ellis taught me to reframe rigid demands as flexible preferences, to aim high but not crumble if I fell short. By embracing the inevitability of human error, I discovered a resilient more mindset.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on Albert Ellis and REBT

verses.title: Use CBT To Challenge Distortions
verses.text:
- Use CBT to identify schemas and challenge automatic, distorted thoughts with evidence. 
- Replace catastrophic internal monologues with measured reframes to change emotions and behavior.

💬 Quote

CBT taught me how my interpretation of a situation through automatic thoughts can skew my emotions and behaviors. It also gave me an incentive to consciously question my negative thoughts.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs on cognitive behavioral therapy

verses.title: Build A Re‑Centering Toolbox
verses.text:
- Keep a toolbox of REBT, CBT, DBT, positive psychology, and mindfulness to return to center. 
- Mix techniques as needed; the goal is intentional responses, not elimination of problems.

💬 Quote

If I get thrown off center, I reach for one or more of them. REBT, CBT, DBT, positive psychology, or mindfulness.
— Brad Jacobs

Brad Jacobs summarizing his recentering methods

chapters.title: A conversation on focus and finding your life's work
chapters.description: Join Patrick O’Shaughnessy, host of Invest Like the Best, for an engaging discussion on focus and finding your life's work. He dives into the crucial role of focus in entrepreneurship, contrasting deep commitment with the distractions of modern life. The conversation highlights the power of passion, advocating for genuine fulfillment over external accolades. Patrick also critiques society's obsession with wealth and status, encouraging listeners to seek meaningful contributions rather than mere financial gain. A thought-provoking exploration of enduring success!

verses.title: Guard Your Time
verses.text:
- Daniel Ek advised David Senra that more and more people will ask for your time. 
- Daniel says that if you don't guard your time, greatness will disappear.

💬 Quote

If you don't guard your time, greatness will disappear.

chapters.title: Dan Heath: Ideas That Stick and Stories That Resonate
chapters.description: Dan Heath, a renowned professor and bestselling author, dives into mastering the art of effective communication. He emphasizes the importance of memorable ideas and the concept of 'upstream' thinking to solve societal issues like homelessness. Heath also explores the deeper meaning of ikigai, advocating for fulfillment beyond financial gains. Listeners will enjoy anecdotes about unique storytelling in professional life, along with insights on the creative processes behind podcasting and writing, all woven with humor and personal experiences.

verses.title: Making Ideas Stick
verses.text:
- To make ideas memorable and impactful, use the SUCCES framework.
- It stands for Simple, Unexpected, Concrete, Credible, Emotional, and Stories.

💬 Quote

The way you make your ideas stick is you embrace six principles [...] It spells out an acronym, success minus the final S. [...] Simple, unexpected, concrete, credible, emotional, and stories.
— Dan Heath

Dan Heath on making ideas stick.

verses.title: 4 Villains of Decision Making
verses.text:
- The four villains that negatively affect decisions are: narrow framing, confirmation bias, short-term emotion, and overconfidence. 
- Overconfidence here doesn't refer to cockiness, but rather an inflated sense of knowing about the future.

💬 Quote

There are basically four key villains that afflict our decisions again and again and again. The villains are narrow framing, [...] confirmation bias, [...] short-term emotion [...] and finally, overconfidence.
— Dan Heath

Dan Heath on the common pitfalls of decision-making.

verses.title: Crafting Peak Moments
verses.text:
- Great experiences hinge on crafting memorable peak moments. 
- These moments stand out in our memories, shaping our overall impression of the experience.

💬 Quote

If you care about crafting great experiences, what you really care about is crafting peak moments [...] in our memories, certain moments come to stand above the rest.
— Dan Heath

Dan Heath on the importance of peak moments in experiences.

verses.title: Stadium Beer Vendor's Ikigai
verses.text:
- Howard Hart, a stadium beer vendor for 35+ years, found deep satisfaction in his work despite low pay. 
- He cherished the community he built with fans, finding meaning and purpose beyond financial gain.

💬 Quote

I know that I bring something special to these people and that they bring something special to me. [...] Could I have a good life? Absolutely. I loved what I did.

chapters.title: Debbie Millman: Creating Brands That Stand the Test of Time
chapters.description: Debbie Millman, a design expert and the host of Design Matters, dives into fascinating conversations about the power of branding. She discusses how design can shape perceptions and connection, reflecting on her own journey from childhood drawings to podcasting. Millman examines the emotional connections brands cultivate and why so many fail to recognize design's true value. She also shares insights on the ethics of spec work, the importance of authenticity in branding, and the evolving nature of creativity in an increasingly tech-driven landscape.

verses.title: Mysterious Peony Bush Story
verses.text:
- Debbie Millman cherished a peony bush growing outside her home for over 15 years until it vanished mysteriously.
- Someone replaced it with a plastic bouquet, suggesting shared grief and appreciation for the flower.

💬 Quote

I saw what looked like a replanted little peony bush. And I was astounded. And I mean, truly astounded. And it was plastic. It was a plastic bouquet.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman recounting her story of the lost peony bush.

verses.title: Childhood Dreams and Drawings
verses.text:
- Debbie dreamed of being a teacher and magazine writer in her youth, even making her own magazine named "Debutante".
- Her childhood drawings with logos predicted her future career in branding.

💬 Quote

I feel that even though I'm a native New Yorker, I wasn't living in Manhattan as I grew up. I somehow feel that I predicted my whole future as a brand consultant and designer.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman talking about her childhood dreams and drawings.

verses.title: Late Success Has Benefits
verses.text:
- Success often comes later in life, and that maturity helps in sustaining and appreciating it.
- Early impatience is common, but delayed success can be more fulfilling and sustainable.

💬 Quote

There's something really wonderful about coming into your own later in life because you're that much more mature. I think you have an understanding and a sort of gratitude about it that you wouldn't have had if you did succeed that much earlier.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman on the value of achieving success later in life.

verses.title: Invest in Design for Success
verses.text:
- Companies often fail at design because leadership undervalues it and fears investment risks.
- Investing in great design leads to greater success and brand love over time.

💬 Quote

Companies are generally not helmed by creative people. They don't understand the value that design has. Design is also an investment, and it takes a bit of time to see a return on that investment.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman explaining why many companies fail at design.

verses.title: Brands as Modern Religions
verses.text:
- Brands operate like religions with tribes, icons, and revere marks creating loyal communities.
- Apple's brand exemplifies this by creating a shared identity among its loyal customers.

💬 Quote

You have a place of worship, the Apple stores. You have a mark that's revered, you have a visual language that everybody understands, and you have the zealots that walk around with their white earbuds telegraphing to each other.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman describing brands functioning like religions.

verses.title: Strategic Branding Foundations
verses.text:
- Design great brands by defining strategic positioning and knowing your audience deeply.
- Authenticity and understanding real needs surpass just appealing value propositions or trends.

💬 Quote

Find out what the strategic positioning is or create the strategic positioning first before you go into execution. The idea comes first, not the logo or colors.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman on the essential steps in creating a brand.

verses.title: Recognizing Great Design
verses.text:
- Debbie Millman advises to evaluate design by removing the logo and identifying the brand through its core elements.
- Also, look at whether the brand has loyal customers who are not willing to compromise when the brand is not available.

💬 Quote

One of the things that I often ask my students to do when they're evaluating is take the logo off the product. Can you still identify what it is by these assets, the color, the shape, the typography, the secondary typography?
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman on recognizing great design

verses.title: Brand Creation
verses.text:
- When creating a brand, start with why the product is needed to give it a reason for being.
- Understand that people are interested in brands that make a difference in their lives.

💬 Quote

When creating a brand, my advice to anyone is to, like Simon Sinek would say, start with why. Why do we need this thing? Why is there a reason for being for this product?
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman on brand creation.

verses.title: Virality Needs Strategy & Timing
verses.text:
- Virality in branding is unpredictable and depends on timing, effort, and cultural fit.
- Successful brands like Liquid Death and Stanley Cup have strategic roadmaps for growth and momentum management.

💬 Quote

You can never predict virality... There needs to be a bit of a lightning strike for something like that to happen. A lot has to do with timing as well as effort, zeitgeist, and marketplace.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman on branding virality and growth strategy.

verses.title: Founder Impact on Brand Perception
verses.text:
- The Cybertruck symbolizes overcompensation rather than intended social status.
- Brand founder's reputation can heavily influence brand perception and success.

💬 Quote

It's intended as a symbol of social cachet. In fact, it's now a symbol of grandiosity and overcompensation for other things.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman discussing the Cybertruck from a design perspective.

verses.title: Use Notes For Accuracy
verses.text:
- Use notes during talks if you handle complex data or facts to avoid errors.
- Precision in timing and content delivery matters for effective presentations.

💬 Quote

I used a lot of science, and I'm not a scientist, and I was very nervous to make a mistake and misquote because you can't correct those things in post.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman explaining why she used notes at a TED Talk.

verses.title: Patience and Presentation Mastery
verses.text:
- Anything worthwhile, like building a career or a podcast, takes time and persistence.
- Effective presentations balance relaxation, humor, and emotive delivery.

💬 Quote

Anything worthwhile takes time. Looking back, being in my forties feels like a million years ago, just becoming who I was.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman reflecting on persistence and presenting at 99U.

verses.title: When to Walk Away
verses.text:
- Walk away from commitments only when necessary for your wellbeing.
- Diverse dreams may allow success in different areas at different times, sustaining motivation.

💬 Quote

I've stayed at jobs too long, I've stayed in relationships too long trying to make them work. I've yet to give up on a whole bunch of my hopes and dreams.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman sharing insights on perseverance and knowing when to quit.

verses.title: Value of Self-Generated Work
verses.text:
- Create your own opportunities when collaborators or jobs aren't available.
- Self-generated projects can become life-changing centers of creativity and career growth.

💬 Quote

The podcast for me was very self-generated. I was paying and investing in that effort myself. It turned out to be a wonderful centerpiece to my life now.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman on starting her podcast independently.

verses.title: Avoid Spec Work Trap
verses.text:
- Avoid spec work as it often exploits imbalance of power and undervalues designers.
- Educate about fair design practices and expect compensation for professional work.

💬 Quote

Designers have notoriously been asked to do spec work... It becomes a power exchange where someone with more power asks someone with less power to do something for free.
— Debbie Millman

Debbie Millman discussing the problems of spec work in design.

chapters.title: Jeff Wetzler: The ASK Approach to Better Questions
chapters.description: Jeff Wetzler, author of *Ask* and an educational innovator, unpacks the transformative power of questioning. He explains how most people ask ineffective questions that stifle growth. Wetzler introduces his five-step ASK approach to improve inquiry and decision-making. He discusses the value of diverse perspectives and the importance of psychological safety in conversations. Practical techniques like the ‘doorknob moment’ and active listening are highlighted, demonstrating how asking better questions can lead to meaningful personal and professional change.

verses.title: Only Asking Reveals Truth
verses.text:
- People are poor at reading body language and putting themselves in others' shoes does not reliably reveal what others think or feel.
- The only reliable way to know others' thoughts and feelings is to directly ask them.

💬 Quote

Literally, the research shows there's only one way that reliably allows mere mortals to find out what other people think and feel a no. Of course, that's to ask them, but it's so much easier said than done.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on why asking questions is essential

verses.title: Limitations of Empathy
verses.text:
- People often overestimate their understanding of others' thoughts and feelings, even those closest to them. 
- Relying on intuition, body language, or 'walking in their shoes' is less reliable than simply asking questions.

💬 Quote

Literally, the research shows there's only one way that reliably allows mere mortals to find out what other people think and feel and know. Of course, that's to ask them, but it's so much easier said than done.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on the best way to find out what other people think and feel

verses.title: Choose Curiosity to Ask Better
verses.text:
- Most people assume they know how to ask questions but lack true curiosity and fail to slow down to reconsider initial judgments.
- To ask better questions, intentionally cultivate curiosity and resist rushing to conclusions.

💬 Quote

If I think I know what's going on here... why even bother asking a question? It would be illogical to even ask a question if I'm certain. And so we've got to figure out how to actually get ourselves more curious.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on why people often don't ask better questions

verses.title: Slow Down and Spark Curiosity
verses.text:
- Slow down your thinking to recognize when your brain is constructing a limiting story.
- Inject curiosity by asking, "What else might be going on here?" to open up possibilities.

💬 Quote

If I can begin to realize this is how our brains work... I can begin to inject what I call curiosity sparks into that story. I can say, all right, what else might be going on here?
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on how to cultivate curiosity by slowing thought

verses.title: Learn From Everyone
verses.text:
- There is something important to learn from every single person, even from those you disagree with.
- Ask questions to understand their views and learn how to influence them more effectively.

💬 Quote

There is something important and interesting to learn from every single person.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on learning from people with different views

verses.title: Ask Everyone for Insight
verses.text:
- Learn from every person, including those you disagree with or consider enemies.
- Asking questions builds influence and reveals useful information even from difficult people.

💬 Quote

There is something important and interesting to learn from every single person... You're actually more influential if you know where they're coming from and demonstrate curiosity.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on why you should ask questions even to those you disagree with

verses.title: Optimal Asking Methodology
verses.text:
- Anything is better than not asking. 
- Use polls as a door to open deeper dialogue to learn the 'how' and 'why' behind opinions.

💬 Quote

Anything is better than not asking. And so, if you're doing a survey, if you're doing polling, that's good. That's better than the alternative.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on polling as a form of asking questions.

verses.title: The ASK Approach
verses.text:
- Jeff Wetzler introduces the ASK approach: five steps to uncover what people are likely to not tell you. It includes choosing curiosity and creating a safe space.

💬 Quote

Basically, nothing else I would say matters if you're not genuinely curious to learn from the other person.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler explaining the first step of the ASK approach.

verses.title: ASK Method For Quality Questions
verses.text:
- Follow the ASK approach: Choose curiosity, Make it safe, Pose quality questions, Listen to learn, and Reflect and reconnect.
- Avoid crummy questions like "Does that make sense?" Instead, ask "What's your reaction?" to get meaningful feedback.

💬 Quote

Not everything that has a question mark is a quality question... Does that make sense? is a crummy question... What's your reaction to that? is much more likely to help you understand.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler explaining quality vs crummy questions

verses.title: Quality Questions
verses.text:
- Jeff Wetzler defines a quality question as one that allows you to truly learn from someone. 
- A quality question is not just any question with a question mark at the end.

💬 Quote

This is basically to say not everything that has a question mark is a quality question.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on the 'pose quality questions' step of the ASK approach.

verses.title: Ask for Reactions
verses.text:
- Jeff Wetzler, as a new manager, asked an employee for his reaction after giving instructions. 
- The employee was demoralized because he had different client information, which was quickly resolved with the question.

💬 Quote

If you really want to know my true reaction, I'm completely demoralized by what you just asked me to say.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler recounting an experience as a new manager

verses.title: Listen Actively and Verify
verses.text:
- Listen to learn by giving full attention, not listening to prove a point or respond quickly.
- Use techniques like "pull the thread" and "tell back and test" to deepen understanding and check comprehension.

💬 Quote

There's a listening technique called pull the thread... Tell back and test is the magic move, just check your understanding. It changes everything.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on effective listening techniques

verses.title: The Doorknob Moment
verses.text:
- Psychotherapists experience the 'doorknob moment' where patients reveal crucial information right before leaving.
- The most important information is often buried and requires further probing beyond the initial answer.

💬 Quote

You can't assume that when you ask a question and someone answers it, you're getting the most important thing they have to say.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler describing the takeaway of the 'doorknob moment'.

verses.title: Tell Back and Test
verses.text:
- Use 'tell back and test' to verify your understanding before responding. State what you think you heard and ask if you got it right.
- High-performing teams check if they understood each other; you'll get better information and save time.

💬 Quote

It's just called tell back and test. And so when you're talking to someone and they answer your questions, before you give your response or before you ask the next question, just simply say, I think this is what I heard you say. Let me just check. Did I get that right?
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler sharing a key listening technique.

verses.title: Tell Back and Test
verses.text:
- Use 'tell back and test' to check your understanding during conversations. It improves information, adjusts the conversation's tempo, and shows you care.

💬 Quote

It's just called tell back and test. And so when you're talking to someone and they answer your questions, before you give your response or before you ask the next question, just simply say, I think this is what I heard you say.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler describing a technique to improve understanding in conversations.

verses.title: Reflect and Reconnect After Asking
verses.text:
- Reflect on conversations by sifting key takeaways and turning them over in your mind to update stories and plan actions.
- Reconnect by closing the loop with the other person about what you learned and your next steps to reinforce trust.

💬 Quote

Reflection is how we convert our experiences into takeaways... Closing the loop lets people know they didn't waste their time and deepens connection.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on reflection and reconnecting after conversations

verses.title: Reflect and Reconnect
verses.text:
- Use the 'sift it and turn it' method to reflect effectively. Sift to identify key takeaways, then turn it over in your mind to see how it affects your story, steps, and stuff.
- Remember to reconnect with the person afterward to close the loop and show appreciation.

💬 Quote

Reflection is how we convert our experiences into takeaways or insights and our insights into actions.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on the importance of reflection for learning.

verses.title: Self-Diagnose Question Skills
verses.text:
- To gauge your asking skills, measure your statement-to-question ratio; too many statements indicate poor questioning.
- Avoid yes/no questions; prefer open questions starting with what or how for richer answers.

💬 Quote

If your ratio is a lot of statements and very few questions, you're a lousy asker... If your questions start with do or don't, you'll get surface-level answers.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on signs of poor question asking

verses.title: Evaluate Your Questions
verses.text:
- Evaluate your questions by plotting the ratio of statements to questions in conversations; too many statements indicate you aren't asking enough.
- Also, if you're a leader not receiving critical feedback, you may not be asking the right questions in the right way.

💬 Quote

If you're a leader and you're not getting critical feedback from the people around you, chances are you're not asking the right questions or you're not asking the right questions in the right ways.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on telltale signs of being a lousy question asker

verses.title: Statement-to-Question Ratio
verses.text:
- To self-diagnose if you are a lousy question asker, analyze conversations. 
- If the ratio of statements to questions is heavily tilted toward statements, you're likely not asking enough questions.

💬 Quote

If your ratio is a lot of statements and very few questions overall, you are a lousy asker because you're just not asking many people that questions.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on how to self-diagnose if you are a lousy question asker.

verses.title: Master Asking By Practice
verses.text:
- Master asking questions through conscious practice: recognize weaknesses, try new tactics, and level up gradually.
- Move from conscious incompetence to unconscious competence by repeated deliberate practice.

💬 Quote

The way you master it is no different than any skill... You get consciously incompetent, then practice, then make it natural.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on mastering the skill of asking questions

verses.title: Model and Teach Questioning Skills
verses.text:
- Teach question mastery by role-modeling curiosity, rewarding good questions, and hiring for inquiry skills.
- Encourage openness to feedback by demonstrating curiosity rather than defensiveness.

💬 Quote

The best way to teach it is to role model it yourself... You can build this into your hiring practices too.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on teaching others to ask better questions

verses.title: Fuel Kids' Natural Curiosity
verses.text:
- Nurture children's natural curiosity by accepting and honestly answering their questions.
- Discouraging questions early kills curiosity; fuel their questioning spirit to grow master questioners.

💬 Quote

Kids are born curious... Schools tell kids, sit down and shut up. Stop asking questions, which is tragic.
— Jeff Wetzler

Jeff Wetzler on encouraging kids to ask questions

chapters.title: Keith Ferrazzi: Why Great Leaders Never Lead Alone
chapters.description: In this engaging conversation, Keith Ferrazzi, bestselling author and thought leader on team dynamics, dives into his revolutionary concept of 'teamship.' He discusses how traditional leadership must evolve for high-performing teams to thrive. Ferrazzi highlights the importance of candor and psychological safety in unlocking team potential. He also shares insights on leveraging AI for enhanced collaboration and the shift in workplace dynamics post-pandemic, emphasizing the need for innovative strategies to foster meaningful connections.

verses.title: Teamship Defined
verses.text:
- Teamship is the concept of empowering teams to lead themselves, rather than relying solely on a leader.
-  Teams hold each other accountable, give feedback, and maintain energy, maximizing their potential.

💬 Quote

I think we've over-indexed on leadership, under-indexed on teamship.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the importance of teamship.

verses.title: Teamship vs. Leadership
verses.text:
- Keith Ferrazzi suggests we overemphasize leadership and undervalue teamship.
- Great teams, not just leaders, give feedback, maintain energy, hold each other accountable, and lift each other up.

💬 Quote

I think we've over-indexed on leadership, under-indexed on teamship.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the importance of teamship.

verses.title: Assessing and Improving Team Candor
verses.text:
- Diagnose team candor by asking if members challenge each other, even when risky.
- Implement "candor breaks" in meetings, allowing small groups to discuss unspoken issues.

💬 Quote

Do we challenge each other in the room when it's risky to do so, even when it's outside of our swim lanes or areas of expertise?
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on diagnosing team candor.

verses.title: Team Diagnostics - Candor
verses.text:
- To assess team candor, ask: "Do we challenge each other, even when it's risky or outside our expertise?"
- The average team scores less than two on a zero-to-five scale.

💬 Quote

Do we challenge each other in the room when it's risky to do so, even when it's outside of our swim lanes or areas of expertise?
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on a diagnostic question for team candor.

verses.title: Candor Break
verses.text:
- To increase candor during team meetings, implement "candor breaks."
- Divide participants into pairs and ask them to discuss what's not being said but needs to be for 30 seconds, then reconvene.

💬 Quote

Put onto your agendas of your meetings a simple line that says, we're going to do a candor break. That's when you stop in the middle of the meeting and you ask the team, what's not being said in here that needs to be said.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on how to encourage candor in meetings.

verses.title: Action Over Mindset
verses.text:
- Focus on implementing practical team-building practices instead of solely focusing on mindset shifts.
- Try "stress tests" where team members share achievements, struggles, and plans, then offer each other feedback.

💬 Quote

You don't think your way to new way of acting. You act your way to new way of thinking.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on prioritizing actions over mindsets.

verses.title: 21st Century Collaboration
verses.text:
- Leverage digital tools for collaboration and purposeful bonding in virtual or hybrid teams.
- Prioritize asynchronous collaboration and reduce reliance on traditional meetings.

💬 Quote

Stop using damn meetings as the primary form of collaboration.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the overuse of meetings.

verses.title: Collaboration at Cruise
verses.text:
- Gil West, at Cruise, observed that younger employees prioritized cloud collaboration before meetings.
- They identified problems, barriers, and solutions collaboratively before meeting to finalize decisions.

💬 Quote

We haven't collaborated yet.
— Cruise employees

Cruise employees' response to Gil West's suggestion of a meeting.

verses.title: Purposeful Bonding
verses.text:
- Instead of relying on serendipitous bonding in the office, practice purposeful bonding.
- Implement weekly energy checks where team members share what's draining their energy on a scale of zero to five.

💬 Quote

We're going to not practice serendipity bonding. We're going to practice purposeful bonding. Every week, we're going to do energy checks.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi suggests an alternative to serendipitous bonding for virtual and hybrid teams.

verses.title: Purposeful Collaboration and Bonding
verses.text:
- For virtual or hybrid teams, use purposeful cultural building and 21st-century collaboration tools.
- These tactics create stronger relationships and better performance than relying on serendipitous interactions.

💬 Quote

If you're going to be virtual or hybrid, [...] then you need to use 21st century collaboration and you need to use purposeful cultural building tactics and practices. That will actually get you on a steady state.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on building culture in virtual and hybrid teams.

verses.title: Teamship vs. Lone Genius
verses.text:
- The "lone genius" model, exemplified by figures like Steve Jobs and Elon Musk, may not be scalable for all organizations.
- Teamship fosters innovation through diverse inputs and co-elevation, offering a broader approach.

💬 Quote

The social contract [...] at Elf Beauty [...] is come here. You will thrive. You will grow faster and further than you ever have. But you have to be willing to let your team push you higher.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the social contract at Elf Beauty.

verses.title: Teamship in Government
verses.text:
- Guy Kawasaki asks how Ferrazzi would apply teamship principles to a potentially dysfunctional cabinet.
- Ferrazzi suggests using stress tests and expanding the definition of "team" to include external stakeholders.

💬 Quote

- I would, of course, serve my country in any way that I could. 
- So you would say yes. 
- I would say yes.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on whether he would apply teamship principles to a difficult cabinet.

verses.title: Candor vs. Conflict
verses.text:
- Candor is essential for healthy conflict, driving better solutions and team performance.
- Conflict avoidance, often mistaken for politeness, hinders progress and breeds resentment.

💬 Quote

I would always bet on a company that had healthy and even sometimes unhealthy conflict at its core, than a nice company that has political talking behind each other's backs.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the benefits of healthy conflict.

verses.title: Fostering Candor
verses.text:
- Encourage candor by setting clear expectations and providing simple assignments.
-  Use pre-meeting exercises, like shared Google Docs, to foster open dialogue and psychological safety.

💬 Quote

- I'm going to turn what I want in my culture, which isn't present in most companies. 
- I'm going to turn it into a simple assignment.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on promoting candor through assignments.

verses.title: Hiring for Teamship
verses.text:
- When hiring for teamship, consider candidates with team sports or military experience.
-  These backgrounds often foster grit, resilience, and an understanding of collaborative success.

💬 Quote

Try to find team sports enthusiasts.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on hiring for teamship.

verses.title: Beyond Share Price
verses.text:
- While financial goals are important, focusing solely on share price can be limiting.
- Prioritize re-engineering customer experience and value creation to achieve sustainable growth.

💬 Quote

If any of these companies have a goal of re-engineering shareholder value, but they're looking at it only from finance, not looking at it from a perspective of re-engineering the customer experience, then I think you're right. That's insipid.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the limitations of focusing solely on share price.

verses.title: DE&I and Teamship
verses.text:
- Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DE&I) are naturally integrated into teamship principles.
-  Building diverse teams, ensuring equitable practices, and fostering inclusive collaboration enhance team performance.

💬 Quote

What we found was most of the DE&I agenda is marbled into this book.
— Keith Ferrazzi

Keith Ferrazzi on the integration of DE&I into teamship.

chapters.title: Mike Maples Jr.: Inflection Points and Startup Success
chapters.description: Mike Maples Jr., Co-founder of Floodgate and early investor in Twitter and Twitch, dives into the art of spotting the ‘inflection points’ that can make or break startups. He argues that the wildest ideas often take the crown and explains how being dismissed can be a secret advantage. Maples tackles the nuances of pitching for venture capital and shares how understanding key changes in tech, especially in AI and automotive, can set successful entrepreneurs apart. Plus, he reflects on the importance of knowing when to walk away from success for lasting fulfillment.

verses.title: Inflections for Disruption
verses.text:
- Startups must leverage "inflections," external changes that allow them to disrupt existing markets.
- These inflections enable startups to offer unique value propositions, rendering comparisons to incumbents irrelevant.

💬 Quote

An inflection is a change event that's actually external to a startup [...] and it allows a startup capitalist to compete by changing the subject.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on the importance of inflections

verses.title: Inflections and Asymmetric Warfare
verses.text:
- An inflection is an external change event that startups can leverage.
- It allows them to compete by changing the subject and waging asymmetric warfare against established businesses.

💬 Quote

An inflection is a change event that's actually external to a startup [...] and it allows a startup [capitalist] to compete by changing the subject.
— Mike Maples, Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on the definition of an inflection point.

verses.title: Different, Not Better
verses.text:
- "Better" products often fail because customers prefer established companies.
- Startups should offer something radically different, impossible before the inflection, to truly capture the market.

💬 Quote

Better doesn't matter when you're startup because if you're better, then the customer is going to have an alternative to your startup.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on why "better" isn't enough

verses.title: Inflection Points for Founders
verses.text:
- Founders need a power to change the subject, leveraging inflections.
-  An inflection should provide a 10x benefit, empower many people, and have lasting impact.

💬 Quote

Founders need some type of a power to change the subject. [...] what matters [...] is three things. One is [...] the specific empowerment? [...] The second thing [...] is who does it empower? [...] the third aspect [...] is the empowerment.
— Mike Maples, Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on what founders should care about regarding inflections.

verses.title: Target Future Markets
verses.text:
- Aim for outlier potential by defining new markets, not competing in existing ones.
- Focus on the total future market, leveraging inflections to create something unprecedented.

💬 Quote

I'm not interested in the total existing or total available market. I'm interested in the total future market.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on market focus for startups

verses.title: Jobs' Inflection Mastery
verses.text:
- Steve Jobs, upon returning to Apple, consistently changed the subject rather than directly competing.
- From the iPod's hard disk to the iPhone's macOS integration, he harnessed inflections for differentiation.

💬 Quote

Jobs always found a way to change the subject. With the iPod, he found this inflection in the sense of a tiny little hard disk [...] and with the iPhone, he waited until the technology was ready.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on Steve Jobs' approach to innovation

verses.title: Defining True Startups
verses.text:
- True startups aren't just better versions of existing companies; they seek asymmetric, radical differentiation.
- Companies like Perplexity and Claude are more like future divisions of big tech than disruptive startups.

💬 Quote

I don't really think of them [Perplexity, Claude] as startups [...] they're not really trying to, in a capital efficient way, create some asymmetrically radically difference.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on differentiating startups

verses.title: Evaluate Future Potential
verses.text:
- Assess if a startup idea is "from the future" by looking at its founder's authenticity and obsession with that future.
- Early startup markets are driven by belief and aesthetics, not practicality, attracting early believers who co-create the future.

💬 Quote

Is this from the future? [...] the future's already here. It's just not evenly distributed.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on identifying future-oriented ideas

verses.title: AI Startup Example
verses.text:
- Applied Intuition, a company building autonomous vehicle simulation software, demonstrates a good approach to AI startups.
- It combines deep tech with a multidisciplinary approach, solving a strategic problem for major car companies.

💬 Quote

Applied Intuition [...] make autonomous vehicle simulation software [...] if you want to have a software-defined car, I'm your only path to getting there.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on Applied Intuition

verses.title: Future Potential Markets
verses.text:
- Companies using inflections define new markets, which are impossible to size in advance. 
- Focus on future *potential* markets created by powerful inflections.

💬 Quote

I believe that companies that harness inflections define new markets. And I can't size a future market, right? The future hasn't happened yet.
— Mike Maples, Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on Market Sizing

verses.title: Analyze Past Successes and Failures
verses.text:
- Conduct a forensic analysis of successful companies, including pitch decks, revenue milestones, and business models.
- Learn from false negatives by understanding the signals and reasons behind missed opportunities.

💬 Quote

I keep this database of, I call them hundred bagger startups [...] I study them. I create a time capsule of what it looked like at the seed round.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on analyzing past investments

verses.title: Reaching VCs
verses.text:
- The best way to reach a VC is through a referral from someone they respect.
- Focus on persuading credible individuals who validate your vision and attract early believers.

💬 Quote

The best way is to get a referral from somebody that we respect [...] is a function of your ability to persuade somebody credible that your future matters.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on getting introductions to VCs

verses.title: Crafting a Compelling Pitch
verses.text:
- Start your pitch by clearly explaining what your company does in simple terms, avoiding jargon.
- Focus on conveying your unique insight, early successes, and attracting believers, not countering objections.

💬 Quote

Slide number one, say what you do as if I know literally nothing. [...] Slide number two is what do I know about the future that's not obvious?
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on effective pitch content

verses.title: Focus on Believers
verses.text:
- Focus your energy on people who already believe in your insight.
- If a VC doesn't share your vision, they won't invest, regardless of how you address their objections.

💬 Quote

The only people that matter are the people who believe your insight. If a VC doesn't believe your insight, they're not going to invest. There's no way to overcome their objection. They're not going to invest no matter what until it's proven.
— Mike Maples, Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on focusing efforts when seeking investment.

verses.title: Focus on Believers
verses.text:
- Don't waste energy on those who dislike your idea, as most great ideas are initially unpopular.
- Focus on finding believers who share your vision and join your movement, prioritizing their feedback.

💬 Quote

If most people don't like your startup idea, that's a positive sign. If everybody likes your startup idea, it's too incremental.
— Mike Maples Jr.

Mike Maples Jr. on the value of non-consensus

chapters.title: Nancy Duarte: The Storyteller's Guide to Remarkable Presentations
chapters.description: Nancy Duarte, a presentation expert and founder of Duarte, dives into the art of impactful storytelling. She shares how emotional connection and authenticity can transform presentations into engaging experiences. Discover her unique Duarte method, where empathy plays a crucial role in communication. Duarte also discusses the significance of crafting memorable openings and utilizing visual aids effectively. Her insights reveal that mastering presentations is not just about visuals but also about fostering genuine connections with audiences.

verses.title: Jobs' Effectiveness
verses.text:
- Steve Jobs' effectiveness stemmed from evoking visceral audience reactions every 30 seconds.
-  He also marveled at his products, modeling the desired audience affection.

💬 Quote

in every speech he gives, almost every speech, he has the audience viscerally and physically reacting to what he's saying. So in about 30 second increments, they're either laughing or clapping.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte analyzing Steve Jobs' presentation style.

verses.title: Jobs' Meticulousness
verses.text:
- Steve Jobs meticulously oversaw every detail of his presentations, including pixel-level design.
- Apple's presentations are a significant marketing tool, demonstrating their commitment to communication.

💬 Quote

Steve Jobs cared about every pixel. [...] He spent a lot of time. It's not like these were just all built by someone else, personally fidgeted over every pixel on every slide.
— Guy Kawasaki

Guy Kawasaki on Steve Jobs' attention to detail.

verses.title: Authenticity over Imitation
verses.text:
- Effective presentation doesn't require emulating Steve Jobs.
- Be authentic and empathetic to your audience.

💬 Quote

you don't have to be quote, a Steve, you can be something else and still be a good presenter. Exactly. Be your true self. Be empathetic to the audience and show up as your true self.
— Guy Kawasaki and Nancy Duarte

Guy Kawasaki and Nancy Duarte on being authentic.

verses.title: Storytelling for Any Product
verses.text:
- Even with less exciting products, focus on the story and meaning.
-  Position your product as a tool that helps the customer, the hero, overcome challenges.

💬 Quote

if you can actually set your product [...] where the audience is the hero, because if you put an idea out there and the audience rejects your idea, your idea dies.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte on the importance of audience-centric storytelling.

verses.title: Duarte Method
verses.text:
- The Duarte Method centers on empathy, story, visuals, and delivery.
- It prioritizes understanding the audience's needs and context.

💬 Quote

So the Duarte method is 100% grounded in empathy with the kind of mechanism to it being story and storytelling.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte explaining the core of her method.

verses.title: Opening and Re-engaging
verses.text:
- Open presentations by establishing common ground with the audience and stating the presentation's purpose.
- Use "star moments"–-something they'll always remember–-to re-engage the audience.

💬 Quote

it has to be about the audience. [...] You have to establish common ground.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte on how to start a presentation.

verses.title: Glance Test
verses.text:
- The Duarte glance test ensures slides are quickly processed (under three seconds).
- Slides should amplify the spoken word, not compete with it.

💬 Quote

people can only process one stream of information. They will either process your slides or they will listen to you speak.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte explaining the rationale behind the Glance Test.

verses.title: Glance Test
verses.text:
- Design slides so people can process them in under five seconds, like a billboard seen from a moving car. 
- People can only process one stream of information: either they will read your slide, or listen to you.

💬 Quote

When I wrote Slidology, I realized that slides can't be dense. [...] they will either process your slides or they will listen to you speak. [...] they will read everything while you're talking and they can't do both.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte on the Glance Test.

verses.title: Slide Docs vs. Cinematic Slides
verses.text:
- Create cinematic slides for staged events or slide docs for detailed information sharing.
-  Slide docs resemble magazine spreads, offering visually rich, self-contained information.

💬 Quote

either be cinematic or just call it a document and tell everyone you're just reviewing a document. It just happens to be a visual document.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte introducing the concept of Slide Docs.

verses.title: Number of Slides
verses.text:
- The number of slides depends on content, not a fixed rule.
- Focus on clear narrative and adjust pacing based on time constraints.

💬 Quote

It all depends on how long the script is per slide. There isn't a formulaic way.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte on determining the appropriate number of slides.

verses.title: Slide Background Color
verses.text:
- Use a dark background in a dark room to create a mood.
- Use a light background in a dark room if you want the audience to see each other.

💬 Quote

If you are in a dark room and you're trying to create a mood, I would say a dark background. If you're in a dark room and you want to have the whole audience see each other and that's okay, then you can have a light background.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte on choosing slide background colors.

verses.title: Font Size Test
verses.text:
- To test slide font visibility, step back from a monitor (e.g., 18 inches) a distance equal to its size (18 feet).
- If you can read the text at that distance, it will likely be visible for everyone in the presentation hall.

💬 Quote

So if you have a 15 inch monitor [...] step back that many feet. And if you could see it, that would be what it's like for someone in the back of the room to see your slides.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte on how to test font size for presentations.

verses.title: Remarkable Presentation Tips
verses.text:
- To be a remarkable presenter, start with empathy and understand your audience.
-  Structure your presentation logically, emphasizing key takeaways with impactful moments.

💬 Quote

start with empathy, understand who you're talking to, map it out, chunk it out into really clear structure, have it be logical, pick the most important thing they need to walk away with and turn that into some sort of remarkable moment.
— Nancy Duarte

Nancy Duarte giving advice on how to present.

chapters.title: Why Reinvention Defines Success with Ilana Golan
chapters.description: Ilana Golan, a former F-16 flight instructor and a Silicon Valley entrepreneur, shares her journey of reinvention. She discusses how to embrace uncertainty and turn setbacks into opportunities while navigating biases in leadership. Ilana provides a step-by-step framework for career pivots, emphasizing the importance of personal branding and building support networks. She also critiques divisive leadership and highlights the need for adaptability in today’s fast-changing world. Her inspiring insights are perfect for anyone looking to redefine their career path.

verses.title: First Woman F-16 Flight Instructor
verses.text:
- Ilana Golan became an F-16 flight instructor even though women were not allowed to be combat pilots at the time.
- At 19–20 she had enormous responsibility and learned through failure while training combat pilots.

💬 Quote

They did this like very long, almost pilot school type thing for us... but at the end of the day, we could only be flight instructors.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan on training and early Air Force restrictions

verses.title: Reinvention Is Continuous
verses.text:
- Reinvention is a recurring necessity because careers now require regular relearning and role changes.
- Ilana says people will need to reinvent themselves every year or two to stay relevant.

💬 Quote

We're going into an era that we're going to need to reinvent themselves every year or two.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan on frequency of career reinvention

verses.title: Define Your Next Identity First
verses.text:
- First define what you want to be known for and what you want to do next.
- Then align your story and build ambassadors who will bring hidden-market opportunities.

💬 Quote

The first step is to understand what it is that you want to do and what do you want to be known for.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan on the stepwise reinvention process

verses.title: Use Public Platforms To Break Patterns
verses.text:
- Stop hiding and craft a strong personal brand so people see you in new roles.
- Use stages, panels, boards and advisory roles to create pattern interrupts that elevate your position.

💬 Quote

If I get off a stage of, I was, I don't know, TechCrunch disrupt... The minute you get off the stage, you already are elevated compared to others.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan on public positioning for elevation

verses.title: Get Allies Outside Comfort Zones
verses.text:
- Men will often be the gatekeepers to C-suite rooms, so make them your ambassadors rather than staying in women-only groups.
- Seek stages, boards and advisory roles to change how others template you.

💬 Quote

The people that will get you in the room are not the women. So stop being in women's groups.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan giving tactical advice for women seeking executive roles

verses.title: Adaptability As A Core Skill
verses.text:
- Reinvention is for driven people; there is no magic wand but there is a repeatable engineered process.
- Ilana believes adaptability (AQ) will become a core professional skill and should be taught widely.

💬 Quote

Adaptability and AQ is probably... one of the most important skills for the future of work.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan on why reinvention should be taught

verses.title: AI Surfaces Common Reinvention Themes
verses.text:
- AI can instantly synthesize reinvention advice tailored to public figures, showing common leadership themes.
- Guy used ChatGPT prompts to reveal humility, transparency, and empathy as core reinvention moves.

💬 Quote

Lead with humility instead of provocation... rebuild trust with transparency... bring more empathy to the mission.
— Guy Kawasaki

Guy Kawasaki reading ChatGPT reinvention advice for Elon Musk

verses.title: Reinvention Insights
verses.text:
Ask yourself how you can reinvent your personal brand and leadership style. Explore insights from others and apply them to your own journey of growth and change.

💬 Quote

Lead with humility instead of provocation.
— Guy Kawasaki

Guy Kawasaki on what AI suggested for Elon Musk's reinvention.

verses.title: Time Major Pivots Deliberately
verses.text:
- Timing matters for reinvention; postpone major pivots during intense life phases like late pregnancy.
- Reassess after major life events to confirm you still want the change and have capacity.

💬 Quote

I told somebody who was eight months pregnant... have the baby, wait three months. If you still think that way, let's come back.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan on timing reinvention around life events

verses.title: Build Your Personal Brand
verses.text:
- Prepare for reinvention by building your personal brand early.
- Ilana Golan advises that it is your only insurance policy to leap again and again.

💬 Quote

The only thing that will help you leap again and again is your own personal brand. That's the only insurance policy you have. So build it.
— Ileana Golan

Ileana Golan on what young people should do to prepare themselves to reinvent themselves multiple times.

verses.title: VC Pain From No Online Footprint
verses.text:
- Ilana missed building her personal brand and found investors struggled to validate her online presence when raising capital.
- That gap motivated her to emphasize personal branding as career insurance.

💬 Quote

We googled your name... you're non-existent. We don't even know how to validate this type of information.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan recounting investor feedback during fundraising

verses.title: Treat Your Brand As Career Insurance
verses.text:
- Build your personal brand deliberately; it's the best insurance policy for future leaps.
- Use it to control the narrative and surface opportunities rather than relying on luck.

💬 Quote

The only thing that will help you leap again and again is your own personal brand. That's the only insurance policy you have.
— Ilana Golan

Ilana Golan advising young professionals on branding

verses.title: Organic Personal Brand
verses.text:
- Guy Kawasaki believes a personal brand forms organically through doing good work, not through conscious building.
- He argues that figures like Jane Goodall didn't intentionally build their brands.

💬 Quote

The way to ultimately build your personal brand is to do good sh*t and everything falls from that.
— Guy Kawasaki

Guy Kawasaki on building a personal brand

chapters.title: Blogging for Developers with Mark Downie
chapters.description: Mark Downie, a Principal Product Manager at Microsoft and the lead maintainer of DasBlog-Core, discusses the evolving landscape of technical blogging. He emphasizes its significance in a world dominated by social media, highlighting the value of owning content and fostering community. Mark shares insights on how to write engaging technical blogs that resonate with developers, urging them to anticipate reader needs and cultivate meaningful connections. He also stresses blogging as a fulfilling tool for personal expression, regardless of audience size.

verses.title: Digital Sharecropping
verses.text:
- Mark Downie realized the importance of owning his content after Live Spaces shut down in 2005.
- He felt like he was 'planting food on somebody else's land'.

💬 Quote

I was literally planting food on somebody else's land [...] and I'm wondering why none of the food I've planted is in the place I put it [...] it wasn't your land to start with.
— Mark Downie

Mark Downie on losing content after Live Spaces shut down.

verses.title: Digital Sharecropping
verses.text:
- The concept of "digital sharecropping" highlights the risks of building content on platforms you don't own.
- Users invest effort, but the platform can remove content or disappear.

💬 Quote

Digital sharecropping has been a conversation that's been happening for years [...] where you are building someone else's business with your words.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the risks of building content on platforms you don't own.

verses.title: Own Your Words
verses.text:
- Publish content on your own platform first, then share it elsewhere (POSSE).
- This ensures ownership and control over your content.

💬 Quote

Publish once, syndicate everywhere. [...] starts everything on his blog. If he says anything, any word, it starts on his website and then it proliferates from there.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman explaining the POSSE concept.

verses.title: POSSE - Publish Once, Syndicate Everywhere
verses.text:
- Publish content on your own platform first to maintain control.
- Then, syndicate it to other platforms like Twitter, Instagram, etc.

💬 Quote

Publish once, syndicate everywhere.

verses.title: Blogging vs. Social Media
verses.text:
- Blogging in your own space helps avoid echo chambers and content bubbles.
- Curating your own space allows for unique expression and avoids the noise of social networks.

💬 Quote

I think holding your kind of space, your, your garden and, and curating it the way you want so it doesn't just become part of the overall noise of a particular social network is really, really important.
— Mark Downie

Mark Downie on the benefits of owning your online space.

verses.title: Write for Your Future Self
verses.text:
- Write blog posts that would be helpful to your future self.
- This ensures content is valuable and addresses real needs.

💬 Quote

Is it something that I'd find helpful to me in some future date? Like, that's my first kind of, like, is the principle of why I'm writing this going to be important to me some future point?
— Mark Downie

Mark Downie on his primary principle for writing blog posts.

verses.title: Writing Blog Posts
verses.text:
- When writing blog posts, consider if the information will be valuable to your future self.
- If so, it will likely be relevant to others facing similar questions.

💬 Quote

Is it something that I'd find helpful to me in some future date? [...] if I look through it through that lens, it's probably going to be important to somebody right now.
— Mark Downie

Mark Downie on prioritizing value in blog posts.

verses.title: Provide Context
verses.text:
- Add context to your blog posts, assuming readers know less than you think.
- Context is crucial for understanding and making content valuable.

💬 Quote

- You're always missing a paragraph and you assume that I know way more than I do [...] give me more context please.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman emphasizing the importance of adding context to blog posts.

verses.title: Unexpected Success
verses.text:
- Mark Downie's most popular blog post was a "throwaway" post about yearly reviews.
-  He assumed it was only relevant to him, but it resonated with a wider audience.

💬 Quote

My most popular blog was one that was, um, rooted in, I was trying to figure out how to do my yearly reviews. [...] I threw this away [...] but apparently not everybody does reviews.
— Mark Downie

Mark Downie sharing an anecdote about an unexpectedly popular blog post.

verses.title: Blogging's Reach
verses.text:
- Conserve keystrokes by blogging; your reach is larger than you think.
- Even a small audience is significant compared to individual communication.
