💬 Quote

If three people read your blog, that is a huge audience because it's not, it's, it's you times two extra people.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the impact of even a small blog audience.

verses.title: Publish Drafts
verses.text:
- Publish drafts instead of perfecting them; every unpublished draft is unread.
-  Don't let perfectionism prevent you from sharing your work.

💬 Quote

If you're listening right now, Mark and I are experienced decade, multiple decade long bloggers, publish just the draft because [...] every day that you don't publish that draft is a day where no one's reading it.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman advising to publish drafts instead of perfecting them.

verses.title: Blog When Inspired
verses.text:
- Blog when inspiration strikes, don't worry about a strict cadence.
- Focus on creating valuable content, not adhering to a schedule.

💬 Quote

- Worrying about your cadence, worrying about when the right time, I kind of like to get things done and in the time it takes [...] blog when the feeling hits me.
— Mark Downie

Mark Downie on the importance of blogging when inspired, not on a schedule.

verses.title: Blog vs. Social Media
verses.text:
- A blog is more valuable than a large social media following.
- It demonstrates writing skills and provides a living resume of your work.

💬 Quote

- I would rather someone have a blog than a social media presence. [...] if you could write a blog engine [...] that's going to show me that you're keeping the site up. You're updating the search. You're blogging long form.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the value of a blog over social media presence.

chapters.title: Building a better internet with Cloudflare's Rita Kozlov
chapters.description: Rita Kozlov, Senior Director of Product for Cloudflare Workers & AI, talks about Cloudflare's serverless platform on the edge and gives tips for developers. The podcast covers Cloudflare's mission, CDN importance, WAF, V8, isolates, network components and capabilities, Cloudflare's approach, and Cloudflare's use of GPUs in their global network.

verses.title: CloudFlare: Obsession with Time to First Dopamine
verses.text: The team at CloudFlare is focused on achieving a quick 'time to first dopamine' for developers, which refers to the feeling of satisfaction when getting something up and running rapidly.

chapters.title: Computing History with Atari and Commodore's Leonard Tramiel

verses.title: A Personal Story on the Importance of Access to Technology
verses.text:
1. The speaker's parents made a significant sacrifice in order to provide him with access to a computer
2. Access to information is crucial for children
3. The school district and teachers played a role in providing access to technology

chapters.title: Conscious Leadership with Ginny Clarke
chapters.description: Exploring conscious leadership, Ginny Clarke discusses ego in leadership, gender energy balance, physical presence, authenticity, and challenging the status quo. She reflects on fulfillment, love, empathy, and balancing honesty with empathy in feedback for personal and professional growth.

verses.title: The Importance of Breathing for Relaxation and Anxiety
verses.text: Breathing plays a crucial role in relaxation and managing anxiety. Often, people don't breathe enough, leading to feelings of anxiety and stress. By intentionally focusing on breathing out, individuals can initiate a physical shift in their body, promoting relaxation. Taking the time to focus on breathing allows for moments of calm and can help in dealing with work-related anxiety and stress.

chapters.title: Daring to be Different with Raji Rajagopalan

verses.title: How to Overcome Impostor Syndrome
verses.text:
1. No, the smartest person in the room is not always the best leader.
2. It is important to get on the balcony and listen to what is happening in the field.
3. When engineers are talking, it is important to listen.

chapters.title: Dave Barry is the Class Clown: The Memoirs of a Professional Wiseass
chapters.description: Dave Barry, a Pulitzer Prize-winning humorist and author known for his witty columns, shares his journey through the ups and downs of writing and promotional tours. He humorously discusses the quirks of technology in relationships and age, the chaos of newspaper syndication, and an amusing encounter on The Tonight Show. Barry also reflects on writing for the Oscars, revealing the burdens of comedic expectations, and indulges in light-hearted tales from his nightlife, emphasizing how humor remains a vital part of life.

verses.title: Pulitzer Prize Surprise Story
verses.text:
- Dave Barry missed a trip to Key West because he was called in to the Miami Herald to learn he'd won a Pulitzer Prize.
- He comforted his son by promising a Nintendo, making his son's happiness the highlight during the award announcement.

💬 Quote

So at that moment, I realized two things. One is I'm about to win a Pulitzer Prize, and the other is we're not going to Key West.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry recounting how he won the Pulitzer Prize unexpectedly.

verses.title: Hotel Sign Theft for Oprah
verses.text:
- Dave Barry stole a hotel towel sign for a guest bathroom prank and planned to return it on Oprah.
- When the original sign was delayed, he stole a replacement sign to keep his promise to appear on the show.

💬 Quote

Because I'm on a book tour, I'm willing to steal again and lie to Oprah to promote my book. So we have no ethics at all in this business, none.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry sharing a humorous and borderline unethical story from a book tour.

verses.title: AI Can't Do Humor Yet
verses.text:
- Dave Barry observes AI cannot yet capture humor well, despite many attempts.
- He finds comfort in AI failing to write good humor even as it encroaches on other writing.

💬 Quote

I got at least 100 emails from friends...asking AI to write a humor column Dave Barry's style. All of them sucked.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry discussing AI’s limitations related to humor writing.

verses.title: Neil Diamond Fan Backlash
verses.text:
- Dave Barry's critical humor on Christmas carols sparked passionate responses, especially from Neil Diamond fans.
- The backlash inspired a new column and a book about songs people hate, drawing ideas from reader reactions.

💬 Quote

I wrote a column about the Neil Diamond people being mad at me. That got a whole bunch more mail from people who were on my team.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry discussing the fan reaction to his humorous criticism.

verses.title: Tonight Show and Johnny Carson
verses.text:
- Dave Barry appeared on Johnny Carson’s Tonight Show after a bruising phone interview but had a great experience.
- Johnny Carson’s off-air comment humorously deflated Barry’s confidence with a witty jab.

💬 Quote

He lights a cigarette, turns to me. This is the only interaction I had with him off the air. It's a do-it book. And he goes, I used to do it yourself. You can't do shit yourself.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry recalling his Johnny Carson moment on The Tonight Show.

verses.title: Author Band: Rock Bottom Remainders
verses.text:
- Dave Barry joined the Rock Bottom Remainders, a band of famous authors, despite their bad musical skills.
- The band endures longer than great bands like The Beatles because they have fun, not skill.

💬 Quote

Roy Blunt Jr. describes our genre as hard listening music. We're not good at music but really good at endurance.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry on his author band’s musical ability and longevity.

verses.title: Start with Local Paper Humor Column
verses.text:
- Dave Barry’s first writing job involved dull municipal reporting but also allowed him to write humorous op-eds called ad libs.
- This experience started his column writing career, which he didn’t initially expect to pursue full time.

💬 Quote

They would let you write little essays. They called them ad libs. And I would write a humor one.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry describing his early writing career at a local newspaper.

verses.title: Life as a Class Clown
verses.text:
- Dave Barry was elected class clown in high school and was a lifelong wise ass.
- Teachers told him he couldn’t joke his way through life, which he found was inaccurate.

💬 Quote

I was told by more than one teacher, that's very funny, David, but you can't joke your way through life, which turns out to be inaccurate.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry sharing about his wise ass personality since school days.

verses.title: Vodka Gimlet Spleen Story
verses.text:
- Dave Barry shares a drunken story about confusion over a 'no spleen' note written on his arm at a hotel bar.
- The story humorously connects to concerns about organ harvesting and the dangers of too many vodka gimlets.

💬 Quote

I see something written on my arm. It says, no spleen. I have no memory of why that's there.
— Dave Barry

Dave Barry recounting a funny story about his spleen and drunken misunderstandings.

chapters.title: Finding your Technology Passion with Peter Ayedun
chapters.description: Peter Ayedun, CEO of TruGrid, shares his journey from studying architecture to computer science, discussing his passion for technology and the influence of Nigerian hustle culture. He explores the evolution of thin clients into virtual desktops, the concept of MVP, and the importance of emotional intelligence and diversity in the tech industry.

verses.title: Finding Happiness in Your Career
verses.text: Fulfillment in one's career comes from finding a job that brings happiness, rather than succumbing to parental pressure to pursue traditional professions like law or medicine. Many people regretfully follow these paths, realizing it's not what they truly desired. By prioritizing happiness in their career choice, individuals can lead a more fulfilled life.

chapters.title: From Ethernet to Geothermal Energy with Bob Metcalfe

verses.title: I've tried retiring before several times
verses.text: A computational engineer starting a new career modeling geothermal wells at MIT discusses the challenges and excitement of transitioning careers, the importance of retiring and pursuing what you want to do, and the potential of geothermal energy to change the world.

verses.title: The Pathology of the Internet
verses.text: The rapid increase in connectivity has led to various pathologies, including hacking, pornography, and spam. However, society has learned to manage these issues to an acceptable level. Now, new challenges like fake news and censorship have emerged, and it's up to society to determine what is acceptable in terms of behavior and information on the internet.

chapters.title: Is AI the new UI? Talking to computers with Noelle Russell
chapters.description: Noelle Russell, an AI expert and Alexa developer, dives into the fascinating world of voice technology. She discusses how AI is transforming user interactions and explores the evolution of devices like Alexa and Google Home. The conversation also highlights the importance of ethics and transparency in AI, alongside challenges in naming these technologies. Russell emphasizes the need for businesses to adapt to changing consumer relationships with AI, while reflecting on the economic implications of smart speakers and their sustainability.

verses.title: Trust Diminishes in the Age of AI
verses.text: A Stanford study highlights the complex relationship between trust and the implementation of AI. When a business integrates AI, customer trust inherently diminishes, as the presence of robots in interactions evokes skepticism. This decline in trust escalates significantly if customers discover they were interacting with an AI under the false pretense that it was human or that their communications were not being recorded. Transparency is crucial, as misrepresentation can severely undermine trust further.

chapters.title: Micro.blog and owning your words with Manton Reece
chapters.description: Manton Reece, CEO and founder of Micro.blog, shares his insights on independent blogging and the digital landscape. He discusses the hurdles of domain registration and the need to maintain online spaces amid content degradation. Manton emphasizes the value of simple, affordable subscription models and how they combat the spam epidemic. He also explores the technical advantages of using Hugo for blogging and the importance of personal expression online, urging everyone to start sharing their stories without hesitation.

verses.title: Broken URLs
verses.text:
- Scott Hanselman often compliments actors on social media. 
- He finds that many actors' websites have expired certificates or are deactivated, highlighting the ephemeral nature of URLs.

💬 Quote

It's a deactivated Squarespace site. It's a random, [...] it's just a broken URL. It's, it's not happened once. It's happened dozens of times.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the prevalence of broken URLs, especially for individuals without dedicated web teams.

verses.title: Ephemeral Nature of URLs
verses.text:
Many links break over time, making archiving crucial. Manton Reece's micro.blog combats link rot by archiving linked content and preserving user blogs.

💬 Quote

So many links are broken now it's almost like the kind of default state that things expire and wither away.
— Manton Reece

Manton Reece on the problem of link rot on the internet.

verses.title: Walled Garden Appeal
verses.text:
Walled gardens attract users with frictionless signup and addictive algorithms. These platforms offer easy onboarding and engagement-driven timelines, creating a dopamine loop.

💬 Quote

Is it the frictionless signup or is it the algorithm, the infinite scroll algorithm, or is it the discoverability? What is it that keeps causing people or compelling people to sign up for walled gardens?
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman questioning the appeal of walled gardens.

verses.title: A Quieter Web
verses.text:
Prioritize content over popularity. Micro.blog's discover section is hand-curated, emphasizing quality posts over follower counts, promoting a quieter online experience.

💬 Quote

If you don't want an that shouts at you, this is probably the place to hang out.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman appreciating the quieter nature of micro.blog.

verses.title: The POSSE Principle
verses.text:
Publish on your own site first, then syndicate elsewhere (POSSE). This prioritizes content ownership and control while extending reach to various social platforms.

💬 Quote

Publish on your own site, syndicate elsewhere.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman explaining the POSSE principle.

verses.title: Own Your Content
verses.text:
- Publish on your own site first (using the "Posse" principle).
- Then, syndicate the content to other platforms.

💬 Quote

I post to my own blog where I control it. And then I copy it out to other platforms so that I can still stay connected to people.
— Manton Reece

Manton Reece on the importance of owning your content.

verses.title: Affordable Blogging
verses.text:
Micro.blog offers affordable plans, starting at $1/month.  This low cost makes it accessible for more people seeking to own their content.

💬 Quote

It's a buck, five bucks, or ten bucks. We're literally talking about less than a cup of coffee here.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman highlighting the affordability of micro.blog.

verses.title: Getting Started on Micro.blog
verses.text:
New micro.blog users should connect existing social media accounts and import prior content.  Consider a custom domain or subdomain for your blog.

💬 Quote

What's the thing that I should do first? [...] What's the best way for me to get the most experience if I sign up for this and I don't know what I should do to incorporate micro.blog into my lifestyle?
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman asking for onboarding advice for new micro.blog users.

verses.title: Just Post It
verses.text:
Don't overthink blogging; just post your drafts and share your unique perspective. Everyone has something valuable to contribute online.

💬 Quote

Post the draft. If you have a draft, post that draft.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman encouraging listeners to post their drafts.

chapters.title: Psychology of Software Teams with Dr. Cat Hicks
Startup Mindsets with Earl Valencia
chapters.description: Earl Valencia, a tech leader and venture-backed founder, shares insights from his book, 'Startup Mindsets: A Blueprint to Thrive in an Innovation-Driven and Globally Connected World.' He explores what it takes to succeed beyond Silicon Valley's glamor, focusing on the importance of having a personal mission to make a positive impact. The conversation contrasts traditional leadership with the dynamic mindset required for startups, emphasizing resilience and passion. Valencia challenges the definitions of success and encourages founders to align their goals with market needs for lasting achievement.

verses.title: Not Everyone Should Found Startups
verses.text:
- Not everyone should be a startup founder; it's a difficult path that requires consideration before starting.
- The book guides on important questions to answer before launching a startup.

💬 Quote

Our theory really is that not everybody should be a startup founder.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on startup founders

verses.title: Earl’s Global Journey to Silicon Valley
verses.text:
- Earl grew up in Southeast Asia and studied aerospace engineering aiming to be an astronaut before entering Silicon Valley.
- Silicon Valley’s startup culture is unique, focusing on creating value, not just IPOs or money.

💬 Quote

I was in aerospace engineering land, like you don't really think about startups. And then in Silicon Valley, people talk about unicorns and billion-dollar companies.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on his background and Silicon Valley culture

verses.title: Anchor Startup to Personal Mission
verses.text:
- Connect your startup journey to a personal mission or impact statement to persevere through tough times.
- Values guide hiring, investor choices, and technology ethics during challenges.

💬 Quote

The only way that you can go past the grind is to actually connect it to something bigger than yourself.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on startup resilience

verses.title: Startup Growth vs Traditional Business
verses.text:
- Startup growth often follows an exponential curve with initial losses before rapid scaling.
- Long-term thinking and risk tolerance differentiate startup mindsets from traditional business ones.

💬 Quote

When you see a startup, there's actually multiple months, if not years, that you will lose money. Then hopefully in the future you have this exponential curve.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on startup mindsets versus SME mindset

verses.title: Founder Status Requires Purpose
verses.text:
- Being a founder isn't about glamour or just having a domain or prototype.
- Understand why you want to start a business and stay true to that mission.

💬 Quote

What's more important is making sure that you are true to your mission and actually figuring out why do you want to do this in the first place.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on authentic founder motivation

verses.title: Passion Overcomes Startup Failure Risk
verses.text:
- Passion for solving a problem must be strong enough to persist even if the startup fails.
- Founders rarely regret pursuing meaningful problems despite failure.

💬 Quote

Even if it doesn't succeed, if they worked on something so meaningful to them, they will not regret it a single day.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on startup passion and failure acceptance

verses.title: Startup Motivation
verses.text:
- Center your startup around a problem you're so passionate about solving that you'd take it on even if it will probably fail.
- Earl Valencia found that most founders talked at length about something personal to them, like an injustice they saw.

💬 Quote

Why did you start the company? And the second question is, if the company fails, would you regret it?
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia talking about questions he asked founders for the book

verses.title: Silicon Valley’s Unique Startup Ecosystem
verses.text:
- Silicon Valley's advantage lies in its talent magnet, hyper connectivity, and risk-tolerant mindset.
- These ingredients create an innovation ecosystem hard to replicate elsewhere.

💬 Quote

What's most important is this mindset that people have in order to succeed. This notion of risk tolerance...
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on Silicon Valley's innovation culture

verses.title: Embrace Humility and Manage Risk
verses.text:
- Cultivate intellectual humility by recognizing high risks and uncertain luck in startup success.
- Control risk with open eyes rather than being blinded by idealism.

💬 Quote

I call it intellectual humility, of knowing that you're working on it but also understanding you're in an area of extreme high risk.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on managing startup risk

verses.title: Act Fast, Avoid Wannapreneur Trap
verses.text:
- Distinguish entrepreneurs by their bias for action; they act quickly to solve problems they care about.
- Avoid being a "wannapreneur" who endlessly talks but never executes.

💬 Quote

You know that you have the entrepreneur's DNA when you don't let time pass by because the problem is so important.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on entrepreneur action bias

verses.title: Bias for Action
verses.text:
- Entrepreneurs have a bias for action and solve problems quickly, because the problem they're solving is so important to them.
- Spot entrepreneurs by observing if they act quickly on their ideas instead of delaying.

💬 Quote

You know that you have the entrepreneur's DNA is when you don't let time pass by. Because the problem that you're solving is so important to you that you need to try to solve it as quickly as you can.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on what defines an entrepreneur

verses.title: Investors Bet on Founder Trust
verses.text:
- Investors bet more on trusted, competent founders with founder-market fit than just ideas.
- Founder’s mission alignment assures investors of persistence during hard times.

💬 Quote

You might have the best idea, but to get investment you have to figure out how to make investors trust you.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on investment decisions and founder qualities

verses.title: Investing Heuristic
verses.text:
- Earl Valencia recalls Eric Schmidt's advice that investing happens when greed overcomes fear. 
- De-risking investment involves trusting the person or team's competence and their founder-market fit.

💬 Quote

The point of investing is when greed overcomes fear.

verses.title: Startup Journey Is a Marathon
verses.text:
- Treat building a startup as a marathon that requires patience, resilience, and many experiments.
- Focus on creating a sustainable business rather than quick acquisitions or shortcuts.

💬 Quote

It's a grind and you cannot make it quick. You have to be patient addressing a problem worth solving.
— Earl Valencia

Earl Valencia on startup resilience and patience

chapters.title: Take hold of your own performance with Ash Coleman Hynie
chapters.description: Ash Hynie, CEO of CounterPoint and a seasoned technologist, discusses the evolving dynamics between managers and employees. He emphasizes the need for open communication and respect in performance reviews, challenging the prevailing perceptions within tech. The conversation touches on the struggles of high-performing engineers stepping into management roles and the importance of accountability to foster trust. Hynie advocates for a humanized management approach that balances professionalism with emotional expression and encourages genuine connections in corporate environments.

verses.title: Changing Manager Dynamics
verses.text:
- The dynamic between managers and individual contributors has changed significantly.
- Managers are now often required to be productive, creating tension between managing and doing individual work.

💬 Quote

The dynamics of managers and independent contributors has changed so much over the years [...].
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on the evolving role of managers.

verses.title: Sudden Managers
verses.text:
- Scott Hanselman observes that people are often abruptly promoted to management without proper training.
- This contrasts with the past, where management was a formal career path with dedicated training.

💬 Quote

People wake up one day and become a manager. [...]. It was a career path.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the lack of formal management training.

verses.title: Erosion of Respect
verses.text:
- There's an erosion of respect for managers, with engineers questioning their value.
-  This is partly due to increased busywork, reporting, and superficial metrics, not quality products.

💬 Quote

[...] there's an erosion of respect for the manager job by the line engineers [...]. They just call me and they want status updates.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the diminishing respect for managers.

verses.title: Infantilizing Employees
verses.text:
- The erosion of trust leads to micromanagement concerns, like tracking mouse movements.
-  This infantilizes employees, contrasting with the past focus on task completion and quality products.

💬 Quote

When did we start infantilizing grown-ass adults?
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on treating employees like children.

verses.title: Empathy for Leadership
verses.text:
-  Scott Hanselman suggests encouraging empathy for leadership pressures.
- Understanding a VP's challenges can help individual contributors align with business needs.

💬 Quote

Put yourself in the mind of vice president [...]. What's her, uh, what's her pressures? Who's calling her?
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on understanding leadership perspectives.

verses.title: Job Hopping vs. Loyalty
verses.text:
- Scott expresses a generational bias against frequent job hopping.
- Younger generations prioritize salary growth over company loyalty due to economic pressures.

💬 Quote

[...] I see 18 months at Zoom, 18 months at Google, 18 months at Amazon, 18 months at Netflix.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on his generational bias towards job hopping.

verses.title: Gamified Careers
verses.text:
- The gamification of careers leads to frequent job changes for higher salaries, but impacts leadership.
- This results in less empathetic managers, creating dissatisfaction for both managers and employees.

💬 Quote

However, what we've lost a lot of in that game is people who are empathetic leaders.
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on the loss of empathy due to gamified career paths.

verses.title: Treat Employees Like Adults
verses.text:
-  Treat employees like adults, acknowledging their capacity for critical thinking and pushback.
-  Foster accountability, a key aspect often lost in corporate tech, especially with "adulting".

💬 Quote

We hire adults. We're not hiring children here and we're going to treat them like adults.
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on treating employees like responsible adults.

verses.title: Direct Communication
verses.text:
- Ash Hynie contrasts her direct communication style with her husband's less direct approach.
- She emphasizes clear expectations and accountability in both personal and professional settings.

💬 Quote

I am so sorry, but in my household growing up, we did not get the, you know, dishes are in the sink.
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on the importance of direct communication.

verses.title: Feelings and Accountability
verses.text:
- Feelings have a place in the workplace, but they don't negate accountability.
-  Acknowledge emotions while reinforcing responsibilities, creating a balanced approach.

💬 Quote

I think that, again, with this adult conversation is about the maturity of being able to handle adult feelings [...].
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on balancing feelings and accountability.

verses.title: Humor and Context
verses.text:
- Scott Hanselman uses humor (animated GIFs) at work, but learned to be mindful of context.
-  His VP's feedback ("not helpful") highlighted the need for appropriateness, even with humor.

💬 Quote

And sometimes you just drop an absolute banger. And you know, like I'm trying to focus on some important quarterly thing, but like I, I, animated GIF [...].
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on using humor at work.

verses.title: Level Privilege
verses.text:
-  Seniority brings privileges like faster email responses and fewer spelling errors.
- Scott uses GIFs and emojis to humanize himself and make interactions less formal.

💬 Quote

Someone said that it's level privilege. I've talked about level privilege, like when you unpack all the different privileges, but having a certain level, like having senior in your name means your emails get like done faster.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the privileges that come with seniority.

verses.title: Authenticity and Community
verses.text:
-  Climbing the corporate ladder can lead to losing one's identity and relatability.
-  Ash emphasizes authentic communication and community building to combat workplace loneliness.

💬 Quote

They want you to speak a very specific type of talk that is synonymous with I'm a high-level executive at a company. And I lost my identity making that climb [...].
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on maintaining authenticity in the workplace.

verses.title: Less Formal Communication
verses.text:
- Make work less formal by communicating authentically, like using GIFs appropriately.
-  Recognize the unspoken context and interpersonal dynamics influencing interactions.

💬 Quote

How do I make this less formal and just communicate with people like we're people?
— Ash Hynie

Ash Hynie on making workplace communication less formal.

chapters.title: The Return of Scott's Wife Mo
chapters.description: Scott and Mo celebrate nearly 25 years of marriage, exploring their journey through cultural differences and the quirks of mixing backgrounds. They discuss the philosophy of Ubuntu, the importance of positive intentions in relationships, and the complexities of evolving parenthood as they face an empty nest. The conversation also touches on personal growth, the impact of AI on life choices, and the significance of giving back, all wrapped in humor and insightful tales from their lives.

verses.title: Presume Positive Intent
verses.text:
- In a marriage, presume your partner is on the same side as you, even when they make a mistake.
- Assume positive intent, rather than attributing their actions to malice or negativity.

💬 Quote

Presume that we're on the same side and it was done with positivity [...] rather than assuming.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on maintaining a successful long-term marriage.

verses.title: Teenage Selfishness
verses.text:
- Teenagers are developmentally prone to selfishness.
- While parents can gently encourage better behavior, it's crucial to recognize and accept this as a normal phase.

💬 Quote

Yes, they are. That's exactly what they're supposed to do. Now that doesn't excuse it. That doesn't say a selfish 19-year is a bad thing.

verses.title: Unspoken Expectations
verses.text:
- Avoid unspoken expectations in a relationship, as they can lead to resentment over time.
- Communicate clearly about even simple things, like who's making dinner.

💬 Quote

Unspoken expectations breed resentment. [...] It can be as simple as lunch.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman's marriage advice after 24 years.

chapters.title: The Road to Entrepreneurship with Nanxi Liu

verses.title: Tips for Networking: Adding Value, Hosting Events, and Generosity with Introductions
verses.text: Entrepreneur emphasizes the importance of adding value to others and building connections through hosting gatherings, even with a small budget. He calls these gatherings 'nerd dinners' and believes that bringing together energetic people can lead to meaningful connections. He also encourages lending one's privilege to create inclusive teams and generously making introductions.

chapters.title: Tiny Experiments with Anne-Laure Le Cunff
chapters.description: Anne-Laure Le Cunff, a neuroscientist and founder of Ness Labs, discusses the power of 'tiny experiments' in fostering personal growth. She advocates for a flexible approach to goal-setting, prioritizing curiosity over rigid outcomes. Anne-Laure shares how societal pressures can hinder mental health, especially in youth, and emphasizes the importance of community support in personal projects. She offers practical strategies for overcoming procrastination and embracing imperfection as a pathway to meaningful change, sparking innovation in our lives.

verses.title: Linearity and Goals
verses.text:
- Linear goals can cause fear and competition, yet we often organize our lives around them. 
- This can be problematic as it ties happiness to specific outcomes and creates a sense of failure if not achieved.

💬 Quote

The linear goals cause fear, linear goals cause competition. But this is challenging because we are moving through time linearly in a single direction.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the conflict between linear time and goal setting.

verses.title: Types of Goals
verses.text:
- Differentiate between looking forward to life events and linear goals tied to self-worth. 
- While some goals benefit from linearity, consider an experimental approach for other areas, starting with a question.

💬 Quote

I think there's a difference between looking forward to events in life, activities, moments that you're excited about. And between that and a linear goal, which is an outcome that you organize your life around, where you usually tie your happiness to this happening in a way you want it to happen or not.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff distinguishes between enjoying planned events and tying happiness to rigid linear goals.

verses.title: Tyranny of Purpose
verses.text:
- Scott Hanselman's children feel pressured to have a life plan, leading to the 'tyranny of purpose'. 
- This emphasizes the societal pressure of achieving specific linear goals and the fear of failure.

💬 Quote

And both of them feel pressure to have a plan. [...] And then if you don't, then you're just a failure.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman discusses the pressures his children face to have a defined life plan.

verses.title: Obsession with Purpose
verses.text:
- The obsession with finding one's purpose has increased dramatically. 
- This can lead to tying self-worth to a single goal, causing distress if it fails, as seen with startups.

💬 Quote

The number of mentions of finding your purpose has increased by 700% in books over the past two decades. So there is an obsession with finding our purpose.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff on the rise of the 'find your purpose' narrative and its potential drawbacks.

verses.title: Finding Your Thing
verses.text:
- People who've 'found their thing' rarely achieved it through a linear plan, but rather through exploration and experimentation. 
- They see purpose as a messy, evolving process, not a fixed destination.

💬 Quote

When you talk to people who have found their thing, [...] None of them will tell you that they obsessed over it and they had a plan.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff shares insights on how people discover their passions.

verses.title: Generative Social Media Use
verses.text:
- Instead of simply stopping scrolling on social media, try creating and sharing something useful. 
- Sharing something positive, even once a week, can be more beneficial than complete abstinence.

💬 Quote

Try to replace a little bit of the scrolling with something that is a bit more generative.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff suggests creating content as a healthier approach to social media.

verses.title: Learning in Public
verses.text:
- 'Learning in public' is a spectrum, not just broadcasting to a large audience. 
- It can be as simple as sharing your learning journey with one other person.

💬 Quote

But learning in public is a spectrum. [...] for most people, learning in public is really just making sure that outside of yourself, there is at least one other human being who is aware of what you're currently trying to study.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff clarifies what 'learning in public' entails.

verses.title: YouTube Experiment
verses.text:
- Anne-Laure Le Cunff ran a YouTube experiment despite initial hesitation. 
- After three months, she stopped, demonstrating the importance of allowing yourself to quit.

💬 Quote

And I decided to make that an experiment. [...] And at the end of the experiment, [...] I actually didn't enjoy producing the videos.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff shares a personal story of trying something new and choosing to stop.

verses.title: Tiny Experiment Example
verses.text:
- Start a tiny experiment like taking a short daily walk for a set duration. 
- Reflect on its impact and adjust as needed—even small changes count.

💬 Quote

So I decided to create a tiny experiment where I said, I am going to take a 20 minute walk every day for the next 20 days.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff gives a simple example of a 'tiny experiment'.

verses.title: Walking Experiment
verses.text:
- Anne-Laure Le Cunff shares a tiny experiment where she committed to a 20-minute daily walk for 20 days to combat burnout during her book launch.
- She emphasizes that the action should have a defined duration, allowing for reflection on its impact on mood and well-being.

💬 Quote

I decided to create a tiny experiment where I said, I am going to take a 20 minute walk every day for the next 20 days.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff describing a tiny experiment she did to address burnout.

verses.title: From Experiment to Habit
verses.text:
- Use tiny experiments as a gateway to forming sustainable habits. 
- Instead of lifelong commitments, experiment first and then decide.

💬 Quote

So what if instead we said, let me just experiment with this. Let me just do the thing for two weeks.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff suggests using experiments to explore potential habits.

verses.title: Procrastination
verses.text:
- Procrastination signals something is wrong subconsciously; don't just push through with willpower. 
- Analyze it with the 'triple check': head (rational), heart (emotional), and hand (practical).

💬 Quote

So from a neuroscience perspective, your brain is just trying to send you a signal that something is not quite right, but it's at a subconscious level.
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff provides a neuroscientific perspective on procrastination.

verses.title: Overcome Procrastination
verses.text:
- Listen to the signal of procrastination by having a conversation with it and asking what it is trying to tell you. 
- Determine if the problem comes from the head, the heart, or the hand.

💬 Quote

We should listen to that signal, literally have a conversation with your procrastination and say, hi, you're back. What are you trying to tell me?
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff on dealing with procrastination

verses.title: Procrastination Triple Check
verses.text:
- When you procrastinate, listen to the signal and converse with it to discern the problem's source. 
- Use the triple check by asking your head (rational), your heart (emotional), and your hand (practical).

💬 Quote

Instead, we should listen to that signal, literally have a conversation with your procrastination and say, hi, you're back. What are you trying to tell me?
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff on how to deal with procrastination

verses.title: Converse with Procrastination
verses.text:
- Listen to procrastination as a signal that something is not right and start a conversation with it. 
- Ask if the problem comes from the head (rational), the heart (emotional), or the hand (practical).

💬 Quote

So instead, we should listen to that signal, literally have a conversation with your procrastination and say, hi, you're back. What are you trying to tell me?
— Anne-Laure Le Cunff

Anne-Laure Le Cunff on dealing with procrastination.

chapters.title: What is AI's UI? with Maggie Appleton
chapters.description: Maggie Appleton, a Lead Design Engineer at Normalee, combines design, anthropology, and programming to enhance user experiences. She discusses how cultural factors influence technology interactions, particularly in the context of AI. The conversation tackles the digital divide and the role of AI in education, addressing fears and strategies for integrating AI tools into learning environments. Maggie also shares her thoughts on the complexities of advanced technologies and the potential of AI as a collaborative partner, advocating for deeper understanding in tech development.

verses.title: Prescient Article
verses.text:
- Maggie Appleton's article, "The Dark Forest and Generative AI," predicted the current state of AI-generated content. 
-  She was early to recognize the potential flood of AI-generated content.

💬 Quote

The Dark Forest and Generative AI [...] could have been written last week. And I read it with the lens of having, you know, I'm currently in the present and I'm thinking about last week. And I'm like, oh, she wrote this two years ago.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on Maggie Appleton's foresight.

verses.title: The Dark Forest of the Internet
verses.text:
- The internet is becoming a "dark forest," eerily devoid of human life. 
-  AI-generated content creates a sense of emptiness and loneliness.

💬 Quote

The dark forest is this part of the web that is eerily devoid of human life. It's this sense of like, eh, eh.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the changing nature of the internet.

verses.title: AI Integration
verses.text:
- Avoid simply adding AI chatbots to existing products lazily. 
- Strive to create interfaces tailored to user needs.

💬 Quote

It seems like they're the lazy, maybe too crisp of a word, but it's just, it's the simplest possible thing is to put a text box over there and have it fill up the whole right side and it'll be a co-pilot for the thing.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the lazy implementation of AI.

verses.title: Ambitious Tools for Thought
verses.text:
- Tools for thought should move beyond simple text organization. 
- Consider cognitive science and anthropology to build truly helpful AI tools.

💬 Quote

If we're going to say that AI really is this great thinking tool, this great cognitive aid for all of us, I want people to be a bit more ambitious and take more seriously what kind of work you would need to do to apply that to real problems that are not just organizing text on a page.
— Maggie Appleton

Maggie Appleton on the future of "tools for thought".

verses.title: LLMs as Rubber Ducks
verses.text:
- Use LLMs as rubber ducks or thought partners. 
-  They excel at bouncing ideas off of and prompting further thought.

💬 Quote

This is my favorite use case for it [...] is just bouncing ideas off them. I'm like, hey, I'm trying to work through this problem. I don't think it has the answer for me at all. I think I have the answer, but I have to figure out how to get to that answer. And I need someone to talk it through with and prompt me and ask me questions. And it's great for that.
— Maggie Appleton

Maggie Appleton on how to use LLMs effectively.

verses.title: ChatGPT in Education
verses.text:
- Teachers are using ChatGPT as a writing partner for students. 
- This approach helps students organize thoughts and improve writing, acting as a personalized tutor.

💬 Quote

We did a prompt exercise where I told ChatGPT4, do not write this for me. [...] I want to talk to you about organizing my thoughts about my article. [...] And it was such a fun way to tell it explicitly not to do the thing.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on using ChatGPT for educational purposes.

verses.title: LLM Limitations
verses.text:
- Current LLMs lack long-term memory, hindering personalized learning. 
- They interact as if encountering each user afresh every time.

💬 Quote

The issue is the lack of long-term memory. Like, it's a new, fresh person every time I wake up in the morning and ask it a question. So unless you're keeping track and truly adjusting on a day-by and a year-by basis, they're going to end up talking to the same mirror every day.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on the limitations of current LLMs.

verses.title: Guiding LLMs
verses.text:
- Guide LLMs with narrow, specific tasks within a critical framework. 
- Avoid open-ended prompts, instead direct them towards focused actions.

💬 Quote

I'm trying to instead ask it to just do very narrow specific tasks based on a critical framework that I'm coming up with. [...] I'm sort of saying okay I need you to like identify every single claim I've made in this piece of writing okay now I want you to check whether I've given evidence for every single one of those claims so I'm giving it in fact a very limited set of things it can do I'm not just being like okay one prompt the model and like see what it says because I expect it will just say those are great ideas, go ahead and publish it or something.
— Maggie Appleton

Maggie Appleton on how to effectively guide LLMs.

verses.title: LLM Reasoning
verses.text:
- LLMs are powerful but do not possess reasoning abilities. 
- They excel at pattern recognition and next-word prediction, not genuine understanding.

💬 Quote

It cannot reason. Like I think we just all need to accept that GPT is a, is an interesting and a powerful, um, architecture, but it, it, it doesn't reason.
— Scott Hanselman

Scott Hanselman on whether LLMs can reason.

chapters.title: Will ChatGPT help them write? with John Warner

verses.title: Learn while writing
verses.text: Writing is, as I say in the book and over and over again, and when I talk about these things, writing is thinking. It is both the expression of an idea, that is, we have something to say, right? And we want to get it done on the page. But it's also the exploration of an idea. The attempts at saying it will alter what we think about the idea as we go.

chapters.title: Do You Understand the Problem You’re Trying to Solve?
chapters.description: Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg, an expert in innovation and author of "What's Your Problem?" dives deep into the art of reframing problems for better solutions. He emphasizes that understanding the dilemma is crucial before jumping to fixes. Topics explore how hasty solutions can obscure true issues, using practical examples like a slow elevator. The discussion highlights the importance of questioning techniques and experimentation, revealing that recognizing underlying problems can lead to innovative solutions in both personal and professional contexts.

verses.title: How to Do It Fast in a Meeting
verses.text:
- Define the problem you're trying to solve before jumping into solutions.
- Ask: "Is there a different way to think about what the problem actually is?"

💬 Quote

reframing is really a hard-is-it. It's a method that helps you avoid [jumping to solutions] by taking a second to go in and ask two questions, basically saying, first of all, wait, what is the problem we're trying to solve? And then crucially asking, is there a different way to think about what the problem actually is?
— Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg

Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg on problem reframing.

verses.title: Why Is It a Problem That the Kitchen Is Not Clean?
verses.text:
- When reframing a problem, consider factors and assumptions not initially apparent. 
- Ask if there's anything missing from the problem's initial description.

💬 Quote

Are there like, you know, basically asking, are there factors that we are not talking about right now? [...] What often is missing is actually an element outside of the initial description of the problem that might be really relevant to what's going on.
— Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg

Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg on identifying missing factors in problem descriptions.

verses.title: The Importance of Problem Diagnosis
verses.text:
- Traditional problem-solving tools often encourage digging deeper into the initial understanding of a problem.
- This assumes a single "root cause," which can be misleading, as problems are often multi-causal.

💬 Quote

Those are good tools and they're definitely better than nothing. But [...] those tools tend to make you dig deeper into the first understanding of the problem you have. [...] That in a way is a bad way to work on problems because it really assumes that there's [...] a root cause that you have to dig down and find [...] and everything else was just symptoms.
— Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg

Thomas Wedell-Wedellsborg on the limitations of traditional problem-solving tools.

title: How I Built This with Guy Raz

chapters.title: Achieving greater things with Adam Grant
chapters.description: Join Adam Grant, an organizational psychologist and author of "Hidden Potential", as he shares his insights on cultivating success through character development. He discusses how resilience and humility, rather than innate talent, foster entrepreneurial success. Learn about the power of social connections and teaching in personal growth, plus strategies for overcoming self-doubt and embracing collaboration. Grant also critiques outdated workplace systems, advocating for a reimagined approach that nurtures collective creativity and support.

verses.title: Seeing 'No' as a Beginning
verses.text: Entrepreneurs who can view the word 'no' as a starting point rather than a setback possess a key trait for success. This mindset enables them to persist, innovate, and ultimately excel in their ventures. By reframing rejection as an opportunity for growth and improvement, entrepreneurs gain a significant advantage in navigating challenges and forging their path to success.

verses.title: Invest Your Ego in Future Growth
verses.text: Success comes to those who invest their egos in their future growth instead of their present comfort. Highly successful individuals do have egos, but the key lies in continuously moving forward, bouncing back from failures, and striving to improve. It is crucial to focus on personal and professional development rather than being content with current achievements.

verses.title: Invest in Future Growth
verses.text: Success and resilience come to those who invest their egos in future growth rather than present comfort. When faced with criticism, the key is to focus on how well one can bounce back from it, instead of dwelling on the initial judgment. By shifting the focus on improving and proving oneself, one can turn negative feedback into motivation for personal growth.

verses.title: Team Success vs Individual Narcissism
verses.text: Having a narcissistic point guard can lead to stagnation in the team's performance over the season, hindering improvement in playing together. This can be observed in the number of completed assists. Mark Cuban emphasized the impact of one individual's self-centered attitude on a team, highlighting how it undermines collective potential and team success. The culture often rewards such behavior, despite its adverse effects on team dynamics and achievements.

verses.title: Seek Advice Over Feedback
verses.text: Asking for advice rather than feedback is advised as feedback can often lead to harsh criticism, focusing on poking holes in the work. The speaker criticizes the traditional way of feedback where it is often centered on looking back, either by cheerleaders supporting the best self or critics pointing out the worst self. The key message is to shift towards seeking advice to foster growth effectively by learning from others without the negative connotations associated with feedback.

verses.title: Realizing Hidden Potential
verses.text: It is crucial to differentiate between making your parents proud and making your kids proud, as the former might lead to inheriting their unfulfilled dreams. Everyone carries hidden potential, and focusing on making your kids proud can help you realize your own potential and contributions. The concept of Reflected Best Self-Portrait by Laura Morgan Roberts and Jane Dutton emphasizes that individuals often underestimate their strengths and growth capacities and need external tools to recognize and utilize their hidden potential.

chapters.title: Twilio: Jeff Lawson
chapters.description: Jeff Lawson, Founder and CEO of Twilio, shares his journey from juggling customer calls at a surf shop to creating a groundbreaking communication platform. He discusses the initial skepticism from investors and how vital early customers were in shaping Twilio's direction. Lawson reveals the challenges of transitioning from Amazon Web Services to a startup and highlights Twilio's pivotal moments, including their partnership with Uber. He also reflects on the balance between hard work and luck in building a successful business.

verses.title: How to brainstorm new ideas
verses.text:
1. The first step in brainstorming a new idea is to identify different groups of people that might have interesting problems to solve.
2. Ranking the identified groups based on the most promising areas to explore helps in narrowing down the focus.
3. Once the focus group is narrowed down, the brainstorming process for identifying their problems begins.
4. The end result of the brainstorming process could result in a lengthy list of potential ideas.

chapters.title: Twitter, Medium and Blogger: Ev Williams
chapters.description: Ev Williams, co-founder of Twitter and the mind behind Blogger and Medium, shares his fascinating journey from college dropout to tech innovator. He recounts early struggles with startups, the emotional toll of managing and losing a company, and the pivotal moments that led to Twitter's rise. Ev discusses how real-time connectivity shaped online engagement and reflects on the balance between hard work and luck in achieving success. With humor and insight, he emphasizes the personal growth that comes from failure and innovation in the tech landscape.

verses.title: Value of Intuition over Metrics
verses.text: The speaker shared the early days of Twitter's development when they had an internal joke name 'Friends Stalker' before deciding on the name. Despite initial skepticism and low user numbers after launch, they emphasized the importance of the idea feeling right even when metrics didn't support it. They kept the team small to nurture the idea and prioritized intuition over numbers in the early stages of Twitter's development.

chapters.title: When AI is your personal tutor with Sal Khan of Khan Academy
chapters.description: Sal Khan, founder of Khan Academy, shares his vision for revolutionizing education with Khanmigo, an AI-driven tutoring platform. He discusses how the pandemic accelerated online learning and how AI can become a personal tutor for every student. Sal also addresses the ethical challenges of using AI in education, including issues of bias and academic integrity. He emphasizes the mission to provide free, quality education while exploring sustainable models for non-profits in the ed-tech sector.

verses.title: The Future of Interacting with Historical Figures
verses.text: In the next five years, it will be possible to talk to historical figures and immerse oneself in their world. This is positive for motivated students as it brings history to life and provides a captivating learning experience. AI tutors can role play as historical figures, further enhancing the learning experience. While there may be imperfections, the richness of the experience surpasses a traditional dinner with historic figures.

chapters.title: 10 Lessons from the Best Writer in Tech | Paul Graham | How I Write Podcast
chapters.description: Explore the unique writing philosophy that emphasizes clarity and simplicity, championed by a Silicon Valley icon. Discover how great writing comes from rigorous editing and the power of curiosity. Uncover the importance of surprising ideas to evoke laughter and insights. Reflect on personal preferences and guilty pleasures to inspire a distinctive voice. Finally, learn about the transformative impact of writing, particularly through personal blogging, on a new generation of creators.

verses.title: Simplicity in Writing Fosters Clear Communication
verses.text: Simplicity in writing is crucial for effective communication as complex sentences can hinder the transfer of ideas. The goal of writing is to convey ideas clearly and efficiently from the writer's brain to the reader's. By simplifying writing, readers can better focus on the essence of the ideas being presented, maximizing the impact of the message.

verses.title: Quality over Quantity in Writing
verses.text: Focusing on creating a high percentage of great writing is more important than striving for perfection in every piece. The key is to produce exceptional work and master the art of editing to retain only the best content for publication. Fear of producing subpar work often hinders creativity, but it's essential to accept that most projects start off looking mediocre and then refine them to excellence.

verses.title: Refining Writing through Feedback and Cutting Unconvincing Parts
verses.text: Getting feedback from readers is crucial to improve writing; ask them which parts bore them and which parts seem unconvincing. Boring parts can be easily fixed by cutting them, while unconvincing parts require discussion with others to gain clarity. Talking through ideas with a sparring partner helps to identify unclear sections and clarify them. The curse of knowledge makes it challenging to distinguish obvious parts from unclear ones. Seeking feedback from friends on what to keep and what to cut, focusing on essential elements, is a valuable writing tip learned from Tim Ferriss.

verses.title: Refinement and Creativity in Writing
verses.text: Writing involves a process of refinement and creativity, similar to Leonardo's multiple attempts to get drawings right. Redesigning and rewriting help discover new aspects that would otherwise remain hidden. Expect 80% of ideas to come after starting to write, with half of them being incorrect. Editing is crucial as initial words are often wrong, and rewriting is necessary to achieve precision. Ideas generated while writing are essential, with about half of the ideas in an essay emerging during the writing process.

verses.title: General and Surprising Ideas in Writing
verses.text: Writing generates ideas by helping discover unknown knowns. Valuable ideas are those that are both general and surprising. General ideas without surprise are platitudes. Surprising ideas that are not general are like gossip. To find ideas that are both general and surprising, pay attention to what personally interests you, not what you think others will find interesting.

verses.title: Refine Your Taste for Good Writing
verses.text: Improve your writing by studying writers who excel in specific areas and push boundaries, like Gregory David Roberts' luscious writing in 'Shantaram'. Enhance your taste for good writing by reading extensively and reflecting on what you love and hate, whether it's in art, film, or literature.

chapters.title: 171 Years of Writing Advice in 90 Minutes  | How I Write
chapters.description: Dive into a treasure trove of writing wisdom, where expert insights illuminate the diverse styles that make stories shine. Uncover the nuances of emotional depth and the transformative journey from rough drafts to polished gems. Explore the art of effective storytelling while balancing impactful openings and conclusions. Get inspired by the revival of email communication, turning it into a cherished tool. Discover the iterative process of refinement and the power of engaging narratives that resonate deeply, combining creativity with simple, clear communication.

verses.title: Show, Don't Tell
verses.text:
- Focus on specific details and sensory experiences to bring a scene to life.
- Avoid clichés and generalizations, instead show the reader what the character would see, hear, smell, and feel.

💬 Quote

What would the girl see? [...] the first thing that would really stand out is the amazing thing of how frozen peas work.
— George Saunders

George Saunders on writing advice

verses.title: The Power of Diacope
verses.text:
- Diacope is a powerful rhetorical device that repeats a word or phrase with one or more words in between.
- It enhances memorability, sometimes even creating false memories, as seen with "Fly, my pretties, fly" in The Wizard of Oz.

💬 Quote

A diacopy is a verbal sandwich [...] like, Bond, James Bond, to be or not to be.
— Mark Forsyth

Mark Forsyth on figures of speech

verses.title: Chiasmus's Persuasive Power
verses.text:
- Chiasmus, reversing the order of words in two otherwise parallel phrases, creates memorable and persuasive lines.
- JFK and Obama masterfully used this in speeches like "Ask not what your country can do for you…"

💬 Quote

Chiasmus is just when you say something and then say it in reverse. [...] Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country.
— Mark Forsyth

Mark Forsyth on effective rhetoric

verses.title: Resist Dopamine Culture
verses.text:
- Resist chasing dopamine culture's demand for constant stimulation; focus on deeper engagement.
- Prioritize quality and natural ingredients in writing, build a loyal readership.

💬 Quote

Resist chasing the culture. [...] Be true to your own values; there are people that are hungry for something more substantive.
— Ted Gioia

Ted Gioia on long-term engagement

verses.title: Storytelling with Stakes
verses.text:
- Define storytelling as creating stakes and making people care about outcomes.
- Use stories instead of statistics, as people connect more with narratives.

💬 Quote

If you define storytelling as creating stakes [...] every good story makes you hope for an outcome.
— Shreyas Doshi

Shreyas Doshi on effective storytelling

verses.title: Note-Taking Essentials
verses.text:
- Use a spiral notebook for note-taking: rip pages out, lay them flat.
- Choose pens and paper that feel good to write on; prioritize comfort.

💬 Quote

You definitely want a spiral notebook [...] you definitely want like a kind of paper that is good to write on.
— Jason Fried

Jason Fried on his note-taking system

verses.title: Anecdotes and Cadence
verses.text:
- Use short anecdotes, even one sentence, to add life and connection to your writing.
- Interrupt the cadence of your writing to maintain audience engagement.

💬 Quote

A story, an anecdote, it can be one sentence [...] real quick, right back to the point.
— David Perell

David Perell on effective storytelling

verses.title: Stakes Through Emotion
verses.text:
- Every great story needs stakes, even if they're not extreme.
- Stakes often come from the emotions a character feels, like embarrassment or fear.

💬 Quote

Every great story needs stakes [...] the stakes come from the emotion.
— Sean Purl

Sean Purl on storytelling

verses.title: Story Stakes
verses.text:
- Every great story needs stakes. 
- Stakes arise from the emotions a character is believed to feel.

💬 Quote

Every great story needs stakes [...] if you don't make it clear what's at stake [...] then the story's not going to have [...] be very compelling.
— Speaker 9

Speaker 9 on how to make stories compelling.

verses.title: Story Structure
verses.text:
- Every story revolves around a five-second moment of change where the character transforms.
- This transformation usually occurs at a critical point, like hitting rock bottom or experiencing heartbreak.

💬 Quote

A five second moment of change. [...] Everything [...] in the story comes to this one moment, this five seconds where the characters transform [...].

verses.title: Start Strong, End Punchy
verses.text:
- Start your writing with a strong, intriguing line that makes the reader curious.
- Use short, punchy sentences, especially at the end of paragraphs, to summarize key ideas.

💬 Quote

I know this is going to be good now; I know the whole piece is going to be good.
— Jason Fried

Jason Fried on writing effective openings

verses.title: Iterate and Design
verses.text:
- Rewrite and iterate your copy repeatedly until it's clear, concise, and persuasive.
- Use design and visuals to enhance the copy and tell a compelling story.

💬 Quote

I want to show you how an ad comes together. [...] It's probably about 20 rewrites.
— Harry Dry

Harry Dry on the copywriting process

verses.title: Theme and Sensory Details
verses.text:
- Establish a clear theme or lesson that holds your story together.
- Use sensory details to make your writing more immersive and engaging for the reader.

💬 Quote

I believe there should be like a theme. [...] And so when you have a sense of that, then every detail kind of goes back to it.
— Robert Greene

Robert Greene on the importance of details

verses.title: Built-in Bilingualism
verses.text:
- English speakers have "built-in bilingualism" due to Latin and Anglo-Saxon roots.
- This allows writers to use word choice and sentence structure to create different effects and convey social status.

💬 Quote

You have the possibility of drawing on two completely different histories and origins of words in order to create registral and color effects.
— John McWhorter

John McWhorter on language's power

verses.title: Thiel's Communication Style
verses.text:
- Peter Thiel's communication stands out for its evocative, concise statements that stick in your mind.
- He combines brilliant original thinking with clear, impactful delivery.

💬 Quote

He comes up with these [...] very short statements that really stick in your brain.
— Jason Fried

Jason Fried on Peter Thiel's communication

chapters.title: Alex Hormozi: Copywriting Masterclass | How I Write
chapters.description: Alex Hormozi, a bestselling author known for his impactful writing and impressive social media presence, shares his secrets to writing success. He breaks down his unique writing system that combines extensive planning with creative storytelling. Hormozi discusses the importance of frameworks in simplifying complex concepts and explores the art of captivating audiences through compelling narratives in both books and videos. He also highlights the necessity of revisions and strategic marketing techniques to enhance business outreach. A must-listen for aspiring writers!

verses.title: Hormozi's Writing Cave
verses.text:
- Write in an uninterrupted environment for at least six hours.
- Eliminate distractions by using earplugs, headphones, and closing windows.

💬 Quote

I wake up and then I caffeinate me and then I put earplugs and headphones on. I close all the windows and I really only write on days that I know have at least like six hours or more uninterrupted [...]
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on how he creates a distraction-free writing environment.

verses.title: Structure Over Content
verses.text:
- Outline your book's table of contents first.
- Focus on the structure; the writing itself flows more easily.

💬 Quote

I feel like I'm incredibly unstructured with the writing besides just like violent effort. But that's about it. Like I write what I write. I never had writer's block in my life.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on his approach to writing.

verses.title: Chapter Structure
verses.text:
- Structure chapters with a narrative, a short description, plentiful examples, and personal notes.
- Prioritize selecting the right story and visual framework for each chapter.

💬 Quote

[...] I usually have a game plan of what I'm going to write. [...] each of the chapters I tend to have the same structure because I write the way I would like to read [...]
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on how he structures his book chapters.

verses.title: Crystallizing Knowledge
verses.text:
- Keep detailed notes of stories and lessons learned to avoid forgetting them.
- Crystallize knowledge into artifacts like Excel sheets or personal notes for future reference.

💬 Quote

I write a lot of stuff down because I don't want to forget it. Like I have this, this Excel sheet that has like 600 stories of my life.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the importance of note-taking.

verses.title: Writing as Discovery
verses.text:
- Writing is a process of discovery, clarifying existing ideas and revealing new connections.
- Embrace the challenge of resolving apparent conflicts between true ideas.

💬 Quote

I'd say it's two thirds discovery, one third getting the stuff that I already have out. [...] That's the fun part is when I like encounter some apparent conflict between two ideas that I know are both true, but seem to be conflicting.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the role of discovery in writing.

verses.title: MECE Framework
verses.text:
- Use the MECE framework (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) to break down problems.
- Ensure all options are covered and distinct from each other.

💬 Quote

So it's mutually [...] collectively exhaustive. So an example would be, ah, we're struggling with our content strategy for the business. All right, well, we got three options. We can do more, we can do better, and we can do different.
— David Perell

David Perell explaining the MECE framework and providing an example.

verses.title: A/B Testing Book Covers
verses.text:
- Alex Hormozi A/B tested book titles, subtitles, and cover images extensively.
- "$100M Leads" outperformed variations like "advertising" and "marketing."

💬 Quote

I AB tested probably like three or four of the image of different, basically different, different magnet variations. But the word leads, I tested the hell out of.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the importance of testing book covers.

verses.title: A/B Testing Book Titles
verses.text:
- Alex Hormozi A/B tested different titles for his book "$100M Leads." 
-  He tested variations like "promotion," "advertising," and "marketing," but "leads" performed best.

💬 Quote

I AB tested probably like three or four of the image of different, basically different, different magnet variations. But the word leads, I tested the hell out of. And so I had $100 million promotion, $100 million advertising, $100 million leads, $100 marketing. And leads was the one that won.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on A/B testing his book title and cover.

verses.title: Test Book Titles
verses.text:
- Test book titles and covers extensively, as readers judge books by their covers.
- Prioritize testing titles because rewriting a book is far more challenging.

💬 Quote

Of course. And if you're going to go through all the all the work of writing the book, which is significantly harder than testing the cover in the title. Like, do that.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the importance of testing book titles and covers.

verses.title: Video Optimization
verses.text:
- Focus on the hook, packaging, title, thumbnail, and the first 60 seconds of videos.
- Ensure congruence between what viewers click on and what they get.

💬 Quote

- The biggest bang for the buck is always going to be, it's hook, packaging, title, thumbnail, first 30, 60 seconds is going to be where you have probably the biggest leverage on performance for a video.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on what aspects of videos he focuses on.

verses.title: Writing Style and Editing
verses.text:
- Write in a colloquial tone, like telling a story to a friend.
- Edit in multiple passes, focusing on concision and simpler words.

💬 Quote

I tell it like I would be telling it to a friend. There are probably different styles of writing. I assume you would know this better than I do. But I tend to try and get everything out as fast as I can. And then I kind of look at it as like coats of paint.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on how he writes and edits.

verses.title: Pain Is the Pitch
verses.text:
- Highlight pain points to persuade people to take action.
- Accurately describing a prospect's pain builds trust and credibility.

💬 Quote

Pain is what motivates a lot of people to take action.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the importance of addressing pain points in marketing.

verses.title: Specificity in Pain Description
verses.text:
- Accurately describe a prospect's pain using their language and experiences.
- The more specific you are about their pain, the more knowledgeable you appear, increasing their likelihood of buying your product.

💬 Quote

If you can accurately describe a prospect's pain in their own language, in their own experiences, you can persuade them to buy whatever your product is [...].
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on writing compelling sales copy

verses.title: 10x Effort = 1000x Results
verses.text:
- Investing more effort in a book leads to exponentially greater returns.
- Small differences in quality have enormous practical implications.

💬 Quote

It's kind of the difference between gold medal and fourth place. The real difference is small. The practical difference is enormous.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the impact of extra effort.

verses.title: Winner-Take-All Dynamics
verses.text:
- Putting in 10x more effort, especially in creative work, can yield 100x or even 1000x the results because quality compounds.
- This is because the internet creates winner-take-all dynamics where the best get disproportionately large rewards.

💬 Quote

If you put in 10 times more work into a book, because the quality is better, you end up with 100 or 1,000x [the results].
— David Perell

David Perell summarizing Alex Hormozi's advice on work ethic and writing.

verses.title: Mundane Middle
verses.text:
- Most people only see the start and finish of a marathon, not the 26.2 miles in between. 
- The "mundane middle" is where champions are made, similar to how Alex Hormozi wrote 19 drafts of his book.

💬 Quote

If people saw that I wrote 19 drafts of this book, they wouldn't think it was that special.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on effort and outcomes.

verses.title: The Table of Contents as a Roadmap
verses.text:
- Create a detailed table of contents that serves as the book's outline.
- Use the table of contents to explore and refine ideas before writing.

💬 Quote

I feel like that's how Alex's opinion, I feel like that's how books should write. Like, if you want to figure out if you want to read a book, I feel like you should read the table of contents and be able to make a good decision.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the importance of a good table of contents.

verses.title: Descriptive Titles
verses.text:
- Make book titles and chapter headings descriptive.
- Readers should be able to decide whether they want to read the book based on the table of contents alone.

💬 Quote

I try to make my titles and chapter headings pretty descriptive.  Like this is about where my reach...
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on writing descriptive titles and chapter headings.

verses.title: Deep vs. Wide Content
verses.text:
- Focus on creating content that is both deep (useful for experts) and wide (useful for beginners).
- Aim for usefulness and validity to resonate with a broader audience.

💬 Quote

I think about this in context of deep and wide. Deep versus wide. And so wide, I define as something that is only useful for a beginner. And deep, I define as something that is useful for anyone.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the different types of content.

verses.title: The Big Domino
verses.text:
- Identify the "big domino" that, if accomplished, makes other tasks irrelevant.
- In business, this could be achieving proprietary deal flow or building a strong brand.

💬 Quote

Is there one thing that is so important that I can do that if I do that, it makes the rest of my to-do list irrelevant.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on prioritizing tasks.

verses.title: Audience Over Products
verses.text:
- Focus on building a larger audience for your existing product, not creating new products.
- Launching too many products spreads your attention thin and dilutes quality.

💬 Quote

The number one creator mistake is building new products for their audience rather than building more audience for their product.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the most common mistake creators make.

verses.title: The Business of Books
verses.text:
- Books serve as valuable assets, lead magnets, and reputation builders.
- High-quality content attracts a wider audience and establishes credibility.

💬 Quote

The ultimate lead magnet should be timeless assets. And I think that's where a lot of people mess up when they do what it, like 99% of people will never buy anything from you. And, but 99% of people are what create your reputation.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the purpose of writing books.

verses.title: Who Should Write a Book?
verses.text:
- Discourage writing books unless the individual loves writing and is willing to put in the effort.
- Launching a book tests marketing skills, while long-term sales demonstrate book quality.

💬 Quote

How you launch a book determines how good you are at marketing. How well the book is selling two years later determines how good the book is.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on the importance of writing books.

verses.title: Early Writing Influences
verses.text:
- Explore different writing styles by managing student submissions and artwork.
-  Reading others' work helps improve your own writing skills.

💬 Quote

And so I managed them. And art, and art too, like kids who put art. So like the cover art, you know, we'd get a bunch of paintings and we'd be like, okay, which of these are going to be the cover? And then between stories and between poems, we'd put other art from, you know, the art department from the kids who are, you know, making painting or whatever they're doing, you know, pictures of sculptures and things like that.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on his experience with the literary magazine in high school.

verses.title: Ideal Writing Class
verses.text:
- Define terms clearly, clarify writing objectives, and establish writing rules.
- Use Hemingway Editor and X (formerly Twitter) for practice and feedback.

💬 Quote

So definition of terms, why this matters, and how it's useful for you. And I basically sell them on why they should even do this. Rules of the game. And then practice. And that would probably be everything. The rest would just be practice.
— Alex Hormozi

Alex Hormozi on how he would structure a writing class.

chapters.title: Ali Abdaal: How I Write (Productively)
chapters.description: Ali Abdaal, a prolific online creator and productivity guru, shares his journey from earning his first dollar at 12 to generating millions annually. He delves into the balance between authenticity and engagement in content creation, emphasizing the importance of relatable storytelling. Ali discusses effective strategies like voice transcription and mind-mapping for organizing ideas. He tackles perfectionism, advocating for action over flawless execution, and reflects on the transformative power of sharing personal experiences in writing.

verses.title: The Difference Between How to and How I in Writing and Overcoming Imposter Syndrome
verses.text: Shifting from a mindset of being a guru to being a guide is beneficial in writing and overcoming imposter syndrome. Viewing oneself as a fellow traveler sharing acquired knowledge rather than an expert with all the answers helps in connecting with the audience. This approach allows for a more relatable and engaging writing style, fostering a sense of humility and curiosity in the writer's work.

verses.title: Reclaiming Identity: From Noun to Verb
