- Vary example types to maintain reader engagement, and avoid monotony. 
- Adjust the resolution of your examples, from immersive three-paragraph examples to concise single-paragraph instances.

💬 Quote

You can also vary your example types. As in sometimes you want a three paragraph example that's immersive, but if you did that again, that could be too much.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on how to keep writing interesting

verses.title: Orwell's Traditional Structure
verses.text:
- Orwell structures "Shooting an Elephant" with a traditional conflict, unlike DFW's "Consider the Lobster.". 
- DFW's essay relies on a different organizational principle, focusing on invisible questions.

💬 Quote

So Orwell kind of structures his conflict in a very traditional kind of textbook kind of way.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean contrasting Orwell's structure with David Foster Wallace's.

verses.title: Intention and Obstacle
verses.text:
- Use intention and obstacle as simple words to generate stories.
- Intention is what the character wants, and the obstacle is what's in their way.

💬 Quote

You should be able to stop, stop the film and just point who is the character, what do they want and what is getting in their way.

verses.title: Implicit Questions
verses.text:
- When writing, consider the implicit questions you're answering. 
- Use reverse outlining to identify and refine these questions, improving clarity and focus.

💬 Quote

It's really helpful to think of essays and writing in general as questions and answers.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on the importance of questions in shaping writing.

verses.title: Two-Draft Approach
verses.text:
- Write the first draft for yourself, focusing on emergent material, without strict patterns. 
- Edit the second draft for your reader, using essay architecture to refine and clarify your ideas.

💬 Quote

You need to write your first draft for yourself and your second draft for your reader.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean's advice on the writing process.

verses.title: Focused Editing
verses.text:
- Edit with a focused lens, examining one pattern at a time, like highlighting voice moments. 
- Use color-coding to identify strong areas and sections needing improvement.

💬 Quote

But instead, you can have a razor lens just on one pattern at a time.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean's method for effective editing.

verses.title: Improve Writing Tension
verses.text:
- Focus on tension, not just storytelling, by looking at conflict, threads, and hook.
- Assess if the hook sets up the core dilemmas; ensure there's always conflict being advanced.

💬 Quote

There should never be a point in your essay or your story where there's no conflict being advanced.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on how to improve the writing tension in an essay

verses.title: Slow Cooking Ideas
verses.text:
- Schools should protect the fundamentals of writing because software is always changing and getting easier to use independently.
- When you commit to writing and editing, your mind changes, and you become more than just the output.

💬 Quote

When you're slow cooking in an idea, your mind changes, right?
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on the importance of writing, even with AI advancements.

verses.title: Writing's Cognitive Benefits
verses.text:
- Writing and editing develop critical thinking skills. 
- Schools should prioritize manual writing to ensure students fully grasp fundamental concepts.

💬 Quote

And so the idea is the reason we need to protect writing and editing in schools is that it's not about the outputs but through writing you change and you develop and you and you develop skills to critically examine yourself and your identity and your ideas.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on why writing should be protected in education.

verses.title: Machine-Legible Memories
verses.text:
- Make personal experiences machine-legible by documenting memories and thoughts regularly. 
- Detailed personal writing allows for creating a more accurate and nuanced AI replica of yourself.

💬 Quote

So people say like, oh, language models don't have your memories. That's only if you don't write your memories, right?
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on making personal experiences accessible to AI.

verses.title: AI Replicas
verses.text:
- If you document your thoughts and core memories, an AI replica could become very much like you.
- The masses may start writing more to create a personal and aligned AI mirror.

💬 Quote

If I have a million words of my day-to thoughts and I've written up all of my core memories, this thing will be very much like me.
— Michael Dean

Michael Dean on AI replicas

chapters.title: Michael Jamin: The Hollywood Sitcom Writer | How I Write
chapters.description: Michael Jamin, a seasoned Hollywood sitcom writer with 28 years of experience, shares his insights on character-driven comedy. He emphasizes the importance of rich character development over mere punchlines, explaining how authentic traits can enhance humor. Jamin also discusses the evolution of multi-camera sitcoms and the collaborative process within writers' rooms. He highlights the significance of vulnerability in narratives, the complexities of human behavior, and the balance between AI assistance and personal storytelling in maintaining authenticity.

verses.title: Strong Attitudes Drive Comedy
verses.text:
- Strong attitudes make characters and comedy more engaging and memorable.  
- Dumbness and stubbornness are examples of strong attitudes that are often funny.

💬 Quote

Strong attitudes are funny, right?  If I ask you how is the soup and you go, it's okay. That's not funny. But if you say... this is the best fucking soup I've ever had in my life or this is the worst – this is – the strong attitudes are funny.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on comedy and character attitude

verses.title: Strong Attitudes
verses.text:
- Adopt strong attitudes to make your characters funnier. Being extremely positive or negative is funnier than being neutral.

💬 Quote

Strong attitudes are funny, right?
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on the tricks and tips he has picked up along the way to write funny shows.

verses.title: Comedy Through Character
verses.text:
- Good comedy arises from character, not from characters trying to be funny.
- A character's goal shouldn't be to make the audience laugh, but to speak truthfully from their perspective.

💬 Quote

If a character is trying to be funny, that's not going to [be] fun[ny]. It's never funny. It's always cringy.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on how character intersects with comedy

verses.title: Authentic Character Humor
verses.text:
- Comedy stems from characters being true to themselves, not from trying to be funny.  
- Characters should express their genuine ignorance or naivety for authentic humor.

💬 Quote

If a character is trying to be funny, that's not going to fun. It's never funny. It's always cringy. But if the character is coming from their ignorance or their stupidity... then you can have them say funny things.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on character-based comedy

verses.title: Comedy and Character
verses.text:
- Good comedy stems from character, not jokes. 
- Characters should not try to be funny; humor arises from ignorance or naiveté.

💬 Quote

All good comedy comes from character as opposed to someone saying something silly or as opposed to like the joke.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on the intersection of character and comedy.

verses.title: Story Over Jokes
verses.text:
- Layer humor on top of a strong story for lasting impact. Aim to balance story with jokes, because comedy alone can be disposable and easily forgotten.

💬 Quote

Comedy hits you in the head and drama hits you in the heart. And if you really want someone to feel something after they watch or read your work or whatever, you have to hit them in the heart because comedy is almost disposable.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on the importance of story in comedy writing

verses.title: Plot vs Story
verses.text:
- Plot is just 'shit happening,' but a story needs an emotional underpinning.
- The emotional underpinning tells what the story is really about, for example, redemption.

💬 Quote

Plot is shit happening. What's a story? A story is the emotional underpinning underneath it.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on the difference between plot and story

verses.title: Find Emotional Stakes First
verses.text:
- When breaking a story, always clarify its deeper emotional meaning, not just the plot.  
- Ask explicitly "What is this story really about?" to find emotional stakes.

💬 Quote

When you're breaking the story, that's when you're also figuring out what it's really about on a deeper level. You're literally saying those words. What's this about?
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on story breaking and emotional themes

verses.title: Ferris Bueller's Day Off
verses.text:
- Michael Jamin uses Ferris Bueller's Day Off as an example of a movie that's not about playing hooky but about a best friend trying to save his friend's life.
- He emphasizes that the stakes aren't just about getting a day off school, but about saving a friend's future.

💬 Quote

This movie is about a best friend trying to save Cameron's life. Because he says it. And this is not a mystery. They say it in like the second scene of the movie.
— Michael Jamin

Michael Jamin on the deeper meaning of Ferris Bueller's Day Off

verses.title: The Stakes in Storytelling
verses.text:
- High stakes can exist within mundane situations, like a father reconnecting with his daughter before the world ends. 
- Family stakes are often the highest, which is why there are many family comedies, but office comedies can also work if the office becomes a surrogate family.

💬 Quote

Those are high stakes, the fate of humanity. But that's not what the movie is about. In that movie, it's always about the father has to make good with his estranged daughter before the end of the world.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin discussing stakes in storytelling

verses.title: From Creepy Neighbor to Personal Story
verses.text:
- Michael turned a creepy neighbor's hoarding into a story about judgment and father-daughter connection.  
- The story shifted from the ghoul to personal growth and understanding family dynamics.

💬 Quote

If that's the story I tell, okay, so what? So what? I poked around his house. So how does it affect me? How did it affect me?... The story is about me. You know, and it's about judgment.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on transforming life events into stories

verses.title: Vulnerable Villains Gain Sympathy
verses.text:
- Complex antiheroes like Tony Soprano gain audience sympathy by showing vulnerabilities first.  
- First impressions with relatable weaknesses invite viewers to root for morally complex characters.

💬 Quote

The very first time you meet Tony Soprano, he's nervous and uncomfortable... We're learning his weaknesses, and he's very likable before showing his darker side.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on antiheroes and character introduction

verses.title: Character Introductions
verses.text:
- Always give new characters a good introduction to make them interesting. 
- According to Michael Jammin, this approach benefits both the character and the actor.

💬 Quote

Anytime you introduce a character, for whatever reason, whatever you're writing, you always want to give them a good introduction.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on introducing new characters.

verses.title: Character Introductions
verses.text:
- Always give new characters a good introduction, not just for the character but also for the actor.
- In sitcoms, the rule is to get them in on a joke and get them off on a joke.

💬 Quote

Anytime you introduce a character, for whatever reason, whatever you're writing, you always want to give them a good introduction.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on introducing new characters

verses.title: Make Character Entrances Memorable
verses.text:
- Introduce new characters with strong, clear actions reflecting their personality instantly.  
- Always give them a funny entrance and exit to energize the scene and satisfy actors' egos.

💬 Quote

You bring a character in... You always give them a joke to say. A character never walks out of a scene without saying something funny.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on introducing characters in sitcoms

verses.title: Finish Scripts Quickly to Improve
verses.text:
- Stop over-polishing initial scripts; finish and move on to new projects quickly.  
- Each new script learn from the last, improving exponentially through practice.

💬 Quote

Stop polishing that turd. Finish it. Start another project. Script five will be light years better than script one.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on improving writing through volume and finishing work

verses.title: Comedy Prepares, Drama Resonates
verses.text:
- Comedy lowers the guard so drama's emotional impact hits harder later.  
- A joke after an emotional moment often ruins its effect; let the audience sit with it.

💬 Quote

The first half is very funny to get you to lower your guard. Then, when your guard is down, I hit you as hard as I can in the heart. Don't ruin that with a joke at the end.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on balancing comedy and drama

verses.title: Character-Driven Comedy
verses.text:
- The best jokes come from character; the joke must be congruent with the character.
- If you can swap a laugh line between characters, it's not a great line.

💬 Quote

If you can swap a character, [...] If you could take a line from this character's mouth and put into that character, it's not a great line.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on character-driven comedy.

verses.title: Jokes Must Fit Characters
verses.text:
- The funniest jokes must come uniquely from the character's voice and personality.  
- Reusing jokes across different characters dilutes comedic impact and feels generic.

💬 Quote

If you can swap this line between characters, it's not a great line, especially for laughs. Best jokes come from the character's unique voice.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on writing character-specific humor

verses.title: Comedy Writing Rules
verses.text:
- If you are not funny, then write drama instead of cringy comedy. 
- Use statements instead of questions for punchlines to avoid the audience waiting for an answer.

💬 Quote

Questions aren't funny. Statements are funny.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on comedy writing commandments

verses.title: Statements vs. Questions
verses.text:
- Michael Jamin says that questions aren't funny; statements are funny. 
- If a punchline is a question, the audience waits for an answer, but statements signal it's okay to laugh.

💬 Quote

I often say that questions aren't funny. Statements are funny.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on humor writing commandments

verses.title: Writing Hardship Builds Meaning
verses.text:
- Writing is meant to be hard because transforming personal experience creates meaning and satisfaction.  
- Using AI to replace writing violates the craft's core purpose and diminishes authentic voice.

💬 Quote

Writing is hard. It's supposed to be hard. That's why it feels good. Using AI to replace writing defeats the purpose of being a writer.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on writing, AI, and artistic craft

verses.title: Human Relationships in Storytelling
verses.text:
- Focus on the relationships between characters, because that's what people are tuning into.
- Every story is about a character trying to grow, whether it's a boy becoming a man, a girl becoming a woman, or even a toy becoming human.

💬 Quote

Because it's really about what people are tuning into are about the relationships between the characters.
— Michael Jammin

Michael Jammin on the importance of relationships.

chapters.title: Packy McCormick: How To Publish Your Best Ideas Online
chapters.description: Packy McCormick, the creator of the popular newsletter 'Not Boring' and founder of a $40 million venture fund, shares his journey from just a handful of subscribers to massive success. He discusses his unique writing style that combines humor and depth, which engages a vast audience. Packy explores the intersection of writing and investing, revealing how sharing knowledge can be transformative. He emphasizes creating quality, long-lasting content in a world filled with fleeting distractions and reflects on finding inspiration amidst technological stagnation.

verses.title: Framing an Introduction for Writing
verses.text: Framing the introduction of a piece of writing is crucial as it sets the direction and tone for the rest of the content. By investing time in crafting a strong introduction, the writer can establish the main concepts and arguments that will be elaborated on later in the piece. The analogy of visualizing skiing down a fresh run and plotting your course ahead of time illustrates how framing the introduction helps in smoothly guiding the reader through the content, ensuring coherence and understanding.

verses.title: Plotting Your Arc: Guiding the Reader and Building Momentum
verses.text: Crafting a strong introduction is akin to plotting the direction and arc of a piece, not just for the writer's momentum but also for guiding the reader. It is essential to avoid common tropes and instead focus on finding the most interesting and unique path to convey a story. By steering clear of hyperbole and generic content, an author can subtly shift the reader's perspective and offer a fresh approach to familiar ideas.

verses.title: Barbell Approach to Content Creation
verses.text: The barbell approach to content creation suggests creating short and concise pieces of a few hundred words or less, similar to a screenshot essay, to convey a main idea quickly. On the other end of the spectrum, creating long-form pieces of over 2,000 words provides a definitive take on a subject. The middle ground between short and long-form content may not be as effective in capturing audience interest.

verses.title: Diligence Leads to Insightful Content
verses.text: Thorough research and diligence in gathering information about Tencent's investment portfolio led to the creation of a comprehensive two-part piece. By spending two full days cross-referencing data from Chinese language sites and creating a detailed spreadsheet, the author gained a deep understanding of the company's investments. This effort not only helped in avoiding unreliable data sources but also resulted in the creation of a valuable document for readers, showcasing a link to a spreadsheet with interesting sci-fi ideas. The commitment to thorough research and attention to detail was highlighted as crucial in producing insightful and informative content.

verses.title: The Power of Sponsored Content in Company Stories
verses.text: Sponsored deep dives into companies sparked a positive reaction despite initial reservations, with feedback showing that readers appreciate unique insights into the company's story and operations. Founders often favor these sponsored posts as they provide a platform to share their vision, financials, and operational strategies, which are typically kept confidential. The focus is on uncovering the uniqueness and innovative approaches of each company, showcasing how they navigate challenges and drive success, such as structuring engineering teams for speed and precision in the case of FinTech companies like Ramp.

verses.title: Focus on the Individual, Reach the Stadium
verses.text: Writing with a focus on the individual rather than writing for a large audience paradoxically increases the chances of reaching a broader audience. Despite acknowledging this, the speaker finds themselves gravitating towards writing for a broader audience. They mention that their best writing comes from journaling to a friend daily, emphasizing the intimacy and depth of personal connection in that approach. The speaker expresses reluctance to publish these personal pieces, viewing them as deeply personal and possibly meant for sharing much later in life. Despite the value placed on these writings, the speaker invests significant effort into writing them daily, revealing a deep connection to the process and content.

verses.title: Embrace Your Identity in Learning and Writing
verses.text: Having a desire for more technical depth, the speaker acknowledges their strength in understanding core concepts and being able to explain them effectively. However, they express a longing to delve deeper into the intricacies of subjects. The discussion highlights the importance of surrendering to one's identity and embracing it fully, as it is crucial for personal growth and consistency. Embracing who one is facilitates genuine self-discovery through the process of writing, helping to attain a more profound understanding of oneself and enhance writing skills over time.

chapters.title: Paul Millerd: Why Writing Is The Path For Self Discovery
chapters.description: In this enlightening discussion, Paul Millerd, author of 'The Pathless Path', shares his inspiring journey from corporate life to embracing writing as a means of self-discovery. He emphasizes the importance of authenticity, urging writers to connect with those who truly resonate with their message. Millerd explores the liberating nature of writing true to oneself and the emotional challenges of crafting compelling narratives. He also reflects on the unpredictability of personal paths and the joy of finding a community that supports creative exploration.

verses.title: Create Your Own Culture and Embrace Truth
verses.text: Cultivating one's own culture requires active responsibility and a willingness to embrace potential loneliness and challenges. It often involves significant personal sacrifices, such as financial costs and shifts in career focus, as demonstrated by individuals who prioritize meaningful contributions, like volunteering and personal development, over conventional paths. The emphasis is on authenticity in pursuit of truth, moving away from formulaic nonfiction that relies heavily on anecdotes and research studies. True insight lies in the real experiences and deeper explorations of life, rather than in simplistic productivity hacks.

verses.title: Write for the Unconventional
verses.text: Facing criticism can lead to defensiveness, but it also sparks introspection and honesty. The act of writing transforms personal thoughts into public discourse, serving not just the assumed audience but also those who feel different or marginalized. Authenticity in writing emerges when the focus shifts from pleasing others to addressing personal truths. Recognizing the inherent ego in writing becomes liberating, allowing it to become a medium not only for self-expression but also for connecting with like-minded individuals navigating unconventional paths.

verses.title: Embrace the Unknown for Creative Growth
verses.text: Approaching complex projects, whether writing or consulting, requires a willingness to engage deeply with the details while remaining open to discovery. A bottom-up approach allows one to navigate through information without feeling overwhelmed, trusting the process as you fluctuate between data and insights. Evaluating productivity through personal surprise rather than mere output encourages a more profound creative experience, aligning with the philosophy that true exploration and creativity emerge from stepping beyond conventional constraints.

verses.title: Embrace the Joy of Discovery
verses.text: Engaging in the exploration of ideas transforms life into a fulfilling journey. Sharing discoveries fosters connections with like-minded individuals, enriching personal relationships and enhancing overall enjoyment. This process establishes a positive feedback loop that combines learning and creativity, leading to a deeper understanding of oneself and the world. In contrast, prioritizing the demands of corporate responsibilities detracts from personal passion, highlighting the significance of writing as a vital, inseparable element of one's existence. Ultimately, the thrill of uncovering new concepts and finding joy in the act of writing is paramount for a meaningful life.

verses.title: Embrace the Ought to Self
verses.text: The distinction between the ideal self and the ought to self plays a crucial role in personal development. The ideal self represents aspirational goals, while the ought to self aligns with our responsibilities and obligations to others. Taking leaps and pursuing one's goals can reveal that the ought to self is robust and maintains balance, automatically addressing necessary responsibilities. Even when setbacks occur, the ought to self can correct course through actionable steps. This perspective emphasizes the importance of personal narratives in illustrating growth and movement, prioritizing clarity and flow in writing to enhance engagement and comprehensibility. Streamlining content to reduce friction invites a more conversational tone, making insights accessible and relatable.

verses.title: Align Head, Heart, and Wallet to Find Your Core Idea
verses.text: A central idea is vital for expressing personal identity and maintaining focus. It should resonate across cognitive, emotional, and financial dimensions—what your head, heart, and wallet desire. When these aspects align, individuals uncover a core idea that remains engaging and fulfilling over time. This core concept contrasts with the experiences of others who may tire of their messages due to a lack of personal connection. Ultimately, finding this core idea results in an expansive internal experience while presenting a clear and specific message to the external world.

verses.title: Wander Without Destination to Ignite Creativity
verses.text: Embrace the journey of creativity without a specific destination and be open to discomfort, as it can lead to unexpected insights. In creative endeavors like writing, connecting with one’s emotions is crucial; rational thinking can hinder this process. Exploring various emotional states and experimenting with different approaches can help one tap into deeper feelings, often described as 'hearts on fire.' Striving to discover a fulfilling relationship with your work is essential; this quest can guide and energize your creative pursuits. A powerful exercise to uncover your life purpose involves writing it repeatedly until it evokes strong emotions, often culminating in tears, signaling a deep connection to your truth.

chapters.title: Sahil Bloom: How He Turned Twitter Threads Into 1 Million Followers | How I Write Podcast
chapters.description: Sahil Bloom, a former finance professional turned online writing sensation, shares insights on transforming Twitter threads into a million followers. He discusses his evolution from finance to impactful writing, embracing authenticity in content creation. Bloom explores overcoming writer's block, the importance of clarity in communication, and finding inspiration through exploration. He also reflects on the delicate balance between rapid audience growth and meaningful engagement, emphasizing the need for genuine connections and concise, impactful writing.

verses.title: The Importance of Creative Packaging in Non-Fiction Writing
verses.text: In non-fiction writing, the impact and success of a piece often rely on presenting existing ideas in a more beautifully packaged, concise, and well-written format. While some believe in the power of non-consensus thinking for the best writing, a significant number of successful non-fiction works are a result of taking known concepts and presenting them in a slightly improved manner. By packaging familiar ideas in a clear framework and delivering them effectively, authors like James Clear with 'Atomic Habits' have achieved immense success. It is highlighted that only a few non-fiction books truly revolutionize a field with dramatically different ideas, such as Tim Ferriss' 'The 4-Hour Workweek'. However, the broader impact and widespread success in non-fiction writing often stem from slightly enhancing existing concepts and presenting them compellingly to the audience.

verses.title: The Power of Adding Life to Existing Ideas
verses.text: Transforming existing ideas with vibrancy can make a significant impact on individuals, as exemplified by great teachers who bring life to concepts. Research shows that familiarity and cliches can lead to desensitization in the brain, resulting in a lack of engagement. Therefore, effective nonfiction writing aims to infuse existing ideas with freshness and innovation to create compelling and impactful content.

verses.title: George Gilder's One Liner on Creativity and Innovation
verses.text: Creativity and innovation are rooted in surprise, according to George Gilder. He believes that the essence of value creation lies in introducing useful surprise into the world, as anything that is not a surprise is already anticipated and priced in, hence not innovative.

verses.title: Facing the Fear of Impact
verses.text: The fear of creating something that may not impact people as intended can be daunting. Regardless of the outcome, whether the work goes unnoticed or becomes a huge success, the key is to feel truly connected to the topic and have a deep passion for it. In both scenarios, the crucial aspect is to have an authentic love for the work created, as it will sustain the creator through any outcome, ensuring the effort was worthwhile.

verses.title: Obsession with Metrics on Social Media
verses.text: The obsession with metrics on social media can negatively impact the authenticity and soulfulness of the content being created. Constantly worrying about the number of likes a post will receive can hinder creativity and lead to a focus on generating content for popularity rather than personal expression.

verses.title: Finding Your Shiny Dime
verses.text: The concept of finding your shiny dime emphasizes the importance of identifying the core essence or main point of your message. By pinpointing this central idea, all other elements can revolve around it, similar to how verses in a song relate back to the chorus. This clarity is essential for effective writing.

verses.title: The Erosion of Credentialism
verses.text: In the current age, the traditional walls of credentialism are crumbling, as credibility is now built on achievements and expertise rather than formal degrees. A prime example is James Clear, who gained credibility in discussing habits through building a large audience and providing valuable content rather than holding a specific degree. This shift signifies a departure from the past emphasis on formal education as the sole marker of expertise.

chapters.title: Steph Smith: How to Make Your Writing Stand Out
chapters.description: Join Steph Smith, A16Z podcast host and author, as she unpacks how to elevate your writing in a crowded landscape. Learn to find your unique voice, embrace authenticity, and stand out through storytelling. Steph dissects the effects of homogenization on creativity and emphasizes the importance of emotional resonance in connecting with your audience. Discover how to nurture ideas like a garden and balance the paradox of deadlines to enhance productivity. With insights from her success in the digital space, Steph inspires you to unleash your intrinsic interestingness.

verses.title: Making Decisions for Efficiency
verses.text: Making one decision that will save numerous decisions in the future is crucial. When it comes to visuals, focus on elements like the type of lines, color palette, and stick to a limited choice to streamline the decision-making process effectively.

verses.title: Discovering Blind Spots
verses.text: We often struggle to truly understand ourselves and our interests. Ideas are like placing a finger on our forehead; we are unable to see them clearly without external input or a change in perspective. Our best ideas, the ones we should focus on, are usually right in front of us, but we need others to point them out or we need a different perspective to see them.

chapters.title: Steven Johnson: How to Write with AI | How I Write Podcast
chapters.description: In this discussion, Steven Johnson, an author with a rich background in technology and human creativity, shares his insights on integrating AI into the writing process. He explores how large language models can enhance creativity rather than hinder it, offering tips on capturing ideas effortlessly and structuring narratives effectively. Johnson emphasizes the importance of creative incubation and using tools like NotebookLM to navigate writing challenges. His experiences blur the lines between innovation and storytelling, revealing how technology can spark unique insights.

verses.title: Preparation Over Time Leads to Innovation
verses.text: The speaker had been contemplating making a writing tool for almost 40 years, ever since college. His idea was to have a tool displaying a writing space on the left, notes at the top right, and public sources on the bottom right. This concept stemmed from years of accumulated ideas and thinking. The development of this tool was accomplished swiftly due to the speaker's extensive prior contemplation and preparation.

verses.title: Convincing Readers of Convergence in Writing
verses.text: Ensuring convergence of multiple characters in a narrative requires strategically placing signals in the prose to gain readers' trust in the plot's progression. The challenge lies in uniting disparate characters in a coherent manner, leading to an epic battle or event. Writers need to make readers feel confident early on that the storylines will merge successfully, avoiding the risk of readers feeling lost or disconnected from the overall narrative. This process mirrors the role of LLMs in predicting outcomes in writing, where the writer's ability to maintain reader trust in the convergence of plotlines is key.

verses.title: Converging Characters in Prose
verses.text: Convincing the readers of bringing together multiple characters from disparate backgrounds into a cohesive storyline in the streets of New York requires skillfully embedding signals in the prose. The challenge lies in ensuring that readers trust the convergence of characters early on, without feeling lost or questioning the plausibility. The author's focus was to build reader confidence in the plot's cohesion throughout the narrative, ensuring a smooth progression towards the ultimate epic battle in New York.

verses.title: Building Narrative Convergence
verses.text: Authors aim to surprise readers by bringing together disparate characters in a plot, requiring them to signal in the prose the eventual convergence. The challenge lies in convincing readers that the characters will unify, avoiding the risk of confusing or losing them within the narrative. By strategically incorporating signals throughout the text, the author ensures that the readers remain confident in the story's cohesion, ultimately creating a satisfying and harmonious narrative.

verses.title: Building Tension and Trust in Narrative Convergence
verses.text: Creating a successful narrative involves intriguing the reader with the challenge of bringing together diverse characters, signaling early on that the storyline will converge. The key is to instill confidence in readers that the disparate elements will eventually make sense and come together, avoiding the risk of readers feeling lost or disconnected. This practice is akin to the work done by LLMs, where guessing plays a significant role. The author shares their personal experience with a 10,000-word piece published in the New York Times Magazine that evoked a strong emotional response, highlighting the impact of effectively building tension and trust in narrative convergence.

verses.title: Building Reader Confidence in Narrative Convergence
verses.text: Creating a successful narrative involves threading together various characters and plotlines to form a cohesive story. To achieve this, authors must embed signals throughout the prose to assure readers that everything will come together in the end. The challenge lies in making readers trust the convergence of disparate elements early on, preventing them from feeling lost or disconnected. By strategically building reader confidence through the narrative, authors can ensure that their story resonates with the audience.

verses.title: LLMs: Diversity, Accessibility, Speed
verses.text: LLMs excel in providing diverse interpretations from multiple sources, offering accessibility through free tools, and delivering information instantly, unlike the time-consuming process of reaching out to authors directly. The technology's strength lies in its ability to offer diversity, accessibility, and speed to users seeking information.

verses.title: From Information to Insight
verses.text: Moving from raw data to actionable insights can be a tedious process involving hours of structuring and organizing information. Utilizing tools like notebook LM can streamline this process by generating briefing documents with key quotes organized by themes. The transition from information to insight involves filtering out the noise to focus on the core, valuable insights. Platforms like LM excel at distilling complex ideas into concise one-liners or sentences, facilitating the transformation of information into actionable insights.

chapters.title: Steven Pinker: Harvard Professor Explains The Rules of Writing | How I Write
chapters.description: In this insightful discussion, Steven Pinker, a Harvard Professor and renowned author, delves into the intricacies of effective writing. He addresses 'The Curse of Knowledge,' emphasizing the need to simplify language for broader audience connection. Pinker also reflects on the value of childhood perspectives in enhancing creativity and humor. Furthermore, he explores the impact of AI on writing, contrasting human creativity with AI's structured outputs, and urges writers to embrace clarity and brevity in their craft.

verses.title: Curse of Knowledge Hinders Clarity
verses.text:
- Bad writing often stems from the "curse of knowledge," where writers can't imagine not knowing what they know.
- This causes them to use jargon, acronyms, and abstractions that confuse audiences unfamiliar with their field.

💬 Quote

Now, this was not a stupid man, but he was very stupid when it came to communication. Namely, not everyone knows what you know.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on the Curse of Knowledge causing bad writing

verses.title: Use Diverse Readers for Clarity
verses.text:
- Show your writing to diverse readers outside your specialization to catch unclear language.
- Empathy alone isn't enough because the curse of knowledge blinds you to what others don't understand.

💬 Quote

So anyway, I try. But at end the of the day, I show it to people.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on overcoming the curse of knowledge by showing writing to others

verses.title: Visual Imagery Enhances Writing
verses.text:
- Thinking visually enriches writing since the brain relies heavily on sensory and motor imagery.
- Writing should help readers form vivid mental images rather than rely on abstract jargon and concepts.

💬 Quote

Language is a means to an end of getting people to appreciate the ideas you're trying to convey, which are ... very often visual and motoric, that is bodily, emotional, auditory, but they're sensory.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on incorporating visual and sensory detail in writing

verses.title: Favor Concrete Visual Metaphors
verses.text:
- Use concrete language and visual metaphors to make abstract ideas vivid and relatable.
- Relying on jargon might be efficient within a profession but loses readers outside specialist circles.

💬 Quote

Instead of saying something like aggression or antisocial behavior, they might say the spirit of the hawk needed into our flesh.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on the power of visual metaphors in older writing

verses.title: Writing Lacks Immediate Feedback
verses.text:
- Writing is harder than speaking because writing lacks immediate feedback and shared context.
- Writers must provide full context as readers encounter text cold without clues from body language or prior conversation.

💬 Quote

In writing, you're wrenched from the context. Someone's picking a book up off the shelf and they've never met you.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker explaining why writing is naturally more difficult than speaking

verses.title: Balance Generalizations With Examples
verses.text:
- Avoid generalizations without examples because they often confuse readers.
- Use examples to concretize abstract ideas so readers understand precisely what you mean.

💬 Quote

Generalizations without examples, I find, even in my field, nine out of ten times, I just don't know what they're talking about.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on importance of examples alongside generalizations

verses.title: Language Evolves Away From Origins
verses.text:
- Many English compound words and phrases drift from their original literal meanings over time.
- Language evolves with fossilized word origins and irregular semantic relationships within compounds.

💬 Quote

Language is so ancient... a lot of words can completely obscure their origin. You have to go to a dictionary to find them out.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker discussing the evolution and obscurity of English word meanings

verses.title: Read Aloud to Improve Rhythm
verses.text:
- Read your writing aloud to catch awkward rhythm or phrasing.
- Aim for smooth, poetic prose with attention to sound and rhythm, avoiding excessive sibilance.

💬 Quote

Often when you read a draft of your own prose, and if you can't articulate it smoothly, that probably your reader won't... either.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on using oral feedback to refine prose style

verses.title: Bad Academic Writing Wastes Potential
verses.text:
- Bad academic writing wastes intellectual effort and causes misunderstanding.
- Academics should make their work accessible since public funding and wider communication depend on clear writing.

💬 Quote

There's an awful lot of really brilliant work... why are they doing it? Just to entertain each other in a closed little circle?
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker lamenting the inaccessibility of much academic prose

verses.title: Kids' Freshness Inspires Clarity
verses.text:
- Steven Pinker enjoys children's fresh, poetic explanations untouched by clichés and jargon.
- Children's views force them to use clear, concrete images others can understand.

💬 Quote

Because they aren't writing in clichés and partly because they haven't accumulated this mass of abstractions.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on the originality and vividness of children's language

verses.title: Brevity Boosts Wit and Clarity
verses.text:
- Brevity is essential for wit and effective writing.
- Omitting needless words improves clarity, reduces cognitive load, and enhances prose aesthetics.

💬 Quote

Omit needless words. Omit needless words, omit needless words.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on the value of brevity in writing and humor

verses.title: Older Writing Was More Poetic
verses.text:
- Older writing is often more poetic due to classical training and fewer clichés.
- Informalization and cultural shifts favor casual speech over ornate prose today.

💬 Quote

Since they didn't have the benefit of decades and decades of abstractions and clichés... they were compelled to use vivid expressions.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker on causes of poetic style in historical prose

verses.title: LLMs Write Clearly but Blandly
verses.text:
- Large language models produce clear but generic, prosaic text because they synthesize vast samples.
- Their prose may be clearer than much academic writing but lacks stylistic freshness or originality.

💬 Quote

The output of LLMs is peculiar... It's bad in the sense that it is so generic and prosaic, and you can almost recognize the output.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker discussing the style and limitations of writing by large language models

verses.title: Rethinking Writing With AI Insights
verses.text:
- Steven Pinker would rewrite his books considering LLMs' pattern extraction power.
- He sees human learning as richer, involving interaction, unlike pure text-based LLM training.

💬 Quote

I certainly would have had to reconcile the intelligence of large language models with human intelligence with more attention to the power of pattern extraction.
— Steven Pinker

Steven Pinker reflecting on AI's impact on writing and cognition

chapters.title: Steven Pressfield: Every Story Needs a Villain | How I Write Podcast | How I Write
chapters.description: Join Steven Pressfield, an esteemed author known for his insightful writings on creativity and overcoming resistance, as he shares his wisdom on storytelling. He discusses the pivotal role of compelling villains in narratives, revealing how they can enhance character struggles. Pressfield emphasizes the balance between discipline and joy in writing, the importance of clarity, and the need to push through self-doubt. He also touches on the thrilling craft of suspense and the transformative nature of storytelling, drawing on personal experiences and time-tested techniques.

verses.title: Covering the Canvas and Going Around Obstacles in First Drafts
verses.text: In first drafts, the focus should be on covering the canvas by getting something on every part, even if it's not perfect. It's like splattering paint like Jackson Pollack. When facing obstacles, the approach should be similar to Israel Tal's strategy during the Six-Day War - keep advancing and go around the obstacles, even if it means losing some along the way. The idea is to avoid getting stuck in a war of attrition and prioritize continuous movement forward in writing.

verses.title: The Challenge of Prioritizing Creative Work over Meetings
verses.text: Prioritizing creative work over meetings can be a challenge, and some individuals, like the CTO at HubSpot, adopt strategies to depersonalize their stance on avoiding meetings. By sending resources like an article titled 'My Heart Says Yes. But my calendar says no,' they effectively communicate their prioritization of creative work without causing offense. The key challenge lies in balancing tenderness and grace when declining meetings to focus on creative work.

verses.title: The 60 Scenes Method: A Screenwriting Approach
verses.text: Five years ago, the speaker asked the listener for an interview, which they declined. However, the listener sent the speaker 30 books to share with their readers, which was greatly appreciated. The speaker then asks about the '60 scenes method' in screenwriting, where a movie is broken down into 60 scenes represented by index cards. The speaker explains that starting with a few key scenes, one can fill in the blanks and develop the story further. This method allows for flexibility and improvement as better ideas arise.

chapters.title: The Cultural Tutor, on Writing Every Day, Growing a Twitter Audience, and Reading Old Books
chapters.description: The Cultural Tutor, an anonymous writer who soared to 1.5 million Twitter followers in just 15 months, shares his journey from humble beginnings to social media fame. He delves into the importance of daily writing habits versus productivity pressure, and how both handwriting and typing affect creative expression. The conversation touches on navigating the vast information landscape of today’s digital age, the enduring value of classic literature, and the balance between authenticity and the demands of online visibility.

verses.title: Overcoming Productivity Distractions in Writing
verses.text: Don't let productivity and excuses get in the way of writing. Find the time and prioritize what really matters.

verses.title: The Importance of Productivity and Capturing Ideas in Writing
verses.text: Feeling productive is key, regardless of food or sleep. When you're in the zone, nothing else matters. The inspiration to ride all night is strong because you know it may not last. Writing every day is important, even without planning. One good piece of advice is to jot down ideas immediately, as you won't remember them later.

verses.title: The Influence of Handwriting vs. Computer Writing
verses.text: Handwriting allows for more personality and voice as there is no delete key, unlike computer writing. The delete key on the computer leads to a homogenization of writing, while handwriting reflects the originality of thoughts expressed as the words cannot be erased.

verses.title: The Importance of Writing for Yourself and Refining Your Ideas
verses.text: Aspiring writers often make the mistake of not focusing on the fundamental aspects of writing, such as copyright and creating a compelling hook. Additionally, some writers stray from their authentic desires and instead pursue what they believe will bring success, resulting in lackluster writing. While writing solely for oneself may hinder the refinement of ideas to reach their full potential, exclusively pursuing external validation can lead to prematurely abandoning ideas before they fully mature.

chapters.title: The Ultimate Guide to Writing with AI | How I Write
chapters.description: AI is reshaping the writing landscape, provoking both excitement and existential questions. Discover the emergence of new AI writing models and how they humorously enhance creativity. Personal narratives remain crucial in nonfiction, emphasizing authenticity amidst technology's rise. A fascinating journey from atheism to faith showcases the power of individual stories. Explore AI as a transformative partner in writing, elevating both memory and communication for future storytellers.

verses.title: AI's Impact on Writing
verses.text:
- AI is shaking the writing world, and ignoring it is unwise. 
- Good writers will adapt, but the landscape is changing rapidly.

💬 Quote

As someone who's built a career around writing, I think that AI is going to shake the foundations of the writing world. [...] Really good writers are going to be all right.
— David Perell

David Perell on the changing landscape of writing

verses.title: AI Tour Guide
verses.text:
- David Perell used AI as a personalized tour guide in Argentina. 
- He asked questions throughout the day and received compiled answers at night.

💬 Quote

When I travel, I just love to learn about wherever I am. So what I would do is, as I would spend the day in Buenos Aires, I would be at an art museum or see a statue. And I basically have a question and I put it into GPT and then it would get the answer in the background.
— David Perell

David Perell on using AI while traveling

verses.title: AI and Niche Humor
verses.text:
- AI excels at niche humor, as seen with a joke about Tyler Cowen. 
- This signals AI's potential to disrupt various writing professions.

💬 Quote

AI is gonna be really good at super niche humor. [...] I think that what it means to be a journalist, a researcher, an academic, a full-time author is going to be rewritten.
— David Perell

David Perell on the future of writing professions.

verses.title: AI-Generated Humor: Tyler Cowen
verses.text:
- David Perell shares an AI-generated joke about economist Tyler Cowen, finding it surprisingly funny and niche.
-  This highlights AI's potential for generating highly specific humor, surpassing even human capabilities in esoteric areas.

💬 Quote

AI is gonna be really good at super niche humor. [...] the chances that some comedian is also interested in that are going to be pretty low.
— David Perell

David Perell on how AI can generate niche humor.

verses.title: AI-Proof Nonfiction
verses.text:
- Nonfiction writing based on personal experience is less susceptible to AI takeover. 
- Human connection and personal narratives remain valuable.

💬 Quote

The more that a piece of writing comes from personal experience, the less likely it is to be overtaken by AI. [...] We really want to hear personal narratives.
— David Perell

David Perell on the value of personal writing

verses.title: Differentiate Your Writing
verses.text:
- To set your nonfiction apart from AI, focus on your unique experience and expertise.
- Share specific details and insights from your life that LLMs can't replicate.

💬 Quote

The two E's of being differentiated from LLMs in your nonfiction writing [...] are experience and expertise.
— David Perell

David Perell on how to differentiate your writing from AI.

verses.title: Writing in the AI Age
verses.text:
- Focus on experience, expertise, personal stories, and strong opinions. 
- David Perell is writing about his conversion to Christianity as an example.

💬 Quote

If you want to make money as a writer, your writing is going to have to get better and better. [...] This piece, right, it's deeply personal. It's deeply opinionated.
— David Perell

David Perell on his current writing project

verses.title: Two Dimensions of Quality
verses.text:
- Writing quality has two dimensions: objective quality and how it's tailored to interests. 
- AI excels at tailoring content to specific interests, even if objective quality isn't as high.

💬 Quote

I've realized that there's really two kinds of quality when you sit down to read something, okay? [...] It's on the tailored to your interest dimension of quality that AI has massively improved.
— David Perell

David Perell on defining quality in writing.

verses.title: Essential Writing Skills
verses.text:
- Core writing skills remain crucial: taste and a spiky point of view. 
- AI can enhance writing but cannot replicate unique perspectives.

💬 Quote

Whether you're writing with AI or you're writing without AI, the core skills that you need to succeed as a writer are exactly the same. [...] Those are the skills that used to be true. Those are the skills that will be true.
— David Perell

David Perell on the importance of core writing skills

verses.title: AI and the Future of Writing
verses.text:
- AI in writing is like the evolution of sampling in music, initially controversial but eventually accepted. 
- Objective quality will be the ultimate measure of writing, regardless of AI involvement.

💬 Quote

I think AI is gonna be like music. [...] The only thing that's going to matter is going to be the objective quality of a piece of writing.
— David Perell

David Perell on the changing perception of using AI tools.

verses.title: The End of Slop
verses.text:
- AI may end the era of "slop," where publishing frequency trumped quality. 
- The bar for good writing is rising due to AI competition.

💬 Quote

Right now, everyone's talking about AI slop. [...] And he ain't succeeding with that now.
— David Perell

David Perell on the changing standards of online writing

verses.title: Slop is Dead
verses.text:
- David Perell defines "slop" as prioritizing publishing frequency over quality.
- He observes that this strategy, once effective, is now failing in the age of AI, where quality is paramount.

💬 Quote

I define slop as when simply publishing or getting something done is more important than the quality of what you publish. And I would say that so much of the online writing going back the last 10 years was slop under the definition I just gave.
— David Perell

David Perell on why simply publishing frequently is no longer a winning strategy.

verses.title: Writing With AI, Not For AI
verses.text:
- David Perell uses AI as a writing partner, not a replacement. 
- He uses personal anecdotes about San Francisco that AI can't replicate to enhance his writing.

💬 Quote

There's AI that writes for you. There's AI that does writing with you. And I've yet to meet a good writer, a good writer that I admire and respect that thinks that AI can do the writing for you.
— David Perell

David Perell on the proper role of AI in the writing process.

verses.title: Using AI for Feedback
verses.text:
- Use AI for feedback on outlines, prose, arguments, and character development. 
- Leverage AI's ability to interview you and identify weak points.

💬 Quote

- When it comes to writing with chat GPT, here's what I do. [...] I find that the back and forth is so much more generative than me trying to do it all on my own.
— David Perell

David Perell on how AI is a useful partner in brainstorming.

verses.title: Thinking with LLMs
verses.text:
- Jamming with LLMs can be more helpful for idea generation than talking to most people. 
- LLMs excel at challenging ideas and revealing hidden insights.

💬 Quote

- When I'm discovering new ideas, I found that jamming with LLMs is more useful than talking to basically any person in my life, save for like a few people who I'm close to who give really good feedback. [...] And that is what I'm using LLMs for.
— David Perell

David Perell on his effective use of LLMs.

verses.title: Hallucinations in LLMs
verses.text:
- Be cautious of Large Language Models (LLMs) hallucinating or fabricating information.
- Don't accept information from an LLM as fact without verifying it.

💬 Quote

Do not, do not just take something that an LLM gives you and pass it along for fact. Like you really need to be careful there.
— David Perell

David Perell emphasizes the importance of verifying information from Large Language Models.

verses.title: Using the Latest Models
verses.text:
- Use the latest, paid AI models for the best results. 
- Free models lag behind, hindering a true understanding of AI's capabilities.

💬 Quote

The free models are currently like six months behind the cutting edge models and the cutting edge models are just so much better, okay?
— David Perell

David Perell's advice on staying current with AI models.

verses.title: Content Creation vs. Consumption
verses.text:
- Use ChatGPT 4.5 for content creation because it's fast and good at organizing information.
- Use O1 Pro or Deep Research for content consumption. They are slower but better at providing in-depth output.

💬 Quote

I use ChatGPT 4.5 whenever I need to create something. And then whenever I need to consume something, I'll use O1 Pro or Deep Research.
— David Perell

David Perell on which AI models he uses for different tasks.

verses.title: Dictation Tools for Writing
verses.text:
- If you dislike typing, try using dictation tools like Whisperflow or Super Whisper.
- These tools offer accurate speech-to-text conversion, allowing you to speak your thoughts and have them transcribed directly onto the screen.

💬 Quote

I love being able to [...] just speak things out. [...] Whisperflow and Super Whisper are pretty darn accurate when you're just trying to share things.
— David Perell

David Perell on dictating text instead of typing.

chapters.title: Tiago Forte: A Guide to Productive Writing | How I Write Podcast
chapters.description: Tiago Forte, bestselling author of "Building a Second Brain," shares insights from his journey of unlocking creativity. He reveals the delicate balance between writing and editing, stressing the emotional toll of creativity. Tiago explores how to evolve a 'second brain' for effective idea curation and the importance of annual self-reflection for personal growth. He also discusses navigating the publishing industry and the dynamics of creative collaboration in course development, highlighting the synergy of discipline and creativity.

verses.title: The Trade-off of Compression in Ideas
verses.text: When compressing ideas, there is a trade-off involved. Compression offers benefits like increased accessibility and understanding, opening doors to the idea. However, this process also leads to the loss of essential elements such as context, nuance, subtlety, and depth.

verses.title: The Power of Summarizing and Linking in Writing
verses.text: In writing, it's crucial to reference ideas thoroughly, accurately, and credibly while maintaining the flow of thought. Achieving this involves summarizing complex topics effectively and then linking them to supporting points. The power of the hyperlink in online writing lies in its ability to transform how information is presented, allowing for detailed evidence and proofs without length limitations. Summaries, though not something to be proud of, are essential building blocks akin to laying a foundation when constructing a building. They are part of the preparatory work that includes highlighting, distilling, and getting feedback, which ultimately contributes to expressing the true vision in the writing process.

verses.title: The Unexpected Success of Duck Dynasty Merchandise
verses.text: The Duck Dynasty show became an unexpected hit leading to the sale of over 1,000 licenses for a wide range of merchandise such as sleeping bags, baseball caps, shoes, and truck decals. This success stemmed from the simple idea of a family with a Duck Dynasty, which was cleverly translated into a lucrative business strategy resembling Disney's approach to merchandising.

chapters.title: Tim Ferriss: How I Wrote 5 #1 NYT Bestsellers | How I Write Podcast
chapters.description: Tim Ferriss, bestselling author and renowned podcaster, shares insights from his impressive career, boasting five #1 NYT bestsellers and millions of followers. He discusses transformative writing education and the necessity of engaging with captivating ideas. Ferriss emphasizes the art of revision, personal tone, and the importance of brand credibility in writing. He elaborates on finding a unique voice, balancing discipline with creativity, and the role of conversation in enhancing writing skills. Get ready to unlock your own writing potential!

verses.title: The Value of Strong Constraints in Fostering Creativity and Protecting Reputation
verses.text: Having strong constraints is essential for fostering creativity and making faster, better decisions. The scarcity and increasing value of reputation and credibility necessitate the need for policies to protect them as they are considered the new gold.

verses.title: Focusing on Content Before Voice Development
verses.text: The focus should be on creating compelling content rather than worrying about developing a unique voice. Content is king, and it's important to prioritize the 'what' before the 'how.' Even with average writing skills, a great premise can capture the imagination of the audience. The suggestion is to start by doing something more interesting and interviewing less recognized individuals to create compelling content.

verses.title: Importance of Adding Humor and Excitement in Writing
verses.text: Adding inside jokes and humor in writing can make the content highly functional and interesting. It is important to find excitement and fun in the writing process to produce engaging material. The writer also emphasizes the need to make the material interesting during the pre-writing experiential phase, and highlights the significance of combining good material with average writing to create compelling content.

verses.title: The Importance of Regular Writing for Sharpening Thinking
verses.text: Regular writing is crucial for maintaining sharp thinking skills. Writing acts as an annual checkup for one's thinking process, prompting the writer to assess the coherence of their thoughts. Despite the pressure of a large subscriber base, the speaker feels compelled to maintain a regular writing habit. By sharing an experience of exceeding the intended length of a newsletter, the speaker emphasizes the value of starting with a manageable amount of work to build positive momentum towards a new behavior. The key insight is that doing less than one thinks they can do is crucial for establishing a new habit and achieving success.

verses.title: Writing Approach: Self-Sufficient Chapters
verses.text: The insight is about the advantages of structuring the work with self-sufficient, standalone chapters. This approach benefits the reader, allowing them to jump around while maintaining coherence, and facilitating a non-linear writing process. It helps in maintaining momentum and avoiding demoralization when facing an impasse in the writing process.

verses.title: Overcoming Writer's Block with Interviewing Technique
verses.text: The speaker suggests applying questions to review and clarify a stuck chapter. They recount their struggle with finishing a book chapter and how hiring someone to interview them about the chapter helped them overcome writer's block within 60 minutes. The process of speaking out loud and clarifying unclear points during the interview helped the speaker to figure out exactly what to do.

verses.title: Advice for Aspiring Writers
verses.text: 1. Do something interesting or weird first to make writing easier. 2. Aim to be a category of one by identifying gaps or unique perspectives. 3. Consider doing less than you think you can in your writing.

chapters.title: Tucker Max: The King of Memoir Writing | How I Write
chapters.description: Tucker Max, a memoir guru and author of the bestselling "I Hope They Serve Beer in Hell," shares his insights on writing compelling memoirs. He emphasizes the importance of telling the unvarnished truth, showcasing humor as a vital tool for storytelling. From transforming personal struggles into relatable narratives to the healing power of writing, Tucker highlights how authentic experiences resonate deeply with readers. He also discusses crafting engaging book titles and the role of honest reflection in creating impactful stories.

verses.title: Focus Your Writing
verses.text:
- Decide on a specific part of your life to write about, instead of trying to cover everything.
- This focus makes the structure of a memoir much clearer and the writing significantly better.

💬 Quote

How do I structure a memoir is easy if you say, I'm only going to write about the story of Rite of Passage, right? Then it's like, oh, well, it started here, ended here and i'm going to talk about that chronologically is obviously the way to do it.
— Tucker Max

Tucker Max on structuring a memoir

verses.title: Honesty and Relatability
verses.text:
- If you are genuinely honest about your life, it's interesting.
- So few people are truly honest, which makes honesty itself captivating.

💬 Quote

So if you're genuinely honest about your life, it's interesting. So few people are really honest [...].
— Tucker Max

Tucker Max on why honesty is interesting.

verses.title: Everyone has an interesting story
verses.text:
- Everyone has a story with love, heartbreak, sadness, disappointment, joy, and accomplishments.
- If you're honest about your emotions, you can make any story riveting.

💬 Quote

There is no life so small that it does not contain the entire human experience.
— Tucker Max

Tucker Max on the universality of human experience.

verses.title: Memoir Storytelling
verses.text:
- To tell a great story in a memoir, focus on a simple algorithm: 'What happened? How did you feel? What happened next?'
- Repeat this sequence to create a compelling narrative.

💬 Quote

What happened? How did you feel? What happened next?
— Tucker Max

Tucker Max on a simple storytelling algorithm for memoir writing.

verses.title: Memoir Opening Advice
verses.text:
- Start a memoir in the middle of an intense scene to immediately grab the reader's attention.
- Briefly tell the story before explaining how you got there, then rewind and tell the full story.

💬 Quote

Start right in the middle of the action. [...] tell it to like, you get this still, you may have wondered how I got here, like that kind of, without literally doing that.
— Tucker Max

Tucker Max on how to start a memoir.

chapters.title: Tyler Cowen and Alex Tabarrok, on The Story of Marginal Revolution
chapters.description: Tyler Cowen and Alex Tabarrok, co-founders of the Marginal Revolution blog, share insights from two decades of economic commentary. They discuss their contrasts in writing approaches and the intrinsic joy that fuels impactful content creation. The duo reflects on how diverse topics, including popular culture, engage audiences more effectively. They also emphasize the evolution of education through their platform, highlighting the transformative power of self-publishing and technology in making economics accessible, especially in places like India.

verses.title: The Economics of Patents and Innovation
verses.text: The economics of patents and innovation centers on the idea that patents provide a monopoly to innovators in order to recoup their research and development costs. This system is meant to prevent the immediate imitation of a product to ensure that the innovator can charge a higher price to recover their initial investment. However, the current patent law does not fully reflect this economic theory, as it does not consider innovation and imitation costs. To address this, the speaker suggests implementing different patent durations based on the level of research and development investment, such as three-year, ten-year, and twenty-year patents.

chapters.title: Tyler Cowen: The Most Practical Conversation on AI Writing | How I Write
chapters.description: Tyler Cowen, an economist and writer known for his practical insights, discusses the transformative role of AI in writing and research. He emphasizes how AI tools can enhance personal voice and creativity in writing, while cautioning against over-reliance on AI-generated content. Cowen predicts a decline in traditional book writing and a rise in video content. He also explores innovative uses of AI in academia and everyday life, and shares tips for effective AI prompting and personal storytelling through technology.

verses.title: AI as Secondary Literature
verses.text:
- Use AI as secondary literature when researching or reading.
- Interrogate LLMs about topics, improving context and learning.

💬 Quote

I use AI when I read things. So I use it as the secondary literature.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on how he uses AI.

verses.title: Active Reading with AI
verses.text:
- Improve reading comprehension by actively engaging with AI.
- Ask AI questions about books, sparking deeper thinking and connections.

💬 Quote

What are some puzzles? What should I ponder more? How does that connect to something else later in the book?
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on engaging with AI while reading.

verses.title: Shakespearean Puzzles
verses.text:
- Shakespeare's works are full of puzzles and open to interpretation.
- LLMs, having read all of Shakespeare, can offer valuable insights and perspectives.

💬 Quote

Shakespeare's full of puzzles, right? [...]  you can read five to 10 times and still just you're beginning to get a handle on it.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on the complexity of Shakespeare.

verses.title: Generating Out-of-the-Box Questions
verses.text:
- Generate unique interview questions by focusing on specific details.
- Ask AI practical questions about historical examples to inspire original inquiries.

💬 Quote

You just want to ask it about the details of historical examples. [...] And you will yourself come up with out-of questions.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on preparing for interviews.

verses.title: Writing with AI
verses.text:
- Use AI for background research on unfamiliar topics, like legal issues.
- Maintain a unique writing style by writing yourself, even if AI is better.

💬 Quote

I want the writing to be my own. It's like my little baby, so to speak.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on maintaining authenticity in writing.

verses.title: AI for Critique
verses.text:
- Use AI to identify potentially obnoxious or condescending parts of your writing.
- Ask AI to critique your work for tone and style, improving communication.

💬 Quote

Run your writing through the AI and ask it, is in here that some people are likely to find obnoxious?
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on refining writing with AI.

verses.title: Effective AI Use
verses.text:
- Avoid asking AI general questions or relying on it as a time-saving shortcut.
- Engage deeply with AI by asking specific, practical questions to reveal its potential.

💬 Quote

They're not willing to put in enough of their own time generating context.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on common mistakes in using AI.

verses.title: Decentralized AI Networks
verses.text:
- Envision AI evolving into a decentralized system of interacting agents.
- Use multiple AIs to bounce ideas and create a dialogue, mimicking a "republic of science."

💬 Quote

Think of it as a stacked device, not a single box, but a set of interacting agents that in a sense are trying to evolve toward a market with multiple agents that talk to each other.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on the future of AI interaction.

verses.title: Technical Understanding of AI
verses.text:
- Understanding AI's technical workings is helpful but not essential for utilizing its power.
- Practical experience and a general understanding of its capabilities are sufficient.

💬 Quote

I have no deep technical understanding of that, but I could tell you a bunch of things.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on the importance of technical knowledge.

verses.title: AI-Generated Biography
verses.text:
- Tyler Cowen is creating an autobiography by filling in gaps in his online presence.
- He's writing blog posts about his childhood so AI can create a comprehensive biography.

💬 Quote

I can write an autobiography quite simply. There's just a lot of me out there, podcast, blog, essays, books.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on creating an autobiography with AI's help.

verses.title: Learning About AI
verses.text:
- Stay current with AI by using the best models, reading popular science accounts, and being self-critical.
- Focus on practical applications and learn from others to maximize your understanding.

💬 Quote

Work with it. Use it. Be self-critical about what you're doing with it and be willing to learn from other people.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on staying at the cutting edge of AI.

verses.title: Using AI to Order Food
verses.text:
- Tyler Cowen used AI to order food in Paraguay by taking a picture of the menu, some of which was in Guarani.
-  He asked GPT what to order and followed the recommendation, despite not knowing what the alternative would have been.

💬 Quote

I photographed the menu. I asked GPT, "What should I order and why?" It gave me answers. I ordered those dishes. I'll never know the alternative. It seemed to work, and I knew what I was doing all of a sudden.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on using AI to order food from a menu in an unfamiliar language.

verses.title: Secrets and Social Networks
verses.text:
- AI's increasing capabilities may incentivize hoarding information, making secrets more valuable.
- Social networks and in-person interactions gain importance for resource mobilization.

💬 Quote

The future, the AI rich future is also a world replete with secrets. Secrets are super important.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on the importance of secrets in an AI-driven world.

verses.title: Prompting Tips
verses.text:
- Write AI prompts literally, imagining you speak to an alien or animal.
- Use follow-up prompts rather than overly long ones for better responses.

💬 Quote

Put humans out of your mind. Imagine yourself either speaking to an alien or maybe a non-human animal.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on improving prompting skills.

verses.title: Mentorship and Networking
verses.text:
- Get more and better mentors to navigate the changing career landscape.
- Improve your peer network, as it becomes more valuable in the AI age.

💬 Quote

Get more and better mentors and work every day at improving the quality of your peer network.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on advice for young people.

verses.title: Large Context Windows
verses.text:
- Large context windows enable AI to process complex regulatory codes and historical archives.
- Converting existing data into AI-usable formats will be a major human project.

💬 Quote

Working with things like that over time, a new project for humanity that will create a lot of jobs, by the way, is converting data into usable form.
— Tyler Cowen

Tyler Cowen on possibilities with larger context windows.

chapters.title: Ward Farnsworth: 14 Ways To Make Your Writing Memorable | How I Write Classic Episode
chapters.description: Ward Farnsworth, a law professor and author, shares insights on making writing memorable with techniques drawn from classic literature and speeches. He highlights the power of simple Saxon words, exemplified by the King James Bible's profound phrases. Farnsworth delves into rhetorical styles like anaphora and epistrophe, showcasing Churchill and Lincoln as masters of these techniques. He discusses the impact of chiasmus and offers practical advice for effective writing, emphasizing clarity and immersion in exemplary texts.

verses.title: Prefer Simple Saxon Words
verses.text:
- Prefer simple Saxon words over fancier Latinate words to make prose stronger and more immediate.
- End important sentences with short Saxon words to create memorable force and resonance.

💬 Quote

If you have to have one rule, prefer Saxon words to romance words or Latinate words.
— Ward Farnsworth

Ward Farnsworth on choosing Saxon over Latinate words (importance and effect)

verses.title: Use Repetition To Build Momentum
verses.text:
- Repetition at the start (anaphora) grabs the ear and signals importance before the climax.
- Use repeated openings sparingly to build momentum toward a final striking phrase.

💬 Quote

We shall fight. We shall fight. We shall fight.
— Winston Churchill

Ward Farnsworth reading and analyzing Churchill's anaphora from the 1940 speech

verses.title: Set Up Contrast For Impact
verses.text:
- Use contrast between Latinate openings and Saxon closings to heighten impact and create surprise.
- Don't fear passive voice when it improves rhythm and contrast; choose constructions with purpose.

💬 Quote

Everything after that, every word is Saxon.
— Ward Farnsworth

On Churchill's sentence 'Never in the field of human conflict...' and the Latinate-to-Saxon contrast

verses.title: Lincoln's Biblical Apprenticeship
verses.text:
- Abraham Lincoln immersed himself in the King James Bible and Shakespeare and learned rhetorical patterns from them.
- He often said things twice: once Latinate, then again in plain Saxon to reach mind and gut.

💬 Quote

When you ask me, how did Lincoln become Lincoln? The answer is thousands of hours of immersion in Shakespeare and the King James Bible.
— Ward Farnsworth

Ward Farnsworth explaining Lincoln's influences and style formation

verses.title: Pair Mind And Gut In Phrasing
verses.text:
- Combine cerebral Latinate phrasing with a plain Saxon restatement to make abstract ideas emotionally immediate.
- Ending with a gut word like 'hate' or 'evil' makes the principle ring true to readers.

💬 Quote

It is the principle of free thought. Not free thought for those who agree with us, but freedom for the thought that we hate.
— Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.

Ward Farnsworth quoting Holmes on free thought and analyzing its Saxon ending

verses.title: End With Repetition (Epistrophe)
verses.text:
- Use epistrophe (repeat at the end) to make clauses stick and create solemn or memorable cadence.
- Reserve epistrophe for key moments so repeated endings ring instead of sounding rote.

💬 Quote

The world will little note, nor long remember what we say here, but it can never forget what they did here.
— Abraham Lincoln

Ward Farnsworth illustrating Lincoln's Gettysburg epistrophe and its effect

verses.title: The Bentsen One-Liner
verses.text:
- Lloyd Bentsen's debate line 'Senator, you're no Jack Kennedy' used repetition and reversal to deliver a memorable insult.
- He alternated endings and a reversal to satisfy the ear and land the zinger.
