💬 Quote

Senator, I served with Jack Kennedy. I knew Jack Kennedy. Jack Kennedy was a friend of mine. Senator, you're no Jack Kennedy.
— Lloyd Bentsen

Ward Farnsworth recounting Bentsen's vice-presidential debate retort and its rhetorical structure

verses.title: Deploy Chiasmus For Reversal
verses.text:
- Use chiasmus (A B B A) when you want to show a reversal or that someone has it backwards.
- Look for conceptual pairs (give/take, cause/effect) that naturally lend themselves to chiastic framing.

💬 Quote

Ask not what your country can do for you. Ask what you can do for your country.
— John F. Kennedy

Ward Farnsworth explaining chiasmus with JFK's inaugural line

chapters.title: Will Storr: The Science of Storytelling | How I Write
chapters.description: Will Storr, an author and storytelling expert, dives deep into the science behind character development. He asserts that compelling stories build around rich characters rather than plot alone. Storr discusses the transformative power of change in narratives and the importance of pacing to captivate audiences. He reveals how human drives like connection and status shape our narratives, linking personal health to storytelling. By the end, listeners gain valuable tools to enhance their storytelling in daily conversations.

verses.title: Story Simplifies Reality
verses.text:
- Humans use stories to simplify and make sense of an overwhelmingly complex reality.
- Storytelling's power lies in helping us imagine the future and progress as a species.

💬 Quote

One of the things that story does is give us this kind of simple, kind of fairly linear kind of experience of the world.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on why humans process reality as stories

verses.title: Character Over Plot
verses.text:
- Character drives story more than plot, though both are important.
- Memorable stories hinge on deep, well-developed characters, not just events.

💬 Quote

I think it's character that makes amazing stories, you really amazing our most memorable stories stories that mean the most to us are the ones with the most memorable characters.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on what makes stories memorable

verses.title: Start Characters With Theory
verses.text:
- Define a character by a simple sentence expressing their flawed theory of control over the world.
- Use this core belief to shape how the character acts and reacts throughout the story.

💬 Quote

A character is this kind of theory of control, but how do I control the world?
— Will Storr

Will Storr on defining character by their theory of control

verses.title: Conflict From Flawed Worldview
verses.text:
- Story conflict arises from the clash between a character's flawed worldview and reality.
- Suspense grows as the character's assumptions are challenged and must be reexamined.

💬 Quote

The story really begins when they realize... these two things clash. And then that's when the conflict begins.
— David Perell

David Perell on the origin of conflict in stories

verses.title: Build Plot From Character
verses.text:
- Begin story development by clearly understanding your character before outlining plot.
- Let character drives cause-and-effect in plot development for authenticity and interest.

💬 Quote

If you don't understand your character how do you know what's going to happen?
— Will Storr

Will Storr on the primacy of character in plotting

verses.title: Use Simplicity For Depth
verses.text:
- Start with a simple, defining idea for your character’s beliefs, even just one sentence.
- Test and explore that belief thoroughly to create depth and complexity in your character.

💬 Quote

I promise you that that's how you get the most complex and interesting characters.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on simple character ideas creating complex stories

verses.title: Causality Powers Storytelling
verses.text:
- Storytelling mirrors how humans understand the world through cause and effect.
- Causal chains in plots keep audiences engaged by mimicking natural thought patterns.

💬 Quote

Humans, we see causes and effects where there are no causes and effects.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on causality as the language of the human brain

verses.title: Five-Act Character Change Plan
verses.text:
- Use a five-act structure focused on character change: introduce, challenge, transform, test, and resolve.
- Show character transformation explicitly in the final scenes to satisfy narrative arcs.

💬 Quote

The final scene is the character change so in jaws... he's swimming back and says I'm not scared anymore.
— Will Storr

Will Storr describing his five-act story structure

verses.title: Theory of Control
verses.text:
- Will Storr says that our theories of control orient our lives and provide safety. 
- Stories often show a character's transformation and then test if they can handle the ramifications of that change.

💬 Quote

The thing about a theory of control is it's protective. You know, so those ideas around which we orient our lives somehow protect us.
— Will Storr

Will Storr explaining how a theory of control impacts character development.

verses.title: Story Structure
verses.text:
- Will Storr outlines a five-act story structure, emphasizing character transformation.
- Act four tests if the character can handle the ramifications of change, leading to the final act where they either embrace or reject the transformation.

💬 Quote

Can you take the pain of change? It's like, okay, you've changed, but now the ramifications of that change come in or the, you know, so there's a huge escalation in drama.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on the fourth act of story structure

verses.title: Suspense Via Perfect Conflict
verses.text:
- Suspense arises from testing characters with obstacles perfectly designed to conflict with their beliefs.
- Conflict between desires and reality naturally generates gripping suspense in stories.

💬 Quote

Creating the perfect person for your kind of what if... automatic should automatically be creating suspense.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on how suspense emerges from character conflict

verses.title: Survival, Connection, Status
verses.text:
- Human stories revolve around survival, connection, and status—the three core drives.
- Stories teach us how to gain and maintain these essential aspects of life.

💬 Quote

Survival, connection and status are the three things that all humans want.
— Will Storr

Will Storr explaining the fundamental themes of stories

verses.title: Storytelling Drives
verses.text:
- Survival, connection, and status are the three fundamental drives that all humans want.
- These drives are also the subject of all human stories, teaching us how to live.

💬 Quote

Survival, connection and status are also the subject of all human stories.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on the correlation between human drives and storytelling.

verses.title: Use Drives To Manage Anxiety
verses.text:
- When anxious or depressed, identify whether the issue relates to survival, connection, or status.
- Understanding these drives can help diagnose and address mental health challenges.

💬 Quote

When I'm anxious or depressed I think okay so what's the problem is it survival is it connection or is it status?
— Will Storr

Will Storr on mental well-being and human drives

verses.title: Slow Time At Key Moments
verses.text:
- Slow down and enrich the story during moments of change to increase impact.
- Provide detailed sensory information to signal important turning points in storytelling.

💬 Quote

The slow bits of life should be told fast and the fast bits of life should be told slow.
— Will Storr

Will Storr discussing pacing and information density in stories

verses.title: Plan Stories To Avoid Chaos
verses.text:
- Plan your story and character arcs thoroughly before writing to avoid chaos.
- Embrace craft and structure instead of relying on spontaneous creation.

💬 Quote

I'm very much a planning person... the idea just gives me the shivers.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on the importance of pre-planning stories

verses.title: Literary vs. Commercial Story Arcs
verses.text:
- Literary stories often reflect real-life partial character change, unlike full transformations in commercial stories.
- Using story structures selectively can maintain realism in literary works.

💬 Quote

In literary storytelling... you don't see that huge character transformation so...last sentences there's a hint that he might change.
— Will Storr

Will Storr comparing literary and commercial story structures

verses.title: Stories for Cooperation
verses.text:
- Stories enable cooperation among humans by aligning individual brains towards a shared understanding.
- Shared narratives about values, goals, and identities are what bind groups together.

💬 Quote

In order to get all these individual human brains and wire them up together, they're all thinking in the same way so they can cooperate together, you need a story.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on why humans are storytelling animals.

verses.title: Clarify Goals and Obstacles
verses.text:
- Make goals and obstacles always clear in your storytelling.
- Confusion and boredom often come from unclear character wants and obstacles.

💬 Quote

If you ask yourself is it because I've not clear who wants what and what's standing in the way... that will be why.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on the significance of clear goals and obstacles in stories

verses.title: Nomadland Example
verses.text:
- Will Storr uses Nomadland's opening to demonstrate how obstacles and goals are immediately established without much dialogue. 
- The protagonist's situation clearly presents a survival story, setting the stage for the film's core themes.

💬 Quote

With barely a word spoken we're into the story you know already it's well it's a survival story this one you know so we talk about the big the themes of stories survival connection and status this is about pure fucking survival.
— Will Storr

Will Storr explaining why Nomadland is a successful movie

verses.title: Inner Flaws As Obstacles
verses.text:
- Obstacles in stories often manifest internally as flaws within characters themselves.
- Character growth means overcoming these internal barriers to achieve goals.

💬 Quote

In really good storytelling... one of the major obstacles is something wrong with your personality... you've got to fix something in yourself.
— Will Storr

Will Storr on internal obstacles in character-driven stories

chapters.title: 5 essential questions to craft a winning strategy | Roger Martin (author, advisor, speaker)
chapters.description: Roger Martin, a leading expert on strategy and author of "Playing to Win," shares his insights on crafting effective business strategies. He discusses the five essential questions that drive a winning strategy and highlights why many companies fail. Martin advocates for integrating strategic thinking across all levels, not just top executives. Using examples from brands like Procter & Gamble and Southwest Airlines, he emphasizes the importance of differentiation over low cost and critiques the shortcomings of traditional strategy education.

verses.title: Strategy: Choices Drive Customer Action
verses.text: Strategy is defined as an integrated set of choices that effectively compels desired customer actions. It encompasses various factors that a company can control, implying that the understanding of strategic principles varies widely among experts. There is a recognition of differing perspectives on competition, with some views emphasizing hyper-competition, while others, like those of established figures such as Michael Porter, offer alternative frameworks. This highlights the complexity of strategic thinking in business, where diverse lenses can shape interpretations and applications of strategy.

verses.title: Distinction Drives Growth
verses.text: Products that establish a strong brand identity and differentiation in the market, like Lego, can experience significant growth and dominate their category. In contrast, companies like Vanguard, despite having a massive asset base, do not offer distinctive features beyond cost competitiveness. Customer loyalty may be driven by unique branding rather than just pricing strategies.

verses.title: Beware of Illusions in Strategic Moats
verses.text: Economic moats are critical for competitive advantage, yet identifying them can be challenging. Although categorization systems like the 'seven powers' exist, they may oversimplify the complexity of moats. The existence of variants or additional moats is plausible, though unproven. Even renowned investors like Warren Buffett, who emphasize the importance of these moats, acknowledge the potential for error in their assessments. Success in identifying effective moats is admirable, but one should remain cautious of overconfidence, as all strategies carry the risk of being fleeting or misguided.

verses.title: Beware the Pitfalls of Cost-Leadership
verses.text: Opting for a lower-cost strategy can be a challenging path to success. While cost leadership is a valid approach, it typically requires dominant scale in the relevant market for effectiveness. Pursuing niche cost leadership is particularly problematic and seldom leads to success. Leaders must be aware of the implications of choosing such strategies, as achieving a competitive edge at lower costs is rare without significant resources and market presence.

verses.title: Embrace Change, Don't Fight the Tide
verses.text: Honesty and integrity are crucial for businesses aiming to succeed amidst challenges. Companies must adapt to customer preferences and market shifts rather than resist them, akin to building sand castles that inevitably succumb to the tide. While initial efforts may seem fruitful, sustainability requires vigilance in understanding where consumers are heading. Acknowledging and anticipating these shifts is essential for long-term survival and success in the marketplace.

verses.title: Identify the Painful Gap
verses.text: Strategy serves as a problem-solving tool to address the gap between current outcomes and desired outcomes. This gap highlights the disconnect between present results, which stem from past choices and competitive dynamics, and future aspirations. To effect change, one must recognize that improving outcomes requires different decisions than those previously made. Concentrating on the most significant gap, such as shifts in customer behavior or resource access, is crucial for effective strategic action.

chapters.title: A better way to plan, build, and ship products | Ryan Singer (creator of “Shape Up,” early employee at 37signals)
chapters.description: Ryan Singer, former Head of Strategy at 37signals and author of "Shape Up," shares his innovative approach to product development. He critiques traditional Agile methods for leading to endless cycles and introduces an appetite-driven strategy that sets fixed timelines. Singer emphasizes effective collaboration through shaping sessions and flexible team structures to clarify project goals. He highlights techniques like fat marker sketching to enhance communication and align engineering with design, making product development more efficient and empowering teams to thrive.

verses.title: Shape Up's Origin
verses.text:
- Ryan Singer initially didn't realize the broader applicability of Shape Up beyond Basecamp's unique environment. 
- After the book's release, he discovered that various companies faced similar struggles and adapted the methodology.

💬 Quote

It was only after the book came out that it gave me this kind of excuse to start talking to people from all kinds of different companies. And it's been really interesting.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on how he realized that Shape Up can be used by other companies other than Basecamp

verses.title: Basecamp's Early Days
verses.text:
- Ryan Singer, Jason Fried, and David Heinemeier Hansson (DHH) built the first Basecamp with a small, intensely collaborative team. 
- DHH initially worked part-time, coding only 10 hours weekly, which shaped their efficient product development.

💬 Quote

we were a team of three. And we were, I mean, I think it's for any really small team, when you're just starting out, you don't need a process.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on Basecamp's early days.

verses.title: Constraints and Urgency
verses.text:
- DHH only had 10 hours a week to code, which forced the team to maximize efficiency. 
- Jason Fried, already known for urgency, compounded this focus on shipping quickly and iterating.

💬 Quote

Jason has this, I mean, I think many really successful founders and especially CEOs have this thing. It's like, all they want to see is movement.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on Jason Fried's work ethic.

verses.title: Shaping Sessions' Origin
verses.text:
- Early Basecamp used short, intense shaping sessions to define product ideas. 
- These sessions involved collaborative brainstorming and quick sketches to get David's buy-in and avoid wasted development time.

💬 Quote

those sessions with Jason, they were these short, very, very intense sessions where you're trying to crack the nut together.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on early shaping sessions.

verses.title: Shape Up's Core
verses.text:
- Shape Up prioritizes finishing by defining fixed time boxes and flexible scope. 
- It aims to avoid endless sprints and ensure a sense of completion, mirroring the startup ethos of rapid iteration.

💬 Quote

we are not going to start something unless we can see the end from the beginning. We're not going to take a big concept and then say, what's the estimate for this thing?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on Shape Up's core principles.

verses.title: See the End from the Beginning
verses.text:
- Don't start something unless you can see the end from the beginning. Avoid estimating a big concept, and instead, work backward from your appetite for it.

💬 Quote

We are not going to start something unless we can see the end from the beginning.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the first core ingredient of the ShapeUp method

verses.title: Incremental Adoption
verses.text:
- Adopt Shape Up incrementally, addressing specific team problems. 
- Start with six-week cycles, but adjust based on project needs; shorter cycles suit growth teams, while longer ones suit feature work.

💬 Quote

You don't need to do, yeah, any of it. So this is where we kind of, it helps to look at like, what's going wrong and what are we trying to fix?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on adopting Shape Up piecemeal.

verses.title: Shape Up Clarity
verses.text:
- When adopting the Shape Up framework, ensure the team has clarity on the problem to solve so they can make informed decisions. 
- Avoid assigning giant projects with unrealistic expectations, as this sets the team up for failure.

💬 Quote

We can't just take any project, no matter how giant it is, and then throw it at a team and say, figure it out and ship something meaningful in six weeks by cutting away scope, right?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on how to avoid misusing the Shape Up framework

verses.title: Six Weeks as a Guideline
verses.text:
- Six weeks is a maximum timebox, not a requirement, designed to encourage better scope management. 
-  It allows teams to uncover complexity early and doesn't preclude shorter cycles for smaller projects.

💬 Quote

the six-week is only a maximum. And that's really where this number does some work for us.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on six-week timeboxes.

verses.title: Setting Project Timelines
verses.text:
Set a maximum timeline for projects, like six weeks, to help teams identify and manage complexities effectively. This approach encourages clearer decision-making and reveals unknowns before they become issues.

💬 Quote

If we set a ceiling at six weeks, we have a much better chance of [...] reveal that complexity before we're.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the advantages of maximum timelines in project planning.

verses.title: Appetite Adjustments
verses.text:
- Commit to the appetite, but don't rigidly adhere if problems surface late. 
-  Instead of extending, return to shaping, refine the project, and renegotiate scope if needed.

💬 Quote

in real life, if you make a commitment and you get to that end of that six weeks and something is going wrong, [...] we can't just cut off what we agreed to that made this thing valuable.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on adjusting project scope.

verses.title: Re-Shape, Don't Re-Invest
verses.text:
- When projects are off track after six weeks, avoid reinvesting without understanding why. 
- Instead, move back into shaping mode to clarify uncertainties.

💬 Quote

We're not going to keep reinvesting in something that we don't understand. So let's take this out of build mode and bring this back into shaping mode.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on what to do if a project is not on track after six weeks

verses.title: The Shaping Step
verses.text:
- If you run out of time on real product work, don't just cut the scope. Instead, figure out the main moving pieces and narrow down your understanding of the problem to ensure the feature works.
- Get to a level where you can identify specific steps and connections to make the engine turn, indicating that the feature is well-shaped.

💬 Quote

When it comes to real product work, [...] we can't just cut the scope if we run out of time. So what it means is that the shaping work is really working hard together to figure out what are the main moving pieces of this thing, right?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the importance of the shaping step in product development

verses.title: Shaping Session Output
verses.text:
- Shaping sessions produce wireframes/sketches with key flows and components. 
- This shared understanding helps engineers build effectively without overly detailed specifications or vague user stories.

💬 Quote

What we need to do in a shaping session when it's going well is we come out with some kind of drawing or diagram where engineers, product, and design are all looking at that and they're saying, we understand that.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the purpose of shaping sessions

verses.title: Dangers of Detailed Shaping
verses.text:
- Avoid detailed solutioning without involving engineering, as it often leads to painful reality checks.
- Engineering needs to understand the logic and flow behind the UI, which isn't always apparent on the surface.

💬 Quote

When there's a lot of solutioning all the way down to detail without engineering involved, usually that's a painful recipe.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on what shaping is not.

verses.title: Effective Shaping Sessions
verses.text:
- Aim for a drawing or diagram from shaping sessions that engineers, product, and design all understand. 
- Doing so means everyone knows what to build and prevents issues discovered only when engineers get involved.

💬 Quote

What we need to do in a shaping session when it's going well is we come out with some kind of drawing or diagram where engineers, product, and design are all looking at that and they're saying, we understand that.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on what makes a good shaping session.

verses.title: Hidden Complexity
verses.text:
- A fintech team simplified onboarding by using partner bank data, improving conversion. 
-  However, they missed existing code branches, highlighting the need for technical expertise in shaping.

💬 Quote

it all sounds so great and simple. And then you get into the weeds and you realize like, oh, wait a minute.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on unforeseen problems during development.

verses.title: Effective Shaping Session
verses.text:
- Shaping sessions require focused, real-time collaboration, not just document sharing. 
- Encourage fast idea generation, explore multiple approaches, and don't get bogged down in single solutions prematurely.

💬 Quote

people aren't used to working so fast, you know? To like, what are we actually doing right now?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the pace of shaping sessions.

verses.title: Break Ideas
verses.text:
- In shaping sessions, narrow down the problem and try out different ideas.
- Then, try to break them from technical and product perspectives to ensure value and feasibility.

💬 Quote

We have to try to break them. So I want to draw an idea and then I want the technical person to find like, oh, this isn't going to, you know what I mean?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on shaping sessions

verses.title: Effective Shaping Sessions
verses.text:
- Use breadboarding and fat marker sketching to express ideas clearly in detail during shaping sessions.
- The goal is to provide enough detail that a technical person knows exactly what to build.

💬 Quote

It's shaped if we can give this to a technical person and they say, yeah, I know what to go build now.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the output of a shaping session

verses.title: Pilot Project
verses.text:
- Start Shape Up with a pilot project of a manageable size (3-6 weeks). 
-  Choose a meaningful problem the team cares about and align on a timeframe where engineers are available.

💬 Quote

we're going to try a pilot project. And what we want to do is, as you said, kind of choose a problem that's important enough to all of us that we think it's meaningful.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on starting with a pilot project.

verses.title: Effective Kickoff in Shape Up
verses.text:
In a Shape Up world, a kickoff means presenting a well-shaped idea, allowing the team to start the time box with autonomy. This approach empowers skilled professionals to define their own tasks.

💬 Quote

In the shape-up world, you have a single idea that was shaped. This is the thing we shaped, right? [...] Go make your own tasks because you're the professionals.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the differences between Scrum and Shape Up kickoffs.

verses.title: Nine Boxes Exercise
verses.text:
- Ryan Singer suggests a kickoff exercise: divide the shaped idea into nine major implementation chunks. 
- If a six-week time box is used, each chunk should take around four days to implement.

💬 Quote

Take whatever was shaped and just draw a grid with nine boxes. And give me nine boxes of the nine major chunks that you think have to get implemented from an implementation standpoint.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on a helpful kickoff exercise for teams that are not super senior.

verses.title: When to Change
verses.text:
- Try something new when current processes repeatedly fail to deliver. 
- Signs include dragging projects, constant questioning, unclear problem definition, and lack of progress despite effort.

💬 Quote

wait until it hurts more. [...] if there's a good reason to do it, and it's like, look, we've done it the old way. We've tried different experiments.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on recognizing the need for change.

verses.title: Feature Factory Reframe
verses.text:
- The 'feature factory' critique often suggests a lack of focus on value and outcomes, leading to bloat and unused features.
- Ryan Singer argues that a functional 'feature factory' is healthy; it just needs better input to drive meaningful output.

💬 Quote

If you have a feature factory, meaning you're continually cranking out features, you're probably quite healthy. All you need to do is feed a different input to the beginning of the factory, right?
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the value of feature factories with the right input.

verses.title: Basecamp's Uniqueness
verses.text:
- Basecamp's unique structure, like designers who code and founders actively involved in product definition, impacted Shape Up. 
- This close collaboration and lack of external pressures created an environment unlike most companies.

💬 Quote

I had no idea how unique we were until I was outside.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on Basecamp's distinct environment.

verses.title: Addressing Root Causes
verses.text:
- Investigate the root cause of project delays. 
- If clarity is lacking, use Bob Mesta's "jobs to be done" framework to understand customer needs before shaping solutions.

💬 Quote

sometimes people reach out to me because their projects aren't shipping. [...] But the root cause is actually that the input at the very beginning of the process is too unclear.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the importance of problem definition.

verses.title: Demand Side Sales
verses.text:
- Read 'Demand Side Sales 101' to deeply understand customer struggles. Ryan Singer says it helps product people understand what people are really trying to solve.

💬 Quote

It's such a good dive into what are people really trying to solve and, and getting into that mindset of what's the struggle, what's the problem.
— Ryan Singer

Ryan Singer on the value of the book 'Demand Side Sales 101'.

chapters.title: AI prompt engineering in 2025: What works and what doesn’t | Sander Schulhoff (Learn Prompting, HackAPrompt)
chapters.description: Sander Schulhoff, the pioneer of prompt engineering and partner with OpenAI for HackAPrompt, shares his extensive insights into AI's future. He discusses the five most effective prompting techniques and why traditional methods like role prompting are losing effectiveness. Sander reveals real jailbreak tactics still fooling top models and stresses the importance of defending against prompt injection. He also highlights the rising security threat posed by AI agents and provides guidance on getting started in AI red teaming.

verses.title: Prompt Engineering Still Vital
verses.text:
- Prompt engineering remains crucial as good prompts can increase task accuracy from 0% to 90%.  
- Despite predictions of its demise, it continues to be essential with each new model release.

💬 Quote

Is prompt engineering a thing you need to spend your time on? Studies have shown that using bad prompts can get you down to like 0% on a problem, and good prompts can boost you up to 90%.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on the importance of prompt engineering

verses.title: Medical Coding Prompt Success
verses.text:
- Sander increased GPT-4 medical coding accuracy by 70% using examples with explanations in prompts.  
- This practical example highlights prompt engineering's massive impact on domain-specific tasks.

💬 Quote

At the beginning, I was getting little to no accuracy. I attached reasonings to examples and boosted accuracy by 70%.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on boosting GPT-4 accuracy with prompt engineering

verses.title: Use Few-Shot Prompting
verses.text:
- Use few-shot prompting by giving several examples of desired outputs to improve model responses.  
- Choose a common and clear format like Q&A or XML for your examples to ensure better results.

💬 Quote

My best advice on how to improve your prompting skills is actually just trial and error. But if there were one technique that I could recommend people, it is few-shot prompting, which is just giving the AI examples of what you want it to do.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on few-shot prompting

verses.title: Role Prompting
verses.text:
- For tasks focusing on accuracy, avoid using role prompting, as it no longer improves performance.
- Use roles for expressive tasks like writing and summarizing, where style is more important.

💬 Quote

But giving a role really helps for expressive tasks, writing tasks, summarizing tasks. And so with those things where it's more about style, that's a great, great place to use roles.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on role prompting.

verses.title: Decompose Complex Tasks
verses.text:
- Use decomposition: ask the model to list subproblems before solving a complex task step-by-step.  
- Iterate by solving subproblems individually to improve overall problem-solving accuracy.

💬 Quote

Decomposition, the core idea is that there's some task you want the model to do. Instead, you give it this task and say, hey, don't answer this, before answering, tell me sub problems you'd solve first.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on decomposition technique

verses.title: Prompt Decomposition
verses.text:
- Use decomposition in prompt engineering by breaking down tasks into sub-problems.
- Have the model list sub-problems first, solve them individually, and then use the results to solve the main problem.

💬 Quote

So decomposition is another really, really effective technique.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff introducing a new prompt engineering technique.

verses.title: Use Self-Criticism Technique
verses.text:
- Implement self-criticism by having the model critique and then improve its own responses.  
- Repeat one to three times to boost output quality but avoid infinite loops to prevent confusion.

💬 Quote

You ask the LM to solve some problem. It does it, then you ask it, can you go back and check your response, reflect, and then implement the advice.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on self-criticism prompting technique

verses.title: Provide Additional Context
verses.text:
- Provide ample additional information or context about the task to the model to improve performance.  
- Place context at the beginning of prompts for caching benefits and to maintain task focus.

💬 Quote

Including context or additional information about the situation was super, super important to get a performant prompt. I usually put it at the beginning of the prompt.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on adding additional information in prompts

verses.title: Additional Information as Context
verses.text:
- Provide additional information to the model to help it understand the problem and give it context.
- This is useful to help the model give better responses.

💬 Quote

What other additional information can you give it that might tell it more might help it understand this problem more and give it context, essentially?
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky explaining additional information as a prompt engineering technique.

verses.title: Ensemble Multiple Prompts
verses.text:
- Use ensembling by sending multiple differently prompted queries to the model for the same problem.  
- Aggregate results by majority vote to improve reliability and accuracy.

💬 Quote

Ensembling techniques take a problem and use multiple different prompts to solve it, then take the most common answer as the final response.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on ensembling prompting techniques

verses.title: Still Use Chain of Thought Prompts
verses.text:
- Despite advanced reasoning models, explicitly prompt "think step by step" for consistent reasoning output.  
- This ensures thorough reasoning, especially when processing large datasets or many queries.

💬 Quote

Even if models often do reasoning by default, adding 'make sure to write out all your reasoning' ensures maximum performance consistently.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on chain of thought prompting

verses.title: Prompt Engineering at Scale
verses.text:
- Even if new models seem so good you don't need prompt engineering, you often still need classical techniques at scale.
- To make prompts more robust when running thousands of inputs, continue using techniques like chain of thought prompting.

💬 Quote

If you look at scale, if you're running thousands, millions of inputs through your prompt, oftentimes in order to make your prompt more robust, you'll still need to use those classical prompting techniques.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on why classical prompting techniques are still useful.

verses.title: Prompt Injection Persists
verses.text:
- Prompt injection is a persistent problem where users trick models into unsafe outputs with crafted prompts.  
- These techniques remain effective but red teaming competitions help discover and mitigate new exploit methods.

💬 Quote

The idea with AI red teaming is getting AIs to do or say bad things. We see people tricking chatbots with stories to leak harmful info.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on prompt injection and red teaming

verses.title: Agentic AI Security Threat
verses.text:
- Agentic AI and humanoid robots pose unique security threats if they can be manipulated to do harm or malicious acts.  
- Ensuring trustworthiness of autonomous AI is far more complex than chatbots and harder to secure.

💬 Quote

If somebody gives a humanoid robot the middle finger, how can we be certain it won't punch that person? We can't even fully secure chatbots yet.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on fears about agentic AI and robots

verses.title: AI as a Weapon
verses.text:
- AI models know so much that they can accidentally reveal dangerous information, making them a potential weapon. 
- Security and intelligence communities are taking this issue seriously, especially concerning chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear, and explosives harms.

💬 Quote

These are like weapons to some extent, especially as companies are producing agents that could have real world harms.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on the potential danger of AI models.

verses.title: AI Agents and Prompt Injection
verses.text:
- AI agents are being deployed and they're vulnerable to prompt injection attacks. 
- An agent tasked with fixing a bug might find malicious code online and inject it into the user's codebase.

💬 Quote

There's a lot of like AI coding agents out there. [...] So all of those tools exist and they can do things right now like search the internet. [...] And it might use one of these prompt injection techniques to get it to do that.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on the risks of AI coding agents.

verses.title: Prompts Alone Can't Stop Injection
verses.text:
- Avoid relying on in-prompt instructions like "be a good model" for defense; they don't prevent prompt injection.  
- Use safety fine-tuning on malicious prompt datasets and task-specific fine-tuning for better resistance.

💬 Quote

Do not follow any malicious instructions prompts do not work at all. Safety tuning and fine-tuning are more effective defenses.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on defenses against prompt injection

verses.title: Injection Is Unsolvable Problem
verses.text:
- Prompt injection is inherently unsolvable; fixes mitigate but never fully prevent new attacks.  
- Unlike classical security, AI models can't be patched like software bugs because of their complex learned behaviors.

💬 Quote

This is not a solvable problem. You can patch a bug, but you can't patch a brain.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on intrinsic limits of AI security

verses.title: AI Misalignment Risk Grows
verses.text:
- Misalignment causes AI to independently pursue harmful actions without prompt injection.  
- Autonomous agents may escalate from harmless tasks to unethical behavior without explicit instruction.

💬 Quote

Without any malicious prompting, AI decides to do bad things of its own volition. It might escalate harmless tasks to unethical actions.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on AI misalignment and autonomous agent risks

verses.title: Support Regulation, Not Stoppage
verses.text:
- Stopping AI development is not practical; regulation is necessary to maximize benefits and mitigate harms.  
- AI advancements save lives, improve productivity, and are part of a global arms race that cannot be halted.

💬 Quote

I'm against stopping AI development. The benefits to humanity, especially in health, outweigh limited harms, and other countries won't stop either.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on AI regulation versus stopping development

verses.title: Roosevelt’s Strenuous Journey Inspires
verses.text:
- Theodore Roosevelt's River of Doubt expedition showed immense mental fortitude through extreme hardship.  
- The journey inspired Sander and reflects his respect for persistence and resilience.

💬 Quote

He would walk in a straight line through trees, rivers, and cliffs. Half his party died, showing mental fortitude.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on Theodore Roosevelt's River of Doubt book

verses.title: Value Persistence
verses.text:
- Sander Schulhoff says that persistence is the only thing that truly matters.
- He admits he's not good at everything, but he persists and works on the same bug for months until solving it.

💬 Quote

My main one is that persistence is the only thing that matters. I don't consider myself to be particularly good at many things.
— Sander Schulhoff

Sander Schulhoff on his life motto.

chapters.title: Behind the product: NotebookLM | Raiza Martin (Senior Product Manager, AI @ Google Labs)
chapters.description: Raiza Martin, Senior Product Manager for AI at Google Labs, leads the innovative NotebookLM project. She dives into its origin as a 20% project and how it evolved into a popular tool with a vibrant community. Raiza discusses the unique dynamics of Google Labs, future enhancements for NotebookLM, and the engaging new feature, 'Audio Overviews.' The conversation also touches on navigating the ethical challenges of AI and the excitement of collaborating with author Steven Johnson, making for a compelling exploration of tech and creativity.

verses.title: Surprising NotebookLM Use Cases
verses.text:
- Googlers use NotebookLM's audio overview feature to boost their confidence before performance reviews.
- People also use it to create positive audio summaries of their resumes.

💬 Quote

The amount of Googlers that ping me that I don't even know, and they're like, wow, this was such a boost to my confidence just to upload [...] my own check-in notes and then have it generate an audio overview. It's like people are going into meetings feeling really good about themselves.
— Raiza Martin

Raiza Martin on surprising use cases for NotebookLM.

chapters.title: Building Lovable: $10M ARR in 60 days with 15 people | Anton Osika (co-founder and CEO)
chapters.description: Anton Osika, Co-founder and CEO of Lovable, reveals how his AI-driven startup achieved a staggering $10M ARR in just 60 days with a lean team of 15. He demonstrates the platform’s potential by creating an Airbnb clone in just 30 seconds. Anton discusses the unique hiring strategies that fueled their rapid growth and the vital skills product teams will need in the AI era. He also shares insights on how Lovable simplifies product development for non-coders, ultimately reshaping the future of technology accessibility.

verses.title: Mastering Lovable
verses.text:
- Use Lovable's chat mode to understand how software engineering works.
- Be patient and curious, ask questions if you're stuck.

💬 Quote

It takes a lot to master using tools like Lovable and being very curious and patient. [...] It's the best way to be productive. It's also one of the best ways to just learn about how software engineering works, which is you don't have to write the code anymore, but it is useful to understand how software or how building products works.
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on tips for mastering Lovable.

verses.title: Lovable Origin Story
verses.text:
- Anton built GPT Engineer, an open-source tool demonstrating AI's ability to create applications from prompts.
- GPT Engineer's popularity on GitHub led to its temporary shutdown due to server overload.

💬 Quote

I created an open source tool called GPT Engineer, where you write something like create a snake game, and then it spits out a lot of code, a lot of different files, and opens then the snake game. And then I tweeted a video about that. And GPT Engineer is to date the most popular open source tool to showcase the ability for large language models to create applications.
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on how Lovable got started

verses.title: AI's Impact on Labor
verses.text:
- AI's ability to automate cognitive labor surpasses manual labor automation in impact.
- Lovable aims to empower non-coders to bring their software ideas to life.

💬 Quote

I think I was not impressed by what people were doing with the large language models [...]. But now it's actually cognitive labor being done better than humans by machines.
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on the significance of AI-powered software development

verses.title: Hiring at Lovable
verses.text:
- Look for obsessive candidates who deeply care about the product, users, and the team.
- Prioritize generalist skills alongside a strong superpower in a specific area like AI.

💬 Quote

I think the most important thing is that people care a lot and they're not just like oh i'm here for a job i'm here for being as a passenger on this journey. But everyone should really care about the product, the users, and care a ton about the team, how the team works together, and that you're always contributing to making the team work more productively together.
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on what he looks for when hiring

verses.title: Building in Europe
verses.text:
- Anton finds that ambition levels are generally lower in Sweden than in the U.S.
- Lovable's high ambition combined with available talent creates a unique advantage.

💬 Quote

This ambition level that you're talking about in the job ad is more uncommon in Sweden. And I think that is the, like the biggest unlock that someone like me, who sees that this is the, like the time in human history when you have the most impact per work hour.
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on building Lovable in Europe

verses.title: Prioritization at Lovable
verses.text:
- Identify and solve the biggest product problem iteratively.
- Talk to users, read feedback, and stay engineering-led to understand technical constraints.

💬 Quote

I just top line i think identifying what is the biggest bottleneck what's the biggest product problem and iterating or fast on saying okay this is the biggest problem let's really really solve that problem and then picking the next one
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on how to prioritize

verses.title: Sana Labs Failure
verses.text:
- Sana Labs, Anton's previous company, struggled with integrating personalized learning AI into existing products.
- This experience taught him the importance of designing end-to-end user experience first, then incorporating AI.

💬 Quote

The big learning here is, it didn't work very well for the company. [...] The big learning is that you have to start with like, how is this product working end to end? And then add ai or think where should we add ai [...]
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on a learning experience

verses.title: Becoming Proficient with AI
verses.text:
-  Spend a full week using AI tools to solve a real problem to become highly proficient.
-  Surrounding yourself with others obsessed with AI accelerates learning.

💬 Quote

if you spend a full week on trying to reach an outcome the best way to learn is like i want to do this thing and then i want to use ai to do that thing uh and you've spent a full week you're you're in the top one percent in the global population
— Anton Osika

Anton Osika on how to become proficient in using AI

chapters.title: From ChatGPT to Instagram to Uber: The quiet architect behind the world’s most popular products | Peter Deng
chapters.description: Peter Deng, who has shaped products at OpenAI, Instagram, and Uber, shares insights on building tech used by billions. He discusses a one-sentence test for hiring top talent and the idea that innovative products often don’t require groundbreaking tech. Deng explores the five archetypes of product managers, emphasizing team dynamics in crafting successful products. He also highlights the balance needed between short- and long-term goals, while underscoring the importance of effective communication and data workflows in scaling businesses.

verses.title: AGI Requires Human Hustle
verses.text:
- AGI won't solve all problems; builders must add hustle and effort to harness its potential.
- Humans will co-evolve with AI, gradually mastering and adapting to new technology.

💬 Quote

AGI is just necessary, but not sufficient. A lot of the value is still going to require a bunch of hustle from a lot of builders to really turn that new source of energy and channel it into something that we humans want to use that solves some of our problems.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on artificial general intelligence (AGI)

verses.title: Language Shapes Leadership Thought
verses.text:
- Language shapes thought and is critical in product leadership and communication.
- Choosing words carefully in documents prevents misinterpretation and creates downstream positive effects.

💬 Quote

Language actually affects the way you think...I really obsess about the words I choose because there’s a multiplicative and downstream effect for using the wrong word.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on the power of language in leadership

verses.title: Product Isn't Always the Key
verses.text:
- Sometimes the product UI doesn’t matter as much as pricing or ETA, like at Uber.
- Many tech giants succeed through relentless hard work on known tech, not breakthroughs.

💬 Quote

Sometimes your product actually doesn't matter...at Uber, really the price and the ETA was the product.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on Uber's product insights

verses.title: Product Doesn't Matter
verses.text:
- Peter Deng learned at Uber that the core value proposition (price and ETA) often outweighs the importance of the digital product itself. 
- People consume the entirety of the product, and sometimes fixing a bug won't have as much of an impact as price or ETA.

💬 Quote

Sometimes your product actually doesn't matter. And by product, I mean, sort of the pixels you put on the screen or things that you build in your mobile app.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on a counterintuitive lesson he learned at Uber

verses.title: Build Data Flywheels & Workflows
verses.text:
- Build proprietary data flywheels to create defensibility for AI startups.
- Design products that integrate well into specific workflows and deliver superior user experiences.

💬 Quote

Having the right data and data flywheels is so important...workflow—that is going to be more and more important.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on AI startup success factors

verses.title: Plan Ahead, Build Growth Teams
verses.text:
- Scale by planning chess moves ahead and building sustainable systems.
- Use growth teams early to rigorously instrument and analyze product metrics.

💬 Quote

You have to plan your chess moves out in advance...Building a growth team...is a simple razor to uncover what’s really happening in your product.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on scaling product growth

verses.title: Build Teams Like Avengers
verses.text:
- Build product teams as 'Avengers' with members having complementary superpowers.
- Hire people motivated by different focuses to create a healthy tension and balance in your team.

💬 Quote

I helped build teams that are the most successful that have very different superpowers, creating a healthy tension.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on building high-performing product teams

verses.title: Know Your PM Archetypes
verses.text:
- Recognize five PM archetypes: consumer, growth, business, platform, and research PMs.
- Identify each person's primary and secondary archetype to balance team strengths.

💬 Quote

There are five types of product managers...everyone has a primary and secondary archetype that keeps them balanced.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on PM archetype framework

verses.title: Hire for 6-Month Autonomy
verses.text:
- Hire people who become autonomous within six months; if not, hire the wrong person.
- Set this expectation early to create accountability and growth opportunities.

💬 Quote

In six months, if I’m telling you what to do, I’ve hired the wrong person.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on hiring autonomous talent

verses.title: Treat Your Team as a Product
verses.text:
- Be mindful of putting your team together, and treat it as a product.
- Peter Deng optimizes for hiring people who, in six months, will not need to be told what to do.

💬 Quote

Really be mindful of putting your team together and think about that as a product. And you have to really craft that. You have to really care about the team.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on building the right team

verses.title: Interview for Growth Mindset
verses.text:
- Assess growth mindset by asking candidates about a painful mistake and what they learned.
- This approach reveals authenticity and sets a learning tone early in relationships.

💬 Quote

Think about one of the biggest mistakes you’ve made...tell me how you think differently now and work differently as a result.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on assessing growth mindset in interviews

verses.title: Master Managing Up With Repetition
verses.text:
- Use "say you’ll do the thing, do the thing, say you did the thing" for clear communication.
- This repetition manages expectations and ensures alignment with managers.

💬 Quote

Say you’re going to do the thing, say that you’re doing the thing, and then say that you did it.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on managing up effectively

verses.title: Creating New Roles at OpenAI
verses.text:
- At OpenAI, Peter helped create a new 'model designer' role to match a unique skill set.
- He enabled Joanne to craft a role leveraging her technical and product taste strengths.

💬 Quote

She wrote up a job description...we created this role and had her lead it.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on empowering unique talents

verses.title: Balance Craft With Impact
verses.text:
- Successful product managers obsess over craft but know which details matter.
- Focusing on what truly matters, like user peace of mind, drives impactful products.

💬 Quote

You have to obsess about the details of craft while having the wisdom of which details don’t actually matter.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on successful product skills

verses.title: Driving Uber for User Insight
verses.text:
- Peter leased a car and drove for Uber to deeply understand user problems.
- This immersive research influenced product decisions on pickups and global scaling.

💬 Quote

I leased a car, put an Uber sticker on it, and just started driving to learn firsthand.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on user empathy and research

verses.title: IDEO's Design Thinking Framework
verses.text:
- Peter Deng explains IDEO's design thinking framework: empathize, define, ideate, prototype, and test.
- He emphasizes the importance of truly feeling the pain of your customers and not just theoretically understanding problems.

💬 Quote

The framework that really stuck with me is what IDEO preached, which was like there are five stages to great design thinking. Number one is empathize. Two is to define. Three is to ideate. Four is to prototype. And five is to test.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on the framework that he learned at the design school at Stanford.

verses.title: Empathize Deeply in Design
verses.text:
- Emphasize empathizing and defining user problems deeply in design thinking.
- Immersing in user interviews without shortcuts captures full contexts and pain points.

💬 Quote

You have to be in the room, fully immersed, no phones, just actually hearing the words and the intonation.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on design thinking and empathy

verses.title: Work Aligned with Human Needs
verses.text:
- Choose roles aligned with human behavior and fundamental needs for greater impact.
- Optimize for learning opportunities when deciding where to work or stay.

💬 Quote

The team really got the fundamental human desires to connect...the more you match that, the more successful you’ll be.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on career choices and human alignment

verses.title: Learning From Instagram Bolt Failure
verses.text:
- Instagram's Bolt app aimed to reduce pressure to share but failed to retain users.
- The team treated this as a lesson, reusing tech and improving their approach.

💬 Quote

You can’t predict what’s going to hit...failure ain’t a loss, it’s a lesson.
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on a product failure experience

verses.title: Founders: Show Unique Insights
verses.text:
- Early stage founders with unique data and well-crafted workflows attract Peter's investment.
- Show strong insights into what truly matters and scale thoughtfully over time.

💬 Quote

Do you have unique data and a data flywheel? Do you have a crafted workflow you really get after?
— Peter Deng

Peter Deng on startup investing criteria

chapters.title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more
chapters.description: Bret Taylor, co-founder and CEO of Sierra, has a storied tech career including roles as CTO of Meta and co-CEO of Salesforce. He shares how a critical product review almost derailed his Google career, leading to the creation of Google Maps. Taylor discusses transformative AI agents poised to replace traditional SaaS products, innovative outcome-based pricing models, and lessons from early career missteps. He also reflects on the importance of coding skills in an AI-driven future and the vital question he learned from Sheryl Sandberg that maximizes daily impact.

verses.title: From Failure to Google Maps
verses.text:
- Bret Taylor's early Google Local product was a disappointed attempt to digitize Yellow Pages.
- This failure led to the creation of Google Maps, which launched with 10 million users on day one, revolutionizing local search.

💬 Quote

We launched the first version of that product... it was a me too version of Yahoo Yellow Pages... after a tough review, we rebuilt it leading to Google Maps with 10 million users on day one.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on his early Google Local product failure leading to Google Maps

verses.title: Flexible Roles and Daily Impact
verses.text:
- Adopt a flexible identity and embrace roles required by your company's stage.
- Follow Sheryl Sandberg's advice: each morning ask, "What’s the most impactful thing I can do today?"

💬 Quote

I had a moment where Sheryl Sandberg told me to focus on what’s most impactful today, and when I reframed my role that way I became better at my job and I liked it more.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on mindset changes inspired by Sheryl Sandberg's advice

verses.title: Question Your Product Assumptions
verses.text:
- Question commonly accepted reasons for your product's failures to avoid false narratives.
- Seek diverse perspectives and honest feedback to ensure you address the real problem.

💬 Quote

One danger for founders is incorrect storytelling like ‘People don't like my product because of X.’ If you orient your strategy around the wrong problem, your company will fail.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on avoiding false narratives about product problems

verses.title: FriendFeed’s Lessons from Twitter
verses.text:
- FriendFeed, founded by Taylor, invented the Like button and real-time newsfeed but failed commercially against Twitter.
- They lacked the right distribution strategy and external advice, which was the key shortcoming.

💬 Quote

We had 12 engineers building product but lost to Twitter, which focused on celebrity endorsements and distribution while we only polished product.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor recounting FriendFeed’s failure to compete with Twitter

verses.title: How to Choose Good Advice
verses.text:
- Evaluate advice by probing for the reasoning behind it; avoid blindly following confident opinions.
- Ask "who else should I talk to?" to identify credible advisors and develop your own judgment.

💬 Quote

When you ask for advice, don't just ask what to do, but why, like an obnoxious two-year kid, to understand the framework behind the advice.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on evaluating quality advice with critical questioning

verses.title: Computer Science in AI Era
verses.text:
- Studying computer science remains critical to learn systems thinking beyond coding.
- AI will transform coding into operating code-generating machines, requiring deep understanding of software complexity and systems.

💬 Quote

Operating a code-generating machine requires systems thinking, and computer science is a wonderful major to learn systems thinking.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on the importance of computer science understanding in the future of coding

verses.title: Future of Programming Systems
verses.text:
- Coding will shift from human ergonomics toward verification and robustness in AI-generated code.
- Future programming systems will emphasize safe, verifiable code to empower humans managing AI outputs.

💬 Quote

We need to enable humans to have as much leverage as possible using computers to do work on their behalf with verification and formal correctness.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on the evolution of programming languages and AI code generation

verses.title: Teaching Kids AI Integration
verses.text:
- Encourage children to integrate AI tools like ChatGPT early for personalized learning.
- Recognize this shift requires educators to adapt tests and teaching methods to AI's capabilities.

💬 Quote

I’m encouraging my kids to make AI part of their lives and to use it as a personalized tutor that teaches in their best learning style.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on how he teaches his children to use AI tools like ChatGPT

verses.title: AI Market Segments & Agents
verses.text:
- AI market segments include frontier models, tooling, and applied AI agents.
- Agents will become the new software with a focus on business outcomes rather than technical details.

💬 Quote

I think agent is the new app. The market will focus on autonomous AI agents delivering measurable business outcomes.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor explaining the segmentation and future focus of the AI market

verses.title: Applied AI: Agents as the New Apps
verses.text:
- Bret Taylor believes the applied AI market will play out for companies who build agents.
- He considers agents as the new app, representing the product form factor.

💬 Quote

I think agent is the new app.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on the future of AI and agents

verses.title: AI Agents Transform Productivity
verses.text:
- AI agents will enable autonomous job completion, driving the next big wave of productivity gains.
- This shift will transform software pricing toward outcomes-based models aligned with actual value.

💬 Quote

Agents accomplish jobs autonomously, making productivity gains measurable, driving the whole market toward outcomes-based pricing.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor on AI agents' impact on productivity and software business models

verses.title: Outcome-Based Pricing Benefits
verses.text:
- Use outcome-based pricing by charging based on measurable customer success.
- This model aligns vendor incentives with customer outcomes, building true partnerships and driving value creation.

💬 Quote

At Sierra, if the AI agent solves the customer's problem, we get paid a pre-negotiated rate, aligning our business model with our customers'.
— Bret Taylor

Bret Taylor describing Sierra’s outcome-based pricing strategy

chapters.title: How Netflix builds a culture of excellence | Elizabeth Stone (CTO)
chapters.description: Elizabeth Stone, the Chief Technology Officer at Netflix, shares her journey from VP of Data to CTO, revealing the challenges and excitement of her new role. She discusses Netflix's unique high-performance culture, emphasizing the importance of high talent density and the 'keeper's test' for team dynamics. Elizabeth highlights how economics informs decision-making and the balance between excellence and team well-being. With insights on fostering open communication, she champions an environment of freedom and responsibility that drives innovation and builds trust.

verses.title: The Keeper Test and Holding Ourselves Accountable
verses.text: The Keeper Test is a mental framework for holding ourselves accountable in leadership roles. It involves questioning whether we would do everything possible to retain a team member who expresses a desire to leave for another opportunity. If the answer is no, tough conversations about their fit within the team and the company should be initiated. The test helps in addressing difficult situations where one might be hesitant to suggest a team member to leave, ensuring that the right decisions are made for the benefit of both the individual and the organization.

chapters.title: How to break out of autopilot and create the life you want | Graham Weaver (Stanford GSB professor, founder of Alpine Investors)
chapters.description: Graham Weaver, a Stanford GSB professor and founder of Alpine Investors, shares his journey of helping students uncover their true paths. He discusses the importance of breaking free from autopilot living and embracing intentional decision-making. Listeners learn about the 'genie methodology' for identifying passions and the transformative 'Nine Lives' exercise for exploring different life possibilities. Graham also highlights the power of overcoming limiting beliefs and the role of resilience in personal growth, offering a fresh perspective on achieving fulfillment in one's career.

verses.title: Genie Methodology
verses.text:
- Imagine a genie grants you one wish for career success. 
- Pursue that "genie goal," even if it's unconventional, as it's your truest desire.

💬 Quote

Imagine that you're walking home from work, you see this bright, shiny object, and you realize it's a magic lamp, and you rub the lamp, and this genie comes out, and the genie says, hey, I can give you one wish. Whatever you throw yourself into with your whole life and your career, it's going to turn out great.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on discovering your true path.

verses.title: Genie Methodology
verses.text:
- Imagine a genie grants you one wish: guaranteed success in any career path you fully commit to, however hard it may be. 
- What would you choose?

💬 Quote

Imagine [...] a magic lamp [...] and this genie comes out and the genie says, 'Hey, [...] I can give you one wish. And the wish [...] is whatever you throw yourself into with your whole life and your career, it's going to turn out great.'.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver explaining the Genie Methodology exercise.

verses.title: Discovering Your Passion
verses.text:
- Ask yourself powerful questions like, "What would you do if you knew you wouldn't fail?" 
- Consider what excites you, even if it's unconventional or embarrassing.

💬 Quote

What would you do if you knew you wouldn't fail? That's the biggest question.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on identifying your true calling.

verses.title: Overcoming Fear of Failure
verses.text:
- Ask yourself: "What would you do if you knew you wouldn't fail?"
- This question helps uncover your true passions by removing the fear of failure as a barrier.

💬 Quote

What would you do if you knew you wouldn't fail?
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on a powerful question for career clarity.

verses.title: Overcoming Limiting Beliefs
verses.text:
- Identify and write down your limiting beliefs, stripping them of their power. 
- Translate these fears into actionable to-do items to overcome obstacles.

💬 Quote

The first thing I would say about limiting beliefs is they're the most powerful and the most dangerous when you don't even know what they are.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on addressing limiting beliefs.

verses.title: Trojan Horse Class
verses.text:
- Graham Weaver's class at Stanford is about entrepreneurship. 
- However, he focuses on helping students discover their passions and overcome limiting beliefs.

💬 Quote

People take my class to learn how to be a CEO. What they really get is hopefully on the path of doing the thing they want to do.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on his teaching approach.

verses.title: Internal vs. External Scorecard
verses.text:
- Following your passion leads to greater success and fulfillment than chasing external validation. 
-  Genuine excitement fuels sustained effort and attracts like-minded individuals.

💬 Quote

You're going to show up so much differently [in] the thing that you're excited about; that alone is going to make your life a lot better.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the importance of intrinsic motivation.

verses.title: Following Your Passion
verses.text:
- Lenny Rachitsky followed his passion for writing online despite doubts. 
- This led to greater financial success and less stress than his previous career.

💬 Quote

[...] I just started writing things online because the pull was there and people seem to enjoy it. So I just kept following that path.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky on how he followed his passion.

verses.title: Time and Suffering
verses.text:
- Success takes time and suffering; choose a path worth enduring hardships for. 
-  Long-term commitment, not overnight wins, determines ultimate success.

💬 Quote

The missing ingredient in most of the people that fail is time.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the importance of persistence.

verses.title: Worthwhile Suffering
verses.text:
- Life is suffering, so choose something worth suffering for.
-  There isn't a path without suffering, so you might as well pick something you care about.

💬 Quote

Life is suffering. So choose something worth suffering for.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on worthwhile efforts.

verses.title: Worse First
verses.text:
- Positive change requires initial negative steps. 
- Optimizing for short-term comfort prevents long-term gains.

💬 Quote

Everything you want is on the other side of worse first.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on why positive changes require initial negative steps.

verses.title: Accountability
verses.text:
- Create accountability for pursuing your goals through executive coaching or a trusted friend. 
- Talking about your aspirations activates more of your brain than just thinking or writing.

💬 Quote

The answer is accountability. You know, how do you, how do you keep yourself accountable to kind of living the life you want to live?
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on how to stay focused on your goals.

verses.title: Accountability through Coaching
verses.text:
- Hire an executive coach for accountability, similar to a personal trainer for physical health.
- Coaches provide dedicated time for reflection and goal setting, helping you stay on track with your intentions.

💬 Quote

The equivalent of that in your life is an executive coach. [...]  A, they're going to hold you accountable to showing up at a certain time. And B, they're going to show you the exercises.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the value of executive coaching.

verses.title: Daily Goal Setting
verses.text:
- Write down your goals daily, along with three actions to achieve them. 
- This aligns your subconscious mind with your aspirations, leading to increased productivity.

💬 Quote

You will get more done writing down your goal and three things you're going to do to move toward that goal.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on a practical tip for achieving goals.

verses.title: Nine Lives Exercise
verses.text:
- Explore different life possibilities by creating a "nine lives" exercise. 
- Imagine nine different careers or paths, and try incorporating elements of them into your current life.

💬 Quote

So this exercise is to make it less intimidating and you basically come up with nine, nine lives. So you see your first life, life one is the life you have now.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on exploring multiple life paths.

verses.title: Nine Lives Exercise
verses.text:
- If figuring out your career goals feels daunting, try the Nine Lives exercise.
- List nine lives, starting with your current one, that you'd be excited about if you couldn't fail; pick the most energizing one and incorporate it into your current life.

💬 Quote

So you see your first life, life one is the life you have now. [...] And second rule is you have to be excited about all these lives.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver explaining the Nine Lives exercise.

verses.title: Nine Lives Exercise
verses.text:
- Imagine nine lives, all starting today, that excite you.
- Pull the most energizing one into your current life, even as a side hustle, to boost overall energy and potentially find your true path.

💬 Quote

If you knew you wouldn't fail, you would do this one [...] the act of pulling something into your life you're really excited about will give you a tremendous amount of energy.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on energizing one's life by pursuing a passion.

verses.title: Avoid "Not Now"
verses.text:
- Avoid the "not now" mentality; it often leads to never pursuing your dreams. 
-  Realize it's never the perfect time, and take action despite fear or uncertainty.

💬 Quote

Not now, if they're not careful, will turn into not ever.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on overcoming procrastination.

verses.title: Internal vs. External Game
verses.text:
- The true game of life is internal, focusing on self-discovery and fulfillment. 
- Recognize that external achievements don't guarantee internal peace or happiness.

💬 Quote

The most important thing I've learned in the [...] first 50 years of my life is that the true game of life is an internal one, not an external one.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the importance of inner work.

verses.title: External Success, Internal Struggle
verses.text:
- Graham Weaver achieved external success but found it didn't bring lasting happiness. 
- This realization led him to focus on his internal journey and spiritual growth.

💬 Quote

I had a financial event that was [...] like, could I exhale a little bit. [...] But then it hit me that nothing changed, you know, uh, nothing, nothing internally changed at all.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the limitations of external validation.

verses.title: Failures and Setbacks
verses.text:
- Graham Weaver experienced failures in wrestling, crew, and early investments. 
-  He emphasizes that persistence through setbacks is key to eventual success.

💬 Quote

I lost a big match my junior year and I quit and I never, I never wrestled again. And that haunted me.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on a significant failure.

verses.title: When to Quit
verses.text:
- Quit when you no longer believe in the vision or are genuinely excited about it. 
- Look for and scale bright spots, even during challenging times, to build momentum.

💬 Quote

I think the time to quit is when you can no longer see the vision and and you can no longer really believe the vision.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on when to give up on a pursuit.

verses.title: When to Quit
verses.text:
- Quit when you can no longer see or believe in the vision.
- Especially if this persists or you're no longer excited about it, although excitement can wane during tough times.

💬 Quote

I think the time to quit is when you can no longer see the vision [...] and you can no longer really believe the vision [...] and then when that happens for a long period of time or when maybe you're no longer even excited about the vision.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on when to quit pursuing a goal.

verses.title: Define Your Amazing Life
verses.text:
- Define what an amazing life looks like, regardless of how aspirational it seems. 
- Clarity about your desires increases your chances of achieving them.

💬 Quote

Take the time to really figure out [...] what does a wonderful, amazing, incredible life look like?
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the importance of defining your ideal life.

verses.title: Live an Amazing Life
verses.text:
- Define what an amazing life looks like for you, across all aspects, even if it seems crazy.
- Write down the vision for your life, career, relationships, and everything that truly matters.

💬 Quote

You got one life, you get one shot. [...] What does a wonderful, amazing, incredible life look like?
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the importance of defining a fulfilling life.

verses.title: Come Alive
verses.text:
- Don't ask what the world needs. 
- Ask what makes you come alive, because what the world needs most is for *you* to come alive.

💬 Quote

Don't ask what the world needs. Ask instead, what makes you come alive? Because what the world needs most is for you to come alive.

verses.title: Importance of Management Teams
verses.text:
- Graham Weaver believes the management team is the key differentiator for successful companies.
-  A good industry is important, but a world-class management team can make a significant difference.

💬 Quote

The management team is really where we think all the alpha comes from. [...] You can't be wrong on the industry, but you also don't have to be perfect on the industry. You have to have a good enough industry and then a world-class management team.
— Graham Weaver

Graham Weaver on the importance of the management team in private equity investments.

chapters.title: How to discover your superpowers, own your story, and unlock personal growth | Donna Lichaw (author of The Leader’s Journey)
chapters.description: Join Donna Lichaw, an executive coach and author who empowers leaders at companies like Google and Disney. She discusses the transformative power of personal narratives in leadership and how recognizing your superpowers can lead to authentic growth. Donna offers relatable insights on reframing impostor syndrome and emphasizes the importance of acknowledging both strengths and weaknesses. Learn about the Double Diamond framework for personal development, and discover how visualization can ignite creativity and motivation on your journey.

verses.title: The Power of Self-Talk
verses.text: Negative self-talk can shape perceptions and actions, influencing one's identity and interactions. By repeatedly telling oneself limiting or negative narratives, individuals risk internalizing these stories, impacting their self-perception and behaviors. These narratives, whether related to self-doubt, fear of failure, or imposter syndrome, have the potential to overshadow one's daily life and interactions, potentially manifesting the feared outcomes. Constant focus on negative self-talk can lead to these stories becoming reality, affecting personal and professional growth.

verses.title: Zoom and Physical Interaction
verses.text: Virtual interactions like those on Zoom lack physical stimuli, affecting the brain's satisfaction level. In-person interactions are more fulfilling as they provide physical experiences that close circuitry and leave individuals more satisfied. To cope with this lack of physical stimuli on Zoom, using fidget tools to ground oneself during calls can be helpful.

verses.title: Imagining Alternate Realities and Interview Questions
verses.text: Delving into alternate realities can be fun, such as imagining the space industry in a different trajectory. Interview questions can reveal alignment in interest and enthusiasm, like asking candidates what they envision themselves achieving in a few years. It is essential to gauge excitement and passion to determine successful collaboration possibilities.

chapters.title: How to find hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
chapters.description: Albert Cheng, a growth leader with experience at Duolingo, Grammarly, and Chess.com, shares insights on leveraging user psychology and rapid experimentation to unlock growth. He discusses the explore-exploit framework and reveals how displaying premium features to free users can significantly boost upgrades. Cheng emphasizes the importance of resurrecting dormant users and highlights the value of retention and word-of-mouth for consumer subscriptions. Additionally, he explores the transformative role of AI in product experience and growth strategies.

verses.title: Explore Then Exploit Deliberately
verses.text:
- Use an explore-exploit rhythm: explore widely to find human-behavior insights, then exploit by scaling and applying the winning pattern across the product.
- Oscillate between phases until exploitation saturates, then return to divergent exploration.

💬 Quote

When done right, you can oscillate between the two until you saturate out of exploitation mode.
— Albert Cheng
