Albert Cheng on the explore-exploit framework

verses.title: Explore and Exploit
verses.text:
- Albert Cheng learned the Explore and Exploit framework from his engineering partner at Grammarly.
- When exploring, focus on finding the right mountain, and when exploiting, focus on climbing that mountain effectively.

💬 Quote

When you're in exploratory mode, think of it as like finding the right mouse informs to climb. And then when you're in exploitation mode, it's like focusing your resources on climbing that mountain effectively.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on Explore and Exploit

verses.title: Game Reviews Grew By Celebrating Wins
verses.text:
- Chess.com found 80% of users review games after wins, not losses, contrary to expectations.
- They switched reviews to highlight brilliant moves and encouraging copy, boosting reviews 25% and subscriptions 20%.

💬 Quote

80% of people that review their games actually do so after a win.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on discovering when users use game review

verses.title: Use Experiment Patterns To Guide Strategy
verses.text:
- Monitor experiment significance patterns across programs to know when exploitation has been squeezed dry.
- If most tests go non-significant, shift teams back to divergent exploration for fresh ideas.

💬 Quote

If I'm starting to see like more and more experiments that are not statistically significant, that may be a signal to me to say, okay, we might have kind of tried to exploit a little bit too far.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on using experiment explorer tools

verses.title: Automate Ad Hoc Data With AI
verses.text:
- Use text-to-SQL and Slack AI bots to answer ad hoc data questions instantly and scale curiosity across the company.
- This increases data-informed decisions by lowering the barrier to ask and get answers.

💬 Quote

We're working on like kind of training some of these Slack bots to essentially be the first party provider of a lot of these answers.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on AI tools for data requests

verses.title: Prototype Rapidly With AI
verses.text:
- Build quick AI prototypes of core screens (onboarding, home, chessboard) to make ideas discussable and testable faster.
- Use prototyping tools to shorten discovery-to-prototype cycles and align teams earlier.

💬 Quote

When you have those foundational pieces you can then share them with the rest of the company and they can use that as a starting point.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on AI prototypes for PMs and designers

verses.title: Sample Paid Features To Free Users
verses.text:
- Sample premium suggestions into the free experience to showcase higher-value features without full access.
- This ‘reverse trial’ taste nearly doubled Grammarly's upgrade rates by shifting user perception of product value.

💬 Quote

All of a sudden, people were seeing Grammarly as a much more powerful tool than they were before.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng describing Grammarly's monetization change

verses.title: Retention Is The Real Currency
verses.text:
- Retention is the most valuable lever for consumer subscription businesses; poor retention forces day-one monetization pressure.
- For daily-frequency products, D1 retention around 30–40% is a solid heuristic for viability.

💬 Quote

User retention is gold for consumer subscription companies.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on the centrality of retention

verses.title: Consumer Subscription Success
verses.text:
- To build a successful consumer subscription business, focus on organic, word-of-mouth growth.
- Aim for high user retention, with a D1 retention rate around 30-40%.

💬 Quote

What I'm hearing here is you need to find a way to grow through word of mouth for this to have any chance of success.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky summarizing Albert Cheng's advice.

verses.title: Resurrected Users Drive Mature Growth
verses.text:
- Mature products often derive most growth from resurrecting dormant or sporadic users, not new user acquisition.
- Designing deliberate 'resurrected user' journeys can yield high ROI at scale.

💬 Quote

It's actually worth spending some time making sure that that resurrected experience inside the product is really excellent.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on emphasizing resurrected users in mature products

verses.title: Growth Definition
verses.text:
- Growth connects users to the value of a product, and that value changes over time depending on the user's journey. 
- Teams should think from that perspective, then identify problems to solve and form hypotheses.

💬 Quote

Growth as simply your job is to connect users to the value of your product.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky talking about Albert Cheng's definition of growth.

verses.title: Chess.com's Experimentation Goal
verses.text:
- Albert Cheng aims to increase experimentation at Chess.com, targeting 1,000 experiments annually to smooth growth.
- He emphasizes that the target is not just about the number of experiments but also about learning and making an impact.

💬 Quote

Part of having team members that are fanatical about chess is that the company can get pretty damn far just like building for themselves, building for the community and not actually being very experimentation and data oriented.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on why Chess.com wasn't very experimentation oriented initially.

verses.title: Make A Bold Experiments Target
verses.text:
- Set an ambitious experiments-per-year target to shift culture, but focus on systems that enable learning and impact.
- Expand experimentation beyond product to marketing, ASO, and no-code screens to reach high volume.

💬 Quote

I don't actually care that much if we actually hit a thousand. But I think if we get pretty close and we accomplish some of these things, we'll be in really good shape.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on the 1,000-experiments-a-year goal

verses.title: Fix Instrumentation Before Scaling Tests
verses.text:
- Prioritize the experimentation system: clear growth model, proper instrumentation, and observability before running many tests.
- Bad instrumentation can invert results and waste effort, so validate analytics early.

💬 Quote

You need to make sure that you are instrumenting your product in and out. Otherwise, you're going to run experiments and have wonky results.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on the importance of proper instrumentation

verses.title: Duolingo Boosted Virality Via Screenshots
verses.text:
- Duolingo tracked where users screenshot and found streak milestones and funny challenges were highly shared.
- They staffed illustrators to enhance those moments and significantly amplified sharing and growth.

💬 Quote

We invested actually in some time to essentially add screenshot tracking ... and we're able to basically articulate ... streak milestones was the obvious one.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on Duolingo's virality team and screenshot tracking

verses.title: Three Pillars Of Gamification
verses.text:
- Effective gamification has three pillars: core loop, metagame, and profile; all three must be designed for long-term habit formation.
- Tight core loops create daily habit; metagame and profile provide longer-term motivation and identity.

💬 Quote

You have the core loop, you have the metagame, and then you have the profile.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng explaining gamification pillars

verses.title: Prioritize High-Agency Hires
verses.text:
- Hire for high agency, clock speed, and rapid learning rather than only deep domain experience.
- Prefer candidates who ask smart questions, try your product, and show energy beyond scripted interviews.

💬 Quote

Some of the highest performers just being people that had very high agency, had that clock speed, had that energy, but they didn't necessarily need to have deep experience on that matter.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng on hiring high-agency people

verses.title: Chariot's Direct Feature Taught Hard Lessons
verses.text:
- At Chariot Albert led a dynamic-routes feature that failed because it searched for a problem and neglected drivers and operations.
- He learned to validate problems, consider all marketplace sides, and avoid early PR before product-market fit.

💬 Quote

This was kind of a solution searching for a problem.
— Albert Cheng

Albert Cheng recounting a failed product experiment at Chariot

chapters.title: How to find work you love | Bob Moesta (Jobs-to-be-Done co-creator, author of "Job Moves”)
chapters.description: Bob Moesta, co-creator of the Jobs to Be Done framework and author of "Job Moves," shares insights from interviews with over 1,000 career changers. He discusses the four quests driving career transitions and how experiences often outweigh job features like salary. Moesta highlights the importance of recognizing what energizes versus drains you and introduces the concept of a 'jobcation' for career reflection. He also offers guidance on crafting your career story and prototyping potential career paths.

verses.title: Career Progress and Job Seeking
verses.text:
- The moment you stop progressing in your career, you start seeking a new job.
- Employees hire companies more than companies hire employees.

💬 Quote

The moment you stop making progress in your career is the moment you start looking for another job.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on the driving force behind job changes.

verses.title: The Jobcation
verses.text:
- Take a jobcation, a less demanding job, after leaving a startup to recover.
- This allows you to reset, rediscover yourself, and prepare for your next move.

💬 Quote

The moment that you get out of that environment, you need to take the time to reset your mind and your body. I call it a jobcation [...]
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on recovering after leaving a startup.

verses.title: Job Features vs. Experiences
verses.text:
- Job experiences, not features like salary or title, truly retain employees.
- Money often represents respect, necessity, or a sense of deserving more.

💬 Quote

It's the experiences that keep you at your job. It's not just about the money [...]
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on the importance of job experiences.

verses.title: Employees Hiring Companies
verses.text:
- Employees hire companies more often than companies hire employees.
-  The moment an employee stops progressing in their career is when they begin their job search.

💬 Quote

Employees hire companies more than companies hire employees.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on the dynamics between employees and companies.

verses.title: Four Quests for Job Changes
verses.text:
- People leave jobs for four reasons: getting out, taking the next step, regaining control, or realigning.
- Understanding your "quest" clarifies what you seek in a new job.

💬 Quote

out of it, we end up getting what we call pushes and pulls. And these pushes and pulls, we end up with 13 different pushes [...] and 14 pulls [...] but what happened is, is that when you start to look at all thousand interviews, they fall into one of four buckets.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta explaining the four reasons why people leave their jobs

verses.title: Identifying Energy Drivers and Drains
verses.text:
- Reflect on past experiences to identify what energizes and drains you.
- This self-awareness helps define job requirements.

💬 Quote

just reflect on the day and say, where did I get energy today? And where, where did my energy get drained?
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on reflecting to find fulfilling work.

verses.title: Prototyping Job Positions
verses.text:
- Treat yourself like a product and prototype different job positions.
- Conduct informational interviews to explore various roles and industries.

💬 Quote

- How do we prototype different job positions for you? [...]
- So how do we think of you?
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on finding the right job fit.

verses.title: Finding the Right Job
verses.text:
- Distill your skills, energy drivers, and energy drains.
- Explore widely, talk to people in various roles and industries, then narrow your focus to one area to prototype and pursue.

💬 Quote

Distill your skills, distill what gives you energy, distill those things and make sure you're clear on those. [...] Go wide and find what many different industries that can do it. Go talk to friends, people who have these jobs and start to realize what they are and narrow down to one area [...] that you really feel like you can actually go to.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta's advice on finding fulfilling work.

verses.title: The Importance of Trade-offs
verses.text:
- No job is perfect; understand and accept the trade-offs each role requires.
- What are you willing to sacrifice to gain what truly matters?

💬 Quote

No job is perfect. And ultimately, people are looking for the thing that checks all the boxes. And you start to realize like nothing checks all the boxes.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on accepting trade-offs in your career.

verses.title: Craft Your Career Story
verses.text:
- Use a narrative template to craft a compelling career story (Pixar method).
- This helps you and potential employers understand your journey.

💬 Quote

once upon a time, [...] every day, [...]. But at the same time [...] And one day, [...] And because of that, [...] And ultimately, because of that, [...]
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on structuring your career narrative.

verses.title: Hiring for Experiences
verses.text:
- Focus on experiences, not just features, when writing job descriptions.
- Design jobs to fit people, rather than forcing people into pre-defined roles.

💬 Quote

fix the job descriptions and build a process that helps you understand people's energy drivers and drains and helping people be more transparent about what they like.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on improving hiring practices.

verses.title: Self-Awareness for Founders
verses.text:
- Self-awareness is crucial for founders.
- Knowing your strengths, weaknesses, and energy drivers helps shape your startup.

💬 Quote

Part of it is to realize as a founder, you have to do a lot of different things. And so part of it is I think self-awareness is one of the biggest assets you're going to have.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta on the importance of self-awareness for founders.

verses.title: Realignment and Overwhelm
verses.text:
- Bob Moesta used his own framework to overcome overwhelm from his book launch.
- He identified misalignments and delegated tasks to regain energy.

💬 Quote

I was just overwhelmed. I was literally like, come on, like this, like this is not what I want to do.
— Bob Moesta

Bob Moesta sharing a personal anecdote about using the framework for realignment.

chapters.title: How to tell better stories | Matthew Dicks (Storyworthy)
chapters.description: Matthew Dicks, a best-selling author and storytelling expert, shares his insights on crafting compelling narratives. He emphasizes the transformative power of storytelling in business, highlighting how personal experiences can create emotional connections. Dicks discusses techniques like starting with the ending and maintaining audience engagement through humor. He also introduces his 'Homework for Life' practice, a method to capture meaningful moments for storytelling. Plus, he offers tips on overcoming public speaking nerves, making storytelling both relatable and impactful.

verses.title: The Root of All Good Stories: A Singular Moment
verses.text: All good stories are rooted in a singular moment of either transformation or realization in a person's life. This moment, which can be as short as one second, marks a distinct change in thoughts or beliefs. The purpose of a story is to bring this moment to clarity for the audience, allowing them to experience the same transformation or realization as the storyteller. This singular moment is the essence of a story, while the majority of the narrative is dedicated to providing context to lead up to this crucial point.

verses.title: The Power of Storytelling
verses.text: Stories have a unique power to captivate our minds repeatedly, unlike PowerPoint presentations or keynotes. The movie 'When Harry Met Sally' is an example of how storytelling can be engrossing, as evidenced by the speaker's wife wanting to watch it every night. Our minds are naturally drawn to stories, which is why we are inclined to watch movies numerous times, proving the enduring impact and appeal of storytelling.

verses.title: The Elements of a Good Story
verses.text: A good story should include a change, pass the dinner test, and avoid performance art. The dinner test means that a story should be told the same way you would tell it at dinner, without any performance art or weird techniques like opening with unattributed dialogue.

verses.title: The Importance of Telling Your Own Story
verses.text: Telling your own story is crucial because narrating on behalf of someone else turns the story into fiction. The absence of the actual person disconnects the audience, making them perceive the individual as a distant entity. Expressing vulnerability in a story becomes challenging when narrating for someone else, hindering the connection with the audience. Being vulnerable with the audience is a fundamental aspect of storytelling, enabling impactful and meaningful storytelling.

verses.title: How to Tell a Vacation Story
verses.text: Vacation stories should not be a mere recounting of events but should focus on moments of fundamental change. The location of the vacation is irrelevant unless it adds essential context to the story. Start the story as close to the moment of change as possible, keeping it focused and small. Avoid unnecessary details about the vacation and instead, prioritize the key moments of realization or change that occurred during the trip.

verses.title: The Importance of Stakes in Storytelling
verses.text: Stakes are crucial in storytelling as they keep the audience engaged by making them wonder and worry about what will happen next. It is essential for storytellers to make the audience constantly curious and concerned about the outcomes. Stakes create the tension and suspense necessary to maintain the audience's interest. By starting a story with high stakes, like a big spaceship shooting at a small spaceship in Star Wars or a police officer chasing a man in an Alfred Hitchcock movie, the audience is immediately drawn into the narrative and invested in the outcome. Inserting stakes effectively is key to engaging the audience in any type of story, whether it is fiction or based on real life events.

verses.title: Crafting Engaging Narratives: Techniques for Building Suspense
verses.text: Techniques such as breadcrumbs, guns in the room, and hour glasses are used in storytelling to engage the audience. Breadcrumbs are hints that lead to a significant event, creating suspense. The presence of a gun in a story symbolizes future relevance and builds anticipation. The hour glass effect involves slowing down time by providing intricate details at a crucial moment to keep the audience at the edge of their seat. Predicting a future, regardless of accuracy, through crystal balls adds an element of mystery and captivates the audience.

verses.title: Building Connections and Market Potential
verses.text: Being married or in a committed relationship implies having a large total addressable market and a broad connection base. Conversely, being a marathoner represents a small addressable market, but intense connections with other marathoners. To stand out in the corporate world, it is crucial to be unique, full of color, and memorable instead of blending in with the crowd.

verses.title: Strategies for Adding Humor in Business
verses.text: Using nostalgia can add humor to business interactions by reminiscing about past funny or quirky experiences. Nostalgic moments such as outdated technology, unusual childhood practices, or lack of safety precautions can be turned into humorous anecdotes for business settings.

verses.title: Strategies for Creating Memorable Moments and Storytelling
verses.text: The speaker emphasizes the importance of creating memorable moments by stacking strategies and focusing on becoming memorable. They discuss the approach of building a bank of stories, highlighting the benefits of the homework for life framework as a valuable process. The framework involves reflecting on daily experiences to identify moments worth telling as stories, aiming to continually generate new and engaging story content.

verses.title: The Power of Consistent Storytelling and Active Listening
verses.text: Consistent storytelling requires daily practice and faith in improvement over time. Starting with insignificant moments and sharing them with willing listeners helps in honing storytelling skills. Most people's stories are not very good, so putting thought into what you're about to say can make you a better storyteller. Storytelling is more about strategic decision making and active listening than mere language proficiency.

verses.title: Overcoming Stage Fright: Understanding Pre-Talk Nervousness
verses.text: Most of the nervousness people experience before speaking in public dissipates once they start talking. This pre-talk nervousness is the main source of anxiety for individuals. Understanding that the majority of the nervousness occurs before the talk can be a relief, especially when realizing that once speaking begins, the nervousness tends to fade away. Preparation and knowledge of the topic can significantly reduce this pre-talk nervousness, making the speaking experience more comfortable and less anxiety-inducing.

verses.title: Building Smooth Transitions for Public Speaking and Storytelling
verses.text: The key to effective public speaking and storytelling is to build smooth transitions between different sections. By training oneself to anticipate and smoothly transition between topics, one can avoid getting stuck or feeling lost during a presentation. Building these transitions helps in conveying the information effectively without the need for strict memorization, thereby reducing stress and improving the overall delivery of the speech or story.

chapters.title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson (co‑founder of Tiny)
chapters.description: Andrew Wilkinson, co-founder of Tiny, shares his journey from teenage barista to billionaire entrepreneur. He discusses the importance of spotting high-margin niche opportunities and emphasizes starting small to build confidence. The conversation highlights his efficient use of AI tools that act as personal assistants, alongside his thoughts on the future of work. Wilkinson openly addresses the link between mental health and happiness, advocating for discussions around ADHD and medication. He even recounts a memorable encounter with Steve Jobs, offering unique entrepreneurial insights.

verses.title: Start Simple to Build Success
verses.text:
- Start with simple businesses where you can achieve early success and positive feedback.  
- Avoid complex, highly competitive businesses as a beginner to build confidence and muscle gradually.

💬 Quote

You don't want to walk into the gym on day one and try and deadlift 300 pounds.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on starting businesses as a beginner

verses.title: Fish Where the Fish Are
verses.text:
- Andrew Wilkinson advises choosing something you're interested in, but not something everyone else is interested in.
- Look for less competitive markets where fewer people wake up wanting to start a business.

💬 Quote

Charlie Munger, Warren Buffett's longtime business partner, has this amazing quote. He says, fish where the fish are.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on selecting a startup idea

verses.title: Niche Markets
verses.text:
- Target uncompetitive niches like funeral homes or pest control instead of saturated markets like restaurants. 
- The fewer competitors, the higher your margins and the more successful your business can be.

💬 Quote

Fish where the fish are.

verses.title: Scaling to Escape Tedium
verses.text:
- If you don't want to perform a tedious task forever, determine if the business can be scaled.
- With sufficient scale, hire employees to handle unwanted tasks and focus on what you enjoy.

💬 Quote

If this gets to scale, you'll never touch a pressure washer unless you want to.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on scaling a pressure washing business

verses.title: Pivot for Profitability
verses.text:
- Identify your passion and skills, then pivot to the most profitable application.
- Andrew Wilkinson advises a marketing student to target realtors or wealth managers instead of restaurants to increase revenue.

💬 Quote

If you just pivot that idea just ever so slightly, and instead of doing restaurants, you did realtors or wealth managers who have quite a large marketing budget [...] Those people, you can charge $5,000 a month.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson giving advice to a student about marketing.

verses.title: Avoid Overcrowded Markets
verses.text:
- Andrew Wilkinson started 'Flow', a project management app similar to Asana, and lost $10 million trying to compete with venture-backed companies.
- He suggests focusing on boring markets where fewer people compete, as seen with a business making $30 million helping people fill out government assistance forms.

💬 Quote

We made the mistake that so many entrepreneurs make, which is to go after an industry that everybody goes after.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on mistakes to avoid when starting a business.

verses.title: Bootstrapped Businesses Can Scale Big
verses.text:
- Bootstrap businesses can scale to hundreds of millions without venture capital.  
- Choose businesses with moats and low competition for sustainable growth without burning cash.

💬 Quote

We've bootstrapped the entire business and now across all of our companies, we do almost $300 million in revenue.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on bootstrapping vs venture capital

verses.title: Defining Success
verses.text:
- Redefine success as building a sustainable, enjoyable business rather than maximizing market share.
- Andrew Wilkinson admires the founder of 'Things' who prioritized personal fulfillment and a great product over hyper-growth, unlike Dustin Moskovitz.

💬 Quote

If the goal is the founder has an incredible life, probably has three houses, flies all over the place, does whatever he wants, has recurring revenue, and he gets to work with headphones on, building a beautiful piece of software that people love, then I think he's won.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on redefining the measurement of success

verses.title: Fish Where the Fish Are
verses.text:
- Andrew Wilkinson advises to 'fish where the fish are,' avoiding competition with venture-backed companies.
- Find niche markets too small for VCs but large enough to sustain a comfortable lifestyle business.

💬 Quote

You don't want to be fighting against the commercial fishermen, right? If you just want to have a little business where you make enough, you know, you get enough fish to feed your family [...] find the other fishing hole.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on avoiding competition with venture-backed companies.

verses.title: Buying Businesses
verses.text:
- Andrew Wilkinson realized starting businesses was stressful and shifted to buying them. He looks for businesses with a wide 'moat', such as a strong brand or network effects, that are difficult to disrupt.
- This allows for pricing power and customer loyalty, exemplified by brands like Coca-Cola or Advil.

💬 Quote

When I read about Warren Buffett, I just thought, wow, I'm a sucker. I'm running around like a crazy trying to run all these businesses. Why am I not just buying businesses and letting them run?
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on his decision to transition from starting to buying businesses.

verses.title: Hire for Ability, Cut Quickly
verses.text:
- Hire carefully to avoid difficult people, cutting ties immediately if doubts arise.  
- Hire for current ability rather than potential; people rarely change fundamentally.

💬 Quote

If I ever think, should I fire this person, even once I should fire them immediately.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on hiring and people management

verses.title: Firing Fast
verses.text:
- Don't hire people based on gut feeling, thinking you can change them; it doesn't work.
- If you ever think about firing someone, even once, fire them immediately, as it's usually a negative signal.

💬 Quote

The biggest mistake I made in my early career was I would hire people because I liked them on gut and I would think that I could change them.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on hiring

verses.title: Firing Decisions
verses.text:
- If you ever think 'should I fire this person', even once, fire them immediately, as this is a reliable negative signal. 
- Hire people who are already fully formed and can do what you need, instead of hiring for potential and trying to coach them.

💬 Quote

If I ever think, should I fire this person, even once I should fire them immediately.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on firing decisions

verses.title: Use AI Agents to Automate Email
verses.text:
- Automate email and calendar management using AI agents to reduce cognitive load.  
- Use AI workflows to filter, label, and respond to routine requests, increasing productivity.

💬 Quote

So many knowledge work jobs are going to change massively.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on leveraging AI in business and life

verses.title: AI Could Simplify Many Jobs
verses.text:
- AI progress may turn complex jobs into simple prompts handled by AI agents.  
- Massive job displacement is coming, but new opportunities and roles will also emerge.

💬 Quote

The fundamental question is, do all jobs just become a single prompt?
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on the future impact of AI on jobs

verses.title: Prioritize Social Skills for Kids
verses.text:
- Focus on social skills, politeness, and communication for children’s first 10 years of life.  
- Let them lean into their passion to guide future skill development amid AI uncertainty.

💬 Quote

I'm focused on making sure they're socialized and they're polite, very basic.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on what skills to encourage in children

verses.title: Medication Transformed My Anxiety
verses.text:
- Andrew's anxiety persisted despite wealth until SSRI medication helped reduce inner stress.  
- Medication transformed his mental state, proving more effective than money or success for happiness.

💬 Quote

No amount of money or success or attention or anything else had done what this little tiny yellow pill could do for my mental state.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on his journey from anxiety and the impact of medication

verses.title: ADHD Diagnosis Changed Perspective
verses.text:
- Andrew was diagnosed with ADHD after a cognitive test revealed poor working memory.  
- Diagnosis provided empathy for himself and improved both work and personal life management.

💬 Quote

I think a lot of entrepreneurs should consider getting checked for ADHD.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on his ADHD diagnosis and its impact

verses.title: Hard Choices Lead to Ease
verses.text:
- Choose hard choices for easier long-term success and life quality.  
- Avoid easy decisions that create complicated, stressful outcomes later on.

💬 Quote

Easy choices, hard life, hard choices, easy life.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on life motto about choices

verses.title: Easy Choices, Hard Life
verses.text:
- Choose the hard path for a more fulfilling life, as easy choices often lead to difficulties. 
- Andrew Wilkinson finds that making the tough decision to shut down a business or end a project almost always makes his life easier.

💬 Quote

It's easy choices, hard life, hard choices, easy life.

verses.title: Teen Interviewed Steve Jobs
verses.text:
- As a teenager, Andrew secured a Steve Jobs interview by being first in line and persistent.  
- He eagerly engaged Jobs, gaining invaluable experience despite being nervous and starstruck.

💬 Quote

He walks directly up to me because I'm first in line. He shakes my hand. And he says, hi, I'm Steve.
— Andrew Wilkinson

Andrew Wilkinson on interviewing Steve Jobs as a teenager

chapters.title: Identify your bullseye customer in one day | Michael Margolis (UX Research Partner at Google Ventures)
chapters.description: Michael Margolis, a UX research partner at Google Ventures with 15 years of experience, shares insights on identifying your bullseye customer. He outlines a collaborative sprint process that helps startups pinpoint ideal adopters in just one day. Discover common pitfalls that founders encounter when selecting customers and gain tips for conducting impactful interviews. Margolis also emphasizes the effectiveness of simple prototypes and engaging team 'watch parties' to align customer insights, making these methods applicable beyond tech startups.

verses.title: Bullseye Customer Definition
verses.text:
- A bullseye customer is a specific subset of your target market most likely to adopt your product initially.
- Focusing on them helps prioritize development, feedback, and team alignment.

💬 Quote

A bullseye customer is the very specific subset of your target market who initially is most likely to adopt your product or service.
— Michael Margolis

Michael Margolis on defining a bullseye customer

verses.title: Prioritize Bullseye Customer
verses.text:
- Prioritize identifying your bullseye customer to avoid wasting time building the wrong product.
- Focusing on the bullseye customer clarifies pain points, acquisition strategies, and overall product direction.

💬 Quote

Why is this the thing of all the things that you guys have seen along the early stage of a startup journey, you have decided this is where we need to focus and help people.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky on the importance of the bullseye customer

verses.title: Medication Delivery Service Example
verses.text:
- A company developing a medication delivery service initially targeted all specialty medication users.
- Through the bullseye sprint, they discovered the critical need for predictable delivery windows, especially for refrigerated medications, and narrowed their focus.

💬 Quote

So I was working with a company that was developing a new delivery service for people who have specialty prescriptions, medications. So these are the kinds of medications that are expensive and for very specialized chronic diseases.
— Michael Margolis

Michael Margolis shares a case study

verses.title: Comically Narrow Target
verses.text:
- Aim for a comically narrow bullseye customer to maximize learning and avoid wasted effort.
- If no one is thrilled with your product in testing, it's a crucial sign to re-evaluate.

💬 Quote

Why would it have to be so narrow? [...] he described it as, it should feel comically narrow.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky emphasizes the importance of a narrow target

verses.title: Hindsight Bias in Research
verses.text:
- Researchers experience hindsight bias, making it hard to recall what they didn't know before the study.
- Comparing pre-study predictions with findings helps demonstrate research value and reveals true learning.

💬 Quote

There's this hindsight bias that happens, which is as you've gone through the process, it's very difficult to remember what you didn't, you know, that you didn't already know this.
— Michael Margolis

Michael Margolis on the importance of documenting assumptions before user research.

chapters.title: Lessons from 1,000+ YC startups: Resilience, tar pit ideas, pivoting, more | Dalton Caldwell (Y Combinator, Managing Director)
chapters.description: Dalton Caldwell, Managing Director at Y Combinator, shares invaluable insights from his extensive experience with over 1,000 startups. He discusses the mindset of resilience for founders and the pitfalls of 'tar pit ideas.' Caldwell highlights when to pivot a startup and the importance of maintaining direct customer engagement. He also warns about common reasons for failure, emphasizing market size and product fit. With practical advice, he underscores that understanding these dynamics is vital for entrepreneurial success.

verses.title: Persistence vs. Letting Go in Startups
verses.text: The decision to persist with a struggling startup or to let go is nuanced. One key consideration is whether the founders are still having fun and enjoying their work. If the founders are still passionate and derive enjoyment from their work, it may be worth persevering. However, if running the startup has become burdensome and joyless, it might be time to consider moving on.

verses.title: Building on Expertise for a Successful Pivot
verses.text: A successful pivot involves building on expertise and knowledge gained from prior experiences rather than starting from scratch. It is essential to leverage skills and insights from previous ventures to guide the transition effectively.

verses.title: Successful Pivots Leverage Existing Expertise
verses.text: A successful pivot often builds upon what you are already an expert in. By focusing on areas of expertise and knowledge, founders can create ideas that are warmer, closer, and more successful. Examples such as Brexit, Retool, PostHog, and Zap all demonstrate how leveraging existing knowledge and experience can lead to successful pivots and new business ideas.

verses.title: Finding Success Through Strategic Pivots
verses.text: The key insight lies in the journey of Zippry, which underwent six pivots before becoming a billion-dollar business. The founders' expertise and previous experiences, coupled with identifying a market with dissatisfied customers and poor software, led to their successful pivot and growth.

verses.title: Exploring Opportunities for Innovation
verses.text: The speaker discusses various areas like ending cancer, spatial computing, new defense technology, and bringing manufacturing back to America, emphasizing the need for more startups to focus on these hard science and deep tech areas. Additionally, there is a call for better enterprise glue, highlighting the importance of creating software to connect business systems seamlessly and the room for improvement in this field.

chapters.title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
chapters.description: Uri Levine, co-founder of Waze and a serial entrepreneur, shares invaluable insights for startup success. He emphasizes the importance of focusing on problems over solutions to resonate with customers. Levine discusses the iterative process of achieving product-market fit and the critical roles of decisive hiring and effective storytelling in fundraising. He stresses that understanding user behavior is key to product development and highlights the necessity of adapting through challenges, showcasing the dynamic journey of building impactful startups.

verses.title: Fall in Love with the Problem to Create Value
verses.text: Entrepreneurship revolves around value creation, which is most effectively achieved by solving significant problems. A deep understanding and affection for the problem at hand can serve as your guiding principle, or 'north star,' keeping your efforts aligned and focused. This focus not only enhances your chances of success but also makes your story more compelling to potential customers. When your approach is framed around solving a relatable issue, like avoiding traffic jams instead of merely discussing technological innovations, customers become more invested in your success. Hence, every entrepreneurial venture should begin with identifying a substantial problem that, when solved, contributes positively to the world.

verses.title: Passion Fuels Problem-Solving
verses.text: Choosing a problem to work on requires a personal connection and excitement for the challenge. Passion plays a critical role in sustaining motivation throughout the complexities and hardships of the journey. Even if a problem presents a significant business opportunity, lacking personal enthusiasm can hinder success. The journey is arduous, necessitating a deep emotional investment to foster resilience. Additionally, engaging others to share this passion is crucial, as collective enthusiasm can drive the team forward, aligning efforts towards a common goal.

verses.title: Embrace Iteration, Not Perfection
verses.text: Increasing your attempts enhances your chances of success; success depends on persistence rather than perfection. In a basketball analogy, having multiple shots increases the probability of scoring, highlighting that one great attempt is often not enough without practice. The pursuit of perfection can hinder progress, as being 'good enough' is sufficient to compete in the market. The pathway to achieving this involves starting from a position of imperfection and iterating consistently until reaching an adequate standard. This process is typically longer than anticipated, particularly in identifying product-market fit, which varies across different stages of company development. In contrast to startups, established corporations have defined strategies including clear value propositions, product offerings, pricing, target audiences, and market approaches.

verses.title: Stability Fosters Success
verses.text: Startups must consistently execute their strategies while navigating an uncertain journey of discovering product market fit, as it is crucial for survival. The process involves identifying customer value; failure to achieve product market fit results in business failure. Once established, companies maintain their offerings without significant changes, as seen in the consistent user experience of popular applications like Google and WhatsApp, which continue to deliver the same core value since inception. Maintaining product market fit is vital for sustaining customer satisfaction and ensuring long-term success.

verses.title: Prioritize Growth, Then Profitability
verses.text: In the entrepreneurial process, four key phases emerge: generating an idea, discovering product-market fit, deciding between prioritizing growth or developing a business model, and then executing on the chosen path. If a product exhibits high-frequency usage and thrives on word-of-mouth marketing, it is advisable to concentrate on scaling growth initially, as this can naturally lead to expansion. Conversely, if high-frequency usage is not present, it is crucial to establish a viable business model first, as failure to do so will result in perpetual reliance on user acquisition efforts.

verses.title: First Impressions Matter
verses.text: In raising capital, first impressions are crucial and can heavily influence investor decisions. Research indicates that investors often form their opinions about entrepreneurs almost instantly, during initial interactions. Therefore, it is essential for entrepreneurs to present their strongest points immediately, whether it be the significance of the problem being addressed, existing traction, or the strength of their team. This proactive approach ensures that the initial favorable impression sets the tone for the conversation, reducing the likelihood of bias from any subsequent information.

verses.title: Invest in the Story, Not the Facts
verses.text: Early-stage investors prioritize the CEO and their narrative over any measurable traction or accomplishments. Investors consistently express that their decisions hinge on their affinity for the CEO and the compelling nature of the story presented. To optimize the chances for investment, the CEO should conduct meetings solo, avoiding distractions or influences from team members, ensuring they stand out as the focal point. Additionally, it's crucial for the CEO to craft a narrative that fosters emotional engagement and a desire for the listener to become part of the journey, rather than relying solely on factual achievements.

verses.title: Believe in Usability to Attract Investment
verses.text: Investors prioritize the belief in the usability of a product and the confidence in the ability to build it. Starting with the problem is essential, as potential investors also act as users; if they don't see relevance, they'll dismiss the opportunity. There’s a stark difference in conversion rates: while one-third of potential customers may purchase a product when presented, only about 1% of investors are likely to invest, meaning entrepreneurs will often face numerous rejections before securing a single positive response.

verses.title: Lead with Strength to Captivate Investors
verses.text: Successful angel investing relies on identifying the right fit among many startups. It is essential to evaluate and focus on the strongest aspects of your idea when presenting, as investors tend to make decisions quickly and are influenced by initial impressions. Starting with compelling content can positively bias their perception of the entire proposal. Additionally, the first slide of a presentation is critical; it holds importance as it is presented for the longest duration and should clearly and engagingly introduce the company and its concept.

verses.title: First Impressions Matter Most
verses.text: The effectiveness of a presentation largely hinges on the content of the first and last slides. The first slide, often merely an introduction, should convey the strongest point, as it is displayed for the longest duration. This is crucial for capturing attention and establishing importance right from the start. Likewise, the last slide, typically a 'thank you' note, serves as a final opportunity to reinforce your key message. By strategically placing significant information on these slides, presenters can enhance engagement and impact.

verses.title: Conclude with Impact
verses.text: In presentations, leverage the last slide for maximum impact by using it strategically rather than merely for a thank you or contact information. This slide often receives the most attention post-presentation, making it an ideal moment to reinforce key points. Consider integrating compelling elements such as market size or problem descriptions to enhance retention. Additionally, tailor concluding remarks or anecdotes to the audience's interests and the dialogue established during the presentation to ensure relevance and engagement.

verses.title: Authenticity Lies in the Details
verses.text: The authenticity and relatability of a story hinge on its details. When sharing a narrative, rich specifics serve to convince the audience of its reality. A lack of detail diminishes the believability of any message, particularly when explaining concepts or use cases. A well-crafted story with personal anecdotes can engage listeners and foster connections, as demonstrated in a conversation where an investor was intrigued by a personal experience that was recounted multiple times. Thus, detailed storytelling is essential for establishing credibility and engaging an audience effectively.

verses.title: Decisions Define Leadership
verses.text: Making tough decisions in hiring and firing is critical for a startup's success. The distinction between hiring—often seen as easy—and firing, which involves difficult judgments, underscores the need for leaders to embrace the challenge of making hard choices. Entrepreneurs frequently identify team misalignment as a reason for failure, citing unfit team members and communication issues, often linked to ego management. Alarmingly, most entrepreneurs recognized team problems within the first month yet failed to act, indicating that the real issue lay in the leaders' inability to confront difficult decisions. Leaders must prioritize decisive action over complacency to foster effective teams.

verses.title: Retention Requires Tough Choices
verses.text: Top performers are likely to leave organizations that avoid making difficult decisions regarding underperformance or toxic behavior. In a small team environment, it becomes evident when someone is not a good fit, and if leadership fails to address this, the morale and effectiveness of the entire team suffer. Building a successful startup requires not just the right talent, but also the courage to remove those who detract from the team's potential. Regularly reflecting on team dynamics and performance after new hires is essential for maintaining a high-performing culture.

verses.title: Reflect to Redirect Your Path
verses.text: Evaluate employees regularly to ensure alignment with performance expectations. Implement a 30-day review system for new hires to reassess their fit based on current knowledge. Utilize the principle of retrospective questioning in all aspects of life: if circumstances change and you'd make a different choice today, take action to realign your path. Embrace the idea that every day is an opportunity to make better decisions.

verses.title: Cultivate Habits and Connections
verses.text: Understanding the journey of entrepreneurship is crucial, as highlighted by the importance of networking and commonalities with others in the field. Recommended readings include 'That Will Never Work' by Mark Randolph, which emphasizes resilience in business, and 'Atomic Habits,' which focuses on the significance of establishing and measuring habits for continuous improvement.

verses.title: Prioritize Value Over Purchase
verses.text: The essence of true value lies in the time spent engaging with content rather than merely acquiring it. The focus should be on the transformative knowledge derived from reading, which ultimately serves a deeper purpose of creating value for oneself and others. The commitment to respond to inquiries and foster a direct connection with readers reinforces the dedication to enhancing their experience and understanding. True investment is measured not in dollars spent, but in the impact on one's life and growth acquired through knowledge.

chapters.title: Linear’s secret to building beloved B2B products | Nan Yu (Head of Product)
chapters.description: Nan Yu, Head of Product at Linear, shares insights from one of the fastest-growing B2B SaaS companies known for its user-friendly design. The discussion reveals how speed and quality can coexist in product development. Nan emphasizes the need for a deep understanding of user needs and the role of empathy in product management. He also introduces the "double triangle" framework and shares his systematic approach to creativity, offering valuable career advice for aspiring product managers. Expect a blend of innovation strategies and real-world application!

verses.title: Speed vs. Quality
verses.text:
- There's no trade-off between speed and quality in product development. 
- True speed comes from competence and iteration, not rushing.

💬 Quote

I think you see in the team at Linear that a lot of people don’t see, which is that there’s not actually a tradeoff between speed and quality.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky on a common misconception about speed and quality.

verses.title: Rapid Prototyping
verses.text:
- Aim for a workable solution within the first 10% of your allotted time budget. 
- This allows early hypothesis testing and faster iteration.

💬 Quote

What it really looks like is, you know, you have some rough time budget for how long you think something’s going to take. By the time 10% of it has passed, after week one, you have something that works, that tests some kind of key hypothesis internally.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on what speed looks like in practice.

verses.title: First Version Expectations
verses.text:
- Don't expect the first version to be perfect; it's just a best guess to validate assumptions. 
- Nan Yu references Patrick Collison's tweet arguing against the idea that good, cheap, and fast are mutually exclusive.

💬 Quote

I increasingly believe that good, cheap, fast, choose to, maximum is devious misinformation spread by the slow.

verses.title: Prioritizing ICs
verses.text:
- Prioritize individual contributors (ICs) over middle management, especially regarding feature requests. 
- Avoid customizations that simplify reporting but hinder IC workflows.

💬 Quote

The stuff that we absolutely have to say no to is the exact kind of thing that leads to this bloatedness that makes ICs kind of hate their lives.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on avoiding feature bloat.

verses.title: Empathetic Questioning
verses.text:
- On customer calls, dig deep to understand the emotions behind feature requests. 
- Aim to feel the customer's pain to identify true needs.

💬 Quote

My goal is to feel bad in the same way that customers feel bad.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on understanding customer needs deeply.

verses.title: Target Dates for Projects
verses.text:
- Linear's target date feature allows flexible deadlines, solving the pain of false precision. 
- This prevents miscommunication between product and marketing teams.

💬 Quote

So the thing that we did specifically to solve this was on projects in Linear, you can just specify a target date at whatever level of granularity you want.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on solving emotional pain points for users.

verses.title: Extreme Thinking
verses.text:
- Systemize creativity by exploring extreme solutions to problems. 
- Ask, "How extreme can we take this?" to break free from constraints and expand possibilities.

💬 Quote

When you think in that way, when you’re saying, ‘Hey, what’s the, you know, what’s the 11-star experience,’ uh, what you’re, what you’re really asking is like, ‘Hey, what’s like the luxurious most version of this’ [...?]
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on a systematic approach to creativity.

verses.title: Extreme Thinking
verses.text:
- To be more creative, consider how extreme you can take a solution. Ask yourself: what's the most outrageous version of this along some trait?
- Thinking extremely helps you see beyond your defaults and explore the possibilities.

💬 Quote

How extreme can you take it? Like you're designing a product. You're trying to come up with a solution. Like what's the most outrageous version of this along some trait?
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on how to systemize creativity

verses.title: Draft Saving
verses.text:
- Linear explored extreme approaches to draft saving, from unsafe speed to overly safe auto-saving. 
- This led to a balanced solution that interrupts for new issues but auto-saves drafts.

💬 Quote

So the feature where we did this that I remember very clearly because it was kind of recent is we built this feature to save drafts for your issues, right?
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on breaking constraints and building at extremes.

verses.title: B2B as Education
verses.text:
- B2B software not only solves problems but teaches users how to work by embodying best practices. 
- Adopting such software introduces a baseline competency level to an organization.

💬 Quote

When you adopt that tool, you’re not just adopting like the actual software, you’re adopting the idea that this is a practice that you ought to be doing in the first place.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on B2B Software's Educational Role

verses.title: B2B Software
verses.text:
- B2B software is not just about solving problems; it teaches users how to work.
- It is an accumulation of information that helps shape work processes.

💬 Quote

B2B software isn't just solving people's problems. It's also teaching them how to work.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky on Nan Yu's perspective on B2B software.

verses.title: Software Shapes Operations
verses.text:
- Recognize that choosing a tool changes how you operate, influencing your workflow.
- Be thoughtful about the way you want to work, instead of just seeking a solution to a problem.

💬 Quote

When you choose a tool, recognize it's going to change the way you operate and be thoughtful about the way we want to work versus just we just have a problem we want solved.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky on selecting tools.

verses.title: Double Triangle Framework
verses.text:
- Think of product management as a go-to market discipline, similar to sales and marketing. 
-  PMs should bridge the gap between builders (engineering, design) and sellers (sales, marketing).

💬 Quote

We think of product management as partially like a go-to discipline in the same way that sales and marketing are, right?
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on how Linear's product, sales and marketing team collaborate.

verses.title: Serious Deadlines
verses.text:
- Treat deadlines as P0 problems, making them real by prioritizing them above all else. 
- Cut scope as needed to ensure you can ship a functional product by the deadline.

💬 Quote

The only way to make deadlines real is to take them so seriously that they are basically like a P0 problem and like everything else has to not matter in comparison to the deadline [...].
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on deadlines.

verses.title: Job Interview Discovery
verses.text:
- During job interviews, figure out the burning problem the company needs to solve. 
- Make a case for yourself as the unique solution to that specific problem instead of being one of many general candidates.

💬 Quote

It's your job when you're in the interview process to figure out what that burning problem is, right? So like put on your discovery hat, right? And you go figure out like, what is the actual sort of like job to be done of the hiring manager when they're bringing on a new PM onto their team.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on how to approach job interviews.

verses.title: Deadline Strategy
verses.text:
- Minimize the number of deadlines, but take the ones you set very seriously. 
- Missed marketing or communication opportunities are lost forever; respect the rhythm.

💬 Quote

With marketing and communication with customers, you basically have a limited amount of opportunities to do so.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on the importance of respecting deadlines.

verses.title: Sakura Micron Pen
verses.text:
- Nan Yu found a Sakura Micron pen marketed as a bible study kit because it doesn't bleed through thin pages.
- This creative marketing targets people seeking specific qualities for studying their bibles.

💬 Quote

I found a package that said like a bible study kit. I was like, why is, why is this labeled bible study kit?
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on discovering a Sakura Micron pen marketed as a bible study kit on Amazon

verses.title: The Box Cut Tee
verses.text:
- Everlane turned a batch of defective men's t-shirts into a bestselling women's item, the Box Cut Tee. 
-  By cropping the shirts further, they salvaged inventory and discovered unexpected product-market fit.

💬 Quote

So, I mean, to be clear, I witnessed the creation. I don’t think I had a direct hand in it. But yeah, so I saw this advertisement the other day on Instagram for it’s called the Women’s Box Cut Tee.
— Nan Yu

Nan Yu on a bestselling women's shirt.

chapters.title: Naming expert shares the process behind creating billion-dollar brand names like Azure, Vercel, Windsurf, Sonos, Blackberry, and Impossible Burger | David Placek (Lexicon Branding)
chapters.description: David Placek, founder of Lexicon Branding, shares insights from over 40 years of creating memorable brand names like Sonos and Azure. He discusses a unique three-step process that can give companies an edge beyond marketing budgets. Discover why Microsoft once deemed Azure a “dumb name” and how polarizing opinions signal the right choice. Learn about the psychological vibes of letters, the diamond framework for naming, and why the relevance of domain names is changing in the AI era.

verses.title: Sonos Naming Battle
verses.text:
- David Placek fought hard for the name Sonos despite initial client rejection.
- He convinced the team to name it for the market and customers, leading to its success.

💬 Quote

When we presented Sonos, it was rejected because it's not entertainment-like. [...] I really believe in this name and I think it's the right for you.
— David Placek

David Placek on the Sonos naming story

verses.title: Naming Comfort vs Boldness
verses.text:
- Clients often believe they'll "know it when they see it" but rarely do.
- Comfort drives initial reaction, but boldness and discomfort indicate a strong name.

💬 Quote

Most clients, [...] absolutely believing with full confidence that they're going to know it when they see it. And the truth is it almost never happens.
— David Placek

David Placek on clients' initial reactions to naming

verses.title: Comfort vs. the Future
verses.text:
- People are comfortable with what has been successful before, leading to rejections of novel ideas like Harry Potter. 
- David Placek emphasizes that naming is about creating the future and the experience, not just reflecting the past.

💬 Quote

There is no power in comfort, not in the marketplace.
— David Placek

David Placek on the need to embrace discomfort when naming a brand.

verses.title: Azure's Initial Rejection
verses.text:
- Microsoft initially disliked the name Azure, calling it "a dumb idea." 
- Eventually, Azure became a $100 billion brand representing Microsoft’s cloud platform.

💬 Quote

One of the clients said, just a dumb idea [...] but um i think along the way they began to warm up to this. And now, it's a $100 billion brand.
— David Placek

David Placek on Microsoft's initial hesitancy about Azure

verses.title: Power of the Right Name
verses.text:
- A great name delivers asymmetric and cumulative market advantages.
- Unlike descriptive names, the right name becomes distinctive and builds long-term customer bonds.

💬 Quote

Your brand name, nothing's going to be used more often or for longer than that name [...] distinctiveness is what creates that cumulative advantage.
— David Placek

David Placek on why great brand names matter

verses.title: Lexicon's Scientific Naming Process
verses.text:
- Use a three-step process: identify behavior, invent creatively, then implement with support.
- Employ small diverse teams and linguists to generate unique, legally clear names.

💬 Quote

Our process is real. I'll break it down in three steps. First, we have to identify, then we invent, and then we implement.
— David Placek

David Placek explaining Lexicon's naming methodology

verses.title: Creative Team Briefings
verses.text:
- David Placek uses small teams of two or three people with different briefings to generate names.
- For example, when working for Microsoft, one team might think they are working for Apple or naming something entirely different.

💬 Quote

Each team gets a different briefing. One team knows everything about the project, but the other teams don't. We'll replace, if we're working for Microsoft, the second team thinks they're working for Apple.
— David Placek

David Placek on how he briefs different teams

verses.title: Windsurf Naming Case Study
verses.text:
- For Windsurf, different teams imagined unrelated products to spark creativity.
- The final name came from the team thinking in sports and flow metaphors, not technology jargon.

💬 Quote

We have three teams, and each team gets a different briefing. One team knows everything, the second thinks they're working for Apple, the third a bicycle or a cup. Most names come from the second or third team.
— David Placek

David Placek on Windsurf's naming approach

verses.title: Tangible Context
verses.text:
- David Placek uses the example of naming Windsurf to explain the process of assigning different contexts to different teams.
- When the product is intangible, he creates a tangible context for each team to make it easier to name.

💬 Quote

The name of Windsurf before it was Windsurf was Codium, right? And so it's all about a type of code or a process for coding. That's intangible. And even though we do an awful lot of technology work, it is still hard for us to kind of really get a hold of what that is, right?
— David Placek

David Placek on naming intangible products like Windsurf

verses.title: Psychology of Letters
verses.text:
- Letters emit psychological vibrations; for example, 'V' feels vibrant and alive.
- Naming benefits from understanding sound symbolism and letter impact.

💬 Quote

V is the most alive and vibrant sound in the English alphabet [...] B is one of the most reliable sounds. We leveraged these in BlackBerry and Corvette.
— David Placek

David Placek on the psychological effects of letters in naming

verses.title: Letter Vibrance
verses.text:
- David Placek explains that every letter of the alphabet has a specific vibrance; for example, 'V' is the most alive and vibrant sound.
- They used the letter 'B' in BlackBerry because it is one of the most reliable sounds.

💬 Quote

V, from our research that we've done, is the most alive and vibrant sound in the English alphabet.
— David Placek

David Placek explaining the feeling of each letter.

verses.title: Client Types
verses.text:
- David Placek prefers working with clients that aim to win, not just avoid losing. 
- Encourage direct clients to take the lead so that management rallies around the decision.

💬 Quote

We really want to work with clients that play to win, that want to win, that not just want to not lose in a marketplace.
— David Placek

David Placek on working with the right types of clients

verses.title: Diamond Framework for Naming
verses.text:
- Use the diamond exercise: map 'win', 'what you have to win', 'what you need to win', and 'what you need to say'.
- This deepens understanding before brainstorming names and unlocks creative potential.

💬 Quote

Draw a diamond. Top corner: win. Next angle: what do you have to win? Bottom: what do you need to win? Left: what do you have to say to win?
— David Placek

David Placek on a tool for founders to guide naming strategy

verses.title: Diamond Framework
verses.text:
- Draw a diamond shape and label the corners: 'win,' 'what do you have to win?,' 'what do you need to win?,' and 'what do you have to say to win'.
- Use this framework to think about experience and behavior in the marketplace.

💬 Quote

So the first thing I do is to say, okay, let's forget about developing the name for right now. And I will have them, and I think this is a good exercise for anybody...draw a shape of a diamond on a piece of paper in front of you.
— David Placek

David Placek explaining what to do if you have to come up with a name quickly.

verses.title: Diamond Framework
verses.text:
- When naming a company, draw a diamond and fill in the corners with 'win', 'what do you have to win?', 'what do you need to win?', and 'what do you have to say to win?'
- Defining 'winning' often varies among team members, so start there.

💬 Quote

The advice is draw a triangle. [...] Diamond. [...] And so on that next angle there, on the right side is what do you have to win already, right?
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky reflecting on David Placek's advice for coming up with a name.

verses.title: Suspend Judgment When Naming
verses.text:
- Suspend judgment and generate thousands of name ideas without early evaluation.
- Test names by asking: 'What does this name do for us?' not 'Do you like this name?'.

💬 Quote

Stop evaluating, suspend judgment, and speculate. Ask employees, 'What do you think this name could do for us?' not just if they like it.
— David Placek

David Placek advising on early-stage name brainstorming and feedback

verses.title: Choosing a Bold Name
verses.text:
- Focus on names that are new and different, both from your own list and what's already in the marketplace. This approach can give you the permission you need to choose something that feels scary but is ultimately bold.
- Remember that humans pay attention to what is new or different.

💬 Quote

We really, humans only pay attention to what is new or what is different, I should say, right?
— David Placek

David Placek on choosing a bold name

verses.title: Polarization Indicates Strong Names
verses.text:
- Polarization among your team over a name is a strong positive signal.
- If the team is comfortable and unanimous, you likely don't have a great name yet.

💬 Quote

If your team is comfortable with the name, chances are you don't have the name yet. We look for polarization and tension in a team arguing about these things.
— David Placek

David Placek on team reactions signaling naming strength

verses.title: Polarizing Names
verses.text:
- If some of your team loves a name and others hate it, that's a good sign.
- Look for polarization and intensity when choosing a name for a competitive marketplace.

💬 Quote

If half your team, or I don't know, some percentage of your team hates it, some percentage of your team loves it, that's a good sign.
— Lenny Rachitsky

Lenny Rachitsky on choosing a polarizing name.

verses.title: Don't Stress Domain Names
verses.text:
- Domain availability (.com) is less critical today and won't block great names.
- Focus on the right name first; use alternatives like prefixes or .ai domains if needed.

💬 Quote

The.com or URL address has become an area code [...] The principle is get the right name first, then work on the.com or its alternatives.
— David Placek

David Placek on domain name importance in the AI era

verses.title: Seek Naming Inspiration Elsewhere
verses.text:
- Seek creative inspiration outside your product category for naming ideas.
- Connecting unrelated concepts sparks fresh and relevant name ideas through synchronicity.

💬 Quote

Forget about your product. Pick magazines unrelated to your product and jot down words. Synchronicity will find names that relate unexpectedly.
— David Placek

David Placek on creative techniques for naming

verses.title: DreamWorks Name Admiration
verses.text:
- David Placek admires the name DreamWorks and wishes he had created it.
- It combines dream and work, creating an experience that embodies its entertainment mission.

💬 Quote

DreamWorks, just like Sonos, check all the boxes. Compound, dream, you expect something great from DreamWorks.
— David Placek

David Placek sharing a favorite name he didn't create

chapters.title: Pattern Breakers: How to find a breakthrough startup idea | Mike Maples, Jr. (Founding Partner at Floodgate, ex-Product at Silicon Graphics)
chapters.description: In this engaging conversation, Mike Maples, Jr., a renowned early-stage investor and co-author of "Pattern Breakers," shares his insights from backing transformative companies like Twitter and Lyft. He outlines the three elements that define breakthrough startup ideas and discusses the importance of thinking differently to avoid the comparison and conformity traps. Mike emphasizes the power of storytelling and the role of 'lighthouse customers' in crafting successful narratives. He also highlights how a little healthy disagreeableness can drive innovation in entrepreneurship.

verses.title: Embrace the Unfair Fight
verses.text: Building strong informal relationships with founders facilitates genuine discussions, allowing for a deeper understanding of their journeys. This approach enables the extraction of unfiltered insights that are critical for future actions. In a competitive landscape, startups must recognize that they cannot expect fairness; incumbents typically dominate. To succeed, startups should adopt unconventional strategies, leveraging unique advantages that are not immediately apparent to outsiders.

verses.title: Insight Drives Implementation
verses.text: Three key factors indicate the potential for breakthroughs: inflections, insights, and founder-future fit. A correct insight can lead to a first-mover advantage, even if initial implementations are flawed. Navigating towards the right implementation is crucial for achieving product-market fit. It's essential to recognize underlying forces that empower ideas to transcend current limitations. Inflections, defined as external events catalyzing significant shifts in behavior and thinking, play a vital role in this process, impacting the broader context beyond individual startups.

verses.title: Recognizing Inflections: The Turning Points of Change
verses.text: Inflections represent external events that trigger significant shifts in perception and behavior, crucial for transformational change in organizations. They differ from continuous improvement trends like Moore's law, as they denote a turning point that introduces new possibilities. A prime example is the launch of the iPhone 4S, which featured a GPS locator chip, fundamentally enabling services like Lyft by empowering a new operational model. Understanding these inflections is essential for tech startups to leverage new opportunities for innovation and adaptation.

verses.title: Seek the Lighthouse: Find Your Future Customers
verses.text: Identifying lighthouse customers—those who reside in the future and possess advanced technological insight—is crucial for business success. These customers highlight opportunities and can guide product development toward invaluable innovations. Cultivating relationships with these forward-thinking customers positions businesses to create solutions that fulfill untapped needs. Even if one lacks a visionary background, proactive engagement and a forward-focused mindset, like that of Maddie Hall, can facilitate this connection. Observing a company's or individual's technological leadership and their belief in future trends can serve as indicators for identifying these strategic lighthouse customers.

verses.title: Embrace Disruption, Understand Desperation
verses.text: Embracing technological advancements often determines a customer's willingness to adopt new solutions, as evidenced by historical skepticism around the cloud. Early adopters are typically characterized by their use of progressive tools like relational database servers and distributed applications, indicating a proactive approach to innovation. Moreover, the urgency of the customer's need plays a pivotal role in the viability of an idea. To truly resonate with potential users, ideas should aim to address the challenges faced by those in desperate circumstances, thus creating a compelling draw towards the solution provided.

verses.title: Empower Desperation, Not Comparison
verses.text: To generate impactful ideas, focus on the desperation of your target audience rather than comparing your offerings to existing solutions. The key lies in understanding that your product should address the urgent needs of customers who feel a strong pain or challenge. If customers can easily find alternatives or don't feel compelled to choose your solution, they are less likely to engage with a startup. Aim to create a unique offering that stands apart from the competition—like being the first banana in a market saturated with apples—so that customers feel an irresistible draw towards what you provide.

verses.title: Disruption Drives Change
verses.text: Founders must challenge the familiar and inspire change through pattern-breaking actions that contrast the current reality with a compelling vision for the future. This involves creating a strong divide between what is and what could be, fostering an emotional commitment among early adopters. Key components of this approach include forming movements that rally a minority's grievances against the majority, effective storytelling to communicate the vision, and embracing a disagreeable stance that invites necessary disruption. Successful market development is redefined as a movement rather than conventional marketing tactics.

verses.title: Elevate Beyond Comparison
verses.text: Successful brands embody a mission that transcends mere competition, channeling their efforts into a higher purpose that resonates deeply with their audience. By positioning themselves as part of a greater movement, they attract loyalty and engagement without directly undermining rivals. For instance, Tesla emphasizes a global shift toward sustainable energy rather than comparing itself to traditional automakers. Likewise, companies like Airbnb thrive by fostering unique experiences rather than criticizing the status quo, advocating for a richer lifestyle instead of disparaging hotels. This approach not only builds a strong narrative but also transforms the limitations of competitors into opportunities for connection and relevance in the marketplace.

verses.title: Bridge Between Worlds
verses.text: Self-actualization requires meeting individuals at their current stage while inspiring them with a vision of what could be. Effective storytelling involves a dynamic contrast between the current state and a compelling future, illustrating the shortcomings of the present. Utilizing concrete examples, such as Steve Jobs' iPhone launch speech, reveals the power of highlighting existing problems before presenting a transformative solution, thereby motivating an audience to engage with that vision of the future.

verses.title: Seek Believers, Not Doubters
verses.text: Investors fundamentally operate on belief; they either resonate with a vision or they do not. Founders often receive conflicting advice about their pitch decks from individuals who may not share a commitment to that vision. This leads to the creation of overly complex presentations, or 'Franken-decks,' which fail to effectively communicate the core idea to potential believers. Instead, founders should focus on identifying and pitching to those investors who are inclined to support their vision, ensuring these investors understand the compelling reasons to invest. The ideal pitch deck effectively conveys the core message without being clouded by extraneous information, particularly in the early stages before significant data or traction exist.

verses.title: Embrace Power with Balance
verses.text: The principles of power acquisition and influence transcend traditional frameworks and apply equally to innovators and disruptors within any organization. Successful pattern breakers must recognize that not everyone will appreciate their approach. The pursuit of power requires a balance of incentives and consequences, often described as wielding both the carrot and the stick. Relying solely on positive reinforcement may lack the necessary motivation for change, while the desire for acceptance can hinder decisive actions. Acknowledging this duality is essential for navigating the complexities of influence and leading transformative initiatives.

verses.title: Embrace Failure for Breakthrough Success
verses.text: Creating an environment that reduces disruptions can significantly enhance productivity, as illustrated by a team positioned in a different time zone to minimize interruptions. Additionally, companies should allocate a percentage of their profits to high-risk projects that have the potential for substantial rewards, while keeping these projects obscured from the main organization initially. This approach fosters a culture where a willingness to fail is essential for achieving groundbreaking successes.

verses.title: Honor Time by Doing Your Best
verses.text: The understanding of life's value lies in recognizing every day as a gift. The emphasis is on the importance of doing your best rather than being the best—acknowledging that while you may not excel every time, putting forth your best effort honors the time you are given. True achievement and excellence come from valuing every opportunity, as greatness is tied to the belief that every moment counts. This mindset fosters a deeper appreciation for life's many moments and challenges.

chapters.title: The AI-native startup: 5 products, 7-figure revenue, 100% AI-written code | Dan Shipper (co-founder/CEO of Every)
chapters.description: Dan Shipper, co-founder and CEO of Every, discusses the revolutionary impact of AI on startups.
