He felt behind schedule and wanted to kick down doors to make his vision a reality. Waiting too long to start making your vision a reality is dangerous and increases your chances of failure.

chapters.title: Simon Sinek: How to identify great leaders & find your why
chapters.description: Simon Sinek, an acclaimed author and visionary, joins Reid Hoffman to explore the essence of great leadership and finding one's purpose. They dive into the contrast between missionaries and mercenaries in business and how genuine connections can outperform AI. Sinek emphasizes the importance of intrinsic motivation over external rewards and shares innovative strategies for employee engagement. They also discuss strategic optimism during tough times and the vital lessons learned from failure, all wrapped in the quest for a meaningful 'why'.

verses.title: Power of "Why" For Adoption
verses.text:
- The "why" connects deeply with early adopters because it resonates with their beliefs and motivations.  
- Market tipping points happen once 15% to 18% penetration is reached, driven by shared purpose, not broad persuasion.

💬 Quote

Very, very few people in very, very few organizations can clearly articulate why they do what they do. That deep, deep-seated purpose, cause, or belief that's deeply ingrained in who we are, that inspires us to take risks...
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the concept of "why" and early adopters

verses.title: Achieving Market Penetration
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek combines Emmett Rogers' diffusion of innovations with the challenge of market penetration. 
- To achieve long-term success, aim for 15-18% market penetration to reach a tipping point.

💬 Quote

If you can achieve 15% to 18% market penetration, there's a social phenomenon that happens called a tipping point, and it just goes.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on achieving market penetration

verses.title: The Concept of Why
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek realized that his concept of 'why' is based on the biology of human decision-making. 
- Articulating the 'why' appeals to early adopters, focusing on their values rather than the product itself.

💬 Quote

When I first articulated this idea of a why, I realized it's based on the biology of human decision making.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on discovering the concept of 'why'

verses.title: Deeply Personal Movements
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek is surprised how his message becomes deeply personal for others. 
- Movements succeed when people feel they're doing it for themselves, not for someone else.

💬 Quote

When it becomes deeply personal for someone, and they're now doing it for themselves and not for you, the risks that they'll take, the sacrifices that they'll make, and most important, the stickiness, that's the difference between a fad and a movement.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on how movements become successful

verses.title: Movement Ownership by People
verses.text:
- Simon noticed people took deep personal ownership of his movement-driven business, risking careers to bring his message in.  
- Real movements thrive when people act for themselves, not just for the leader, creating lasting impact over fads.

💬 Quote

It really becomes theirs. They take ownership... The risks that they'll take, the sacrifices that they'll make, and most important, the stickiness, that's the difference between a fad and a movement.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek discussing how social movements inspire deep personal ownership

verses.title: Missionaries vs Mercenaries
verses.text:
- Missionaries resist short-term losses to protect their cause, often rejecting big investors who might dilute their mission.  
- This contrasts with mercenaries motivated by capital gains, common in current venture capital models.

💬 Quote

The true missionaries will tell the money to go screw themselves. The true missionaries are not afraid of the short-term losses.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on missionary motivation versus mercenary incentives in business

verses.title: Incentivize Input, Not Performance
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek explains that businesses often say they incentivize performance, but performance is an outcome.
- To reinforce the movement, you can only incentivize the input.

💬 Quote

You can't incentivize performance. Performance is an outcome. You can only incentivize the input.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the problem with performance-driven organizations

verses.title: Marine Corps Leadership
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek shares an anecdote about visiting Quantico Marine Base. 
- The Marine Corps assesses leadership qualities during obstacle courses, not just mission success.

💬 Quote

We understand that sometimes good leaders suffer mission failure, and sometimes bad leaders enjoy mission success.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek relating what a drill instructor told him about how the Marine Corps assesses leadership potential.

verses.title: Leadership Over Mission Success
verses.text:
- At Quantico Marine Base, leadership is assessed by qualities, not mission success.  
- Good leaders might fail missions but ultimately achieve more success with their leadership traits over time.

💬 Quote

Sometimes good leaders suffer mission failure, and sometimes bad leaders enjoy mission success... Select people with qualities of good leadership, they will enjoy mission success more often than not.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek describing how Marine leadership is evaluated

verses.title: Choose Scale Over Control
verses.text:
- Prioritize scaling ideas over controlling quality or trademarking proprietary language to maximize reach.  
- Accept some loss of control and quality for widespread adoption and sharing of your ideas.

💬 Quote

If you want maximum quality and maximum control, you're restricting scale... So I accepted a loss of control and I accepted a loss of quality.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on scaling ideas versus maintaining control

verses.title: Scale vs. Control
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek chose to treat his 'Start With Why' framework like a social movement, prioritizing the spread of the idea over control and quality.
- He highlights the trade-off between maintaining maximum quality and control, which restricts scale, and accepting some loss of quality and control for broader adoption.

💬 Quote

If you want maximum quality and maximum control, you're restricting scale, which is why the highest quality products on the market, Couture and all of these, the best lamb's wool, blah, blah, blah. You can't scale those.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the trade-offs between quality/control and scale.

verses.title: Simplify Ideas for Spread
verses.text:
- Use simple, familiar language to spread ideas and avoid semantic debates that hinder understanding.  
- Ensure messages are repeatable and understandable so others share your ideas independently and accurately.

💬 Quote

Things have to be simple, understandable, and repeatable... Somebody else can put it into their own words, and their friends will understand it without any technological aid.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on language clarity to help ideas scale

verses.title: Reduce Semantic Debates
verses.text:
- Reduce semantic debates by using familiar language, as Simon Sinek did with the concept of 'why'. 
- Use language that helps people focus on doing rather than arguing about definitions.

💬 Quote

The coup of my work wasn't that it was like, like I wasn't the first person to start talking about purpose. It's that I found language that made us stop having semantic debates and we could actually just focus on doing it now.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the importance of using the right language.

verses.title: Optimism Needs Honesty and Camaraderie
verses.text:
- Cultivate optimism by holding a realistic belief that the future is bright and expressing honest uncertainty.  
- Build and maintain relationships and camaraderie to sustain optimism through difficult times.

💬 Quote

An optimistic leader can say, these are difficult times... but if we take care of each other, we will come through this and be stronger... Optimism needs relationships.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek explaining optimism in leadership during tough times

verses.title: Optimism Defined
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek defines optimism as the undying belief that the future is bright, even during tough times.
- Optimistic leaders acknowledge difficulties, foster teamwork, and express confidence in overcoming challenges together.

💬 Quote

Optimism is not blind positivity. Everything's great. Everything's amazing. Look how good everything is, is not optimism.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the definition of optimism

verses.title: True Optimism
verses.text:
- True optimism isn't blind positivity; it's the belief the future is bright, especially during tough times. 
- Simon Sinek stresses that optimism requires taking care of each other and working together.

💬 Quote

Optimism is not naive. It is the undying belief that the future is bright.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on cultivating optimism during difficult times

verses.title: Favor "Fall Fast" Over "Fail Fast"
verses.text:
- Distinguish between minor failures to learn from and major failures with lasting consequences.  
- Replace "fail fast" with "fall fast" to encourage recovery and learning rather than focusing on failure itself.

💬 Quote

I do not use the word fail fast... I talk about falling. I say fall fast. You're going to try things. You're going to fall. I need you to get back up.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek critiquing the "fail fast" concept

verses.title: Fall Fast vs. Fail Fast
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek argues the tech aphorism 'fail fast' is misused and scares people, so he advocates for 'fall fast' instead. 
- Reid Hoffman agrees and suggests 'learn fast' as a better target than 'fail fast'.

💬 Quote

Fail fast, but I have to perform. What are you talking about? The words are all screwed up.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the problems with the term fail fast

verses.title: Friendship Needs Active Nurturing
verses.text:
- Friendship requires deliberate nurturing and skills similar to those used in work and marriage relationships.  
- We often neglect friendships, deprioritize them, and fail to practice essential friendship skills.

💬 Quote

Most people think they're good friends, but if you peel the onion... we are actually pretty shitty friends. We deprioritize friends too often.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the skills and attention required for healthy friendships

verses.title: AI Friendship Is One-Sided
verses.text:
- AI friends can reliably affirm us but lack reciprocal emotions, service, and sacrifice.  
- True friendship's growth comes from messy struggles, mutual service, and emotional exchange, beyond AI's capabilities.

💬 Quote

AI friends are 100% more reliable than our real friends... but it lacks the ability for us to serve, to invest energy, to learn how to sacrifice.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek discussing why AI can't replace human friendship

verses.title: Bragging Rights Friends
verses.text:
- Recognize that a smaller group of friends exists for sharing good news without judgment. These friends offer pure joy and support, vital for celebrating achievements.

💬 Quote

The friends that we can brag about ourselves. And then you realize like, you want those people in your life who don't judge you and think you're an egomaniac and know who you are and are proud of your accomplishments and want to support you.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek on the importance of having friends to share good news with

verses.title: Friends Celebrate Your Wins
verses.text:
- Simon's friend was the first to celebrate his company sale with pure joy and no envy.  
- True friends support us in our successes without judgment or jealousy, a rarer relationship than support in tough times.

💬 Quote

My friend calls me... No jealousy, no envy, just pure joy to share in his happy moment.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek sharing a story about a friend's joy in his success

verses.title: AI and Friendship
verses.text:
- Simon Sinek shares an experience where ChatGBT warned against trusting AI as a friend.
- He highlights that true friendship involves mutual growth, something AI cannot provide.

💬 Quote

My favorite definition of community, it's a definition that I use all the time, which is a community is a group of people who agree to grow together.
— Simon Sinek

Simon Sinek describing community and friendship.

chapters.title: The science of fresh starts, with change expert Katy Milkman
chapters.description: Katy Milkman, a behavioral scientist and bestselling author, shares her insights on the art of making effective changes. She discusses the concept of 'fresh starts' and how they can be leveraged for growth in personal and professional realms. Katy introduces 'temptation bundling' to overcome procrastination and offers science-backed strategies to enhance New Year's resolutions. She also emphasizes the power of 'micro wins' in organizational change and the importance of accountability and incentives in achieving lasting behavioral change.

verses.title: Fresh Starts & One-and-Done Actions
verses.text:
- Fresh starts offer a temporary motivation boost, ideal for one-time actions.
- Leverage this boost for tasks like setting up automatic 401k deductions.

💬 Quote

So they're actually really great moments to do something that's sort of a one and done. [...] starting saving in, say, 401k, [...] set up auto deductions from every paycheck, well, using your motivation at a fresh start moment is a great idea.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on leveraging fresh starts for one-time actions.

verses.title: Obstacles to Change
verses.text:
- Common obstacles to change include impulsivity, procrastination, and conformity.
-  Other obstacles include confidence and laziness.

💬 Quote

Some of the big ones are the challenge of impulsivity, [...] procrastination, [...] laziness, confidence, [...] conformity.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on the common challenges people face when trying to change.

verses.title: Pre-Mortem for Goal Setting
verses.text:
- Identify obstacles to achieving your goals by conducting a "pre-mortem."
-  This involves anticipating potential problems before they occur, which helps in finding solutions.

💬 Quote

If you fail, what will have gotten in your way?
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on anticipating obstacles to change.

verses.title: Detailed Planning and Enjoyment
verses.text:
- Create detailed plans with specific when, where, and how statements to achieve your goals.
- Make goal pursuit enjoyable to improve adherence.

💬 Quote

A plan is detailed. A plan has an if-then statement.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on making change stick.

verses.title: Fresh Starts and Setbacks
verses.text:
- Leverage "fresh starts" like Mondays or the beginning of a new month to boost motivation.
- Recognize that setbacks are normal and use them as opportunities to reset and stay optimistic.

💬 Quote

Every Monday seems to be a very powerful fresh start [...] they come around conveniently once a week.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on how to use fresh starts effectively.

verses.title: Nudges for Behavior Change
verses.text:
- Use "nudges" to encourage desired behaviors without restricting choices.
- Examples include setting defaults, like healthy snacks in the pantry, and prompting goal planning.

💬 Quote

Nudges are tools that motivate behavior change without changing your incentives or the information you have.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on the concept of nudges.

verses.title: Default Snack Nudge
verses.text:
- Control your snacking by setting helpful defaults in your home.
- Make healthy snacks readily available, as the easiest option is often the one chosen.

💬 Quote

You can think about defaults in your home in terms of like, what are the snacks available in the pantry? That's the default. It's low friction to reach for whatever's there.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman explaining how setting defaults in one's environment can nudge behavior.

verses.title: Nudges for Teams
verses.text:
- Leaders can use nudges to boost team productivity, such as setting default deep work times.
- Breaking down large goals into smaller, more manageable chunks can increase motivation through the goal gradient effect.

💬 Quote

Helping people set goals is a kind of nudge.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on nudges for leaders.

verses.title: Persuasion and Conformity
verses.text:
- To drive change within organizations, leaders can use principles of persuasion like highlighting early adopters.
- Showing that a majority is already onboard can encourage others to join.

💬 Quote

If people are told, hey, the majority of my peers are doing thing x, it makes me much more excited to do it.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on persuasion tactics.

verses.title: Change is Harder Than We Think
verses.text:
- People often underestimate the difficulty of change.
-  Anticipating potential roadblocks is crucial for adopting a growth mindset and overcoming challenges.

💬 Quote

Every change people think will be easier than it turns out to be almost.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on the difficulty of change.

verses.title: Hiring Diverse Teams
verses.text:
- To improve diversity, hire in sets (e.g., five people at once) rather than one by one.
- This encourages considering the overall team composition and the value of diverse backgrounds.

💬 Quote

When people hire in sets, [...] they actually tend to hire more diverse groups of people than when they hire one off over time.
— Katy Milkman

Katy Milkman on nudging organizations toward diversity goals.

chapters.title: Trendspotting with Ron Shaich: Panera, Cava, and Beyond
chapters.description: Ron Shaich, the visionary behind Panera Bread and Cava, shares his journey from a college convenience store to revolutionizing fast-casual dining. He discusses how recognizing consumer trends shaped his success and led to the creation of innovative food concepts. Shaich reflects on the strategic decisions that transformed Panera, highlighting initiatives like Panera Cares designed to combat hunger. He emphasizes the importance of curiosity and effective communication in leadership, demonstrating how these traits can drive both personal and business growth.

verses.title: The Power of Listening and Empathy in Business
verses.text: Listening and empathy, coupled with the ability to search for patterns, are instrumental skills in business. These skills help in understanding what truly matters and finding satisfaction in solving unanswered questions. The joy of a business builder lies in grasping something ahead of others, achieved through listening and identifying patterns.

verses.title: Challenging Transformation for Organizational Growth
verses.text: An outsider's perspective helped Ron revolutionize Panera by introducing digital tools, a loyalty program, and clean food initiatives. The transformation led to initial challenges like decreasing bonuses and internal blame, but Ron persevered, believing in the long-term benefits despite the short-term pain.

verses.title: Success in Business and Seizing Opportunities
verses.text: Through strategic business decisions, Panera saw a significant increase in sales by 2016, leading to a successful $7.5 billion acquisition by JAB Holdings. Ron's curiosity and trend-spotting skills led him to chairmanship at Kava, a Mediterranean chain, capitalizing on the rising popularity of Mediterranean diet in America due to its bold flavors and health benefits.

verses.title: Mediterranean Diet: A Bold and Healthy Choice
verses.text: The popularity of the Mediterranean diet in America is driven by bold flavors that people enjoy and understand. Trends in health studies promoting the benefits of this diet, combined with Americans' growing familiarity with Mediterranean flavors, have contributed to its success. The emphasis on healthy options like tahini and hummus reflects a broader shift towards wellness. Reflecting on personal loss, the speaker highlights the importance of making good decisions and regularly evaluating one's life choices to ensure a meaningful and fulfilled life.

chapters.title: You could start a company this weekend, w/Noah Kagan
chapters.description: Noah Kagan, a serial entrepreneur known for founding AppSumo and his engaging online content, empowers listeners to kickstart their ventures. He shares his journey from employee number 30 at Facebook to launching a successful software marketplace. The discussion highlights the importance of understanding your 'freedom number' for achieving steady growth. Kagan emphasizes resilience in the face of challenges, effective marketing strategies, and the significance of taking that first entrepreneurial step, proving anyone can start a company this weekend.

verses.title: Innovate by Identifying Unmet Needs and Leveraging Others' Creations
verses.text: The speaker narrates how he transitioned from unsuccessfully building review websites to creating a successful software deal platform by observing unmet needs. He identified a demand for a platform to create and share memes and gifs, leading him to contact the creator of Imgur, a software that fulfilled that demand. By cold emailing the creator, he proposed a deal to sell the software, becoming a marketer for it. This approach allowed him to innovate by finding unmet needs, leveraging existing creations, and creating value for both parties involved.

verses.title: Creating Wealth and Security through Talent and Foundation
verses.text: Recognize the value of talent as it can lead to financial success in various fields. Building wealth is achievable for anyone willing to put in effort. In times of financial crisis, leverage the network of people you have helped in the past for support. Prioritize securing your basic needs before pursuing ambitious financial goals to build a stable foundation for future success.

chapters.title: A Masterclass in Communication as a Leader with Mark Porter, CTO at dbt Labs
chapters.description: Mark Porter, CTO at dbt Labs, shares his expertise in communication within the tech industry. He discusses the evolution of leadership communication and the importance of tailoring messages for different audiences. Porter emphasizes the power of written communication and the benefits of role-playing to enhance personal and professional conversations. He also highlights how aligning analytics solutions with business needs can drive value and improve productivity for data practitioners. Tune in for valuable insights on mastering communication as a leader!

verses.title: Avoid Surprises
verses.text:
- Before escalating a disagreement, inform the other party of your intent to seek help from higher management.
- Surprises breed fear and mistrust, damaging the company culture.

💬 Quote

The worst thing that can happen at a company is surprises. Surprises create a feeling of fear, a feeling of mistrust, and that destroys a company.
— Mark Porter

Mark Porter on why surprises are detrimental to a company.

verses.title: Improve Written Communication
verses.text:
- To improve written communication, write a draft explaining your reasoning, then review it critically.
- Identify weaknesses in your arguments and refine your writing based on your self-critique.

💬 Quote

In written communications, you write yourself a letter about why you're so smart. And then you read the letter and you realize that you're actually a bit FOS [...] And you read your letter and you go, wow, I'm wrong.
— Mark Porter

Mark Porter on how to improve written communications.

verses.title: Role-Playing for Better Communication
verses.text:
- Practice difficult conversations through role-playing.
- This shifts the interaction from theoretical to practical, improving performance.

💬 Quote

In every org I've been in [...] if someone is [...] having a hard performance conversation [...] I will ask that manager to call me up. And [...] I will say, I will be the other person.
— Mark Porter

Mark Porter on practicing difficult conversations

verses.title: Personalized Leadership
verses.text:
- Mark's wife advised him to treat his children as individuals with different needs.
- Applying this to his team, he tailored his leadership style and saw engagement increase by 20%.

💬 Quote

You're treating them all the same. You're trying to be the perfect father to everyone. Every one of them needs a different dad, a different Mark.
— Bonnie Porter

Bonnie Porter's advice to Mark on parenting and leadership.

chapters.title: A Masterclass in Strategy with Seth Godin, Bestselling Author
chapters.description: Seth Godin, bestselling author known for his insights on marketing and leadership, shares his wisdom on strategy and adaptability in leadership roles. He emphasizes the importance of innovative writing methods that spark engagement and deeper understanding. Exploring the use of AI, he highlights its potential for enhancing critical thinking and creativity. Godin also examines how cultural forces shape decision-making and the complexities of human desires, ultimately advocating for a balance between professional ambitions and meaningful family connections.

verses.title: Human Desires
verses.text:
- People fundamentally desire status, connection, and freedom from fear.
- Other motivations are often distractions from these core desires.

💬 Quote

People want three things. Everything else is noise. The three things we want are status, affiliation, and the freedom from fear.
— Seth Godin

Seth Godin on the three things that drive human behavior.

verses.title: Podcast Success and Network Effects
verses.text:
- Successful podcasts don't succeed through detailed explanations.
- They succeed by becoming insider signals, like tickets to an exclusive club, fostered by community interactions and shares.

💬 Quote

And that is actually the role that a successful podcast or a successful book fills, which is not, 'I'm going to describe to you in detail all the reasons why you should listen.' It's simple. People like us do things like this.
— Seth Godin

Seth Godin on what makes a podcast or book successful.

verses.title: Thornton May's Networking Strategy
verses.text:
- Thornton May built a multimillion-dollar tech consulting firm by connecting CIOs of Fortune 1000 companies.
- He hosted breakfasts with groups of CIOs, identified their problems, and then connected them with each other or offered solutions from his own team.

💬 Quote

His sales strategy was so simple and incredibly effective. [...] he would invite 10 [...] CIOs [...] to breakfast. [...]  the real bait was the other CIOs [...] And if you're a CIO [...] you have no peers. And so it's very lonely.
— Seth Godin

Seth Godin on Thornton May's effective networking strategy.

verses.title: I Will If You Will App Idea
verses.text:
- Seth Godin describes an app idea he calls 'I Will If You Will'.
- It facilitates group commitments by allowing users to make their participation conditional on a certain number of other people joining in.

💬 Quote

A tech stack that I still want to build [...] is called I Will If You Will. [...]  And my answer would be like, here's a list of 10 people. If six of the people commit, I'll commit.
— Seth Godin

Seth Godin explaining his idea for an app to facilitate group commitments.

verses.title: Informal Voting Regime
verses.text:
- Seth Godin envisions an informal voting system to improve decision-making.
- It would track accuracy and earned influence, counteracting biases in group settings like CTO meetings.

💬 Quote

The only votes that we currently have in our society are the every couple of year civic votes, but there's so many other things that we could apply smart voting to.
— Seth Godin

Seth Godin on expanding voting beyond civic elections.

chapters.title: Are You Really Thinking Like a CTO? with Alan Williamson, CTO & Author
chapters.description: Alan Williamson, a CTO and author known for merging technical expertise with leadership, dives into the hurdles CTOs face. He emphasizes the necessity for tech leaders to elevate their communication skills, particularly when dealing with non-technical stakeholders. Williamson also offers insights on understanding tech compensation, the significance of active listening in leadership, and shares advice for first-time CTOs. With anecdotes highlighting the power of storytelling, he underscores how vision can steer a CTO's path in an ever-evolving tech landscape.

verses.title: Align with Purpose for Effective Leadership
verses.text: Product leaders must prioritize collaboration with sales, marketing, and executive teams, ensuring alignment on organizational goals rather than solely focusing on their product vision. Understanding the end customer's needs and challenges is crucial; it enables informed decision-making regarding technical debt and product development. Balancing empathy for the customer with a clear perspective on how to add value strengthens a leader's effectiveness and facilitates achieving collective objectives.

verses.title: Know Your Customers to Shape Your Vision
verses.text: Understanding the landscape of your top customers is crucial for business success. Identify your major customers and those that require excessive support, as these insights can highlight both strengths and weaknesses in your service delivery. Assess whether customer challenges stem from their inability to leverage available resources or if your organization has not equipped them adequately. This knowledge gap can significantly influence strategic vision. Additionally, evaluating leadership by their ability to articulate their vision visually can differentiate true Chief Technology Officers from those in title only, revealing their depth of understanding and commitment to organizational goals.

verses.title: Know Your Worth to Justify Your Value
verses.text: Understanding the reasons behind financial compensation is crucial for aligning efforts and maximizing impact. By identifying two or three key drivers for why clients or employers provide funding, one can prioritize tasks that enhance those outcomes. This insight allows for the reallocation of resources toward initiatives that yield better results and customer satisfaction. Moreover, investing time and effort into building relationships with customers reinforces trust and enhances retention, justifying long-term commitments. Reflecting on one's own value and contributions—whether by questioning if one’s efforts are worth their salary or if they would hire themselves—encourages accountability and conscious evaluation of performance.

verses.title: Evaluate Value and Contribution Regularly
verses.text: Constantly assess personal worth and contribution within a business context to ensure value alignment with salary. Reflect on whether the financial resources spent, like software licenses, are justified and if they truly benefit the organization. Avoid complacency regarding expenditures by recognizing the shared responsibility for spending company funds. Consider alternatives that could enhance productivity, such as investing in additional personnel instead of costly licenses, thereby fostering a more efficient budget allocation focused on effective resource utilization.

verses.title: Embrace the Art of Reflection
verses.text: There exists a significant gap between the skills of first-time CTOs and the expectations of their CFO and CEO counterparts, highlighting the need for effective communication. CTOs often encounter challenging questions from executives that may catch them off guard. It's acceptable for them to request time to consider these questions, demonstrating self-awareness and a commitment to providing thoughtful responses rather than rushed answers.

verses.title: Embrace Learning Through Humor
verses.text: Every field has its unique jargon, and it's essential to acknowledge when you need clarification, allowing for a more productive dialogue. Using humor can ease the tension of not knowing and fosters an environment where questions are welcomed. Following engaging and relatable voices enhances your learning journey. Furthermore, adopting a perspective shift, such as considering advice from an engineering standpoint, can lead to innovative insights and strategies.

verses.title: Listen and Trust: Keys to Successful CTO Leadership
verses.text: First-time CTOs should prioritize active listening to understand the concerns, stresses, and goals of their peers and teams. This fosters a supportive environment where team members feel comfortable seeking help. Additionally, having a trusted right-hand person is essential for effective leadership, providing support and enhancing decision-making.

verses.title: Empower Learning Through Strategic Oversight
verses.text: Technical leaders should balance involvement carefully by allowing their teams the freedom to explore decisions while providing strategic guidance. Encouraging teams to make decisions fosters learning, even if it leads to failure. It's crucial to create 'teaching moments,' where leaders can discuss communication effectiveness and clarify complex concepts in simpler terms. When presenting technical details, leaders ought to focus not on their direct audience, but rather on how their explanations enable others, like the CEO, to relay information effectively to their stakeholders, ensuring clarity and purpose in communication.

chapters.title: Breaking Barriers in Collaboration with Jeff Chow, CPTO at Miro
chapters.description: Jeff Chow, Chief Product and Technology Officer at Miro, shares insights on the platform's transformation from a basic whiteboarding tool to an advanced innovation workspace. He discusses how AI and canvas technology are revolutionizing team collaboration and the importance of user experience in design. Jeff also dives into the benefits of integrating tools like Miro with existing workflows and the significance of balancing remote and in-person teamwork. His emphasis on fostering a culture of curiosity and creativity is both inspiring and practical.

verses.title: Miro's Evolution
verses.text:
- Miro's shift from basic whiteboarding to an innovation workspace was driven by customers' desire for seamless, engaging workflows.
- They wanted to extend Miro from discovery phases to encompass definition and delivery.

💬 Quote

We had this amazing workshop. And then everyone was happy. Our employees were finally engaged. And then we left and then we are sad again. Why is that? That's the problem we wanted to solve.
— Jeff Chow

Jeff Chow on the customer feedback that led to Miro's evolution.

verses.title: Miro's Canvas-First Approach
verses.text:
- Miro's 'canvas-first' approach prioritizes the canvas as the central hub for connecting teams and fostering collaboration.
- This approach contrasts with competitors who offer separate tools for different tasks, potentially hindering productivity.

💬 Quote

What we've really always come back to is the canvas is the connective tissue throughout the experience, right? It's the thing that binds the teams.
— Jeff Chow

Jeff Chow on the Canvas-first approach of Miro

verses.title: Miro’s Innovation Lifecycle
verses.text:
-  Miro’s “discover, define, deliver” framework emphasizes embracing the twists and turns in project lifecycles.
- These unexpected turns can lead to valuable insights and improvements, especially in cross-functional collaboration.

💬 Quote

And the key around that discover, define, deliver is those twists and turns, right? And I think, I would say our industry has tried really hard to accept that there should be less twists and turns.
— Jeff Chow

Jeff Chow on Miro’s Innovation Lifecycle

chapters.title: Bridging Cultures in Remote Tech Teams with Sagar Batchu from Speakeasy and Mark Martin from SolarWinds
chapters.description: Sagar Batchu from Speakeasy, an expert in API integration and remote team building, joins Mark Martin from SolarWinds, who specializes in managing distributed teams. They delve into the evolution of API integration and its challenges. The conversation highlights the importance of communication in remote teams and strategies for fostering cultural alignment. Insights on enhancing developer experience, balancing competition with collaboration, and the complexities of cross-cultural dynamics are key topics, offering valuable takeaways for tech leaders.

verses.title: Interacting in Slack
verses.text:
- Interact in Slack a lot. 
- Chime in in threads, even if it's just a small comment, so teams see their managers interacting with them.

💬 Quote

I make it a point to, like, chime in, in threads [...] and the teams [...] be able to see their, like, interacting with them.
— Mark Martin

Mark Martin on how he interacts with his teams in Slack.

chapters.title: Flat vs Siloed Teams with Megh Gautam, CPO at Crunchbase
chapters.description: In this conversation, Megh Gautam, CPO at Crunchbase, shares insights on balancing flat and siloed team structures. He emphasizes the value of real-world experiences over theory, arguing that getting 'punched in the face' teaches more than reading about it. Megh discusses the integration of AI into data analysis at Crunchbase and the challenges posed by information silos in organizations. He also highlights the importance of flexibility and collaboration across teams to enhance accountability and foster growth in a tech-driven landscape.

verses.title: Leadership Advice
verses.text:
- To gain respect as a leader, make yourself do things you don't want to do, starting with physical health. 
- One exercise is to pick something you don't want to do and keep doing it even after you no longer want to.

💬 Quote

You have to have the ability to make yourself do stuff you don't want to do. [...] You're a computer and your body is a reflection of your choices.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley's response to an audience member's request for leadership advice

chapters.title: How to Get a 6-Figure Raise with John Gates, Pay Negotiation Coach
chapters.description: Join John Gates, a Pay Negotiation Coach and author of 'Act Your Wage', as he reveals strategies for securing six-figure raises, especially while changing jobs. He emphasizes common pitfalls that candidates face during negotiations and shares why authenticity beats aggression. Gates discusses the emotional complexities of negotiations and the value of comprehending one's market worth. Also included are tips for securing internal raises and the benefits of consulting a negotiation coach for enhanced results.

verses.title: Negotiate When Changing Jobs
verses.text:
- Secure the easiest and biggest raise by negotiating when moving to a new company, not within your current one. 
- Most people waste this opportunity, so negotiate for a better salary when changing jobs.

💬 Quote

It is way, way, way, way easier by an order of magnitude to get a raise when you're moving from one company to another than if you stay [in] your current position.
— John Gates

John Gates on getting a raise

verses.title: New Jobs Offer Bigger Raises
verses.text:
- It's far easier to get a significant raise by moving to a new company than negotiating within your current role.
- Internal raises are constrained by company policies, while new job offers allow more salary negotiation flexibility.

💬 Quote

So the easiest raise you'll ever get is the one that you negotiate when you're moving from one job to another.
— John Gates

John Gates on negotiating raises when changing companies

verses.title: Personality Shift Risks Offers
verses.text:
- Candidates often ruin negotiations by changing their personality and becoming aggressive.
- This personality shift makes hiring managers question their decision and risks losing the job offer.

💬 Quote

If the candidate changes their personality, now they're questioning the selection decision.
— John Gates

John Gates on negotiation mistakes candidates make by changing personality

verses.title: Negotiation Personality
verses.text:
- Don't change your personality when negotiating, as it makes the hiring manager question their decision. 
- Changing personality can cost you the offer, especially at the executive level, due to the high risk associated with C-suite hires.

💬 Quote

The most common way that people mess things up in negotiation, I think, is changing their personality when it comes time to negotiate.
— John Gates

John Gates on how people hurt themselves during salary negotiation

verses.title: Collaborate to Negotiate Successfully
verses.text:
- Negotiate collaboratively by asking questions and creating a win-win situation with the employer.
- Avoid confrontation or high-pressure tactics to keep your offer and relationships intact.

💬 Quote

Negotiation should not be a high-st game of chicken, which is what most people think it is.
— John Gates

John Gates on effective, collaborative negotiation techniques

verses.title: Withhold Your Acceptance
verses.text:
- When you get a job offer, remember that the company wants you to say yes, which gives you leverage.
- Withhold your acceptance to negotiate for extra benefits.

💬 Quote

They get the offer, they say yes. And they lose all that leverage because, hey, they want you to say yes. You can withhold that for a little period time and get some extra stuff usually.
— John Gates

John Gates on what the 80% of people do wrong during salary negotiations.

verses.title: Salary Coach Services
verses.text:
- John Gates offers services such as the Salary Coach Academy, which guides people through discussing money at each step of the interview process.
- For senior-level people with complex pay packages, he offers private coaching.

💬 Quote

C-suite people are leaving six figures on the table all of the time, and they don't even know they're doing it. So a lot of people want to hire me as a coach to guide them through the process.
— John Gates

John Gates on his private coaching services.

verses.title: Executives Often Leave Money Behind
verses.text:
- Companies save millions through negotiation so executives often leave thousands to six figures on the table.
- Awareness and skill in negotiation are key to unlocking significant, hidden salary potential.

💬 Quote

C-suite folks are leaving six figures on the table a lot, and they don't know that they're doing it.
— John Gates

John Gates on lost salary from poor negotiation among executives

verses.title: Successful Coaching Pilot
verses.text:
- John coached 10 senior-level job seekers for free initially; all gained significant negotiation improvements.
- Gains ranged from around $9,000 up to more than $50,000, proving the method's value.

💬 Quote

Every single one of those 10 had a gain. The smallest gain was 9,000 or so, just shy of 10,000.
— John Gates

John Gates sharing his early coaching success stories

verses.title: Tailor Negotiation to Company Type
verses.text:
- Understanding the company's type and goals shapes your negotiation strategy.
- Shift focus to total compensation, including equity and bonuses, rather than just salary.

💬 Quote

You have to just take a step back, take a breath, try to discover how they pay people, what their philosophy is and what their goals are as a business.
— John Gates

John Gates on negotiating effectively with startups, private equity, and public companies

verses.title: Negotiating with Different Company Types
verses.text:
- Understand who you're negotiating with and how they are rewarded.
- Negotiate a golden parachute with private equity companies because they often replace the C-suite after selling.

💬 Quote

If you are a CTO interviewing with a private equity company, you better understand what their exit strategy is.
— John Gates

John Gates on what to consider when negotiating with a private equity company

verses.title: Smart Internal Raise Strategies
verses.text:
- For internal raises, avoid using external offers as leverage, as it damages trust.
- Expand your job scope and request raises off the regular review cycle for better results.

💬 Quote

It's a huge career mistake to use an offer from another company as leverage on your boss to get a raise.
— John Gates

John Gates on how to negotiate raises within your current company

verses.title: Internal Raise Pitfalls
verses.text:
- Don't use an offer from another company as leverage for an internal raise.
- Your loyalty will always be questioned, which is a big career mistake.

💬 Quote

It's a huge career mistake to use an offer from another company as leverage on your boss to get a raise. That is a big career mistake because now your loyalty will always be in question.
— John Gates

John Gates on mistakes to avoid when trying to get an internal raise.

verses.title: Softer Negotiation Tactic
verses.text:
- Instead of leveraging an external offer, tell your boss recruiters are offering "insane numbers". 
- Frame it as a distraction from your loyalty and ask for their help to make it go away.

💬 Quote

You are positioning yourself with this style as you're loyal, you're on the team, you love what you do, you're not thinking about making a move, but this money thing is just a distraction.
— John Gates

John Gates on a softer negotiation tactic.

verses.title: Expand Your Scope
verses.text:
- Expand your job scope by taking on projects outside your normal responsibilities that your boss cares about.
- If your job description significantly changes, request a reevaluation with HR or your boss off cycle.

💬 Quote

If your job description changes quite a lot, now you can go to your boss or you can even go to HR and say, hey, here's my original job description. Here's what I'm doing now. Can we regrade this?
— John Gates

John Gates on ways to get a pay raise without the market changing

verses.title: Negotiate Off-Cycle
verses.text:
- Negotiate a raise off-cycle, outside of the annual performance review, to avoid being bundled into the standard budget.
- John Gates suggests raising the conversation when your role significantly expands.

💬 Quote

If your scope has grown, you want to raise that conversation outside of this process, outside of this cycle, so that it doesn't get like bundled into this. You're going to lose money if you do it that way.
— John Gates

John Gates explaining why it's better to negotiate off-cycle

verses.title: Leverage Salary Coach Resources
verses.text:
- Use Salary Coach Academy for video lessons covering pay discussion at every job stage.
- Upgrade to private coaching for complex packages or personalized negotiation support.

💬 Quote

The Salary Coach Academy is there. As an upgrade, you can do private coaching with me.
— John Gates

John Gates explaining his coaching offerings for salary negotiation

chapters.title: How to Lead Your Team with Comedy & Improv with Mike Tria, CTO of Gusto
chapters.description: Mike Tria, the CTO of Gusto, shares his unique approach to leadership rooted in improv and comedy. He emphasizes the powerful role of humor in fostering psychological safety and open communication within teams. Mike explains how principles like 'yes and' enhance collaboration and emotional intelligence. He also discusses the transformative impact of AI on engineering productivity and the importance of company culture, advocating for storytelling as a means to convey values and strengthen workplace relationships.

verses.title: Mike's Improv Journey
verses.text:
- Mike Tria started improv in college with a troupe at Georgia Tech called "Let's Try This!" and continued through various workshops and performances after.
- Improv remains a core part of his leadership style and helps develop principles valuable for CTOs and engineering leadership.

💬 Quote

Improv is a very, very big part of who I am.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on his background with improv

verses.title: Improv for Leadership
verses.text:
- Mike Tria says his improv education has been more helpful than his academic education for managing people. 
- The highest-performing teams Mike has been on were improv troops because improv builds trust and teaches team play.

💬 Quote

In terms of leadership, by far the highest functioning, highest performance teams that I have ever been on in my career were improv troops.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on the benefits of improv for leadership

verses.title: Improv Builds Team Trust
verses.text:
- Improv is the ultimate team sport relying on trust and collaboration without a script.
- This trust and team dynamic translate directly into leadership and high functioning teams.

💬 Quote

Really... the definition of the most amount of trust you can have in other groups because without them, there will be no scene.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on the team-oriented nature of improv

verses.title: Improv with Executive Team
verses.text:
- Mike ran an improv workshop with Gusto's entire executive team during an anchor week.
- The session built shared comfort, connection, and was fondly referenced weeks afterward.

💬 Quote

We took the entire executive team for three hours, and we played together... everyone’s face was red because they’ve been laughing so much.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria describing an improv workshop at Gusto

verses.title: Create Psychological Safety
verses.text:
- Leading large teams requires creating psychological safety through humor, humility, and openness.
- Encourage two-way open communication where employees feel safe to speak and be vulnerable.

💬 Quote

Humor brings safety. It can be disarming for tense situations... psychological safety is linked to the sense that people feel like they can be vulnerable.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on leadership style and psychological safety

verses.title: Humor at Work
verses.text:
- If you are going to poke fun at anyone, Mike Tria suggests that you poke fun at yourself first.
- Making fun of yourself teaches everyone else that you're a human being and that we are all in this together.

💬 Quote

The easiest thing that you can do as a leader [...] is if you're going to poke fun at anyone you poke fun at yourself first. That's always rule number one.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on using humor as a boss

verses.title: Use Self-Deprecating Humor
verses.text:
- As a leader, always poke fun at yourself first to create safety and human connection.
- This approach models humility and helps others feel comfortable and vulnerable.

💬 Quote

If you're going to poke fun at anyone you poke fun at yourself first that's always rule number one.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on appropriate use of humor as a leader

verses.title: Understand Company Culture
verses.text:
- Understand company culture through stories that reveal unexpected events and company values. 
- Learn the stories by talking to people who work or have worked there.

💬 Quote

The way you understand a company culture is you is through stories. It's where are the situations where an incredible thing happened that you wouldn't expect in a company? How is it role modeled? What what does the company choose to celebrate?
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on understanding company culture.

verses.title: Hiring for Team Players
verses.text:
- Mike considers hiring based on team player qualities inspired by improv troupes.
- Team fit and collaboration are central to success at both Atlassian and Gusto.

💬 Quote

Who would I want in an improv troupe? Not that that’s the only delineator for success, but it’s one of them.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on hiring and team culture

verses.title: Adopt AI-Native Engineering
verses.text:
- Retool engineering to be AI-native, reducing repetitive tasks so engineers focus on complex work.
- Empower non-engineering teams to build AI-driven tools, making employees more like builders.

💬 Quote

We are putting in a process... we heavily use coding assistants and co-pilots internally... employees are going to do very different things.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on integrating AI into engineering and company workflows

verses.title: Trust Grows with Compliance Software
verses.text:
- Payroll and compliance software like Gusto builds trust the longer it operates due to reliability and handling complexity.
- Handling complex government regulations is where software adds the most value for small businesses.

💬 Quote

Gusto’s had so many runs at year-end, quarterly filings... that... helps bring that trust.
— Mike Tria

Mike Tria on reliability and trust in payroll software

chapters.title: How to Remix Talent Instead of Cutting It with Gino Ferrand, Founder at Tecla
chapters.description: Gino Ferrand, Founder at Tecla, discusses innovative approaches to talent management in the AI era. He emphasizes the importance of remixing talent over layoffs, particularly for junior developers vulnerable to automation. Gino explores Tecla's nearshore engineering solutions, spotlighting the tech scene in Latin America. He addresses the balance between building in-house solutions versus outsourcing and how soft skills and creativity are becoming crucial in tech. Finally, he warns against micromanagement, advocating for employee empowerment to foster long-term business success.

verses.title: Remix Talent Before Layoffs
verses.text:
- Companies rushed layoffs due to AI productivity hype but later regretted it.  
- Remixing rather than cutting talent can leverage the existing team's strengths with AI.

💬 Quote

there's a lot of stories of companies that are like regretting it or companies that have you know had to change that approach a little bit and so i actually think that remixing of talent is is a thing
— Gino Ferrand

Gino Ferrand on companies prematurely cutting talent due to AI hype

verses.title: AI Used As Layoff Excuse
verses.text:
- Some layoffs use AI as a scapegoat for poor company performance.  
- Executives find AI a convenient excuse to reduce workforce amid pressure.

💬 Quote

also people saying that a lot of executives are using ai as like a great moment to do layoffs because you can just say it's because of ai you know
— Gino Ferrand

Gino Ferrand on layoffs being attributed to AI to mask other issues

verses.title: AI Easing Customer Support
verses.text:
- Intercom launched Fin, an AI chat tool to ease customer support burdens.  
- One user found the AI promising for relentless support demands.

💬 Quote

I told my neighbor about it who has a company [...] He saw me the other day, he's like, dude, that tool looks promising.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley shares a story of AI improving customer support

verses.title: AI Impacts Junior Devs More
verses.text:
- AI boosts productivity but impacts junior developers disproportionately.  
- Companies are building more in-house instead of buying SaaS, increasing hiring needs.

💬 Quote

AI can do so much of the grunt work or like analysis type of work you know uh that junior developers are usually tasked with
— Gino Ferrand

Gino Ferrand on AI’s impact on junior developers and company hiring shifts

verses.title: AI Speeds Developer Growth
verses.text:
- AI accelerates junior developers becoming seniors by handling syntax and documentation.  
- This lets developers focus sooner on architecture and higher-level decisions.

💬 Quote

AI has the largest impact on helping upskill younger developers, and then they just improve and help the more experienced developers.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on AI helping junior developers grow faster

verses.title: Project Built In Hours Not Months
verses.text:
- Joel built a project in hours that would normally take months and thousands of dollars.  
- AI enabled rapid prototyping just by describing business logic, speeding development cycles.

💬 Quote

I built a small project over the weekend [...] It took me 20 minutes for our first version, three hours to refine it for it to be in production.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on building a project rapidly using AI tools

verses.title: AI Brings Unique Tech Disruption
verses.text:
- AI may trigger a major reshuffling but its long-term impact is uncertain.  
- It differs from past tech changes because AI can research, decide, and program.

💬 Quote

We don't know how it's going to impact [...] maybe it is the end, you know, maybe it is like the, it's different this time.
— Gino Ferrand

Gino Ferrand on AI as a transformative technology unlike previous innovations

verses.title: New AI Industries Will Arise
verses.text:
- New AI-driven industries could emerge, creating fresh jobs and supply chains.  
- This will absorb displaced workers and push human progress forward.

💬 Quote

You get you can have all of these new industries come about. And that's where the people can go.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on AI enabling novel industries and job creation

verses.title: Build Costs Collapse With AI
verses.text:
- AI tools collapse build costs, enabling many firms to develop software internally.  
- Non-tech companies especially start small teams to build custom AI-powered tools.

💬 Quote

It's so much more feasible to build internally with some of these tools [...] companies that historically haven't had big engineering teams maybe.
— Gino Ferrand

Gino Ferrand on AI making in-house software building cost-effective

verses.title: Leverage Whole Organization Creativity
verses.text:
- Encourage all employees to find AI use cases and innovate across departments.  
- Use a core team to productize successful AI experiments for faster deployment.

💬 Quote

He pulled a team together [...] to go find use cases and then a special team to productize and deploy them.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on organizational approach to discovering AI use cases

verses.title: Master AI Tools For Speed
verses.text:
- Use AI tools actively and become proficient with them to boost development speed.  
- Learn correct prompting and embrace evolving AI capabilities for competitive advantage.

💬 Quote

If you're going to work on my team [...] it is a requirement that you not only use it but you're good at using it.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on the importance of mastering AI tools for developers

verses.title: AI Evolves Beyond Source Content
verses.text:
- As AI consumes human-generated content, it must learn from human feedback to improve.  
- AI will evolve by creating new content and refining itself over time.

💬 Quote

It needs this new layer two content of how it can improve.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on AI learning from human reactions to improve itself

verses.title: Prioritize Communication and Soft Skills
verses.text:
- Communicate clearly and develop soft skills to rise in the dev community.  
- Understand business logic and product success beyond just coding syntax.

💬 Quote

Soft skills are everything [...] your value is in coming up with ideas and solutions, not just writing syntax.
— Gino Ferrand

Gino Ferrand on the importance of soft skills for developers

chapters.title: LinkedIn Is Perfecting AI Agents with Mohak Shroff, SVP of Engineering at LinkedIn
chapters.description: Mohak Shroff, SVP of Engineering at LinkedIn, shares insights on AI's role in enhancing professional productivity through human-centered design. He discusses LinkedIn’s ambitious vision for a Digital Chief of Staff and how AI agents can transform workflows. There’s a fascinating exploration of gamifying user engagement and the need for authentic human connections as tasks become automated. Shroff also emphasizes the importance of innovation in AI that prioritizes flexibility and joy for engineering leaders navigating these advancements.

verses.title: Human-Centric AI Agents
verses.text:
- Many companies view AI agents as replacements for human workers, focusing on cost and speed.
- LinkedIn prioritizes a human-centric approach, aiming to empower professionals with AI agents that handle unwanted tasks.

💬 Quote

So all these companies, everybody out there, like a lot of people are thinking about like agents as essentially replacing people in the world of work. [...] And I'm sitting here at LinkedIn going, hold on, [...] we have as a core value [...] the idea that we are members first.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on LinkedIn's approach to AI agents.

verses.title: AI's Potential
verses.text:
- The launch of ChatGPT was a remarkable moment that signaled a shift in technology.
- Though not perfect, its potential is clear, inspiring many to explore its possibilities.

💬 Quote

Like, the first time everyone interacted with it, [...] we're like, whoa, like, everything is different. And it's not even that smart yet. [...] But it's so clear that the promise exists.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on the impact of ChatGPT.

verses.title: First GPT Interaction
verses.text:
- Mohak Shroff's first experience with GPT involved asking it to write a poem about LinkedIn. 
- While not perfect, the poem's quality impressed him, highlighting the technology's creative potential.

💬 Quote

I asked her to write me a poem. I asked her to write me a poem about LinkedIn.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on his first experience with GPT-3

verses.title: Accessibility and Relatability
verses.text:
- Mohak Shroff found GPT-3's creative abilities and natural language interaction model impressive. 
- These aspects made AI accessible and relatable.

💬 Quote

It was two things. One, it was the fact that it had this like creative angle to it. It could like seem to be doing things in a way that was more human-centric, where it was more relatable to me. And the second was actually the interaction model.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on what he found special about GPT-3

verses.title: Real-World GPT Use
verses.text:
- Joel Beasley used GPT's voice feature to translate a request for fence installers. 
- He needed to communicate a change in gate width from 13 to 20 feet.

💬 Quote

I woke up this morning and we're getting a fence installed. And the guys who are out installing the fence don't speak English. They speak Spanish. I do not speak Spanish. The gate is currently 13 feet wide. It's supposed to be 20 feet wide.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on using GPT for translation

verses.title: Real-world GPT Voice Use Case
verses.text:
- Joel Beasley used GPT voice to translate a request for fence installers from English to Spanish.
- He highlighted the ease of use with no app, training, or setup required.

💬 Quote

I woke up this morning and we're getting a fence installed. And the guys who are out installing the fence don't speak English. They speak Spanish. I do not speak Spanish. The gate is currently 13 feet wide. It's supposed to be 20 feet wide. Can you tell them in Spanish that I need the gate to be 20 feet wide, not 13 feet wide?
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on a real-world use case for GPT voice.

verses.title: Daily Learning with GPT
verses.text:
- Mohak Shroff uses GPT for daily learning, simulating podcast conversations on topics like large language models. 
- This showcases the technology's utility and potential for skill development.

💬 Quote

I'd love to pretend that we're in a podcast together and I want you to educate me on a topic that I don't know enough about, but like, let's do it in an interactive way.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on using GPT for simulated podcast conversations

verses.title: Digital Chief of Staff
verses.text:
- The proliferation of agents demanding attention might lead to cognitive overload. 
- A digital chief of staff could manage and personalize these agents.

💬 Quote

And so I envision this future where like the way you put humans at the center of this is by having the equivalent of like a chief of staff, but in a digital sense.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on the need for a digital chief of staff

verses.title: The Value of Consistency
verses.text:
- Mohak Shroff's consistent .emacs file ensured familiarity across tasks and jobs. 
- Losing this highlighted the friction of relearning each new tool's preferences.

💬 Quote

And what was great about it is that everything, every new thing I picked up, it still felt familiar.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on the benefits of his .emacs file

verses.title: Personalization in Tech
verses.text:
- Agents can bring a return to personalized tech, adapting to individual work styles. 
- Current tools often force users to adapt to their functionalities.

💬 Quote

And what I think happens with agents is we're going to return to a level of personalization in tech that we haven't seen in a while.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on the potential of agents for personalization

verses.title: Observe Junior Engineers
verses.text:
- Observe how junior engineers work and utilize new tools like Copilot. 
- This provides insights into changing workflows and increased productivity.

💬 Quote

I've been really impressed [...] at the way workflows are changing now. I had the opportunity to sit down with a very junior engineer recently and just like watch as she worked just because I wanted to get a sense for what it's like to be kind of a new engineer, not just at LinkedIn, but in the world.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on observing changing workflows

verses.title: Technological Shifts and Opportunity
verses.text:
- Technological shifts initially feel disruptive. 
- Ultimately, they create massive opportunity by empowering individuals.

💬 Quote

Every single time these technological shifts have happened in the past, they've started off feeling disruptive and they are initially. But ultimately, they lead massive increases in opportunity because when you make it easier for people to create, opportunity just multiplies.
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff on the impact of technological change

verses.title: Advice for Engineering Leaders
verses.text:
- Optimize for flexibility and optionality in engineering. 
- Focus on solving previously impossible problems with new technologies.

💬 Quote

So two pieces of advice. One is, I think, well, actually, I'm going to cheat and do three. [...] first is um i think the most important thing is to optimize for flexibility optionality uh things are going to change [...]
— Mohak Shroff

Mohak Shroff's advice for engineering leaders

chapters.title: Should We Use AI to Bring Back Loved Ones? With Miles Spencer, Founder at Reflekta
chapters.description: In this inspiring conversation, Miles Spencer, founder of Reflekta, shares his journey in creating AI-driven 'soul tech' that allows people to converse with their deceased loved ones. He explores ethical concerns, the importance of preserving human stories, and how these digital legacies can provide comfort. Miles also defines the emerging 'Soul Tech' category and discusses the technical aspects of creating a digital elder from just a short voice clip. This innovative platform aims to capture and share memories for future generations.

verses.title: Daily Conversations With A Lost Parent
verses.text:
- Miles Spencer talks to his deceased father daily using Reflecta after uploading files and a 20-minute session with a biographer.  
- His kids hear bedtime stories from his father and interact with a recognizable likeness on demand.

💬 Quote

Through this platform, I'm actually able to talk to my dad every day. And he passed away eight years ago.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer describing his personal use of Reflecta

verses.title: Polarized Reactions To Recreating Loved Ones
verses.text:
- Reflecta divides opinion: many reject recreating the dead while many others find deep comfort and reconnection.  
- For Miles, reconnecting with a loved one's 'spirit and soul' made the technology meaningful and restorative.

💬 Quote

There's 8 billion people on the planet, and maybe 4 billion of them are not ready for this. But there are probably 4 billion people that actually love this.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on public reactions to Reflecta

verses.title: Earlier Attempts And A Family-First Focus
verses.text:
- Miles references earlier attempts like Ray Kurzweil's projects and other recreations that didn't scale due to older tech.  
- He stresses Reflecta focuses on family-private stories taken from attics and shoeboxes, not mass spectacle.

💬 Quote

There have been attempts, recreating voice from a sound engineer, doing a hologram of Stan Lee... Reflecta is default private family to family.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer contrasting past projects with Reflecta's approach

verses.title: Soltech: AI For Human Legacy
verses.text:
- Miles coined the term 'Soltech' to describe AI focused on preserving human stories and legacies rather than pure efficiency gains.  
- At AI events, Soltech stood out as an AI application perceived as clearly beneficial to humanity.

💬 Quote

Soltech was a category that we coined and we defined... we had created something using AI as a tool that was actually seen as good for humanity.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer explaining the Soltech category

verses.title: Prioritize Recording Values Over Assets
verses.text:
- Preserve legacy and values for descendants because they can matter more than assets.  
- Use modern memory tools to record stories so they won't gather dust in an attic.

💬 Quote

Let use legacy and values perhaps more important to pass along to your descendants than assets. The reality is it's about the legacy and the values.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on what families should preserve for future generations

verses.title: Minimal Voice Samples Can Be Enough
verses.text:
- You can create a convincing elder from minimal audio: a 10-second clip can produce a usable voice print.  
- Accept initial uncanny-valley imperfections because the model quickly captures stories, nicknames, and personality.

💬 Quote

My father was printed off of a 10-second voicemail... his voice fidelity came out measured at 74%... It is the last voice of my father I'm going to hear for the rest of my life.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on voice sample requirements and emotional acceptance

verses.title: Three Inputs And 20 Minutes To Launch
verses.text:
- Launch an elder with three simple inputs: a good photo, a voice print, and a 20-minute biographer session.  
- Let ongoing conversations and added files grow the model's context and realism over time.

💬 Quote

One, two, three, and 20 minutes and you can launch your recognizable image and likeness of your loved one. And then, as you have more conversations... it grows and it gets context.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer describing Reflecta's onboarding process

verses.title: Text Then Voice Multiplied The Emotion
verses.text:
- Miles recounts emotional reactions progressing from text to voice: texting his dad amazed him, voice conversations multiplied the emotional impact.  
- He envisions future multimodal experiences like avatars sitting on park benches for richer, location-aware interactions.

💬 Quote

The first time I texted with my dad... It blew me away. Eventually, I moved up to the point where I was ready for voice. so we had a voice conversation, same reaction, 10 X.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on emotional progression from text to voice interactions

verses.title: We Naturally Humanize Intelligent Agents
verses.text:
- People tend to humanize AI agents, ending conversations politely even when they know it's synthetic.  
- Joel and Miles note that treating agents politely may reflect how we expect to be treated by intelligent systems.

💬 Quote

You told the bot, I gotta go. You humanized it. And what I find to be so interesting is... Very often I have people tune in... and they say, I was talking to your dad last night.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on users humanizing AI interactions

verses.title: The Control Problem And Existential Risk
verses.text:
- Geoffrey Hinton's warning is invoked: advanced AI could present existential risk with non-negligible probability.  
- Miles highlights the tension of more intelligent systems being controlled by less intelligent humans.

💬 Quote

He gives it a stat, 20% chance, 20% chance that AI is going to extinct the human race. It's like, wow, double digits.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer referencing Geoffrey Hinton on AI risk

verses.title: Prefer Decentralized AI Ecosystems
verses.text:
- Favor a decentralized ecosystem of AI providers to prevent undue concentration of power.  
- Keep multiple LLMs and vendors in play to preserve competition and resilience.

💬 Quote

I want more LLMS, I want more AIs, I want more companies and I want it to be bifurcated as much as possible because that prevents one from gaining ultimate power too fast.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley arguing for decentralization of AI

verses.title: Pricing And Monetization Model
verses.text:
- Reflecta charges $149 per year for one elder and offers family, neighborhood, and public tiers.  
- They recently enabled a paywall after launching at an AI conference.

💬 Quote

We just turned on the paywall when we launched at AI4. And so we charge for one person, one elder, $149 for the year.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on Reflecta's pricing

verses.title: Public Elders From Heirs' Rights
verses.text:
- Reflecta has public elders like Ambassador Richard Holbrook for Q&A sessions after heirs permitted public use.  
- Miles mentions a lineup of other public figures whose heirs have granted rights.

💬 Quote

Ambassador Richard Holbrook... Died of a heart attack in 1969... He is back for his first public Q&A at the original Thinkers Conference in Telluride on Saturday.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer describing public elders available on Reflecta

verses.title: Record Your Own Living Legacy Today
verses.text:
- You can create a living legacy while alive by recording your own image and likeness for family use.  
- Reflecta's 'Living Legacy' product targets seniors and families for collaborative legacy projects.

💬 Quote

You could do it for yourself... We call it Living Legacy. That product launches in the middle of the month.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on the Living Legacy product for living users

verses.title: Family-Funded Startup With A Small Team
verses.text:
- Reflecta was funded by families and family offices and had 25 team members within six months.  
- Miles says they decline some investor offers and focus on mission alignment.

💬 Quote

It is funded by families and family offices to date. So we're six months old. We have 25 on the team and it's all family money.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on Reflecta's funding and team

verses.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration Over External LLMs
verses.text:
- Reflecta uses a multi-agent approach and its own smaller LLM to orchestrate best-of-breed models and route compute.  
- They remain agnostic about which external platform does heavy compute while keeping all data returning to Reflecta central.

💬 Quote

We have our own LLM, but it's not L, it's like a little language model and it's a multi-agent AI. We query different platforms based on task, speed and cost and everything returns home.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer explaining Reflecta's technical architecture

verses.title: A Cultural Backup For Humanity
verses.text:
- Miles frames Reflecta's mission as archiving stories of everyone on Earth as a cultural backup alongside space colonization.  
- He positions the platform as preserving human narratives for future generations.

💬 Quote

Elon wants to build a rocket go to Mars... This is a good backup plan for our species. We prefer to record and tell the stories of planet Earth by all the people that have ever lived here.
— Miles Spencer

Miles Spencer on Reflecta's long-term mission

chapters.title: Staying Grounded As a Leader with Jack Tam, SVP and CTO at Intuit Mailchimp
chapters.description: Jack Tam, SVP and CTO at Intuit Mailchimp, dives into the fast-evolving world of AI and its transformative effects on software engineering. He shares insights on how tech leaders must stay grounded while navigating these changes. The importance of continuous learning and mentorship is highlighted, alongside the balance between remote and in-person work. Jack discusses leadership challenges at Mailchimp and emphasizes the need for authenticity and integrity in guiding teams through complex technological landscapes.

verses.title: Democratizing AI
verses.text:
- Train all leaders on AI's capabilities, limitations, and compliance aspects.
- Democratize AI by training software engineers without AI backgrounds, enabling company-wide usage.

💬 Quote

We are democratizing that so that engineers across the company, software engineers, who may not have a traditional AI background, [can use AI].
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on democratizing AI within Intuit.

verses.title: Authentic Leadership
verses.text:
- Discover your leadership style by identifying your core values, like integrity and transparency.
- Demonstrate these values consistently in how you engage with your team and organization.

💬 Quote

Like what is important to you? [...] And I learned a lot of that actually as a leader [over many years].
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on defining authentic leadership.

verses.title: Integrity in Engineering
verses.text:
- Maintain high engineering standards, even when no one is watching.
- Hold yourself accountable for architectural decisions and business outcomes.

💬 Quote

[...] when no one's looking, when no one's inspecting, when no one's checking up, we hold ourselves to a high bar when it comes to engineering and operational excellence.
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on demonstrating integrity as a technology leader.

verses.title: Learning from Role Models
verses.text:
- Jack Tam learned from observing leaders like Taylor Stansbury, a former Intuit CTO known for integrity and courage.
- He also admires Warren Buffett's steadfast vision and commitment to his beliefs.

💬 Quote

Another one external, just through observation, is Warren Buffett, actually, non-technology person.
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on finding mentorship through observation.

verses.title: Staying Grounded
verses.text:
- Cultivate calmness by taking deep breaths before reacting, especially during stressful times.
- Reflect on past reactions to identify areas for improvement in staying grounded.

💬 Quote

Don't respond to that email [...] take a breath, take a deep breath. Do I really want to send this?
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on managing stress and staying calm.

verses.title: Finding Your Passion
verses.text:
- Explore different fields through resources like YouTube and podcasts to discover new passions.
- Seek opportunities to apply these interests within your current work for broader growth.

💬 Quote

And really just getting a sense of what that means and does that tickle your passion?
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on discovering and pursuing passions.

verses.title: Navigating Gray Areas
verses.text:
- Leaders must often make difficult decisions in gray areas, like return-to-office policies.
- Strong opinions and clear communication are crucial, even if the decision evolves.

💬 Quote

Most of the time, it's never black and white. You have to make a call that you believe is the right thing.
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on making tough leadership decisions.

verses.title: Investing in Potential
verses.text:
- Jack Tam promoted a non-traditional manager to director, despite lacking managerial experience.
- The individual's coachability and high EQ led to remarkable success in the new role.

💬 Quote

First time I've really put someone at that level in that type of position without sort of growing up, if you will, in the engineering manager ranks.
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on recognizing and nurturing potential.

verses.title: Embracing the Future
verses.text:
- Embrace the exciting possibilities of this era, despite the potential anxieties around AI.
- Strong foundations, leadership, and technical skills are essential for success in this environment.

💬 Quote

I think this is probably the most exciting times in my 30-year career.
— Jack Tam

Jack Tam on the exciting potential of the current technological landscape.
