chapters.title: Taking the Stage & Conquering Communication with Geoffrey Huck, Public Speaking Coach
chapters.description: Geoffrey Huck, a public speaking coach with a background in software engineering, shares his journey from battling social anxiety to helping tech professionals communicate effectively. He discusses overcoming communication barriers and the importance of conveying complex ideas to non-technical audiences. Geoffrey highlights authenticity in speaking, the power of storytelling, and developing personal narratives. He also touches on the emotional aspects of public speaking, encouraging listeners to align their actions with their values and embrace fear as a path to growth.

verses.title: Communication struggles in tech
verses.text:
- Geoffrey Huck struggled with communication during his 12-year software engineering career.
- His social anxiety and shyness hindered him despite his technical skills.

💬 Quote

communication was my biggest struggle. I used to have social anxiety.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on his past struggles with communication

verses.title: Pressure in high-stakes calls
verses.text:
- Making important calls was difficult and draining for Geoffrey Huck.
- He would often feel like he lost his intellectual abilities because of the pressure.

💬 Quote

making an important [call] was so draining and so difficult. And the worst is that when you finally get the courage to do it and you somehow lose your intellectual abilities because the pressure is just so high
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on the challenges of important calls

verses.title: CTO's role in communication
verses.text:
- CTOs must abstract technical details for non-technical audiences.
- Their job is to explain the impact of technology, not the intricate workings.

💬 Quote

if you are like the CTO of a company, your job is to abstract the technical to the other people who don't understand it.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on the CTO's communication role

verses.title: Explaining tech to non-technical audiences
verses.text:
- Focus on the impact, scalability, limitations, and cost of technology.
- Avoid explaining the intricate technical details unless specifically asked.

💬 Quote

What you need to know is what's the impact of this tech? What can you do with the technology? What are the limitations in terms of scalability, number of users, speed?
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on simplifying technical explanations

verses.title: Natural body language
verses.text:
- Focus on owning your words and expressing yourself authentically.
- Natural body language will follow automatically when you're truly engaged with your message.

💬 Quote

if you know and if you use your words and if you really own what you say body language is very natural and automatic.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on achieving natural body language

verses.title: Finding your unique voice
verses.text:
- Start by speaking about silly topics to find your natural way of expressing yourself.
- Once you own your unique voice and style, focus on the technical aspects of public speaking like eye contact and voice modulation.

💬 Quote

What we start with, with my customers, is to be able to speak, but about very silly topics. The idea is to find your way of expressing yourself that is natural, that is very you.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on the starting point of his coaching

verses.title: Experimenting with styles
verses.text:
- Try many different speaking styles, including animation, questions, emotions, and physical movements.
- Experimenting helps you discover what resonates with you and overcome fear.

💬 Quote

trying trying many things trying everything that you can think of it can be very creative you can do what you like other speakers to do things that you so typically try to do animation, like warming up the room.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on finding your style

verses.title: Crafting your story
verses.text:
- Crafting your own story is difficult; ask someone to help.
- By understanding your obstacles, achievements, and motivations, others can create a compelling narrative for you.

💬 Quote

Pro tip, crafting your own story is extremely hard. [...] But whenever you talk to someone and they share their story with you, you try to dig a little bit.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on how to craft a story

verses.title: Focus on the audience
verses.text:
- Focus on the message's value to the audience, not on yourself.
- Put the spotlight on the audience to reduce insecurities and improve connection.

💬 Quote

people don't care that you're smart or not They don't care if it's you or another person. What they care about is the message and what value, what message, what things that they can take that might change their life.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on the key to effective communication

verses.title: Embrace fear
verses.text:
- Seek out situations that involve fear to expand your comfort zone.
- Using fear as a compass can lead to significant growth as a speaker.

💬 Quote

look for fear. And I try to find settings, environments, things to do in which there's some fear.
— Geoffrey Huck

Geoffrey Huck on using fear for growth

chapters.title: The 9 Skills Every Tech Leader Needs in the Age of AI with Andrea Iorio, Author of "Between You and AI"
chapters.description: Andrea Iorio, author of "Between You and AI" and former Chief Digital Officer at L'Oréal, discusses essential skills for tech leaders in a rapidly changing landscape. He emphasizes the importance of asking better questions and the value of reverse mentoring with Gen Z. Andrea shares insights on becoming antifragile through smart mistakes and highlights the need for emotional skills like empathy in an AI-driven world. He warns against over-reliance on AI, advocating for a balance that keeps human engagement at the forefront.

verses.title: Skills Gap Between Tech And Humans
verses.text:
- Andrea Iorio saw a gap: lots of technical AI content, few frameworks for human skills needed to thrive with AI.
- He wrote Between You and AI to map skills humans must develop to stay complementary to AI.

💬 Quote

There was not really a compelling theory that was around what are the skills needed to thrive in the age of AI.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio on why he wrote Between You and AI

verses.title: Caronte At L'Oréal
verses.text:
- As L'Oréal's Chief Digital Officer Andrea acted like Caronte, ferrying people from fear of digital to seeing it as an ally.
- His goal was to democratize digital across the company so the role wouldn't be needed forever.

💬 Quote

I was finding, you know, very, very similar role to Caronte. I had to take people that didn't understand digital or was not believing in digital or were fearful of replacement to a place where they understood that, of course, digital and AI now is an ally.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio describing his role at L'Oréal

verses.title: Three Pillars Framework
verses.text:
- Iorio organizes nine skills into three pillars: cognitive, behavioral, and emotional, each with three skills.
- The model highlights where AI substitutes humans and where humans must double down.

💬 Quote

So mapping out basically what any professional or any leader does, I understood that basically we do things across three big dimensions.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio explaining the book's structure

verses.title: From Processing To Data Sense Making
verses.text:
- Data sense making shifts focus from processing data to critiquing data AI was trained on.
- Leaders must spot biases in training data to avoid automated harm in decisions like lending.

💬 Quote

It's not anymore about our ability to process the data, but it's about our ability to critically analyze the data sets AI has been trained with.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio on data sense making

verses.title: Practice Reperception Regularly
verses.text:
- Ask better questions rather than hoarding answers; cultivate reperception to update beliefs with new evidence.
- Give up past 'good decisions' when new information shows they no longer fit exponential change.

💬 Quote

Re-perception is disability not only to make good decisions, but to give up the good decisions of the past in order to open ourselves to new information, new changes.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio defining reperception

verses.title: Reverse Mentoring At L'Oréal
verses.text:
- At L'Oréal Andrea reversed mentoring: juniors taught leaders about Gen Z habits and TikTok as a search engine.
- Exposing leaders to fresh perspectives broke entrenched assumptions and sparked new strategies.

💬 Quote

We would have the young talents... mentor our leaders about things such as... how Gen Zers make decisions, consumer decisions based on TikTok videos.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio describing reverse mentoring example

verses.title: Embracing An Internal Influencer
verses.text:
- A young L'Oréal employee ran a YouTube channel reviewing competitors, which initially worried leaders.
- The company embraced her channel and leveraged internal talent instead of banning external reviews.

💬 Quote

She had a YouTube channel where she was doing product reviews... of brands that were not L'Oreal brands... at first, this created an internal problem... eventually the decision we made [was to promote it].
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio recounting the influencer channel decision

verses.title: AI Multiplies Creative Choices
verses.text:
- Research shows AI boosts human creativity by expanding the number of choices rather than deciding for us.
- AI acts as a companion that surfaces broader options early in ideation and brainstorming.

💬 Quote

AI is a great boost for human creativity, not because it makes decisions on our behalf, but because... maximizes the number of choices.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio citing MIT study on AI and creativity

verses.title: The Google Maps Effect
verses.text:
- Tools like Google Maps reduce cognitive engagement and create dependency, a phenomenon called 'AI obesity.'
- Overreliance risks losing the ability to act independently when tools fail.

💬 Quote

If we rely on Google Maps to go to work, well, maybe we'll find a solution, we'll improvise. But if we have all the processes... running on AI, then we'll have a problem.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio on reduced brain engagement with tools

verses.title: Waze in Brazil
verses.text:
- Andrea Iorio shares an example from Brazil, where Waze and Google Maps might suggest dangerous routes. 
- This is because they don't factor in safety issues, so people need to stay aware and be in the loop.

💬 Quote

Waze and Google Maps, they tend to not be able to interpret, for example, the dangers in some areas. They optimize the route, right? But only the human is able to discern whether, oh, this is bringing me to an area I should not get because Brazil has some safety issues.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio discussing an example of how Google Maps may not be useful.

verses.title: Always Review AI Outputs
verses.text:
- Keep humans in the loop: review, grade, and correct AI outputs rather than blindly accepting them.
- Teach people to audit AI results immediately to preserve judgment and safety.

💬 Quote

We should not only teach our kids to solve their homework, but we should teach them to grade it, review it, correct it.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio referencing educational advice from Carnegie Mellon professor

verses.title: Experiment With Smart Mistakes
verses.text:
- Use freed capacity from automation to run more experiments and accept controlled failure as learning.
- Design 'smart mistakes' whose learning value exceeds their cost and track those costs closely.

💬 Quote

We now have more time available to do something that seems counterintuitive... which is to make more mistakes, but not all type of mistakes... smart mistakes.
— Andrea Iorio

Andrea Iorio on antifragility and smart mistakes

chapters.title: The AI Conversation CTOs are Having Behind Closed Doors with Robert Duffy, CTO at HealthEdge
chapters.description: In a thought-provoking discussion, Robert Duffy, CTO at HealthEdge, sheds light on the secret conversations CTOs are having about AI. He reveals the challenges of integrating AI across large organizations and balancing innovation with regulatory compliance in healthcare. Duffy delves into the transformative potential of AI for automating healthcare claims and enhancing efficiency. He also addresses the cultural shifts needed to foster innovation while adhering to ethical guidelines in technology. A must-listen for anyone interested in the future of AI in healthcare!

verses.title: Demo Fatigue Hinders Real Adoption
verses.text:
- Robert Duffy says demos haven't advanced people's ability to deploy AI at scale. 
- Teams see potential but struggle to convert flashy demos into production ROI.

💬 Quote

People are struggling to take that flashy demo and flashy use case and actually put it into production environments and scale it and get it into a system that's delivering real ROI.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on demo fatigue and production gaps

verses.title: AI Migration Is A Big Organizational Shift
verses.text:
- Robert Duffy compares moving work to AI to a cloud migration in scale and impact. 
- He argues organizations need the same processes, tooling, and wave planning for AI migrations.

💬 Quote

The migration of work from human cognition to AI and agentic systems is probably about as large as a cloud migration would be in terms of moving things from legacy on-premise infrastructure to cloud infrastructure.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on treating AI shifts like cloud migrations

verses.title: Real Estate Software Taught Implementation Is Everything
verses.text:
- Joel Beasley recounts selling software where 100-person teams shrank to 10 after rollout. 
- He learned implementation, customer service, and behavior change dominate success.

💬 Quote

The only thing they really focused on, like 90% of the conversation was on implementation, customer service, rolling out, behavior change.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on implementation and behavior change

verses.title: Engineer Roles Will Shift Toward Supervision
verses.text:
- Robert Duffy acknowledges fear that AI will eliminate jobs but sees role shifts. 
- He expects engineers to supervise AI systems and focus on architecture, review, and prompt design.

💬 Quote

We're strongly of the opinion that what it means is that a lot of the work that software engineers are doing is going to be, you know, supervising AI energetic systems.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on how engineering work will change

verses.title: One Project: Eight Weeks To Three Hours
verses.text:
- Joel describes a project that used to take eight weeks and $7,000, finished in three hours with AI. 
- He says the result met his high standards and proved AI's immediate practical value.

💬 Quote

A very specific process I would go through that would take me eight weeks and about $7,000 and involve roughly three different freelancers... I was able to achieve in about three hours.
— Joel Beasley

Joel Beasley on rapid productivity gains using AI

verses.title: Embed AI Across The Whole SDLC
verses.text:
- Robert Duffy recommends embedding AI agents across the entire software lifecycle to get scale and ROI. 
- He shows upstream LLMs writing PRDs and downstream agents producing tested code to compress cycles drastically.

💬 Quote

To scale it, you actually have to embed it, this AI and these agents all the way along the software development lifecycle, right from the point of ideation, all the way through to the point of testing.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on scaling AI by integrating it across the SDLC

verses.title: AI Scaling
verses.text:
- To scale AI, embed AI agents across the entire process. This includes writing product requirements documents, user stories, acceptance criteria, tests, and code.
- Point solutions produce good demos, but embedding AI end-to-end scales it.

💬 Quote

To scale it, you actually have to embed it, this AI and these agents all.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on scaling AI in software development

verses.title: Pilot Used Pickles To Spark Adoption
verses.text:
- Robert Duffy ran a 53-person Claude pilot with gamification, leaderboards, and a jar of spicy pickles prize. 
- That grassroots energy produced shareable projects and AI champions on every team.

💬 Quote

One of the things that I did was offer a jar of spicy pickles to a person who produces the best tools.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on gamifying AI adoption with pickles

verses.title: Turn Experiments Into Reusable Tools
verses.text:
- Start with low-cost experimentation in chat UIs, then formalize the best prompts into shareable system prompts. 
- Convert those into integrated tools before moving to production to avoid overengineering early.

💬 Quote

There's this path from low-cost experimentation... and then you have a very, very clear path to, okay, let me put this into a tool that I can share with other people.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on the experimentation-to-production path

verses.title: Create A Work Transformation Team
verses.text:
- Create a dedicated team to convert project prompts into production-grade tools and monitor them. 
- Have that team manage interfaces, model drift, usage, and ROI metrics for each tool.

💬 Quote

We basically have an ai and ml platform tool we call them the work transformation team... take that system prompt... and turn that into a tool that you can basically take visio charts and it will produce pull requests that a human can then go and review.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on the role of a work transformation team

verses.title: AI Can Cut The Cost Of Manual Claims
verses.text:
- HealthEdge focuses on automating claim adjudication and reducing manual work in the 5% of complex cases. 
- Duffy says AI can collate scattered data and let humans execute SOPs faster and more accurately.

💬 Quote

One of the biggest things that we can do with ai ... is remove the burden on the human for finding and collating that information from all these different systems and putting that information right in front of them.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on automating manual claims processing

verses.title: Scale Pilots In Tranches, Not Floodgates
verses.text:
- Scale pilots in tranches and short runs rather than opening floodgates to everyone at once. 
- Prioritize high-energy participants to create momentum and avoid low-engagement churn.

💬 Quote

If you just open the floodgates, what you'll find is, you know, 50% of the people that come through those floodgates won't have, you know, any fire or any passion and they'll just have been given another tool.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on how to scale AI pilots across an organization

verses.title: Lead Change By Building Movements
verses.text:
- Don't rely on mandates alone to drive change; bring teams along and explain the why. 
- Build movements and communities instead of forcing adoption with top-down edicts.

💬 Quote

You can have a mandate. You can have a letter from Jeff Bezos... But as soon as you pull it out and try and use it, it loses all of its power.
— Robert Duffy

Robert Duffy on leadership and driving organizational change

chapters.title: From PayPal Intern to Starting 4x Billion-Dollar Companies - Joe Lonsdale Interview
chapters.description: In this engaging conversation, Joe Lonsdale, Managing Partner of 8VC and an early investor in Anduril, shares how a single PayPal internship sparked his journey to founding multiple billion-dollar companies. He discusses the importance of hiring for raw IQ, nurturing talent into unicorn founders, and leveraging insights from finance legends like Peter Thiel. Joe also dives into how to spot business opportunities, the dynamics of big swings versus base hits, and the significance of staying focused on core missions.

verses.title: The Power Law of Success
verses.text:
- Focus on one core reason for pursuing a venture, as multiple reasons often indicate a lack of a strong foundation.
- Startups rarely have multiple equally successful products or revenue streams, especially early on.

💬 Quote

Usually there's one reason that dominates everything else. [...] And that's true inside of companies as well.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on building a focused strategy.

verses.title: The Importance of Setting Goals
verses.text:
- Seek out and learn from the smartest people you can find, even early in your career. 
- Prioritize working with those who have interesting intellects and can push your thinking.

💬 Quote

Peter [Thiel] to me had by far the most interesting intellect. He was clearly just one of the smartest people I ever met.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on seeking mentors.

verses.title: How to Hire the Right People for Your Machine
verses.text:
- Prioritize hiring for raw talent, ambition, and hard work over expertise, especially in early-stage ventures or complex fields. 
- Look for individuals with high IQ and a drive to learn and iterate.

💬 Quote

I'd much rather hire for talent and ambition and hard work than hire for expertise.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on hiring strategy.

verses.title: The Power of Networking
verses.text:
- Over 20 people who worked directly for Joe Lonsdale have started successful companies. 
- He attributes this to a combination of their inherent talent and the frameworks they learned while working together.

💬 Quote

Over 20 people who've worked directly for me who've started huge companies. [...] we've given them useful frameworks.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on his success in fostering entrepreneurs.

verses.title: Strategies for Building a Company
verses.text:
- Identify opportunities by spotting conceptual gaps in existing industries or processes. 
- Evaluate not just the potential improvement, but the feasible path to achieve it, considering existing actors and incentives.

💬 Quote

You're looking for conceptual gaps in the world. [...] Here's where it could be if it was using [...] the right new technology or the right new incentives.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on how to identify new business opportunities.

verses.title: How to Be a Sass Entrepreneur
verses.text:
- Focus on solving hard problems in industries not yet saturated with technology. 
- Avoid creating yet another restaurant app or to-do list app.

💬 Quote

You want to kind of take your advantage is we have a really tough technology culture. Let's figure out how to expose it to something that's not likely to have seen that before.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on finding opportunities in underserved markets.

verses.title: How to Start a Business Process Outsourcing Company
verses.text:
- Joe Lonsdale suggests automating legal work around trusts and estate management with AI, which is a large market. 
- Another idea is Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) for local governments.

💬 Quote

I think that the process of starting a trust and [...] the actual legal work around this stuff could be very automated with AI.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on potential business ideas.

verses.title: The Importance of Networking
verses.text:
- Joe Lonsdale attributes his entrepreneurial success to a combination of factors, including a strong talent network and effective iteration. 
- He emphasizes generosity with co-founders and early employees, fostering shared ownership and commitment.

💬 Quote

I have a huge talent apparatus. [...] We have a huge advantage of [...] knowing the people who run a lot of big industries [...] and having good relationships with them.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on his entrepreneurial advantages.

verses.title: How to Make a Million Dollars From Investing
verses.text:
- Invest time in developing skills and building relationships, rather than solely focusing on monetary investments, especially early in your career.
- Find your passion and what you're good at, then find people who excel in those areas and learn from them.

💬 Quote

If you want to make a million dollars, it's not about how you invest your money. It's about how you invest your time.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on how to build wealth.

verses.title: The Five Standard Deviation Talent
verses.text:
- Surround yourself with people who are better than you to challenge and inspire growth. 
- Being around top talent helps you recognize and appreciate true expertise, which is valuable for building successful teams.

💬 Quote

You can't really appreciate a talent unless you're within two standard deviations of it. [...] if you yourself are second or third standard deviation, you really start to appreciate that.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on the importance of being around talented individuals.

verses.title: How to Build a Successful Company
verses.text:
- Build a top-tier engineering culture that attracts and empowers the best engineers.
- Focus on creating an environment where engineers are respected, have autonomy, and are key decision-makers.

💬 Quote

[T]he ones that are attracting the very best engineers [...] were able to try 10 times as much really quickly. [...] [W]e need to build somehow a really top engineering culture.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on the importance of a strong engineering team.

verses.title: How to Hire a CEO
verses.text:
- When approaching institutions or potential partners, consider the "casting" and present yourself in a way that builds credibility and trust.
- Leverage introductions and existing relationships to position your venture strategically and gain access to decision-makers.

💬 Quote

[S]ubstance is important, but [...] you can't lead with substance because substance is not taken seriously. So you have to have the substance to win, but you have to have all the trappings of why they're taking it seriously.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on how to present yourself effectively.

verses.title: Peter's Advice for Startups
verses.text:
- Peter Thiel's strategic guidance was crucial in Palantir's early stages, particularly in avoiding partnerships that could limit growth.
- He provided financial support and helped the team focus on long-term value creation over short-term gains.

💬 Quote

Peter was [...] key on [...] he stopped us from a couple early partnerships. [...] [A] lot of partnerships will cap your upside.
— Joe Lonsdale

Joe Lonsdale on Thiel's contributions to Palantir.

chapters.title: How Gary Vee Predicts The Next Facebook (Every Time)
chapters.description: Gary Vaynerchuk, a serial entrepreneur and social media expert, shares insights into his most successful investments and the importance of maintaining a cash safety net. He discusses the evolving landscape of attention as the key business currency and reflects on valuable lessons learned from figures like Zuckerberg and Logan Paul. Gary highlights the unique experience of networking at adult camps and offers a fascinating take on the distinct mindsets of millennials versus billionaires, all while revealing his entrepreneurial journey and growth.

verses.title: Attention is the Universal Currency
verses.text: The key insight is that attention is the most mispriced asset that impacts everyone. Attention is essential for various purposes like raising funds, being present, promoting a podcast, selling products, or even parenting effectively. It is a universal asset needed by all, and the ability to capture attention is crucial in today's world of mass fragmentation.

verses.title: Anticipate Technological Evolution and Adaptation Strategy
verses.text: Anticipate the shift in technologies like VR and wearable tech as the future landscape. Recognize the potential for contact lenses with advanced functionalities. Emphasize the importance of adapting to emerging technologies, like social media over search engines. Strategize by building towards the future and being open to acquiring current technologies.

verses.title: Gratitude in the Micro
verses.text: Gratitude should not just be about the big things such as family and health but also about finding moments of gratitude in everyday situations like being in an elevator. Being grateful in the micro can be a practice that shifts one's perspective and leaves a lasting impact.

verses.title: Choosing Positivity in Daily Moments
verses.text: The key to happiness lies in finding gratitude and positivity in everyday moments, such as appreciating the fresh air, silly antics of loved ones, or a sunny day. By making a conscious effort to focus on what one has rather than what is lacking, happiness can be achieved without the need to accumulate or achieve external milestones.

chapters.title: How to Get Rich (without luck, talent or a trust fund)
chapters.description: Shaan Puri, an entrepreneur and wealth-building expert, shares insights on achieving financial success. He emphasizes mastering a money-making skill, urging listeners to choose one and practice intensely. Shaan also discusses the importance of equity over trading time for money and highlights patience in seeing results. He introduces three business levers—code, content, and capital—to turn skills into income. Lastly, he advocates for surrounding oneself with like-minded people to unlock opportunities, showing how these principles can lead to a wealthier life.

verses.title: Master One Core Money Skill
verses.text:
- Pick and master one of four money-making skills: selling, making, designing, or hunting.  
- Practice daily at work and study nights to accelerate mastery.

💬 Quote

Rule number one, you need one money-making skill.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on choosing a primary money-making skill

verses.title: Master a Skill
verses.text:
- Pick one money-making skill out of selling, making, designing, or hunting. 
- To master your skill, practice it every day.

💬 Quote

You need to pick one of these skills and master it.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on how to build wealth

verses.title: Mr. Beast's Idea Generation
verses.text:
- Mr. Beast decided at age 12 to master making YouTube videos. 
- He would open a dictionary, pick a random word, and force himself to think of 20 video ideas using that word.

💬 Quote

At the age of 12, I decided my skill was going to be making YouTube videos.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri recounting a story Mr. Beast told him about mastering a skill

verses.title: MrBeast's Relentless Practice Routine
verses.text:
- MrBeast practiced idea generation and execution from age 12 by forcing 20 video ideas per word and incrementally improving.  
- He made hundreds of videos before earning significant subscribers or income.

💬 Quote

At the age of 12, I decided my skill was going to be making YouTube videos in his case.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on MrBeast learning to make videos

verses.title: Buffett's Hunt For Mispriced Businesses
verses.text:
- Warren Buffett became a master hunter by reading massive corporate manuals cover to cover to find mispriced businesses.  
- Over his career, a handful of good investing decisions produced most of his success.

💬 Quote

Buffett used to take the entire Moody's manual...and he would read it cover to cover.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on Warren Buffett's hunting method

verses.title: Combine Skills To Multiply Outcomes
verses.text:
- Mastering one money skill can lead to millions; mastering two rare complementary skills can lead to billions.  
- Aim to be top 20% in two uncommon skill combos rather than top 1% in one.

💬 Quote

If you master two of them, that's a path to billions.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on skill combinations and leverage

verses.title: Combine Uncommon Skills
verses.text:
- Become top 20% at two uncommon skills instead of trying to become the top 1% at one thing. 
- An engineer who's also good at marketing is more valuable because this combination is rare.

💬 Quote

What you need to do is become top 20% at two things that are not common.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on combining uncommon skills.

verses.title: Don't Rent Your Time, Own Equity
verses.text:
- Stop renting your time with a salary and instead own equity through business or investment.  
- Convert your skill into ownership rather than trading hours for dollars.

💬 Quote

Renting out your time is not a path to get rich.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on why salaried time-renting caps wealth

verses.title: Turn Skill Into Scalable Business
verses.text:
- Build a business from your skill by focusing on code, content, or capital.  
- Productize your work so one asset sells to many customers without trading more hours.

💬 Quote

There's three things, three words to know. They all start with the letter C. Code, content, or capital.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on three scalable business routes

verses.title: Alex Hormozi's Content-To-Scale Example
verses.text:
- Alex Hormozi turned selling skill into books, courses, and videos and generated massive launch revenues.  
- He monetized content rather than selling consulting hours to scale income exponentially.

💬 Quote

His last book launch sold 3 million copies...he made a hundred million dollars in a single weekend.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on Alex Hormozi monetizing content

verses.title: Hunting With Other People's Capital
verses.text:
- Shaan's cousin learned multifamily real estate, then did deals buying $40M of property using investor capital.  
- He hunted great deals and used other people's capital to scale returns quickly.

💬 Quote

In his first year doing his own deals, they bought about $40 million of real estate using down payments or equity from other investors.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on his cousin's real estate approach

verses.title: Act Quickly But Wait For Results
verses.text:
- Be willing to wait; plant the seed and let your effort compound instead of expecting instant returns.  
- Act with urgency but be patient for results: 'impatient with action, patient with results.'

💬 Quote

You can't plant a seed in the soil, and then the next day come dig it up and scream at it, why aren't you growing yet.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on the importance of patience

verses.title: Proximity Accelerates Success
verses.text:
- Move closer to the 'white hot center' of your industry to speed learning and create luck.  
- Live and work near ambitious peers to gain introductions, ideas, and faster progress.

💬 Quote

Proximity is power...you want to be as close as you can to the white hot center of action.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on moving to centers of industry activity

verses.title: One-Way Ticket To San Francisco
verses.text:
- At 24 Shaan bought a one-way ticket to San Francisco with no plan because he wanted smarter peers and opportunity.  
- Living with founders created connections, introductions, and rapid learning despite rough conditions.

💬 Quote

I bought a one-way ticket to San Francisco with no plan.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on moving to San Francisco to change his odds

verses.title: Flip The Odds With Repeatable Rules
verses.text:
- Follow the four rules and you flip the odds from business failure to likely success over time.  
- Founders fail at ideas, not at persistence; learning rate and consistency matter more than single ideas.

💬 Quote

Startups, individual business ideas do fail, but founders don't. If you keep going...you will not fail.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri on persistence and flipping startup odds

verses.title: Steve Martin's 40-Year Banjo Bet
verses.text:
- Steve Martin committed to playing banjo for 40 years and improved enough to win Grammys within a decade.  
- Long-term commitment beats short-term impatience when learning hard skills.

💬 Quote

I'm going to play the banjo for 40 years because I can't imagine that somebody who's been playing the banjo for 40 years still sucks at it.
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri recounting Steve Martin's mindset on long-term practice

verses.title: Steve Martin's Banjo
verses.text:
- Steve Martin, struggling to learn the banjo, was told he didn't have natural talent. 
- He decided to play for 40 years, believing consistent practice would eventually lead to mastery.

💬 Quote

Instead of trying to be great at the banjo today, let me just make a decision. He said, I'm going to play the banjo for 40 years because I can't imagine that somebody who's been playing the banjo for 40 years still sucks at it.

chapters.title: Pieter Levels:  Making $2.7M A Year With No Employees
chapters.description: Pieter Levels, a solopreneur and digital nomad, shares how he earns nearly $3M a year without any employees. He discusses the balance between travel and work while building multiple successful online businesses. Pieter emphasizes the importance of transparency in entrepreneurship and reveals his unique strategies for scaling and investing. He also dives into the digital nomad lifestyle, advocating for meaningful relocations over constant travel. Additionally, Pieter explores minimalism and the concept of the giving economy to enrich community connections.

verses.title: All Your Revenue Together in One Chart
verses.text:
- Pieter Levels' revenue chart reveals slow growth until 2019, reaching $600k-$700k annually.
- Revenue increased significantly to $2.5M after 2020, likely due to the pandemic's impact on remote work.

💬 Quote

if you look at the chart, it's kind of like [...] it doesn't really go anywhere, and i was like thinking, ok, this is bullshit. [...] But generally wasn't very, it wasn't like a star up where
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels on his revenue growth trajectory.

verses.title: You Can Only Surfeit
verses.text:
- Recognize that overnight success often takes ten years of work and iteration, just like Pieter's journey.
- Progress typically involves periods of rapid growth followed by plateaus.

💬 Quote

a ten year over success, [...] by the time you hear about something, you don't know the ten years of kind of toiling and tweaking and iterating that it took before the big kind of races happened
— Shaan Puri

Shaan Puri discussing the misconception of overnight success.

verses.title: Will Your Numbers Be Bigger?
verses.text:
- Many indie hackers think small, focusing on modest passive income rather than substantial businesses.
- Pieter Levels is an exception, demonstrating greater ambition with larger revenue numbers.

💬 Quote

one of the things that bothers me about this indi hacker movement is [...] people think pretty small
— Sam Parr

Sam Parr expressing his concern about limited ambition within the indie hacker community.

verses.title: Are You a Millionaire?
verses.text:
- Many indie hackers who surpass $1M in annual revenue quickly seek VC funding to scale further.
- Pieter Levels is an exception, preferring a "chill" approach and not wanting to build a large company.

💬 Quote

Once you pass a million dollars a year, they will switch [to thinking] 'let's go big. Let's go become a billion-dollar company.'  And I think I'm the exception [...] I don't want to be building a big company.
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels explaining why there aren't many multi-million dollar indie hackers.

verses.title: How to Build a Million Dollar Business
verses.text:
- Aim for $20k monthly revenue, as a $20k/month business often implies a $1M valuation.
- This is a more achievable goal than aiming for a million-dollar business from the start.

💬 Quote

if you're app does 20,000 a month in revenue, you're probably already a millionaire
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels advising on a realistic target for aspiring entrepreneurs.

verses.title: Hotel Rooms
verses.text:
- Pieter Levels sees potential in serviced apartment hotels for remote workers.
- He highlights the benefits of kitchen access, comfortable living spaces, and reliable service, unlike inconsistent Airbnbs.

💬 Quote

- So i've been living like, i used to live in hostels, [...] then i started, like private rooms in hotels. Then the rooms got a little bit more luxurious because i had more money. And then i started discovering like, apartment hotels. And it sounds like bullshit, but it works so well
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels sharing his personal experience and identifying a business opportunity in apartment hotels.

verses.title: What Do You Do With Your Money?
verses.text:
- Pieter Levels invests most of his profits in ETFs, focusing on long-term growth.
- He also holds cryptocurrency, acknowledging the inherent risks.

💬 Quote

i read a blog post by [...] and he's like, i just put everything in etfs. So vanguard etf, s&p 100. [...] But i also invest in crypto, [...] bitcoin, ethereum
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels describing his investment strategy.

verses.title: What Are You Doing To The Head Of Vanguard Etf China?
verses.text:
- Pieter Levels strongly believes in Asia's future, viewing Western negativity towards China as a blind spot.
- He invests in Asian markets despite acknowledging the associated risks.

💬 Quote

- I'm heavily invested in Asia, because i believe in Asia. I believe in the future [...] so much stuff is very futuristic
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels expressing his optimism about Asian markets.

verses.title: Is It Real That You Just Don't Play In Anything?
verses.text:
- Pieter Levels maintains a low monthly spending of $4k-$5k despite earning substantial income.
- He prioritizes a simple life focused on experiences and time with friends.

💬 Quote

i spend about four or five k a month or something like that. So most of it is just [...] it's just this nice chill life
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels discussing his monthly expenses and lifestyle.

verses.title: Is It Worth the Money?
verses.text:
- Prioritize spending on high-quality food and experiences rather than material possessions.
- Material goods provide only temporary happiness, while experiences create lasting memories.

💬 Quote

good food, [...] organic, free roaming [...] grass fat kobe beef, [...] happy animals, organic vegetables
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels explaining his spending priorities.

verses.title: What'sy levels?
verses.text:
- Sam Parr shares an anecdote about Sam Corcos, the founder of Levels, who lives minimally, owning only essential clothing and electronics.
- Corcos exemplifies extreme minimalism by owning only what he wears, buying and donating as needed.

💬 Quote

he only owns the clothes that i'm wearing right now, [...] plus another pair of underwear, a jacket, this bag, and my laptop
— Sam Parr

Sam Parr recounting his encounter with Sam Corcos and his minimalist lifestyle.

verses.title: What Are Some Misconceptions About Your Business?
verses.text:
- People assume Pieter Level's websites and apps are simple, but they are more complex than they appear.
- Many features and edge cases require attention behind the scenes, making replication difficult.

💬 Quote

- Yes, i think generally people think that every website or app or company that they're customer of is more simple than it is, because you can't see behind the hoods. And it's actually way more complicated than you think
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels explaining the hidden complexity behind seemingly simple products.

verses.title: Do Customers Sport Forinseff Am So Yo?
verses.text:
- Pieter emphasizes the importance of transparency in business.
- Sharing both successes and failures can create a more wholesome and honest business environment.

💬 Quote

the reason i'm so transparent is [...] to show other people that you can build a nice india company [...] by sharing every up and downs of it
— Pieter Levels

Pieter Levels advocating for transparency in business.

chapters.title: The Side Hustle King: 11 Easy Businesses Anyone Can Start
chapters.description: In this engaging discussion, Chris Koerner, known as the 'side hustle king' and a serial entrepreneur, shares a treasure trove of unique business ideas anyone can start right in their backyard. From installing in-ground trampolines to mobile fuel delivery and even selling novelty ice cream, Chris emphasizes practical, niche markets. He also explores innovative concepts like robot manicure machines and secret pickleball courts. Plus, listeners get tips on leveraging AI for small business growth, ensuring profits keep rolling in!

verses.title: Porch Pumpkins Origin Story
verses.text:
- Heather Torres built Porch Pumpkins by decorating her own porch and monetizing requests from friends after posting on Instagram.
- She scaled to ~$1.5–2M/year with reusable inventory, 20% COGS, and upsells like removal and coaching.

💬 Quote

She's doing seven figures a year across three months.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner describing Porch Pumpkins success

verses.title: Validate Seasonal Services Fast
verses.text:
- Start simple: copy a viral design, film a time-lapse on your phone, and post to local mom groups to test demand.
- Use organic short-form videos to find best creatives, then scale with targeted paid ads to your zip code.

💬 Quote

I'm going to film a time lapse just using my iPhone, and I'm going to post it to all the local mom groups... and start doing it for people in the comments.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner on how to launch a porch-decorating side hustle

verses.title: Sport Courts Are A New Backyard Trend
verses.text:
- Sport courts are emerging as the 'next pools' with rising searches and limited supply, creating local demand.
- Selling the dream (Backyard Funhouse) enables upsells into outdoor kitchens, pools, and landscaping.

💬 Quote

If you look at the keyword tool the searches are exploding for this and the supply is not keeping up with demand.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner on demand for sport courts

verses.title: In-Ground Trampoline Installation
verses.text:
- An excavator operator charges about $5,000 to dig an in-ground trampoline hole after buying the trampoline for ~$4,500.
- Trampolines.com links buyers to certified installers, creating a lead channel for local dig-and-install services.

💬 Quote

They get paid five grand to dig out pools. And it takes him a day.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner recounting costs and installer demand for in-ground trampolines

verses.title: Turn Liquidation Into Local Flips
verses.text:
- Use liquidation marketplaces (GovDeals, B-Stock) to buy discounted pallets and relist high-margin local items on Facebook Marketplace.
- Automate by building an agent that filters deals and posts listings, then buy only when inquiries convert.

💬 Quote

Build an agent that finds items for sale according to your filters... and then it just posts them all for sale on Facebook Marketplace at a set markup.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner explaining a liquidation arbitrage play

verses.title: Mobile Fuel Delivery Model
verses.text:
- Mobile fuel delivery services top off tanks at a ~20% markup and add subscription fees, driving high annual revenue per customer.
- Customers rarely churn because convenience creates strong habitual retention.

💬 Quote

People are paying to not have to go to the gas station anymore. The churn is next to zero.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner describing mobile fuel delivery demand and retention

verses.title: Comments Reveal Excuses, Not Barriers
verses.text:
- Viral comment sections reveal more excuses than real obstacles; most concerns (insurance, liability) are solvable.
- Execution beats critique: people invent reasons not to start rather than build and test.

💬 Quote

People are very creative in coming up with excuses and reasons why they won't do something.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner reflecting on audience reactions to business ideas

verses.title: RV Parks As Stable Cashflow Assets
verses.text:
- Chris prefers RV parks because you maintain infrastructure, not units, and demand holds in good and bad economies.
- He bought parks, extended seasons, raised rents, and grew net profit from ~$700K to ~$1.2M annual EBITDA.

💬 Quote

When the economy's good, millennials travel. When it tanks, people live in your RV parks full-time.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner explaining why RV parks hedge economic cycles

verses.title: Sell AI Implementations To SMBs
verses.text:
- Offer AI automation implementations to small businesses for free initially to earn trust, then charge a flat monthly fee.
- Start with clear wins like 24/7 AI voice agents for after-hours calls and emergency patch-throughs to owners.

💬 Quote

I would want to implement AI in small businesses for free to get my foot in the door, earn their trust, show myself as an expert, and then start charging.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner on launching an AI automation agency for small businesses

verses.title: Buy And AI‑Enable Businesses
verses.text:
- Buying established service businesses and adding AI can extract margin improvement faster than organic agency growth.
- Use AI to cut operational costs and boost EBIT to revalue the business at acquisition multiples.

💬 Quote

Go buy a business at a fair price today... and I know that I have this lever of AI that the 55, 65-year business owner wasn't going to do.
— Sam Parr

Sam Parr discussing buying businesses and applying AI for value uplift

verses.title: Start With Marketing, Then Build
verses.text:
- Start product development from marketing and brand first: pick a name and channel, then build the minimal product around that demand.
- Use audience and simple marketing hypotheses to validate before heavy engineering or feature builds.

💬 Quote

I start with a marketing channel in mind. And then I build an idea around that.
— Chris Koerner

Chris Koerner on beginning ventures with domain/name and marketing strategy

chapters.title: A Buyer-Centric Approach to Competitive Positioning
chapters.description: Exploring competitive positioning in B2B sales, the podcast covers strategies for facing status quo, direct, and 'do nothing' competitors. It emphasizes the importance of value proposition, deal champions, objection handling, and reducing customer indecision. Tips include disqualifying unqualified leads, addressing objections, and educating customers on trade-offs.

verses.title: 3 Types of Competitors
verses.text:
- There are three main types of competitors: status quo (what the customer is currently doing), direct competitors (alternatives considered by the customer), and 'do nothing' (customer chooses no solution).
- Direct competitors are only truly competitors if they appear on a customer's shortlist, differing from product management's broader view.

💬 Quote

In my mind, I think we have three big buckets of competitors [...] status quo. [...] The second type [...] direct competition [...] And then the last competitor [...] 'do nothing', which is often mistaken for [...] status quo.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on defining the three main competitor categories in her customer positioning work.

verses.title: 3 Types of Competitors
verses.text:
- There are three main types of competitors: status quo, direct, and 'do nothing'.
- Status quo competitors are existing solutions, often manual or legacy systems, that customers currently use.

💬 Quote

In my mind, I think we have three big buckets of competitors [...] status quo. That's whatever the customer happens to be doing now. That might look like something that you would consider a competitor, but quite often it's things that don't look anything like us.
— April Dunford

April Dunford explaining the various types of competitors that companies encounter.

verses.title: Three Competitor Types
verses.text:
- There are three main types of competitors: status quo, direct, and 'do nothing'.
- Status quo competitors represent what the customer is currently doing, often involving manual processes or legacy systems.

💬 Quote

In my mind, I think we have three big buckets of competitors [...] status quo. That's whatever the customer happens to be doing now.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on the three types of competitors companies face.

verses.title: Reduce Pain of Change
verses.text:
- When competing against the status quo, demonstrate the value of switching to your product.
- Equally important, minimize the pain of change through tactics like data migration tools, training, and professional services.

💬 Quote

Often the customer will look at other options and decide that status quo is OK because the pain of change is simply too much. So we have to be very careful if our main competition is status quo. We can't rely just on demonstrating the value of coming to our side. We also have to show that the pain of change is worth the value.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on competing with the status quo.

verses.title: Disqualify Ruthlessly
verses.text:
- Ruthlessly disqualify accounts that don't fit your ideal customer profile (ICP), especially when facing direct competition.
- Focus resources on accounts that highly value your unique offering.

💬 Quote

If we have direct competition, we need to really understand what our differentiated value is [...] and then we need to be really, really tight on what are the characteristics of a target account that make them really, really care a lot about the value that only we can deliver.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on competing against direct competitors.

verses.title: Help Your Champion
verses.text:
- When customers are indecisive, ensure the deal champion is prepared.
- Equip them to address objections from other stakeholders involved in the B2B purchase.

💬 Quote

But we have maybe not armed the champion sufficiently to handle the objections of all the other players involved in making the deal happen.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on why customers can become indecisive.

verses.title: Handling Objections
verses.text:
- Anticipate potential objections from stakeholders like the IT department (e.g., integration issues).
- Arm your champion with answers to these objections so they can address them even without you present.

💬 Quote

If you find you're losing a lot of these deals [...] due to objections [...] with other stakeholders, we need to get really clear on what those objections are and [...] do a better job of handling those objections in advance and arming the champion to be able to do it even if we're not in the room.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on addressing sales objections from different stakeholders.

verses.title: Overcome Indecision
verses.text:
- To help close deals, make specific recommendations to customers, especially when there are many options.
- This reduces the burden of choice and increases their confidence.

💬 Quote

What I would recommend [...] is this package with these options. [...] it improved your chances of closing the deal by 144 percent.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on making specific recommendations to improve closing rates.

chapters.title: Getting to the Root of the "We Have No Differentiation" Problem
chapters.description: Dive deep into the myth of product differentiation in B2B tech. Discover why many companies fail to recognize their unique value. Explore how competitive alternatives shape buyer decisions and the pitfalls of focusing solely on losses. Learn about 'value blindness' in marketing teams and the dangerous spread of 'product pessimism' within organizations. Uncover the vital role of cross-functional collaboration in aligning positioning strategies and ensuring sustained business growth.

verses.title: No Differentiation = Failing Product
verses.text:
- In B2B tech, a product with no differentiation is likely failing.
- This leads to poor sales, lack of growth, and overall business problems.

💬 Quote

In my experience, in a B2B technology company, that is simply not the way it works.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on why positioning won't help a truly undifferentiated product.

verses.title: Value Blindness
verses.text:
- Fast-growing companies sometimes mistakenly believe they have no differentiation.
- In reality, there *is* differentiated value; they just don't recognize it yet.

💬 Quote

I heard it from another company that was doing 50 million revenue and growing at about 40% year on year. Like these things just don't match up [...] because every day a customer is looking at you and all their other choices and they're picking you.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on why fast-growing companies sometimes mistakenly think they lack differentiation.

verses.title: Focus on Wins, Not Losses
verses.text:
- To better understand your differentiation and market positioning, focus deeply on your wins.
- Analyze why customers choose you, whom they compare you against, and what value means to them instead of obsessing over losses.

💬 Quote

I think it's actually super, super instructive to get really deep on your wins. [...] The more we understand that, the better we're going to do and the better we're going to understand our differentiation.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on why focusing on wins is crucial for understanding differentiation.

chapters.title: How to Turn a Competitor's Strength into a Weakness
chapters.description: Explore the intriguing concept of turning competitor strengths into weaknesses using innovative marketing strategies. Discover how challenger brands can find opportunities in niche markets. Learn about the distinctions between platform and tool positioning, and the strategies for competing against legacy brands and low-cost products. Delve into the importance of appealing to end users versus decision-makers and how to create compelling value propositions. Real-world examples from companies like Salesforce and IBM illustrate successful positioning tactics.

verses.title: Tool Vendor Strategies
verses.text:
- Tool vendors can target specific platform weaknesses, highlighting their specialized expertise. 
- Focus on faster deployment, lower risk, and best-of-breed features.

💬 Quote

Some parts of the platform kind of suck, man. Some of the pieces, there's always the underserved, not paid very much attention to crappy part of the suite.
— April Dunford

April Dunford on how individual tools can win.

chapters.title: The Cataclysm Sentence
chapters.description: In this engaging discussion, former producer Rachael Cusick shares her unique perspective on Feynman's cataclysm sentence—what essential wisdom to pass on if all knowledge were lost. The conversation spans emotional farewells, the interplay of science and art, and the quest for humanity's legacy. Cusick reflects on childhood apocalyptic fears and their influence on growth, underscoring resilience and connection across generations. The dialogue weaves humor with profound insights, leaving listeners to ponder their own parting messages.

verses.title: The Only Innate Fears: Falling and Loud Noises
verses.text: As a child, I used to send letters to my family, but they would always return them. This caused a lot of pain and division within our family. I couldn't understand why something as insignificant as skin color would be a reason to hate someone forever. I believe that fear is the root cause of racism and many other conflicts in the world. Our fear holds us back from trying new things, traveling, and meeting new people. The only innate fears we have are falling and loud noises.

chapters.title: Befriending your impostor syndrome with Iceland's president Halla Tómasdóttir
chapters.description: Halla Tómasdóttir, the newly elected President of Iceland, shares her inspiring journey from self-doubt to leadership. She discusses overcoming impostor syndrome and the importance of listening in building trust with constituents. Halla reveals the origins and significance of the 'scarf revolution,' emphasizing unity and positive campaigning. She also explores Iceland's innovative spirit and the need for inclusivity in leadership, making a case for resilience and collaboration in tackling global challenges.

verses.title: Befriending Imposter Syndrome
verses.text:
- Self-doubt and imposter thoughts are normal reactions to big challenges.
- These thoughts can be beneficial and should be befriended, not fought.

💬 Quote

I have learned to befriend them [imposter thoughts and self-doubts] throughout life and not let them prevent me from taking on challenges.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on dealing with self-doubt.

verses.title: Befriending Imposter Syndrome
verses.text:
- Halla Tómasdóttir views imposter syndrome as a "healthy dose of self-doubt" and has learned to befriend it.
- She believes hubris is more dangerous in leadership than self-doubt.

💬 Quote

I have learned to befriend them [imposter thoughts and self-doubts] throughout life and not let them prevent me from taking on challenges.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on dealing with self-doubt.

verses.title: Embracing Emotional Wisdom
verses.text:
- Befriend your self-doubt by treating it as a critical friend, not an enemy.
- Draw on your emotional wisdom and values to overcome fear and take action.

💬 Quote

But I mean it because you could try to fight it or you can befriend it and think about it almost like this very critical roommate or friend who really asks you all the hard questions that one should always ask of themselves.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on befriending imposter syndrome.

verses.title: Overcoming Self-Doubt
verses.text:
- When facing self-doubt, drop from your head to your heart.
- Find courage by focusing on what you truly care about, think, and what matters more than your fear.

💬 Quote

If I'm able to [...] drop from my head to my heart a little more and find some of the wisdom [...] like, what do I really care about? What do I sincerely think? What truly matters to me more than my fear?
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on dealing with self-doubt.

verses.title: Choosing Courage over Comfort
verses.text:
- Halla Tómasdóttir initially hesitated to run for president again after losing in 2016.
-  She realized she'd regret not running more than the challenges of campaigning.

💬 Quote

It would have been easier to not do it, to be honest. But it was a decision that just felt like one I couldn't look back and regret.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on her decision to run for president again.

verses.title: Rethinking Leadership as Service
verses.text:
- Many people avoid leadership roles due to the burdens and sacrifices involved. 
-  Leadership should be viewed as service, asking "How can I serve?" not just "How can I lead?"

💬 Quote

But then I also think we need to rethink leadership as not something that sits in the few and far between presidents and CEOs and people in big positions of power.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on redefining leadership.

verses.title: The Scarf Revolution
verses.text:
- Halla Tómasdóttir wore a scarf during a debate, sparking a "scarf revolution."
- It became a symbol of solidarity and support for her campaign.

💬 Quote

And somehow we created this sense of a community, somewhat accidentally. And so the scarf revolution became a bit of a symbol [...] for us all being in this together.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on the unexpected impact of her scarf.

verses.title: Leading with Hope
verses.text:
- Run positive campaigns based on hope and inspiration, not fear and anger.
-  Focus on sincerity, compassion, and collaboration.

💬 Quote

But in that context, find for myself, and I certainly found in both campaigns, that you can't meet fear, hate, and violence with more of that.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on the importance of positive campaigning.

verses.title: Positive campaigning
verses.text:
- Invite people into something hopeful, inspirational, and joyful, where they feel seen, heard, and valued.
- Voters recognize and crave sincerity, which is often in short supply in politics.

💬 Quote

If we are invited into something better, something that is hopeful, inspirational, joyful, makes us feel good [...] there's a huge appetite for it, but very little supply.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on positive campaigning.

verses.title: The Power of Questions
verses.text:
- Good leaders should focus on asking the right questions, not having all the answers. 
- Key questions include: "Who are you going to choose to be?" and "How do we bridge divides?"

💬 Quote

Well, the first and most important one, I think, is towards each one of us as individuals. It's the question I often ask myself and others, and I asked myself when I was encouraged to run, is who are you going to choose to be at a time like no other for humanity?
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on the importance of asking the right questions.

verses.title: Prioritizing Listening
verses.text:
- To improve listening skills, host more conversations and fewer speeches. 
- Create spaces for authentic dialogue and co-creation.

💬 Quote

I'm trying to hold fewer speeches and host more conversations. [...] But if you want me to come and be in a dialogue with someone from your organization or your event and then invite the audience into a dialogue, then I'm interested.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on how she practices listening.

verses.title: Listening Leadership
verses.text:
- To improve listening skills as a leader, hold fewer speeches and host more conversations.
- Engage in authentic dialogues and invite questions for more sincere interactions.

💬 Quote

I'm trying to hold fewer speeches and host more conversations. [...] I actually find that the conversation format is far more engaging when we allow ourselves to authentically rumble with the challenges and opportunities that are in front of us.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on how she puts listening into practice.

verses.title: Questions over Answers
verses.text:
- Questions are more crucial than answers in leadership.
- Exploiting fear and division is not true leadership.

💬 Quote

I think questions are far more important than the answers, Adam. And I have far more questions than answers.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on the importance of questions.

verses.title: Acknowledging Pain, Inspiring Hope
verses.text:
- Acknowledge the existing pain and anger in the world. 
-  Use challenges as opportunities for growth and progress.

💬 Quote

The fact of the matter is there is a lot of pain. There is a lot of anger. [...] But I know in my own life that I have never really made great progress in anything I've done without going through a bit of a dark alley before.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on the importance of acknowledging pain.

verses.title: Small is Beautiful
verses.text:
- Iceland demonstrates that small entities can be highly creative and impactful.
- Small countries can act as lighthouses for creative solutions and partnerships.

💬 Quote

So I guess I dare to say that Iceland, maybe together with the Nordics, can be a lighthouse for the creative solutions that we need with two challenging problems, particularly along the lines of sustainability, a fully gender-inclusive societies where human rights and democracy thrive because we choose to be peaceful societies that stand for a peaceful world.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on Iceland's potential as a model.

verses.title: Navigating Uncertainty
verses.text:
- Help people navigate uncertainty by addressing their fear, anger, and hate. 
- Focus on fostering hope and resilience, especially among young people.

💬 Quote

Because my single biggest fear, Adam, is the loss of hope or the hopelessness that I'm starting to sense, in particular from young people, that a better world awaits them.
— Halla Tómasdóttir

Halla Tómasdóttir on her biggest fear.

verses.title: Finding Bright Spots
verses.text:
- Find and build upon existing "bright spots" within communities and organizations. 
- Identify, study, and scale what's already working well.

💬 Quote

And they say the virtue of being in a group that's hundreds or thousands or tens or hundreds of thousands of people is there are lots of pockets of excellence. There are versions of the future happening in local communities, in small teams that you want to see. And so your challenge is to find those and then draw attention to them, study them, learn from them, and then scale and spread what's working for them.
— Adam Grant

Adam Grant on identifying and scaling successful practices.

verses.title: Focus on Solutions, Not Just Problems
verses.text:
- Don't only focus on problems, also look for what's working and build on it (Adam).
- Find 'bright spots,' pockets of success in communities or teams, then study, learn, and scale those solutions.

💬 Quote

In addition to asking what's broken, also asking what's working and how do we build on that?
— Adam Grant

Adam Grant on appreciative inquiry

verses.title: Find the Bright Spots
verses.text:
- Don't just focus on problems.
- Also look for what's working well and build upon those successes.

💬 Quote

And I am intrigued by what David Cooperrider has called appreciative inquiry, which is the idea of walking into a community or a workplace or a country. And in addition to asking what's broken, also asking what's working and how do we build on that?
— Adam Grant

Adam Grant on balancing problem-solving with appreciating what's already working.

chapters.title: Brené Brown and Simon Sinek on the leadership skills we need to build
chapters.description: Brené Brown, a research professor known for her work on vulnerability and courage, joins Simon Sinek, an ethnographer and author celebrated for his insights on purpose. Together, they delve into the crucial skills leaders need, emphasizing vulnerability, accountability, and self-awareness. They tackle engaging topics like the dynamics of modern leadership, the balance of empathy and self-care, and the evolving expectations of younger generations. Their lively discussion is packed with humor, anecdotes, and practical insights for cultivating better leadership.

verses.title: Extrovert Thinking
verses.text: The narrator's internet went down, so they left a meeting to think out loud. They would pretend to go to the bathroom and people would worry they ate something bad. This behavior was described as being an extroverted thinker, which is different from being an extrovert.

verses.title: The Tricky Balance between Pausing and Pacing in Public Speaking
verses.text: When I started public speaking, I was told to be more like Pernet and Simon - pause between sentences, slow down, and let my points sink in. But I didn't care about sounding smart, I wanted to be effective. Research showed that thinking and talking slower reduces excitement. So I discovered that varying my pacing and pitch allows me to exude energy while also being understood. You both do this naturally, like professional actors.

chapters.title: Building atomic habits with James Clear
chapters.description: James Clear, author of the bestselling 'Atomic Habits', shares insights into transforming habits and behavior change. He emphasizes that building effective habits involves small, enjoyable changes rather than drastic actions. Clear highlights the power of identity in habit formation, where each small habit contributes to the person you wish to become. He also discusses the importance of evaluating priorities and focusing on meaningful activities for personal growth and success.

verses.title: The Power of Small Improvements and Genuine Interest in Building Better Habits
verses.text: Changing habits is not just about making a single big change, but rather a collection of small improvements that compound over time. To build better habits, it's important to select activities that genuinely interest you, rather than relying on superficial solutions. For example, simply downloading an audiobook app won't make you read more, but it can be a helpful piece of the puzzle. Ultimately, the outcome is driven by the accumulation of many small improvements.

verses.title: The Importance of Consistent Effort and Quality Habits in Achieving Success
verses.text: The key to winning in diving is not just the number of dives, but the consistent effort and quality of practice. The 10,000 hours rule is about 10,000 iterations, not just hours. It's about getting 1% better each day and constantly improving.

verses.title: Focusing on who you want to become instead of what you want to achieve
verses.text: The Cravicon says it's not about putting in 10,000 hours, but rather 10,000 iterations. The key is to strive to be 1% better each day and constantly improve. It's not just about showing up, but about actively seeking ways to carve out advantages. The important question to ask is what is the system oriented toward and what do you want your days to look like. Instead of solely focusing on goals, shift your attention to the kind of person you want to become.

verses.title: The Reinforcing Power of Habits and Identity
verses.text: Habits reinforce your desired identity. Every action is a vote for the type of person you want to be. Align your goals and identity to achieve important results. It's more effective to frame actions as reflections of identity, like being a cheater or a helper, to influence behavior.

verses.title: Finding Compressed Wisdom and the Art of Worldly Wisdom
verses.text: There's an old book called the Art of Worldly Wisdom that I love because it's full of compressed wisdom. It's amazing how this guy was blogging 500 years ago! I used to think compression was just hacking, but you changed my perspective. The downside is that it lacks nuance, but the upside is it's easy to remember. It's like having a bumper sticker that represents a PhD thesis. Surprisingly, you said I'm good at reframing ideas, and that might be my only value.

verses.title: The Most Important Problem
verses.text: There's this page called Great Talks where I collect transcripts from different speeches. One talk by Richard Hamming stood out and it's called You and Your Research. It's not just about scientific research but also about life lessons. Hamming asks thought-provoking questions, like asking scientists about the important problems in their field and why they're not working on them. This question can be applied to personal life too, making us realize that we should prioritize important problems instead of wasting time on low priority ones.

verses.title: Don't imitate one person
verses.text: Pause and reflect on activities that interfere with higher priorities. Beware of imitating successful people without considering context. Learn from patterns and commonalities among 100 people, not just one example. Reflecting on biographies may trigger a knee-jerk reaction.

verses.title: The Most Meaningful Way to Succeed
verses.text: The speaker started a podcast as a way to learn and share interesting information with others. They realized that having two days a week with no scheduled activities helps them be more productive. They believe that helping others succeed is the most meaningful way to succeed themselves. Despite some failures, they still strive to achieve their goals. The speaker appreciates being asked questions and enjoys the fun aspect of their work.

verses.title: The Importance of Contrast in Titles
verses.text: When trying to position an idea, be both specific and general at the same time to create distinctiveness. Habits are personal and universal, specific and general, which makes them a great topic. Contrast is important for catchy titles. Best-selling books often surprise or invert expectations. The focus should always be on what is most useful and actionable for the reader.

chapters.title: Explaining the Dunning-Kruger effect and overcoming overconfidence with David Dunning
chapters.description: Baratunde Thurston, an Emmy-nominated writer and cultural critic, joins David Dunning, a renowned psychologist, to delve into the fascinating Dunning-Kruger effect. They discuss how limited knowledge can lead to overconfidence and the importance of self-awareness. The conversation touches on the dangers of 'doing your own research' and offers strategies for combating overconfidence, both for amateurs and experts. They also explore the role of emotional intelligence in connecting with others and the necessity of shared experiences in overcoming cognitive disagreements.

verses.title: Life Stories and Well-being
verses.text:
- People's life stories often follow either contamination (downhill) or consistently positive arcs.
- Surprisingly, consistently positive narratives aren't the happiest; redemption stories, where negative experiences lead to positive outcomes, are linked to greater well-being.

💬 Quote

If you ask people to narrate the story of their lives, some people end up with contamination narratives where they kind of go downhill. [...] other people end up having pretty consistently positive journeys. And those people do not end up as happy.

On how different types of life stories relate to happiness and well-being.

verses.title: The Value of Hurt
verses.text:
-  Reflecting on the interview, Thurston realized he hadn't fully confronted his past hurt. 
- This realization allowed for a richer, more complete understanding of his life story.

💬 Quote

And in the decade-plus sense, I've been able to see and been willing to sit with the negativity, the hurt, the pain of some moments of my life. And it just adds richness, appreciation...
— Baratunde Thurston

Baratunde Thurston on acknowledging past hurt.

verses.title: Emotional Intelligence
verses.text:
-  Adam Grant noted a disconnect between recognizing and regulating emotions. 
-  Suppressing emotions too quickly can hinder understanding oneself and others.

💬 Quote

I'm so quick to regulate emotions that I don't always even fully process them. And that can make me not only worse at navigating my own complex experiences, but also it makes it harder to understand what other people's emotions are.
— Adam Grant

Adam Grant on emotional intelligence.

verses.title: Shared Emotional Reality
verses.text:
- Shared emotional reality can bridge divides where shared facts fail. 
-  Connecting through pain and other emotions can foster understanding and empathy.

💬 Quote

Our facts can be miles and miles apart. Pain is the same. And that's a bridge we can work with.
— Baratunde Thurston

Baratunde Thurston on shared emotional reality.

verses.title: Curiosity and Learning
verses.text:
- Adam Grant views curiosity as the foundation of learning.
- Without curiosity, personal growth stagnates.

💬 Quote

To me curiosity is the foundation of learning. So if you're not curious, you've stalled your own growth.
— Adam Grant

Adam Grant on the importance of curiosity.

verses.title: Industrializing Creativity
verses.text:
- The unstated assumption driving AI development is that 'more faster is better,' similar to the forces behind fossil fuel extraction and forced labor.
- This industrialization of creativity leads to an overabundance of content, requiring further AI intervention to manage it.

💬 Quote

We are industrializing creativity. We're industrializing art. We're just ratcheting it up.

chapters.title: Finding the joy of service with Milk Bar's Christina Tosi and restaurateur Will Guidara
chapters.description: Christina Tosi, a James Beard award-winning chef and author, joins her partner Will Guidara, a top restaurateur known for transforming Eleven Madison Park. They discuss the transformative power of service in both the culinary world and personal life. Listeners learn about the concept of 'unreasonable hospitality' and the importance of thoughtful gestures. They also emphasize nurturing workplace culture for employee fulfillment and reflect on how meaningful connections enhance the dining experience. With humor, they share personal stories about success and the joy of relationships.

verses.title: Memorable Hospitality
verses.text:
- Restaurant patrons rarely remember specific dishes.
- Thoughtful gestures create lasting memories and surpass culinary experiences in impact.

💬 Quote
