- Expect tension between product managers and engineers about feature scope and technical feasibility.
- Engineers must communicate constraints like UI complexity and localization challenges clearly to PMs.

💬 Quote

The tension is usually between engineering and product because the product people want the button to be 50 times bigger. And then the engineer says, look, we can't make it 50 times bigger because there's this comment button... in Arabic, it's on the wrong side.
— Taylor Hughes

Taylor Hughes on natural tensions between software engineers and product managers

verses.title: Christmas App Crash Anecdote
verses.text:
- Taylor caused a major Christmas morning outage for a toy's companion app due to a compatibility issue.
- His quick fix overwhelmed the servers, crashing the app and frustrating children worldwide.

💬 Quote

And the first thing you have to do is you have to install an iPhone app to control it. [...] Then you download the stupid app, obviously, and then you have to link up your sign in or whatever. And then it just crashes. And so the children around the world are just having this awful time with this.
— Taylor Hughes

Taylor Hughes on a Christmas app crash caused by his fix

verses.title: Manage Technical Debt Pragmatically
verses.text:
- Manage technical debt by balancing quick feature shipping with eventual quality improvement.
- Encourage periodic focus on paying down tech debt to keep codebase healthy.

💬 Quote

Tech debt is kind of like when you keep working like that, you build up, you know, a bunch of broken stuff along the edges and you need to eventually pay down the tech debt by just focusing on quality.
— Taylor Hughes

Taylor Hughes explaining tech debt and the tension with product priorities

verses.title: Savoring Bug Hunting
verses.text:
- Taylor enjoys reproducing mysterious bugs and tracing them to systemic issues.
- Diagnosing and fixing such bugs is a puzzle that reveals deeper system insights.

💬 Quote

I love to reproduce a weird bug, a bug report from a user. [...] It's extremely pleasing to be like, holy crap, like our system's actually really bad at birds.
— Taylor Hughes

Taylor Hughes on the joy of investigating and diagnosing complex software bugs

title: Wisdom From The Top with Guy Raz

chapters.title: 'The Decency Quotient' in Leadership | Ajay Banga
chapters.description: Ajay Banga, a former leader who transformed Mastercard, discusses the vital 'Decency Quotient' in leadership. He emphasizes that a supportive workplace culture hinges on more than intelligence; respect and kindness are crucial. Banga shares how his Indian upbringing shaped his inclusive approach and the importance of diversity in leadership. He also explores the balance of compassion with accountability, reflecting on his journey through corporate transitions and the significance of humility and collaboration in effective management.

verses.title: Career Advice
verses.text:
- Don't overthink career planning or be afraid to switch industries. 
- Focus on understanding consumers or customers, and a lot becomes clearer.

💬 Quote

Don't over plan your career and don't overdo this. Just take things as they come.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga's advice on career planning.

verses.title: Career Advice
verses.text:
- Don't overthink or overplan your career.
- Never refuse a transfer suggested by your boss.

💬 Quote

Don't over plan your career and don't overdo this. Just take things as they come. I never said no, by the way, through all my career to a transfer that my boss has suggested to me. Never.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on career planning.

verses.title: Mastercard's Transformation
verses.text:
- Mastercard shifted from primarily credit-related payments to a diversified technology company.
- This involved expanding into services like data analytics, AI, and cybersecurity, representing a significant portion of their revenue.

💬 Quote

Now you go forward 10 years and we get 27% of our revenue from services. [...] We're now seen as a company that's doing technology and innovation and data and analytics and AI and cyber security.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on Mastercard's transformation.

verses.title: Decency Quotient
verses.text:
- Banga advocates for a winning culture grounded in decency. 
- This involves supporting colleagues and creating a safe environment for seeking help.

💬 Quote

Your DQ is your decency quotient. [...] It's the environment you create for people around you to succeed.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on his leadership philosophy.

verses.title: Decency Quotient
verses.text:
- Ajay Banga emphasizes the importance of a "Decency Quotient" (DQ) in leadership.
- DQ is about creating a supportive environment where people feel comfortable seeking help and are empowered to succeed.

💬 Quote

You gotta bring your DQ to work, and your DQ is your decency quotient. [...] It's the environment you create for people around you, above you, and below you to succeed.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on the importance of decency in leadership

verses.title: Kindness vs. Fairness
verses.text:
- Kindness isn't avoiding tough conversations; it's caring enough to invest in others. 
- Use learning experiences to help people improve and seize future opportunities.

💬 Quote

Kindness is not avoiding a difficult conversation. Kindness is caring enough about the person to invest in them.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on the meaning of kindness in leadership.

verses.title: Leadership as a Privilege
verses.text:
- Bring your heart and mind to work; leadership is a privilege. 
- Guide, help, and provide constructive feedback to those who report to you.

💬 Quote

Bring your heart and your mind to work because leadership is a privilege.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on the responsibility of leadership.

verses.title: Deliberative Leadership
verses.text:
- Deliberative leadership doesn't necessitate quietness; it's about thoughtful action. 
- Combine urgency with listening, gathering information before making decisions.

💬 Quote

Deliberative can still be fairly wanting to build a winning culture. Right, you know what I mean?
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on the nature of deliberative leadership.

verses.title: Staying Grounded
verses.text:
- Banga emphasizes the importance of listening to avoid becoming the smartest person in the room. 
- He values feedback from his team, board, and CFO, which helps him recognize his mistakes.

💬 Quote

- Banga emphasizes the importance of listening to avoid becoming the smartest person in the room. 
- He values feedback from his team, board, and CFO, which helps him recognize his mistakes.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga on the importance of feedback.

verses.title: Self-Critique
verses.text:
- Banga's self-critique highlights his tendency to procrastinate on decisions. 
- This cautiousness, despite his risk-taking nature, remains a challenge for him.

💬 Quote

I think that I'm always dinging myself on being slow on getting things done.
— Ajay Banga

Ajay Banga's self-assessment.

chapters.title: Finding Work That's Got a Mission: Ken Coleman
chapters.description: Ken Coleman, known as “America’s Career Coach,” shares his inspiring journey from various jobs to his true passion in broadcasting. He discusses the importance of finding meaningful work and the pivotal question, "What do you wonder about doing?" Ken explains his transition to broadcasting at age 31 and the challenges faced, including networking and overcoming fear. He emphasizes the Proximity Principle, highlighting how surrounding yourself with the right people can unlock career potential and lead to fulfillment.

verses.title: Reagan Inspiration
verses.text:
- At age 10, Ken Coleman was captivated by Ronald Reagan's speech.
- This sparked his early interest in public service and political communication.

💬 Quote

I watched his acceptance speech for his second term and I was enthralled. And I began to ask a lot of questions about him and learned that my dad was a big fan of his.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on his early interest in public service.

verses.title: Government Boredom
verses.text:
- After a successful gubernatorial campaign, Coleman felt bored in his government role. 
- He realized campaign life's fast pace was more appealing than the slow pace of governing.

💬 Quote

I'm bored out of my skull because political campaign life is very different than governing life.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on his disillusionment with government work.

verses.title: Oprah Inspiration
verses.text:
- An Oprah interview on Larry King Live sparked Coleman's interest in broadcasting.
- Oprah's influence through media resonated with him.

💬 Quote

I influence way more people than I could ever influence if I was a politician.
— Oprah Winfrey

Oprah Winfrey on why she prefers media over politics, inspiring Ken Coleman.

verses.title: Broadcasting Grind
verses.text:
- Coleman took broadcasting classes, interned at a radio station, and did high school football broadcasts. 
- He emceed local events to gain experience and network.

💬 Quote

I emceed at [...] our local town [...] Suwannee Day, figuring I just need to do anything and everything I can to meet people so they see me as this host and broadcaster.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on his early efforts to gain broadcasting experience.

verses.title: First Radio Show
verses.text:
- Coleman pitched and hosted "The Ken Coleman Show" on a small AM radio station.
- His show focused on practical, positive advice across various life areas.

💬 Quote

I wanted to touch all the areas of people's lives where they want to be better and where they had pain points.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on the concept of his first radio show.

verses.title: Start Small, Grow Slow
verses.text:
- Start small and grow slow in your chosen field, like learning to ride a bike on grass.
- This minimizes damage from early failures when few people are paying attention.

💬 Quote

Start small, grow slow.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on building a career.

verses.title: Work vs. Fulfillment
verses.text:
- Societal pressures and educational marketing often prioritize good jobs over fulfilling work.
- This leads to a disconnect between talent, passion, and mission.

💬 Quote

We're so programmed to get good grades so we can get to a good school and get a good job that we forgot how to wonder.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on societal pressures leading to unfulfilling work.

verses.title: Networking vs. Connecting
verses.text:
- Avoid networking events; focus on one-on-one connections.
- Ask for advice and connections, making people feel valued.

💬 Quote

So stop networking, start connecting.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on building genuine relationships.

verses.title: Networking Events
verses.text:
- Avoid traditional networking events.
- Instead, prioritize one-on-one interactions like coffee, lunch, or virtual calls for more meaningful connections.

💬 Quote

I don't know anybody. I don't have a big network. You actually very specifically recommend that people not attend networking events [...] Why?
— Guy Raz

Guy Raz asking Ken Coleman why he recommends avoiding networking events.

verses.title: Networking vs. Connecting
verses.text:
- Stop networking and start connecting.
- Instead of attending networking events, have one-on-one conversations where you ask questions and learn from others.

💬 Quote

And you don't have to have a moleskin or a pencil. [...] But you better be there to learn. And you better be there to ask questions. And that provides value.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on the importance of learning and asking questions in one-on-one conversations.

verses.title: Hidden Networks
verses.text:
- Most people get jobs through acquaintances, not close friends. 
- Intentional conversations in everyday settings can lead to unexpected opportunities.

💬 Quote

It is those simple conversations that many times are the rock that needed to be turned over for that opportunity or for that door to swing wide open.
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on leveraging acquaintances for career opportunities.

verses.title: Two Leadership Questions
verses.text:
- Be a good leader by regularly asking two questions: "How are you doing?" and "How can I help you win?".
- Act on the responses with empathy and support.

💬 Quote

How are you doing? How can I help you win?
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on practical leadership.

verses.title: Two Simple Leadership Questions
verses.text:
- Regularly ask your team two questions: "How are you doing?" (personally) and "How can I help you win?" (professionally).
- Act on their responses with empathy and support.

💬 Quote

You will be a good leader if you just do these two things. These are two questions you ask your people that you're leading, your team, on a very regular basis. The first is, how are you doing? [...] And the second question is, how can I help you win?
— Ken Coleman

Ken Coleman on simple but effective leadership.

chapters.title: Finding Work with Meaning, ft Ken Coleman
chapters.description: Ken Coleman, America's Career Coach, shares insights on finding work with meaning. He discusses his journey to broadcasting, the challenges and importance of connections in the industry, and the revival of the Ken Coleman Show. He emphasizes the importance of aligning talent, passion, and mission to find fulfillment and success in work.

verses.title: Value-Packed Promotions and Expert Career Coaching
verses.text: Offers for new customers including $45 upfront for three months plus taxes and fees at Mint Mobile.com. 1-800-FLOWERS.COM emphasizes heartfelt gift-giving experiences. Ken Coleman, America's Career Coach, assists individuals in finding meaningful work and forming valuable connections through his books and shows, drawing from his personal experiences switching careers in his 30s.

verses.title: Embracing Influence Beyond Politics
verses.text: The insight here is about Oprah's moment of clarity when she chose not to pursue a political career despite being at the peak of her fame. She valued the influence she had through her show, magazine, live events, and other endeavors, realizing that she could impact more lives by staying in the media industry than by entering politics.

verses.title: Work Smart, Not Hard
verses.text: In the 70s, a marketing message aimed at kids and parents emphasized the importance of working smart rather than hard through posters showing a smiling college grad with the message 'work smart' versus a frowning worker in overalls with the message 'not hard, work smart.' This messaging aimed to instill the notion of efficiency and strategic thinking over just putting in hard labor.

verses.title: The Proximity Principle
verses.text: Being around people who are doing the work you want to do and in places where that work is happening is crucial for learning and achieving your goals. The principle emphasizes the importance of surrounding yourself with individuals in the same craft or industry to gain the necessary knowledge and skills.

verses.title: Authentic Passion Trumps Networking
verses.text: The speaker recounts a personal experience where he had to question his commitment to a task, realizing that his passion was strong enough to endure any challenges. This event taught him the importance of having a true passion for something. He also advises against attending traditional networking events, likening them to gatherings of self-serving individuals. Instead, he recommends building relationships through more authentic and personalized interactions like coffee meetings or Zoom calls, emphasizing the value of genuine connections over superficial networking events.

verses.title: The Value of Asking for Connections
verses.text: Asking for connections can open doors and make people feel valuable. Many individuals underestimate the number of connections they have, even if they are introverted or from a small town. By being intentional and reaching out to people for advice, individuals can tap into extensive networks. People should not believe the myth of not knowing enough people, as even a conservative estimate of connections can lead to valuable opportunities.

chapters.title: Peloton: Dara Treseder

verses.title: The Importance of Brand Desire for Creating Brand Awareness
verses.text: Brand desire is the secret sauce of marketing, according to experts. It's important to focus on the target audience and build desire for those people first. Once you have proof points and customer stories, they become ambassadors for your business.

chapters.title: The Gospel of Slow Growth, ft. Jason Fried
chapters.description: Jason Fried, CEO of 37signals, advocates for limited work hours and unconventional work practices. Topics include promoting work-life balance, resisting growth to prioritize independence, Basecamp's remote work culture, advocating for a 40-hour workweek, staying small for long-term success, and the debate of born skills vs learned skills.

verses.title: Value Independence over External Permission
verses.text: The speaker values independence more than a billion dollars, emphasizing that success doesn't solely depend on external funding or validation from venture capitalists. They believe in running a business based on personal values and priorities, taking care of employees, and making a positive impact without the pressure to scale excessively. The speaker prefers staying true to their vision and purpose, even if it means not achieving maximum growth or risking competition from larger companies. They prioritize running a business on their terms and going out of business on their own terms rather than being successful while compromising personal values and vision.

verses.title: Maximize Efficiency with 40-Hour Workweek
verses.text: Implementing a 40-hour workweek maximizes efficiency as it provides ample time for focused work. Working for eight hours a day can be strenuous, akin to an eight-hour flight, emphasizing the need for balance between work and personal time. Employees should aim to complete their tasks within the designated hours and refrain from working after-hours, as most things can wait until the next workday.

verses.title: Focus on Quality over Quantity
verses.text: The speaker, despite being financially successful, does not prioritize accumulating more money. They believe luck plays a significant role in success and do not set financial, revenue, or customer acquisition targets in their business. Instead, they emphasize doing the best work possible and allowing outcomes to unfold naturally.

chapters.title: LEADERSHIP LAB: The Craft of Writing Effectively
chapters.description: Larry McEnerney, Director of the University of Chicago's Writing Program, shares his insights on effective writing for graduate students. He debunks misconceptions in formal writing, emphasizing the importance of audience awareness. McEnerney challenges the typical view of writing as a solitary endeavor, highlighting its role in enhancing reader engagement. He also explores unconventional methods, like using charts to clarify ideas. Ultimately, his approach advocates for adapting writing styles to varied academic audiences to foster meaningful communication.

verses.title: Experts Use Writing to Think
verses.text:
- Experts, unlike journalists or high school students, use writing as a tool to facilitate complex thinking.
- This means their writing process serves a different purpose, helping them explore and solidify ideas at the frontiers of knowledge.

💬 Quote

Unlike a journalist, almost surely you are using your writing process to help yourself think. [...] you have to use writing to help yourself do your thinking.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on how experts approach writing.

verses.title: Experts' Writing Interferes with Reading
verses.text:
- Experts write using language patterns that help them think.
- However, these same patterns interfere with how other experts read, even in the same field.

💬 Quote

Experts use language in one set of patterns to do their thinking, but those very same experts read with a different pattern. So [...] you are interfering with their reading process when you're writing.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on how experts' writing habits conflict with their reading habits

verses.title: Reader Reactions to Mismatched Writing
verses.text:
- When readers encounter text not written in their expected style, they first slow down, rereading passages.
- Then, they struggle to understand, become frustrated, and eventually stop reading.

💬 Quote

You either read more slowly the first time or you have to read and reread and reread and reread. You slow down. [...] Second, you don't understand. Third, [...] you get aggravated. Then, you're done.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney explaining how readers react to poorly written text.

verses.title: Focus on Reader Value
verses.text:
- Prioritize the value of your writing to readers over clarity, organization, and persuasion.
- Understand that different audiences perceive value differently, making reader awareness crucial.

💬 Quote

Value lies in readers, right? Not in a thing.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on the importance of understanding audience value in writing.

verses.title: Professional Writing
verses.text:
- Professional writing is not about conveying your ideas to your readers. 
- It's about changing their ideas.

💬 Quote

You think writing is conveying your ideas. It's not. [...] You think that writing is communicating your ideas to your readers. It is not. What is professional writing? [...] It's not conveying your ideas to your readers [but instead] changing their ideas.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on the purpose of professional writing.

verses.title: Valuable vs. Original Work
verses.text:
- PhD students should focus on doing valuable work, not original work. 
- Creating new knowledge is easy, but creating valuable, impactful knowledge is what truly matters.

💬 Quote

You are not here to do original work. You are here to do valuable work.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on the goal of PhD work.

verses.title: Improving Academic Writing
verses.text:
- To improve your writing, spend 15 minutes weekly circling words that create value for the reader in articles from your field. 
- Look for words like "nonetheless," "widely," "however," "although," "inconsistent," "reported," and "anomaly."

💬 Quote

Go through and circle every word in the writing that is creating value to the readers.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on a practical exercise to improve writing.

verses.title: Focus on the Reader
verses.text:
- The function of writing is to move the conversation forward and change other people's thoughts.
- It's not about preserving your ideas indefinitely or sharing your feelings.

💬 Quote

The function of your writing is to move this conversation forward. [...] Your function is to move a conversation forward. It's not to preserve indefinitely.
— Larry McEnerney

Larry McEnerney on the purpose of academic writing.
